nodes.title: What Is Evolution? Course Intro
nodes.subtitle: Why evolution unifies biology + practical applications (Lec 1 slides 1-9)

popup.heading: Lecture 1 — What Is Evolution?

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Evolution = any change in the inherited traits of a population that occurs from one generation to the next. Evolution occurs ACROSS generations, NOT within an individual's lifetime. Evolution is a SCIENTIFIC THEORY supported by multiple independent lines of evidence — on the same epistemic footing as gravitation, plate tectonics, and aerodynamics. Dobzhansky (1973): "Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution."

sections.label: WHY IT MATTERS
sections.body: Evolution underlies every sub-discipline of biology. Practical applications include agriculture (crop breeding), medicine (antibiotic and vaccine design), conservation (managing genetic diversity), immunology (understanding pathogen adaptation), and epidemiology (predicting outbreak evolution).

sections.label: KEY FIGURES
sections.body: Theodosius Dobzhansky (1973 essay). Charles Darwin (Origin of Species, 1859). These authors established evolution as the central explanatory framework of modern biology.

examples: Dobzhansky: "Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution"
Practical applications include antibiotic resistance, vaccine design, crop improvement, conservation.
warnings: WATCH OUT: Evolution is NOT "organisms improving over time" — it is POPULATION-LEVEL change in allele frequencies across generations.
WATCH OUT: Individuals do not evolve — populations do. A giraffe's neck does not grow longer because it stretches. The population shifts toward longer necks over generations.
popup.mnemonic: CHANGE = Change in allele frequencies over generations — the minimal definition of evolution.

flashcard.front: Why is evolution considered the unifying theory of biology?
flashcard.back: Every subfield of biology — from cell biology to ecology — rests on the insight that organisms share common ancestry and have been modified over time by natural selection, drift, mutation, and gene flow. Without evolution, biological diversity and shared traits across species have no causal explanation (Dobzhansky 1973).

quiz.question: Which of the following is the most precise scientific definition of evolution?
quiz.correct: A change in the frequency of alleles in a population across generations
distractors: Organisms becoming more complex over time
The appearance of new species through sudden mutation
Improvement of individuals during their lifetime

quiz.question: Dobzhansky wrote "Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution." Which scenario BEST illustrates this claim?
quiz.correct: The fact that all organisms use the same genetic code (DNA → RNA → protein) is explained by universal common ancestry — without evolution, this shared molecular machinery would be an inexplicable coincidence
distractors: Antibiotic resistance develops fastest in hospital settings — which proves that bacteria sense when antibiotics are present and mutate in response
Organisms that live in dark caves lose their eyes over time because they stop using them — demonstrating Lamarckian inheritance still operates alongside natural selection
Humans and chimpanzees share 98.7% of their DNA — which means humans evolved FROM chimpanzees over the last 6 million years

quiz.question: A student argues: "My grandfather got stronger by lifting weights, so his children inherited bigger muscles — that is evolution." What is WRONG with this reasoning?
quiz.correct: Changes acquired by somatic (body) cells during an individual's lifetime cannot be passed to offspring; evolution requires heritable changes at the population level across generations
distractors: The grandfather did not produce enough offspring to affect population allele frequencies, so the effect is too small to qualify as evolution
Evolution only applies to natural, not human-influenced, environments — gym training is artificial and exempt from evolutionary theory
Muscle size is not heritable at all, so this trait can never evolve regardless of how strong selection is

quiz.question: Antibiotic resistance is a textbook example of evolution. If a doctor prescribes antibiotics and the patient's infection is "cured" but 10 resistant bacteria survived, which statement is most accurate?
quiz.correct: Selection has eliminated susceptible bacteria and left only the resistant variants, which will reproduce and potentially transfer resistance genes — the population has evolved even if the patient feels better
distractors: The bacteria evolved resistance in response to the antibiotic — they sensed the danger and produced resistance mutations to survive
Since only 10 bacteria survived out of millions, evolution cannot occur because the population is too small to maintain genetic variation
The antibiotic cured the infection by killing all bacteria — any survivors are a statistical fluke and cannot represent true evolutionary change

quiz.question: Which practical application is NOT a standard example used to motivate the study of evolution in BIOL 4230?
quiz.correct: Predicting earthquake patterns from tectonic plate motion
distractors: Designing antibiotics that are less likely to select for resistance
Developing annual vaccine formulations for rapidly evolving pathogens
Improving crop yields through directed breeding programs

quiz.question: Conservation biologists rely on evolutionary principles to manage endangered populations. Which specific concern is MOST directly grounded in evolutionary theory?
quiz.correct: Ensuring that captive breeding programs maintain sufficient genetic variation so the population retains its capacity to respond to future environmental change via selection
distractors: Building larger enclosures to reduce stress hormones in captive animals, improving individual welfare
Providing optimal nutrition so that captive animals reach larger adult body sizes than wild ones
Training animals with positive reinforcement to behave more cooperatively toward human handlers

quiz.question: A key distinction between evolution and individual "change" is TIMESCALE and LEVEL. Which statement correctly contrasts these?
quiz.correct: Evolution operates on populations across generations via changes in allele frequency; individual-level change (growth, learning, acclimation) operates within a single organism's lifetime and is not evolution
distractors: Evolution is any change in a trait, whether at the individual or population level — the timescale distinction is arbitrary
Evolution operates only on somatic cells within individuals; population-level changes are actually ecological, not evolutionary
Evolution and individual change are the same process at different scales — a fast-growing individual is evolving faster than a slow-growing one

quiz.question: Dobzhansky's famous 1973 essay title refers to evolution as the ONLY light by which biology makes sense. Which field of biology would be MOST impoverished without evolutionary theory?
quiz.correct: All fields equally — from molecular biology (why do homologous proteins exist) to ecology (why do niches emerge) to medicine (why do pathogens adapt) — because evolution provides the causal explanation for all shared and divergent biological features
distractors: Only systematics and paleontology — other fields like biochemistry or cell biology function perfectly well without evolutionary concepts
Only medicine — because evolutionary principles are needed for drug design, but cellular mechanisms can be understood without evolution
Only ecology — because evolution shapes species interactions, but molecular and cellular biology are independent of evolutionary theory

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: In what year did Dobzhansky publish the famous essay that defined evolution as the unifying framework of biology?

L3 COMPARISON: A scientist says "evolution is JUST a theory." Evolution is referred to as a scientific THEORY — but with what other well-established scientific theories is it epistemically comparable in the lecture?
quiz.correct: Gravitation, plate tectonics, and aerodynamics — all are scientific theories in the technical sense (well-tested explanatory frameworks), not guesses
distractors: Homeopathy, astrology, and numerology — all called "theories" in some contexts
Theories of literary criticism and political theories — which are equally rigorous scientifically
Mathematical theorems like the Pythagorean theorem — which are directly comparable to scientific theories in how they are proven

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: Which single sentence BEST captures the core scientific definition of evolution as presented in Lecture 1?
quiz.correct: Evolution is any change in the inherited traits of a population that occurs from one generation to the next
distractors: Evolution is the gradual improvement of individual organisms over the course of their lifetimes
Evolution is the spontaneous appearance of new species through single mutational events
Evolution is the extinction of species and the replacement of their ecological niches by unrelated new species

visual.description: Evolution as the unifying hub of biology

regions.label: Core claim
items: Change in allele frequencies over generations

visual.mnemonic: Allele frequency change = evolution. Everything else is a consequence.
visual.trap: Common misconception: evolution means "progress" or "improvement." Evolution is directional only in the sense of local fitness — there is no global ladder.

flashcards.front: What is the precise definition of biological evolution?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Evolution is the change in inherited (heritable) traits within a population across successive generations. It occurs at the population level, not the individual level. It encompasses changes in allele frequencies over time.

**ELI5:** Imagine a jar of mixed jellybeans. Each generation, the mix of colors changes slightly. Evolution is like the jar's color mix shifting over time -- no single jellybean changes color, but the whole jar looks different after many rounds.

flashcards.front: Why is evolution considered the unifying theory of biology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Evolution connects all subdisciplines of biology -- genetics, ecology, physiology, anatomy, development, behavior -- under one explanatory framework. Every biological phenomenon, from molecular to ecosystem level, can be understood through the lens of evolutionary processes (natural selection, drift, mutation, migration).

**ELI5:** Evolution is like gravity for biology. Just like gravity explains why balls fall AND why planets orbit AND why tides happen, evolution explains why bacteria resist drugs AND why flowers are colorful AND why whales have tiny leg bones. One idea ties everything together.

flashcards.front: Who said 'Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution' and when?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Theodosius Dobzhansky, in a 1973 essay published in The American Biology Teacher. This statement encapsulates evolution's role as the central organizing principle of the biological sciences.

**ELI5:** A famous biologist named Dobzhansky said in 1973 that trying to understand biology without evolution is like trying to understand a movie by watching random scenes out of order -- nothing makes sense until you see the whole story.

flashcards.front: What is a scientific theory, and how does it differ from the everyday use of 'theory'?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A scientific theory is a well-substantiated, testable explanation of natural phenomena supported by a large body of evidence from multiple independent lines of inquiry. It has predictive power and has withstood repeated attempts at falsification. It is NOT a guess or hypothesis. Examples: theory of gravity, germ theory, evolutionary theory.

**ELI5:** In everyday talk, 'theory' means a guess ('I have a theory about who stole the cookies'). In science, a theory is more like a proven instruction manual -- it's been tested thousands of times and keeps working. Evolution is a theory like gravity is a theory -- extremely well-proven.

flashcards.front: How does evolution apply to agriculture?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Evolutionary principles guide selective breeding of crops and livestock for desirable traits (yield, disease resistance, drought tolerance). Understanding evolution also helps manage pest resistance to pesticides and herbicides through resistance management strategies.

**ELI5:** Farmers have been using evolution for thousands of years without knowing it -- picking the biggest corn ears to plant next year is basically guiding evolution. It also explains why bugs keep becoming resistant to pesticides.

flashcards.front: How does evolution apply to medicine?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Evolutionary medicine explains antibiotic resistance (natural selection favoring resistant bacteria), virulence evolution, cancer as somatic evolution, pharmacogenomics (population-level drug response variation), and vulnerability to diseases due to evolutionary mismatch with modern environments.

**ELI5:** When you take antibiotics but don't finish the full course, the toughest bacteria survive and multiply. That's evolution happening inside your body. Doctors need to understand evolution to stay one step ahead of germs.

flashcards.front: How does evolution apply to conservation biology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Conservation biologists use evolutionary principles to maintain genetic diversity in endangered populations, manage inbreeding depression, identify evolutionarily significant units (ESUs) for protection, and predict adaptive potential to environmental change (e.g., climate change).

**ELI5:** If all the cheetahs in the world are almost genetically identical (which they nearly are), one disease could wipe them all out. Conservation scientists use evolution knowledge to keep populations genetically healthy, like making sure a sports team has players with different skills.

flashcards.front: How does evolution apply to immunology and epidemiology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Pathogen evolution drives antigenic variation (flu, HIV), necessitating updated vaccines. Epidemiological modeling incorporates evolutionary dynamics to predict outbreaks, track strain emergence, and design surveillance programs. The immune system itself is shaped by coevolutionary arms races with pathogens.

**ELI5:** Germs are constantly evolving to dodge our immune system, like a burglar learning new tricks. Scientists who study diseases have to track how germs evolve to predict the next outbreak and make vaccines that actually work.

flashcards.front: When was On the Origin of Species published, and by whom?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection was published by Charles Darwin on November 24, 1859. It presented the theory of evolution by natural selection with extensive supporting evidence from biogeography, paleontology, embryology, and morphology.

**ELI5:** Charles Darwin published his big book in 1859. It was like dropping a bombshell in science -- he showed that all living things are related and change over time through a process called natural selection.

flashcards.front: MISCONCEPTION: Do individuals evolve?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** No. Individuals do NOT evolve. An individual's genotype is fixed at conception (barring somatic mutations). Evolution is a population-level phenomenon -- it is the change in allele frequencies within a population over generations. An individual can be selected for or against, but the individual itself does not evolve.

**ELI5:** You don't evolve during your lifetime. You're born with your genes and you keep them. Evolution is like a classroom where each year the mix of students changes -- no single student transforms, but the class as a whole shifts over time.

flashcards.front: MISCONCEPTION: Is evolution progressive or goal-directed?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** No. Evolution has no inherent direction, goal, or purpose. It does not trend toward 'improvement' or 'higher' organisms. Natural selection favors traits that increase relative fitness in the CURRENT environment. What is adaptive today may be maladaptive tomorrow. There is no teleology in evolutionary processes.

**ELI5:** Evolution isn't climbing a ladder toward perfection. It's more like water flowing downhill -- it just goes wherever the ground takes it right now. Bacteria aren't 'lower' than humans; they're incredibly well-adapted to their own world.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between population-level and individual-level change?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Individual-level change (e.g., muscle growth, tanning, learning) is phenotypic plasticity -- non-heritable modification within a single lifetime. Population-level change is evolution -- heritable shifts in allele/trait frequencies across generations due to selection, drift, mutation, or gene flow.

**ELI5:** If you lift weights and get strong, that's YOU changing (not evolution). But if over many generations, a whole group of people tends to be born stronger because strong people survived better, THAT's evolution. The difference: one is temporary and personal, the other is permanent and population-wide.

flashcards.front: List at least 5 applied fields where evolutionary thinking is essential.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Agriculture -- crop/livestock breeding, pest resistance management. (2) Medicine -- antibiotic resistance, cancer biology, evolutionary medicine. (3) Conservation -- genetic diversity management, ESU identification. (4) Immunology -- vaccine design, immune system evolution. (5) Epidemiology -- disease surveillance, pathogen tracking. Also: forensics, bioinformatics, biotechnology, ecology.

**ELI5:** Evolution matters for: (1) growing better food, (2) fighting superbugs at the hospital, (3) saving endangered animals, (4) making flu shots, and (5) tracking disease outbreaks. It's basically everywhere in biology.

flashcards.front: Why is evolution compared to gravity as a scientific theory?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Both are scientific theories supported by overwhelming evidence from multiple independent lines of inquiry. Both have immense predictive power. Both explain observable phenomena (falling objects / biodiversity patterns). Neither is 'just a guess.' The comparison highlights that calling evolution 'just a theory' misunderstands what 'theory' means in science.

**ELI5:** Nobody says 'gravity is just a theory, maybe things will float tomorrow.' Evolution has the same level of proof. Saying it's 'just a theory' is like saying gravity is 'just a theory' -- technically true in science-speak, but it's one of the most proven ideas we have.

nodes.title: Flu Virus: Evolution in Action
nodes.subtitle: H1N1 rapid evolution — a real-time case study (Lec 1 slides 10-21)

popup.heading: Lecture 1 — Influenza as Evolution Case Study

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Influenza is a microscopic shell of protein encasing 13 genes distributed across 8 RNA segments. Hemagglutinin (HA) binds the virus to host cells; Neuraminidase (NA) enables new virus particles to detach. Evolution by mutation + selection happens in real time and can be observed within a single infection.

sections.label: MECHANISM
sections.body: Each infected cell produces thousands of new virus copies. Error-prone RNA replication generates mutational diversity. Selection then spreads variants that escape host immunity. ANTIGENIC DRIFT = gradual HA/NA amino acid substitutions that reduce vaccine effectiveness. REASSORTMENT = two strains co-infect one host cell and exchange entire RNA segments, producing novel pandemic strains.

sections.label: KEY EXAMPLES
sections.body: H1N1 (2009) arose by TRIPLE REASSORTMENT of bird, pig, and human flu genes. Hensley et al. (2009, Science) passaged flu through vaccinated mice for 9 cycles — the virus evolved altered HA; unvaccinated control mice showed NO HA change. This directly demonstrated selection for vaccine escape. The annual vaccine is needed because HA and NA continually accumulate amino acid substitutions.

sections.label: KEY TERMS
sections.body: HA (hemagglutinin), NA (neuraminidase), antigenic drift, antigenic shift / reassortment, 8 RNA segments, H1N1.

examples: H1N1 2009 pandemic: traced back to swine-origin triple reassortment of bird, pig, and human flu genes; tracked in real time via global genomic sampling.
Hemagglutinin and neuraminidase surface proteins accumulate amino acid substitutions — driving antigenic drift.
Hensley et al. 2009 Science: vaccinated mice → flu evolved altered HA after 9 infection cycles; unvaccinated controls showed no HA change. Direct demonstration of vaccine-escape selection.
warnings: WATCH OUT: Not all mutations are selected for — most are neutral or deleterious. Only rare beneficial substitutions are fixed by selection.
popup.mnemonic: HA-NA = HemAgglutinin and NeurAminidase — the flu's two most-evolving proteins and the targets of vaccines.

flashcard.front: How does influenza virus illustrate rapid evolution by natural selection?
flashcard.back: Influenza has an 8-segment RNA genome and a high error-rate RNA polymerase (no proofreading). Errors create variants constantly. Host immunity selects for variants whose surface proteins (HA, NA) evade recognition, so amino acid substitutions accumulate on the surface. Comparing samples worldwide showed that the 2009 H1N1 pandemic strain descended from reassortment between human, avian, and swine flu lineages — tracked in real time by phylogenetic analysis.

quiz.question: Which feature of influenza most directly explains why new vaccine formulations are needed each year?
quiz.correct: High mutation rate in surface proteins HA and NA drives antigenic drift
distractors: The virus physically grows larger each year
Humans gradually lose immunity to all pathogens over time
New animal hosts invent new flu types annually

quiz.question: Antigenic DRIFT and antigenic SHIFT both create new flu strains, but they differ in mechanism. Which scenario correctly describes antigenic SHIFT?
quiz.correct: A pig is simultaneously infected with both a human flu strain and an avian flu strain; the two viruses' 8-segment RNA genomes reassort inside the pig, producing a novel strain carrying surface proteins that human immune systems have never encountered
distractors: A flu virus replicates inside a human cell and the error-prone RNA polymerase substitutes one amino acid in the HA protein — gradually accumulating small surface changes over many replication cycles
A flu virus mutates so rapidly that the patient's immune system cannot produce antibodies fast enough, allowing the virus to evade immunity through sheer mutation rate
A new flu strain emerges each year when influenza acquires a proofreading polymerase by horizontal gene transfer from a bacterium it co-infects in the respiratory tract

quiz.question: The 2009 H1N1 pandemic strain was reconstructed phylogenetically and traced to a "triple reassortment" event. What does this mean, and which evolutionary principle does it most directly illustrate?
quiz.correct: The strain combined RNA segments from three independent lineages (human, avian, and swine flu) — illustrating that genetic variation in influenza can arise not just from mutation but from recombination between entire viral genomes (reassortment)
distractors: The strain mutated three separate hemagglutinin amino acids simultaneously in a single infected pig, illustrating the multiplicative effect of high viral mutation rates
Three separate flu pandemics merged into one dominant strain through natural selection, eliminating less fit strains and retaining only the most virulent combinations
The strain underwent three rounds of rapid evolution within a single human host whose immune system was compromised — demonstrating that intra-host evolution is the primary source of pandemic strains

quiz.question: A flu vaccine researcher notes that HA mutations on the outer surface of the protein are far more common in circulating strains than HA mutations that change internal structural residues. Which evolutionary explanation is MOST correct?
quiz.correct: Mutations on the outer surface face antibody-mediated immune selection every generation — variants that escape existing antibodies have higher fitness, so surface mutations are selectively favored and fixed faster than neutral structural mutations
distractors: Internal HA residues mutate more slowly because the RNA polymerase has lower error rates when copying sequences that code for structurally important regions
Surface mutations are more common because the outer regions of HA are encoded by a different RNA segment that has an inherently higher mutation rate than segments encoding internal proteins
Immune selection removes any mutation that changes internal HA structure because the host immune system specifically targets internal epitopes that are exposed during viral assembly

quiz.question: The influenza genome consists of how many RNA segments, and why is this structure critical to antigenic shift?
quiz.correct: 8 segments — a segmented genome allows reassortment (genome-level mixing) when two different flu strains co-infect the same host cell, so entire RNA segments can be exchanged wholesale
distractors: 2 segments — one for HA and one for NA, so reassortment can only swap one protein at a time
1 single RNA molecule — reassortment works via recombination of this single strand during replication
23 segments — matching the number of human chromosomes, which is why humans are frequent hosts

quiz.question: Flu's RNA polymerase is described as "error-prone" and lacks proofreading. Why is this feature the foundation of flu's rapid evolution?
quiz.correct: Without proofreading, every replication cycle introduces many mutations — generating a huge pool of genetic variation each day within a single infected host, which is the raw material for immune escape and antigenic drift
distractors: Error-prone polymerase is inefficient, so flu replicates more slowly than DNA viruses, giving natural selection more time per generation to act
Error-prone polymerase directly causes reassortment by breaking RNA segments into multiple pieces during copying
The polymerase specifically targets the HA and NA genes for mutation while preserving other segments, which is why only surface proteins evolve

quiz.question: HA and NA are highlighted as the two most-evolving flu proteins. Why are these TWO proteins, rather than internal proteins, the primary targets of annual vaccine redesign?
quiz.correct: HA and NA sit on the virion surface and are the main targets of neutralizing antibodies — vaccines elicit antibodies against them, and the virus must evolve these specific proteins to escape immunity
distractors: HA and NA are the only two proteins encoded by the flu genome, so vaccines by definition target them exclusively
HA and NA mutate randomly while internal proteins are protected by evolutionary constraints that prevent any mutation at all
Internal flu proteins are identical to human proteins, so vaccines cannot target them safely; HA and NA are the only viral-specific targets

quiz.question: Why is flu evolution considered a "real-time" case study of evolution rather than a historical inference?
quiz.correct: Global genomic sampling allows researchers to sequence circulating strains week by week and directly observe allele frequency changes in HA/NA, constructing phylogenies that track evolution as it unfolds
distractors: Flu is the only pathogen that has been directly observed to speciate in the lab under controlled conditions
Flu samples are preserved in the fossil record of sedimentary rocks, allowing direct measurement of ancient mutation rates
Flu evolution is instantaneous within individual hosts, so researchers can observe complete speciation events within a single patient

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: How many genes and how many RNA segments does influenza have?
quiz.correct: 13 genes on 8 RNA segments
distractors: 8 genes on 13 RNA segments
23 genes on 23 RNA segments
100 genes on a single circular DNA molecule

quiz.question: L2 MECHANISM: In the Hensley et al. (2009) Science experiment, how did the experimenters directly demonstrate that vaccination drives HA evolution in flu?
quiz.correct: They passaged flu through vaccinated mice for 9 infection cycles and observed altered HA sequences, while flu passaged through unvaccinated control mice showed no HA change — isolating vaccine-mediated selection as the cause
distractors: They inserted random mutations into HA and observed that vaccinated mice got sicker — demonstrating that HA mutations increase virulence independently of selection
They compared HA sequences between wild pigeons and vaccinated chickens and found that vaccinated birds carried more HA variation — evidence for vaccine-driven diversification
They used CRISPR to edit HA in vitro and confirmed that only vaccinated hosts could support replication of edited variants — showing selection at the genome-editing level

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: The 2009 H1N1 pandemic strain is described as a "triple reassortment." Which three source lineages contributed genes to it?
quiz.correct: Bird, pig, and human flu lineages — their genes reassorted inside a pig host to produce the novel pandemic strain
distractors: Bat, horse, and dog flu lineages
Three independent human flu lineages with no animal origin
Only swine flu lineages from different pig farms

visual.description: Flu evolution loop: error-prone replication → surface mutation → selection by host immunity
visual.mnemonic: Error → Drift → Escape → Spread.
visual.trap: Antigenic DRIFT (small mutations, gradual) ≠ antigenic SHIFT (reassortment of whole RNA segments, sudden pandemic strains).

flashcards.front: What is the function of hemagglutinin (HA) in influenza?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Hemagglutinin (HA) is a surface glycoprotein on the influenza virion that binds to sialic acid residues on host cell surface receptors, mediating viral attachment and entry into the host cell via receptor-mediated endocytosis. It is the primary target of neutralizing antibodies.

**ELI5:** HA is like a key on the outside of the flu virus. It fits into a lock on your cells to break in. Your immune system tries to recognize this key, which is why the virus keeps changing it.

flashcards.front: What is the function of neuraminidase (NA) in influenza?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Neuraminidase (NA) is a surface enzyme that cleaves sialic acid residues, enabling release of newly assembled virions from the host cell surface. It prevents viral self-aggregation and facilitates spread to new cells. Drugs like oseltamivir (Tamiflu) inhibit NA.

**ELI5:** NA is like a pair of scissors. After the virus copies itself inside your cell, NA cuts the new viruses free so they can escape and infect more cells. Tamiflu works by jamming those scissors.

flashcards.front: What is antigenic drift?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Antigenic drift is the gradual accumulation of point mutations in genes encoding surface antigens (primarily HA and NA) during viral replication. Because RNA polymerase lacks proofreading, errors accumulate over time, causing incremental changes in antigenic properties. This is the primary reason for seasonal flu epidemics.

**ELI5:** Antigenic drift is like a person slowly changing their disguise -- a new hat here, different glasses there. Each year the flu changes just enough that your immune system doesn't fully recognize it anymore.

flashcards.front: What is antigenic shift (reassortment)?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Antigenic shift is the abrupt, major change in influenza surface antigens resulting from reassortment -- the exchange of entire RNA genome segments between two or more different influenza strains co-infecting the same host cell. This produces novel viral subtypes to which the population has little or no immunity, potentially causing pandemics.

**ELI5:** Antigenic shift is like two people swapping entire outfits instead of just accessories. When two different flu viruses infect the same cell, they can mix and match their gene pieces to create a totally new virus nobody's immune system has ever seen. That's how pandemics start.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between antigenic drift and antigenic shift?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Drift = gradual point mutations in HA/NA genes during replication (small changes, seasonal epidemics). Shift = sudden reassortment of entire RNA segments between different strains co-infecting one cell (major changes, potential pandemics). Drift is continuous; shift is episodic. Drift affects existing subtypes; shift can create entirely new subtypes.

**ELI5:** Drift = slowly repainting a car one panel at a time (it's still recognizable but a bit different). Shift = swapping the entire engine, doors, and hood between two different cars to make a Frankenstein car nobody recognizes.

flashcards.front: How many RNA segments does the influenza genome contain?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The influenza A genome consists of 8 single-stranded, negative-sense RNA segments encoding approximately 13 proteins (some segments encode multiple proteins through alternative splicing or overlapping reading frames). The segmented nature enables reassortment.

**ELI5:** The flu's instruction manual comes in 8 separate booklets (not one big book). Because they're separate, two different flu viruses in the same cell can shuffle booklets like trading cards -- that's what makes the flu so good at reinventing itself.

flashcards.front: What was the origin of the 2009 H1N1 pandemic virus?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The 2009 H1N1 ('swine flu') pandemic virus was a triple reassortant containing RNA segments from avian, swine (North American and Eurasian lineages), and human influenza strains. This novel combination arose through sequential reassortment events in pigs, which served as mixing vessels.

**ELI5:** The 2009 swine flu was a Frankenstein virus -- it had pieces from bird flu, pig flu (two different kinds), and human flu all mixed together. Pigs caught all three types and their cells accidentally shuffled the pieces into one super-new virus.

flashcards.front: Describe the Hensley et al. (2009) experiment on flu evolution.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Hensley et al. (2009, Science) serially passaged influenza virus through vaccinated mice for 9 sequential cycles. The virus evolved altered HA with mutations in antigenic sites, enabling immune escape. Control virus passaged through unvaccinated mice showed no such antigenic changes. This demonstrated that immune pressure drives directional selection on HA.

**ELI5:** Scientists took flu virus and kept putting it into mice that had been vaccinated, over and over, 9 times. Each time, the virus that survived was slightly better at dodging the vaccine. By the end, the virus had changed its disguise. Flu in unvaccinated mice didn't change -- proving the vaccine pressure forced the virus to evolve.

flashcards.front: Why do we need a new flu vaccine every year?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Continuous antigenic drift in HA and NA means circulating strains progressively diverge from vaccine strains. Annual reformulation is necessary to match the predicted dominant circulating strains for the upcoming season. The WHO Global Influenza Surveillance Network monitors viral evolution to recommend vaccine composition.

**ELI5:** The flu keeps changing its outfit (antigenic drift), so last year's wanted poster (vaccine) doesn't match this year's criminal. Scientists have to take a new photo every year and update the poster so your immune system knows what to look for.

flashcards.front: What makes the influenza virus's replication error-prone?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Influenza's RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) lacks 3'-to-5' exonuclease proofreading activity, resulting in a mutation rate of approximately 10^-3 to 10^-4 substitutions per nucleotide per replication cycle. This high error rate generates extensive genetic variation upon which selection can act.

**ELI5:** When the flu copies its genes, the copying machine has no spell-check. It makes about 1 typo for every 1,000-10,000 letters it copies. Most typos are meaningless, but occasionally one helps the virus escape your immune system -- and that mutant wins.

flashcards.front: Are most mutations in influenza beneficial to the virus?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** No. The vast majority of mutations are neutral (no fitness effect) or deleterious (reduce fitness). Only a small fraction are advantageous. However, given the enormous population sizes and rapid replication of viruses, even rare beneficial mutations (e.g., immune escape mutations in HA) arise frequently in absolute numbers.

**ELI5:** Most mutations are like random typos in a recipe -- they either don't matter or ruin the dish. But the flu makes billions of copies of itself, so even if only 1 in a million typos is helpful, that still means thousands of 'lucky' mutants appear. It's a numbers game.

flashcards.front: How does reassortment differ mechanistically from point mutation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Point mutation = a single nucleotide change within one RNA segment during replication. Reassortment = the physical exchange of entire RNA segments between two or more different viral strains co-infecting the same host cell. Reassortment requires co-infection; point mutation does not. Reassortment produces much larger antigenic changes in a single event.

**ELI5:** Point mutation is like changing one word in a sentence. Reassortment is like ripping out entire chapters from two different books and binding them into one new book. One is a tiny tweak; the other is a major remix.

flashcards.front: What are the H and N numbers in flu naming (e.g., H1N1, H5N1)?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The H number designates the hemagglutinin subtype (18 known: H1-H18) and the N number designates the neuraminidase subtype (11 known: N1-N11). Different combinations (e.g., H1N1, H3N2, H5N1) represent distinct surface antigen profiles. Subtypes are classified by antigenic reactivity, not sequence alone.

**ELI5:** Think of H and N as the virus's first and last name. H is the type of key it uses to break into cells (18 varieties), and N is the type of scissors it uses to escape (11 varieties). H1N1 and H5N1 are as different as 'John Smith' and 'Emily Johnson.'

flashcards.front: What is the basic structure of an influenza virion?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Influenza A is an enveloped virus with a lipid bilayer derived from the host cell membrane. HA and NA glycoproteins project from the envelope. Inside is a protein shell (matrix protein M1) surrounding 8 ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes, each consisting of an RNA segment wrapped around nucleoprotein (NP) with associated RNA polymerase.

**ELI5:** The flu virus is like a tiny ball wrapped in a stolen piece of your cell's skin. Sticking out of it are HA keys and NA scissors. Inside the ball are 8 little instruction scrolls (RNA segments) wrapped in protein.

flashcards.front: What did the Hensley experiment's control group demonstrate?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The control group (virus passaged through unvaccinated mice) showed no significant antigenic changes in HA over the same number of passages. This demonstrated that the mutations observed in the experimental group were not simply due to serial passage or genetic drift, but were specifically driven by immune-mediated selection pressure.

**ELI5:** The control proved it wasn't just random change. Flu that went through mice with no vaccine didn't bother changing its disguise. Only the flu facing the vaccine had a reason to evolve. No pressure = no change. That's how we know the immune system was the driving force.

nodes.title: Lecture 1 Take-Home & Course Info
nodes.subtitle: Why evolution matters + course logistics (Lec 1 slides 22-30)

popup.heading: Lecture 1 — Take-Home & Course Information

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Evolution is PREDICTIVE. Flu surveillance demonstrates that strains can be tracked (and predicted to spread) before widespread detection. Monitoring pig farms is evolutionarily motivated because pigs serve as "mixing vessels" for avian and human flu strains in reassortment events. The course textbook is Evolution: Making Sense of Life (2nd Edition). Dr. Travis Robbins' own research centers on the ecology and evolution of lizards (Sceloporus), reptile physiology, life history, and thermal adaptation.

sections.label: WHY IT MATTERS
sections.body: Understanding evolutionary principles equips students to anticipate pathogen adaptation, design conservation strategies, and engage scientifically with applied problems in medicine, agriculture, and climate change.

sections.label: COURSE EXPECTATIONS
sections.body: BIOL 4230 emphasizes reading assigned material, attending lectures, and applying concepts — passive memorization is insufficient.

popup.mnemonic: Read → Attend → Apply: passive reading isn't enough for BIOL 4230.

flashcard.front: What are the three main reasons the instructor gives for why understanding evolutionary principles is important?
flashcard.back: (1) It explains patterns we observe in all living organisms; (2) It has direct practical applications (medicine, agriculture, conservation); (3) It provides the only coherent framework for asking "why" questions in biology.

quiz.question: According to Lecture 1, which feature most distinguishes evolutionary biology as a SCIENCE rather than just a historical account of life?
quiz.correct: It generates testable, falsifiable predictions about allele frequencies, fitness, and adaptation — not just post-hoc narratives
distractors: It relies on direct observation of speciation events, which occur over human-observable timescales
It explains only past events and makes no predictions about future population change
Its claims are insulated from genetic evidence because behavior cannot be fossilized

quiz.question: A student says "Evolution is just a theory, so we should not consider it established science." What is the BEST response using the scientific meaning of "theory"?
quiz.correct: In science, "theory" refers to a well-tested explanatory framework supported by multiple independent lines of evidence — the same status as atomic theory or germ theory, not a guess
distractors: Evolution is actually a scientific law, not a theory, because it has been observed in the lab and in the wild and cannot be disputed
The statement is valid: until scientists observe a new species forming in nature, evolutionary theory remains speculative and cannot claim the status of established science
Science does not use the word "theory" — the correct term is "hypothesis," and evolution remains a hypothesis until it can be verified by direct experiment on deep time scales

quiz.question: Which practical application most DIRECTLY depends on evolutionary principles being correct?
quiz.correct: Designing annual flu vaccines by tracking which surface-protein variants are spreading due to immune-mediated selection in the human population
distractors: Developing better fertilizers by testing which nitrogen concentrations produce the highest crop yield in controlled greenhouse trials
Mapping the human genome by sequencing DNA fragments and assembling them based on overlapping nucleotide sequences
Producing insulin for diabetics by inserting the human insulin gene into bacterial plasmids and fermenting the bacteria at scale

quiz.question: Robbins emphasizes that students should arrive to BIOL 4230 prepared to READ, ATTEND, and APPLY rather than passively memorize. Which exam question type BEST requires application rather than recall?
quiz.correct: A novel scenario where a population of island lizards shows dramatic color divergence from the mainland — students must identify which evolutionary force(s) are operating and predict what evidence would distinguish them
distractors: Listing the five assumptions of Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium in order from most to least important
Defining "genetic drift" in two sentences as it appears in the course glossary
Matching each pre-Darwin thinker (Linnaeus, Cuvier, Lamarck, Lyell) to his major contribution

quiz.question: A student asks: "Can evolutionary theory predict the FUTURE course of evolution?" What is the most scientifically rigorous answer?
quiz.correct: Evolutionary theory generates testable short-term predictions (e.g., the breeders equation predicts response to selection one generation ahead) but long-term predictions are constrained by environmental unpredictability and the stochasticity of mutation and drift
distractors: No — evolution is purely historical and cannot make predictions; it describes only past events
Yes — evolutionary theory can predict the exact form of all future species over any timescale using the breeders equation alone
Yes, but only if the population is in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, since HWE is the only predictive framework in evolutionary biology

quiz.question: Which of the three main reasons the instructor identifies for studying evolution aligns with Dobzhansky's claim that "nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution"?
quiz.correct: Evolution explains the patterns we observe in all living organisms — from shared genetic code to adaptations to geographic distributions
distractors: Evolution ensures that all species will eventually improve over geological time toward a perfect form
Evolution justifies religious interpretations of biological origins and is philosophically necessary for ethical reasoning
Evolution provides a framework for ranking species from primitive to advanced, which is the foundation of biological classification

quiz.question: Robbins highlights that evolution has the three pillars: Mechanisms, Evidence, and Applications (MEA). Which course activity most directly addresses the "Evidence" pillar?
quiz.correct: Examining fossil records, homologous structures, and phylogenetic data that document descent with modification across lineages
distractors: Calculating expected heterozygosity using the Hardy-Weinberg equation
Developing a new antibiotic drug for clinical testing
Listing the names of 19th-century naturalists in chronological order

quiz.question: The course emphasizes that passive reading is insufficient for BIOL 4230. Which study strategy most closely aligns with the course's "Read → Attend → Apply" model?
quiz.correct: Reading assigned material before lecture, attending to refine understanding, then working practice problems that require applying concepts to novel scenarios
distractors: Memorizing every term in the textbook glossary and reviewing flashcards exclusively
Attending lecture without reading the textbook and relying only on class notes
Reading the textbook multiple times without attempting any practice problems

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: What is the textbook used in BIOL 4230 (as described in Lecture 1)?
quiz.correct: Evolution: Making Sense of Life (2nd Edition)
distractors: On the Origin of Species, Darwin
Principles of Genetics, Hartl
The Selfish Gene, Dawkins

quiz.question: L2 MECHANISM: Why does Dr. Robbins emphasize monitoring pig farms as an evolutionary concern?
quiz.correct: Pigs serve as a "mixing vessel" in which avian and human flu strains can co-infect and reassort, producing novel pandemic strains — monitoring pigs allows early detection of such recombinant viruses
distractors: Pigs are the only mammal species in which flu can mutate — other mammals do not support flu replication
Pigs produce a unique enzyme that accelerates HA evolution beyond what is possible in other hosts
Pig farms are legally required to report all animal diseases, making them the easiest surveillance sites for flu

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: Dr. Travis Robbins' own research program focuses on:
quiz.correct: Ecology and evolution of lizards (Sceloporus), reptile physiology, life history, and thermal adaptation
distractors: Molecular phylogenetics of bacteria
Human evolutionary psychology
Marine mammal acoustic communication

visual.mnemonic: MEA: Mechanisms → Evidence → Applications.

flashcards.front: Why are pigs considered 'mixing vessels' for influenza?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Pigs express both avian-type (alpha-2,3-linked sialic acid) and human-type (alpha-2,6-linked sialic acid) receptors in their respiratory tract. This dual receptor expression allows co-infection by avian and human influenza strains, enabling reassortment of RNA segments and generation of novel pandemic-potential viruses.

**ELI5:** Pigs have two types of locks on their cells -- one that bird flu keys fit and one that human flu keys fit. So pigs can catch both at the same time. When both viruses are inside the same pig cell, their gene pieces get shuffled together like a deck of cards, creating brand-new viruses.

flashcards.front: How does evolutionary biology make predictions (using flu as an example)?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Evolutionary theory predicts that RNA viruses with high mutation rates and large population sizes will undergo rapid adaptation under selection pressure. This predicts: (1) circulating flu strains will diverge from vaccine strains (requiring annual updates), (2) antiviral resistance will emerge under drug pressure, (3) reassortment in mixing vessels can produce pandemic strains. Global surveillance programs are built on these evolutionary predictions.

**ELI5:** Because we understand how evolution works, we can predict the flu will keep changing its disguise. That's why scientists watch flu viruses around the world all year -- they're trying to guess what next year's flu will look like so they can update the vaccine in advance. Evolution lets us see the future (sort of).

flashcards.front: What organism does Dr. Robbins study?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Dr. Robbins studies lizards in the genus Sceloporus (fence lizards/spiny lizards). This research focuses on evolutionary biology questions using these squamate reptiles as a model system.

**ELI5:** The professor studies a group of lizards called Sceloporus -- they're the spiny lizards you might see sunning themselves on rocks or fences. They're great for studying evolution because there are lots of different species that live in different environments.

flashcards.front: What is the course textbook for Evolution (BIOL 4230)?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Evolution: Making Sense of Life, 2nd edition. This is the primary text for the course and covers the breadth of evolutionary biology from molecular to macroevolutionary scales.

**ELI5:** The textbook is called 'Evolution: Making Sense of Life' (2nd edition). It's the main book you need for the class.

flashcards.front: What are the three main course expectations for BIOL 4230?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Read the assigned material before class. (2) Attend lectures regularly. (3) Apply concepts, not just memorize facts -- the course emphasizes understanding evolutionary processes and applying them to novel scenarios.

**ELI5:** Three rules: (1) Do the reading before class (not after!), (2) Show up to lectures, (3) Don't just memorize -- actually understand how evolution works so you can use the ideas on new problems you haven't seen before.

flashcards.front: Why is evolution described as a 'predictive' science?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Evolutionary theory generates testable, falsifiable predictions about: future trait distributions, resistance emergence timelines, phylogenetic relationships, fossil intermediates, and adaptive responses to environmental change. The flu surveillance system is a direct application of evolutionary prediction -- forecasting strain evolution to design vaccines months in advance.

**ELI5:** Evolution isn't just about the past -- it helps us predict what's coming next. Scientists predicted bacteria would evolve antibiotic resistance before it happened. They predict which flu strains will be common next winter. That's the power of understanding the rules of the game.

flashcards.front: How does the flu case study illustrate all core evolutionary concepts from Lecture 1?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The flu demonstrates: (1) heritable variation (HA/NA genetic variants), (2) population-level change (strain frequencies shift over time), (3) natural selection (immune pressure selects for antigenic escape mutants), (4) practical application (vaccine design, surveillance), (5) predictive power (forecasting dominant strains). It is NOT individual change -- viral populations evolve.

**ELI5:** The flu is a perfect mini-example of everything in Lecture 1. There's variety in flu genes, some survive better than others when your immune system attacks, and over time the population of flu viruses changes. Scientists use this knowledge to make vaccines. It's evolution happening in fast-forward.

nodes.title: Pre-Darwin Evolutionary Thinking
nodes.subtitle: Linnaeus, Cuvier, Lamarck — the road to Darwin (Lec 2 slides 1-9)

popup.heading: Lecture 2 — Evolutionary Thinking Before Darwin

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Before Darwin, Western thought largely held that species were fixed and unchanging (the "Great Chain of Being" from the Ancient Greeks onward). A series of naturalists and geologists gradually chipped away at this view during the 18th-19th centuries — documenting extinction, deep time, and even proposing mechanisms of change — culminating in the mechanism of natural selection.

sections.label: KEY FIGURES
sections.body: Ancient Greeks (Great Chain of Being; species fixed). Archbishop Ussher (1664): calculated Creation at October 23, 4004 BC, 9 am. Linnaeus (1707-1778): binomial nomenclature and hierarchical classification under a Divine Plan. Buffon (1707-1788): proposed "organic particles" transforming organisms; argued Earth was old. James Hutton (1726-1797): uniformitarianism. Cuvier (1769-1832): paleontologist who documented EXTINCTION and proposed catastrophism. William Smith (1769-1839): organized geological history by fossils. Lamarck (1744-1829): FIRST formal theory of evolution via inheritance of acquired characteristics (mechanism wrong, but correctly recognized change).

sections.label: WHY IT MATTERS
sections.body: Darwin did not arrive at evolution in a vacuum. The groundwork of extinction (Cuvier), deep time (Hutton/Lyell), classification (Linnaeus), and the first mechanistic theory (Lamarck) made natural selection conceptually possible by 1859.

quotes: "Special creation" = the pre-Darwinian view that each species was divinely created in its present form.
examples: Linnaeus (1707-1778): classified organisms into hierarchical groups — kingdom, class, order, genus, species.
Cuvier (1769-1832): documented extinction using fossils, proposed catastrophism.
Lamarck (1744-1829): first formal theory of evolution — inheritance of acquired characteristics (incorrect mechanism, but correctly recognized change).
Lyell: uniformitarianism — geological processes are gradual and ongoing (influenced Darwin).
Archbishop Ussher (1664): calculated the Creation at 9 am on October 23, 4004 BC.
William Smith (1769-1839): organized geological history by fossils.
popup.mnemonic: LCLLL = Linnaeus (classification) → Cuvier (extinction) → Lamarck (first mechanism) → Lyell (deep time) → Darwin.

flashcard.front: What was Lamarck's theory of evolution and why was its mechanism ultimately rejected?
flashcard.back: Lamarck proposed that organisms change during their lifetime in response to use/disuse, and that these acquired traits are passed to offspring (e.g., giraffe necks stretching). Modern genetics shows that changes to somatic tissues are not inherited — only changes to germ line DNA pass to offspring. Lamarck was correct that evolution happens and that it is an ongoing process, but the mechanism (inheritance of acquired characteristics) is false for most traits. Lamarck deserves credit for being the first to propose a mechanistic theory.

quiz.question: Which of these pre-Darwin thinkers FIRST articulated a formal mechanism for evolutionary change, even though that mechanism was wrong?
quiz.correct: Jean-Baptiste Lamarck
distractors: Carl Linnaeus
Georges Cuvier
Charles Lyell

quiz.question: Lamarck proposed that a blacksmith's children would inherit stronger arms because the father developed them through use. Modern genetics shows this is false. But which modern phenomenon is sometimes superficially compared to Lamarckism, and why is the comparison misleading?
quiz.correct: Epigenetic inheritance — chemical modifications to histones or DNA methylation can sometimes persist across one or two generations, but this involves regulatory tags on fixed DNA sequences, not the acquisition of structural changes in body tissues, and the effect rarely persists beyond F2
distractors: Genetic drift — small population effects can cause rare alleles to increase in frequency without selection, which resembles Lamarck's idea that the environment directly modifies the genome
Phenotypic plasticity — a frog that grows longer legs in deep water passes that trait to offspring, proving Lamarck was essentially correct about environmentally induced heritable change
Horizontal gene transfer — bacteria can acquire useful genes from other bacteria in the environment, which is the molecular equivalent of the inheritance of acquired characteristics Lamarck described

quiz.question: Cuvier used catastrophism to explain why species in deeper (older) rock strata differ from those in shallower strata. How does this differ from the uniformitarianism that Lyell advocated, and which view better supported Darwin?
quiz.correct: Lyell's uniformitarianism — that the same geological processes operating today (erosion, sedimentation, uplift) operated in the past at the same rates — implied Earth is enormously OLD, giving Darwin the deep time necessary for gradual natural selection to produce species-level differences
distractors: Catastrophism was actually more useful to Darwin because sudden catastrophic events could produce rapid speciation, which is more consistent with the abrupt species boundaries seen in the fossil record

Both views were equally useful to Darwin: uniformitarianism provided deep time, while catastrophism explained mass extinctions that created ecological opportunities for new species to fill vacated niches
Lyell's uniformitarianism undermined Darwin because gradual geological change implied environments were too stable to generate the variation in selective pressures that drives natural selection

quiz.question: A 19th-century biologist who accepted Cuvier's work (extinctions are real, species are fixed by divine creation) but rejected Lamarck (no mechanism for change) would most likely hold which view about diversity?
quiz.correct: Multiple separate creation events produced modern species — extinctions eliminated earlier creations, and new species were divinely created to replace them (the "progressive creationism" position)
distractors: All present-day species are descended from the original Creation and none have gone extinct — apparent fossil differences reflect incomplete preservation, not true extinction
Species gradually transform through use and disuse into the forms we see today — Cuvier's extinctions provide the raw material for Lamarckian change across geological time
Evolution occurs but is divinely guided — God uses Lamarckian mechanisms to improve species over time, with Cuvier's extinctions representing failed experiments

quiz.question: Carl Linnaeus (1707-1778) is best known for which foundational contribution to biology that — despite his commitment to special creation — later aided evolutionary thinking?
quiz.correct: Developing a hierarchical classification system (kingdom, class, order, genus, species) that organized organisms into nested groups — the nested pattern itself is a prediction of common descent
distractors: Proposing that species can transform over time through the inheritance of acquired characteristics
Documenting extinction using fossils found in distinct rock strata
Developing the principle of uniformitarianism to explain gradual geological change

quiz.question: Which contribution is CORRECTLY matched to its thinker?
quiz.correct: Lyell — uniformitarianism (geological processes are gradual and ongoing)
distractors: Lamarck — catastrophism (sudden destruction events shape species)
Cuvier — inheritance of acquired characteristics
Linnaeus — deep-time geology and the age of the Earth

quiz.question: "Special creation" is defined in Lecture 2 as the pre-Darwinian view that:
quiz.correct: Each species was divinely created in its present form and has not changed since
distractors: Each species arose once through a single mutation and then gradually transformed through use and disuse
Species are created fresh with each generation to match current environmental conditions
Species are created gradually over geological time through continuous divine intervention

quiz.question: Lyell's principle of uniformitarianism was especially important for Darwin because it implied:
quiz.correct: The Earth is enormously old — providing the deep time required for gradual natural selection to produce species-level differences
distractors: Catastrophes periodically reset biodiversity, requiring divine re-creation of species
Geological processes operate differently in the past than today, making historical inference impossible
All species must be younger than 6,000 years because geological processes are too slow to produce older rocks

quiz.question: Which statement BEST captures Lamarck's actual historical contribution, giving him appropriate credit while noting his error?
quiz.correct: Lamarck deserves credit for being the first to propose a formal mechanism for evolution and correctly recognizing that evolution is ongoing — his specific mechanism (inheritance of acquired characteristics) was wrong, but proposing a mechanism at all was a breakthrough
distractors: Lamarck was entirely wrong about evolution — his work has no historical value and should be dismissed in a modern evolution course
Lamarck was entirely correct — modern genetics has confirmed that acquired traits can be inherited across generations via DNA methylation, validating his original theory
Lamarck only contributed to taxonomy and classification, not to evolutionary mechanism — he should be remembered alongside Linnaeus

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: Archbishop Ussher (1664) calculated the exact date of the Creation. According to his calculation, when did it occur?
quiz.correct: October 23, 4004 BC, at 9 am
distractors: January 1, 10000 BC, at midnight
March 21, 1000 BC, at dawn
July 4, 1776 AD, at noon

quiz.question: L4 APPLICATION: A paleontologist in 1800 shows the French Academy a skeleton of an extinct mammoth recovered from Siberia. This evidence most directly supports which pre-Darwin thinker's work?
quiz.correct: Cuvier — who documented extinction using fossils and argued that species could disappear completely from the Earth
distractors: Linnaeus — whose hierarchical classification depends on extinct forms
Lamarck — whose theory of acquired characteristics applies only to extinct lineages
Ussher — whose biblical chronology specifically predicted the existence of such fossils

visual.mnemonic: Linnaeus named, Cuvier buried, Lamarck mechanized, Lyell stretched time.
visual.trap: Do NOT confuse Lamarck's (wrong) inheritance of acquired characteristics with modern epigenetic inheritance — the latter is real but rare and does not explain most adaptation.

diagram.title: Darwin — Five Ingredients of Natural Selection (Lec 2)

nodes.label: High reproductive potential
nodes.detail: Every species can produce far more offspring than can possibly survive. Lec 2 examples: Aphis fabae can generate 524 billion aphids in a single year; Staphylococcus aureus would blanket Earth 7 feet deep in 24 hours with no limits; from one elephant pair, Darwin calculated 19 million elephants in 750 years. Thomas Malthus (1766–1834) formalized the idea in his 1798 "Essay on the Principle of Population," which Darwin read in October 1838 and explicitly credited as the spark for natural selection.
nodes.mnemonic: 524B aphids/yr — no population actually reaches its reproductive ceiling.
nodes.watchOut: This is an OBSERVATION, not a cause. Overproduction alone produces no change.

nodes.label: Populations stable in size
nodes.detail: Despite enormous reproductive potential, populations do not grow without bound. Malthus argued that populations grow geometrically while food supply grows arithmetically — so most offspring must die before reproducing. Alfred Russel Wallace independently reached the same insight in 1858 while in the Malay Archipelago, also citing Malthus.
nodes.mnemonic: Malthus: geometric births meet arithmetic food → stability is enforced by death.
nodes.watchOut: Short-term booms happen, but long-term equilibrium is the rule.

nodes.label: Limited resources → struggle
nodes.detail: Food, mates, territory, water, and nest sites are finite. Individuals must compete — the "struggle for existence" (Darwin 1859, Ch. 3). The struggle is often passive: outgrowing a neighbor, tolerating drought, evading a parasite.
nodes.mnemonic: Struggle is mostly quiet, not bloody.
nodes.watchOut: Struggle does not require direct combat — differential survival is enough.

nodes.label: Phenotypic variation
nodes.detail: No two individuals are identical. Variation spans morphology, physiology, behavior, and performance. Darwin documented variation across pigeon breeds, barnacles, and Galápagos finches. Without phenotypic differences, selection has nothing to grip.
nodes.mnemonic: No variation → no selection, period.
nodes.watchOut: Non-heritable differences (scars, sunburn, learned tricks) don’t evolve.

nodes.label: Heritability
nodes.detail: Trait values must be transmitted from parents to offspring. Darwin had no mechanism — Mendel’s 1866 peas were unknown to him, and genetics was not rediscovered until 1900. Yet breeders had shown heritability empirically for centuries. Wallace, co-announcing the theory at the Linnean Society on 1 July 1858, also lacked a transmission mechanism.
nodes.mnemonic: Parents must "send" the trait to offspring for selection to stick.
nodes.watchOut: If heritability is zero, NS cannot occur even with perfect variation and DRS.

nodes.label: Differential reproductive success
nodes.detail: The logical inference: given overproduction, limited resources, heritable variation, and competition, individuals with advantageous heritable traits will leave disproportionately more offspring. This is the operational definition of natural selection — not "survival of the fittest" but "differential reproductive success conditional on phenotype."
nodes.mnemonic: VHI-DRS: Variation + Heritability + Inference → Differential Reproductive Success.
nodes.watchOut: Fitness = lifetime reproductive output, not just survival to adulthood.

nodes.label: Evolution by natural selection
nodes.detail: Over generations, allele frequencies shift toward variants that improve relative fitness in the current environment. Populations — not individuals — evolve. Joint Darwin/Wallace presentation was read at the Linnean Society on 1 July 1858, and Darwin’s "On the Origin of Species" followed on 24 November 1859. (Wallace mention: the theory is correctly called Darwin–Wallace natural selection, though Darwin’s evidence was more extensive.)
nodes.mnemonic: allele frequencies change — that IS evolution.
nodes.watchOut: Selection acts on phenotypes, but evolution is measured as allele frequency change.

diagram.mnemonic: VHI-DRS: Variation + Heritability + Inference → Differential Reproductive Success → Evolution.

flashcards.front: What was the Great Chain of Being?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The Great Chain of Being (scala naturae) was a pre-evolutionary worldview positing a fixed, hierarchical ordering of all matter and life, from minerals to plants to animals to humans to angels to God. Species were considered immutable, perfectly designed, and occupying fixed positions. This framework precluded evolutionary thinking.

**ELI5:** People used to think everything in nature was arranged on a permanent ladder -- rocks at the bottom, plants above them, animals higher, humans near the top, angels above, and God at the very top. Nothing could change its rung. It was like assigned seating that could never be rearranged.

flashcards.front: What was Archbishop Ussher's calculation, and when did he publish it?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Archbishop James Ussher (1664) calculated, based on biblical genealogies and chronologies, that Earth was created on October 23, 4004 BC, at approximately 9:00 AM. This young-Earth estimate (~6,000 years) severely constrained the time available for evolutionary change and was widely accepted for centuries.

**ELI5:** In 1664, a church leader named Ussher added up all the ages in the Bible and decided Earth was created on October 23, 4004 BC, at 9 in the morning. That made Earth only about 6,000 years old -- way too short for evolution to work. (We now know Earth is about 4.5 billion years old.)

flashcards.front: What were Linnaeus's contributions, and what were his dates?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Carl Linnaeus (1707-1778) developed binomial nomenclature (genus + species naming, e.g., Homo sapiens) and the hierarchical classification system (Kingdom, Phylum, Class, Order, Family, Genus, Species). Though he believed species were fixed and created by God, his nested hierarchical system inadvertently reflected evolutionary relationships.

**ELI5:** Linnaeus (1707-1778) invented the naming system we still use -- giving every organism a two-part name like a first and last name (Homo sapiens = 'wise human'). He also organized life into groups within groups, like folders inside folders on a computer. He didn't believe in evolution, but his filing system accidentally showed how species are related.

flashcards.front: What were Buffon's contributions, and what were his dates?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Georges-Louis Leclerc, Comte de Buffon (1707-1788) proposed that Earth was much older than Ussher's estimate, suggested organisms could change over time through 'organic particles,' and noted similarities between species in different regions. He moved thinking toward an ancient, dynamic Earth, though he did not propose a mechanism for species change.

**ELI5:** Buffon (1707-1788) was one of the first to say 'Hey, Earth might be WAY older than 6,000 years, and animals might change over time.' He noticed that similar animals in different places looked a bit different. He didn't figure out HOW they changed, but he planted the seed of the idea.

flashcards.front: What was James Hutton's contribution, and what were his dates?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** James Hutton (1726-1797) proposed uniformitarianism -- the principle that the same natural laws and processes operating today have always operated in the past, and that geological features are explained by slow, continuous processes over immense time periods. This 'deep time' concept was essential for evolutionary theory.

**ELI5:** Hutton (1726-1797) looked at rocks and said, 'The same rain, wind, and rivers we see today carved these mountains and canyons -- it just took millions of years.' This idea of slow change over huge amounts of time (uniformitarianism) was a crucial ingredient Darwin needed later.

flashcards.front: What is uniformitarianism?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Uniformitarianism is the geological principle that natural processes (erosion, sedimentation, volcanism, etc.) operate at roughly the same rates and by the same mechanisms today as they have throughout Earth's history. 'The present is the key to the past.' Proposed by James Hutton, it implies Earth is extremely old (deep time), providing the temporal framework necessary for gradual evolutionary change.

**ELI5:** Uniformitarianism means nature's rules don't change. The same forces shaping Earth today -- rivers wearing down rocks, volcanoes building mountains -- have been doing the same thing for billions of years. If you watch a river slowly carve a small groove, imagine it doing that for a million years. That's how you get the Grand Canyon.

flashcards.front: What were Cuvier's contributions, and what were his dates?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Georges Cuvier (1769-1832) established extinction as a scientific fact through comparative anatomy of fossil and living organisms. He proposed catastrophism -- the idea that periodic sudden, violent events (floods, volcanic eruptions) caused mass extinctions, after which new species appeared. He opposed evolutionary transmutation but demonstrated that species could disappear entirely.

**ELI5:** Cuvier (1769-1832) proved that species could go extinct -- before him, people thought God wouldn't let that happen. He studied fossil bones and showed they belonged to animals that no longer exist. He thought big disasters wiped them out (catastrophism). He didn't believe in evolution, but proving extinction was a huge step.

flashcards.front: What is catastrophism?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Catastrophism is the geological doctrine that Earth's features and the fossil record are primarily explained by sudden, violent, short-lived events (catastrophes) rather than gradual processes. Proposed by Cuvier as an alternative to uniformitarianism. Each catastrophe supposedly destroyed existing life, followed by repopulation (sometimes interpreted as new divine creation).

**ELI5:** Catastrophism says big, sudden disasters (like massive floods or eruptions) shaped the Earth and wiped out animals, and then new ones appeared. It's like thinking the world gets rebuilt after each disaster, rather than changing slowly and steadily.

flashcards.front: What was William Smith's contribution, and what were his dates?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** William Smith (1769-1839), the 'Father of English Geology,' discovered the principle of faunal succession -- that rock strata could be identified and correlated across distances by their characteristic fossil assemblages. He produced the first large-scale geological map of England, organized by fossil content. This showed a consistent temporal ordering of life forms.

**ELI5:** William Smith (1769-1839) figured out that specific types of fossils are always found in specific layers of rock, and these layers are always in the same order everywhere. It's like finding that the same songs always play in the same order on a playlist -- it tells you which layer is older or younger.

flashcards.front: What was Lamarck's theory of evolution, and what were his dates?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Jean-Baptiste Lamarck (1744-1829) proposed the first comprehensive theory of biological evolution, involving two mechanisms: (1) the principle of use and disuse (organs used frequently become stronger; unused organs degenerate), and (2) inheritance of acquired characteristics (modifications gained during an organism's lifetime are passed to offspring). While the mechanism was incorrect, he correctly proposed that species change over time.

**ELI5:** Lamarck (1744-1829) was the first to publish a real theory of evolution. He thought if a giraffe stretched its neck to reach high leaves all its life, its babies would be born with longer necks. That mechanism is wrong (your gym muscles don't get passed to your kids), but he deserves credit for saying 'species change over time' before Darwin.

flashcards.front: Why was Lamarck's mechanism of evolution wrong?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Inheritance of acquired characteristics is incorrect because changes to somatic (body) cells during an organism's lifetime do not alter the DNA in germline (reproductive) cells. Only mutations in germ cells are heritable. August Weismann demonstrated this with his mouse tail-cutting experiment (no effect on offspring tail length across 22 generations). Modern genetics confirms the Weismann barrier between soma and germline.

**ELI5:** If you cut off a mouse's tail, its babies are still born with tails. That's because what happens to your body doesn't change the instruction manual (DNA) in your egg or sperm cells. Working out at the gym doesn't give your kids bigger muscles -- your body changes aren't sent back to your genes.

flashcards.front: How did pre-Darwin thinkers collectively set the stage for evolutionary theory?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Each contributed a necessary piece: Hutton/Lyell provided deep time (uniformitarianism). Cuvier established extinction as fact. Smith demonstrated temporal ordering of life. Buffon suggested species change and an old Earth. Linnaeus provided a classification system reflecting natural groupings. Lamarck proposed the first transmutation theory. Together, they dismantled the static worldview and created the intellectual conditions for Darwin's synthesis.

**ELI5:** Darwin didn't come out of nowhere. Hutton said 'Earth is super old.' Cuvier said 'Species can go extinct.' Smith said 'Life comes in a sequence.' Buffon said 'Animals change.' Lamarck said 'Here's how (sort of).' Each one laid a brick, and Darwin built the house.

flashcards.front: Put these pre-Darwin thinkers in chronological order by birth: Cuvier, Hutton, Lamarck, Linnaeus, Buffon, Smith.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Linnaeus 1707-1778 and Buffon 1707-1788 (born same year), (2) Hutton 1726-1797, (3) Lamarck 1744-1829, (4) Cuvier 1769-1832 and Smith 1769-1839 (born same year). Key note: Linnaeus/Buffon were contemporaries, as were Cuvier/Smith.

**ELI5:** Timeline: Linnaeus & Buffon (1707) came first, then Hutton (1726), then Lamarck (1744), then Cuvier & Smith (1769). Fun fact: two pairs were born the same year! It's like a relay race where each runner passed the baton to the next generation of thinkers.

flashcards.front: What was the significance of establishing deep time for evolutionary theory?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Deep time (Earth being billions of years old rather than thousands) was essential because evolution by natural selection requires vast time periods for small, incremental changes to accumulate into the diversity of life we observe. Without deep time, there is insufficient time for gradual evolutionary change. Hutton's uniformitarianism and Lyell's elaboration provided this framework.

**ELI5:** If Earth were only 6,000 years old, there wouldn't be enough time for a fish to eventually become a human through tiny changes. But 4.5 billion years? That's plenty of time. Discovering that Earth is ancient was like giving evolution the time it needed to work.

nodes.title: Darwin's Voyage of the Beagle
nodes.subtitle: The observational foundation of evolutionary theory (Lec 2 slides 10-12)

popup.heading: Lecture 2 — Darwin's Voyage

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: At age 22, Charles Darwin joined the HMS Beagle (1831-1836) as the ship's naturalist. The 5-year circumnavigation took him along South America and through the Galápagos, and he also read Lyell's Principles of Geology during the voyage, absorbing uniformitarianism in real time.

sections.label: KEY OBSERVATIONS
