sections.body: Darwin collected specimens of Galápagos birds that London ornithologist John GOULD later identified as all FINCHES — the key insight that catalyzed his thinking. Fossil and living armadillos in South America suggested an ancestral connection (descent with modification). The Andes uplift and volcanic island formation gave him first-hand evidence for Lyell's deep time.

sections.label: PUBLICATION TIMELINE
sections.body: Darwin waited OVER 20 YEARS before publishing — meticulously collecting evidence from barnacles, pigeon breeding, plant hybridization, and biogeography. Alfred Russel WALLACE independently reached identical conclusions; their joint 1858 Linnean Society presentation went unnoticed at the time. Origin of Species was published in 1859.

sections.label: KEY FIGURES
sections.body: Charles Darwin, John Gould, Charles Lyell, Alfred Russel Wallace.

examples: 1831-1836: HMS Beagle circumnavigation. Darwin is 22 at departure.
Galápagos finches: slight morphological differences between islands — a key observation that later fueled natural selection theory.
John Gould (London ornithologist) identified Darwin's Galápagos birds as all FINCHES — the insight that led Darwin to see adaptive divergence.
Fossil and living armadillos in South America suggested ancestral connections.
Darwin waited over 20 years before publishing On the Origin of Species (1859), partly due to fear of controversy.
Alfred Russel Wallace independently reached the same conclusions; joint 1858 Linnean Society presentation went unnoticed.
popup.mnemonic: Beagle→Book: 5 years voyage (1831-36) + 23 years thinking = Origin (1859).

flashcard.front: Why did Darwin wait so long (more than 20 years) to publish his theory of evolution by natural selection?
flashcard.back: Darwin was a meticulous scientist who wanted his argument to be airtight. He spent the decades after the Beagle voyage accumulating evidence from barnacles, pigeon breeding, plant hybridization, biogeography, and correspondence with other naturalists. He also knew the theory would be religiously and socially controversial. He was finally prompted to publish in 1858 when Alfred Russel Wallace independently arrived at the same theory and sent Darwin a manuscript — both read joint papers to the Linnean Society in 1858, and Origin followed in 1859.

quiz.question: Darwin's Voyage of the Beagle (1831-1836) contributed most directly to his theory because:
quiz.correct: He observed variation between isolated populations that no single region alone would have revealed
distractors: He isolated DNA from island species
He performed laboratory breeding experiments on finches
He measured gene frequencies across populations

quiz.question: Darwin waited over 20 years after returning from the Beagle voyage before publishing Origin of Species in 1859. What event finally forced his hand?
quiz.correct: Alfred Russel Wallace independently arrived at the same theory of natural selection and sent Darwin a manuscript — both presented joint papers at the Linnean Society in 1858, prompting Darwin to publish the following year
distractors: Charles Lyell threatened to publish his own evolution theory before Darwin, creating a competitive priority dispute that forced Darwin to rush his manuscript to press in 1859

Darwin's data from pigeon-breeding experiments finally met his own rigorous threshold for proof in 1858, which he set at confirming that artificial selection could produce at least 10 distinct varieties within a single decade
The Catholic Church issued an official condemnation of evolutionary ideas in 1858, which Darwin calculated would suppress the theory permanently unless he published his complete argument immediately

quiz.question: Darwin observed Galápagos finches on the voyage, but did not realize their significance until after returning to England. What does this reveal about the scientific process?
quiz.correct: Scientific insight often requires theoretical frameworks that are not yet available during data collection — Darwin needed years of reading, breeding experiments, and discussion to recognize what his observations meant
distractors: Darwin's observations were too limited to be scientifically valid — the voyage is overemphasized and his actual theory was developed entirely from library reading after returning to England
This reveals that the Galápagos finches were NOT actually important to Darwin's theory — the tortoise shells and mockingbirds were the key observations, and the finch story is a myth added later

Darwin did not notice the finches because he was untrained in ornithology — he recognized the finches' significance only after the ornithologist John Gould identified the specimens as distinct species in London

quiz.question: During the Beagle voyage Darwin also observed extinct South American mammals in rock strata that were clearly related to living South American species in the same region. How does this biogeographic pattern support evolution?
quiz.correct: Related species appearing in the same geographic region across time (in strata) implies descent and modification, not independent creation — the extinct forms are ancestral relatives of the living ones, not unrelated creations placed in the same rocks by chance
distractors: This observation supports catastrophism — the extinct forms were destroyed by regional catastrophes and then new, similar forms were created to replace them, which explains the geographic clustering
The pattern supports special creation because God would naturally use similar "design templates" in the same region for ecological compatibility — unrelated to evolutionary change

The extinct and living forms look similar because they experience the same South American climate, which independently converges all South American mammals toward the same morphological solutions regardless of ancestry

quiz.question: The HMS Beagle voyage lasted from 1831-1836, and Darwin was how old when he departed?
quiz.correct: 22 years old — he was a young naturalist at the start of his career when he made the observations that would eventually transform biology
distractors: 45 years old — he was an experienced senior scientist by the time of the voyage
15 years old — he was a teenage prodigy still in school
60 years old — he was already famous and used the voyage to confirm pre-existing theories

quiz.question: Origin of Species was published in 1859. Roughly how long did Darwin spend developing his theory after returning from the Beagle voyage in 1836?
quiz.correct: About 23 years — he spent over two decades accumulating evidence from barnacles, pigeon breeding, plant hybridization, biogeography, and correspondence before publishing
distractors: About 2 years — he rushed to publish immediately after returning from the voyage
About 50 years — he only published after his death was imminent at age 80
About 5 years — he published shortly after the voyage but spent decades defending the book

quiz.question: Which islands provided Darwin with observations of slight morphological differences between populations that later fueled his theory of natural selection?
quiz.correct: The Galápagos Islands — he observed variation in finch beaks and tortoise shells between islands that, in retrospect, suggested adaptive divergence
distractors: The Caribbean Islands — where he documented parrot color variation
The Hawaiian Islands — where he first observed silversword plants
The Falkland Islands — where he recorded penguin populations

quiz.question: On the Beagle voyage Darwin also directly experienced geological phenomena that supported Lyell's uniformitarianism — including the Andes uplift and volcanic island formation. How did these observations support his later evolutionary thinking?
quiz.correct: They gave Darwin first-hand evidence that geological processes are ongoing and gradual, supporting Lyell's deep-time view — which provided the enormous timescale needed for natural selection to produce species-level differences
distractors: They convinced Darwin that catastrophism was correct and that species arise suddenly after catastrophic events
They proved that the Earth is only 6,000 years old, forcing Darwin to develop rapid-speciation theories
They were unrelated to evolution — Darwin's geological observations had no influence on his biological thinking

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: Which London-based ornithologist identified Darwin's Galápagos birds as all belonging to the finch group — a critical insight that helped Darwin recognize adaptive divergence between islands?
quiz.correct: John Gould
distractors: Thomas Huxley
Alfred Russel Wallace
Charles Lyell

quiz.question: L3 COMPARISON: Which statement best describes the role of Alfred Russel Wallace in the publication history of evolutionary theory?
quiz.correct: Wallace independently arrived at a theory nearly identical to Darwin's natural selection; their joint 1858 Linnean Society presentation preceded Darwin's 1859 Origin of Species
distractors: Wallace disputed Darwin's theory and proposed an alternative based on Lamarckian inheritance
Wallace was Darwin's graduate student and merely assisted with data collection
Wallace lived in the 20th century and reinterpreted Darwin's theory posthumously

visual.mnemonic: FIVA: Finches, Island biogeography, Volcanoes, Andes.
visual.trap: Darwin did not "see evolution in action" on the Beagle — he built the theory later from the totality of his observations.

flashcards.front: How old was Darwin when he joined the HMS Beagle, and what were the voyage dates?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Charles Darwin was 22 years old when he departed on HMS Beagle on December 27, 1831. The voyage lasted approximately 5 years, ending on October 2, 1836. It was a circumnavigation of the globe, primarily for cartographic surveying, with Darwin serving as the ship's naturalist and gentleman companion to Captain FitzRoy.

**ELI5:** Darwin was just 22 -- barely out of college -- when he hopped on a ship called the Beagle in 1831. The trip lasted 5 years and went all the way around the world. He was basically an unpaid nature nerd on a mapping expedition, and it changed science forever.

flashcards.front: What book did Darwin read on the Beagle, and how did it influence him?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Darwin read Charles Lyell's Principles of Geology, which advocated uniformitarianism and an ancient Earth. This framework profoundly influenced Darwin by providing the deep-time perspective necessary for gradual evolutionary change. Lyell's evidence for slow geological change suggested analogous slow biological change was possible.

**ELI5:** Darwin brought along a geology book by Charles Lyell that argued the Earth changes very, very slowly over millions of years. Reading this was a lightbulb moment -- if rocks and mountains can change slowly, maybe living things can too. The book basically gave Darwin permission to think about very slow change.

flashcards.front: What was John Gould's role in Darwin's thinking?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** John Gould was the ornithologist at the Zoological Society of London who examined Darwin's Galapagos bird specimens in 1837. Gould identified them as a closely related group of finches (not the various unrelated species Darwin had assumed). This was pivotal -- it showed that similar but distinct species on different islands had diverged from a common ancestor, suggesting speciation.

**ELI5:** Darwin collected birds from different Galapagos islands but didn't think much of them. When he got home, a bird expert named John Gould told him, 'These are all finches -- they're all related but each island has its own type!' That was a huge clue that species can split into new ones.

flashcards.front: Why were the Galapagos finches so important for Darwin's theory?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The Galapagos finches demonstrated: (1) closely related species occupying different ecological niches on different islands, (2) morphological divergence (especially beak shape) correlated with diet/ecology, (3) geographic patterns consistent with descent from a common ancestor that colonized the archipelago and diversified (adaptive radiation). This was direct evidence that species are not fixed.

**ELI5:** Each island had finches with different beak shapes -- big crushers for hard seeds, thin pokers for insects, etc. They were all clearly related (like cousins), but each adapted to the food on its own island. It was like finding the same family living in different houses, each modified for a different job.

flashcards.front: What was the significance of Darwin's fossil and living armadillo observation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In South America, Darwin found fossils of giant extinct armadillos (glyptodonts) in the same geographic region as living armadillos. The morphological similarity between extinct and extant forms in the same location suggested a genealogical connection -- that modern armadillos descended from the ancient forms. This pattern (geographic continuity of related forms through time) supported descent with modification.

**ELI5:** Darwin found giant armadillo fossils buried in the ground right where small armadillos were still living. It was like finding a photo of your great-great-grandfather in the attic -- he looks different but clearly related. Why would God make a giant armadillo fossil and a small armadillo in the same place unless one came from the other?

flashcards.front: Why did Darwin wait over 20 years to publish his theory?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Multiple factors: (1) He wanted to amass overwhelming evidence (spent years on barnacle taxonomy, pigeon breeding, biogeography). (2) Fear of social/religious backlash. (3) Awareness of the radical implications of his theory. (4) Desire for scientific credibility through exhaustive documentation. (5) Personal health issues. He was only spurred to publish when Alfred Russel Wallace independently arrived at the same conclusions in 1858.

**ELI5:** Darwin knew his idea was explosive -- saying humans aren't specially created would upset a LOT of people. So he spent 20+ years gathering mountains of evidence to make his case bulletproof. He probably would have waited even longer, but then another scientist (Wallace) had the same idea, and Darwin had to publish or lose credit.

flashcards.front: Who was Alfred Russel Wallace, and what was his role?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Alfred Russel Wallace (1823-1913) was a British naturalist who independently conceived the theory of evolution by natural selection while conducting fieldwork in the Malay Archipelago. In 1858, he sent Darwin a manuscript outlining the theory. This prompted the joint presentation of both men's work at the Linnean Society on July 1, 1858, and spurred Darwin to finally publish On the Origin of Species in 1859.

**ELI5:** Wallace was another explorer-scientist who, on the other side of the world, came up with the exact same big idea as Darwin. He mailed his paper to Darwin, who panicked because someone else had figured it out too. Their friends arranged for both of them to present together in 1858, and then Darwin rushed to publish his book.

flashcards.front: What happened at the 1858 Linnean Society presentation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** On July 1, 1858, papers by both Darwin and Wallace were read jointly at the Linnean Society of London, arranged by Charles Lyell and Joseph Hooker. Neither Darwin nor Wallace was present. Ironically, the presentation generated little immediate reaction. It established priority for both scientists and prompted Darwin to write an 'abstract' that became On the Origin of Species (1859).

**ELI5:** In 1858, Darwin's and Wallace's ideas were read aloud at a science meeting in London. Neither of them was even there! Weirdly, nobody in the audience seemed that impressed at the time. But it lit a fire under Darwin to finally finish his book, which came out the next year and changed everything.

flashcards.front: When was On the Origin of Species published?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** On the Origin of Species by Means of Natural Selection, or the Preservation of Favoured Races in the Struggle for Life was published on November 24, 1859. The entire first printing of 1,250 copies sold out on the first day. Darwin published 6 editions during his lifetime, with substantial revisions.

**ELI5:** Darwin's famous book came out on November 24, 1859. Every single copy sold on day one -- it was a scientific bestseller. People were hungry for this idea, even though it was controversial. It's one of the most important books ever written.

flashcards.front: How did Lyell's geology influence Darwin's biology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Lyell's Principles of Geology convinced Darwin that: (1) Earth is immensely old (deep time), providing sufficient time for gradual biological change; (2) slow, continuous natural processes (uniformitarianism) can produce large cumulative effects; (3) the natural world is dynamic, not static. Darwin applied this gradualist thinking directly to biological evolution -- small variations accumulating over millions of years.

**ELI5:** Lyell showed that tiny forces (rain, wind) can reshape entire continents if given enough time. Darwin thought: 'If that works for rocks, maybe it works for living things too. Small differences adding up over millions of years could turn one species into a totally different one.' Geology gave Darwin the recipe: small changes + lots of time = big transformations.

flashcards.front: What was the HMS Beagle's primary mission?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The primary mission of HMS Beagle's second voyage (1831-1836) was to survey the coastline of South America (particularly Patagonia and Tierra del Fuego) and establish a chain of chronometric stations around the world for accurate longitude measurements. Darwin's position as naturalist was secondary to the navigational objectives.

**ELI5:** The Beagle's real job was to make maps of the South American coast -- Darwin was basically just along for the ride as the ship's nature enthusiast. Nobody expected the side project (collecting specimens and thinking about where species come from) to be more important than the maps.

flashcards.front: What did Darwin observe about the geographic distribution of species?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Darwin noted that: (1) species on islands resembled those on the nearest mainland (suggesting colonization and divergence), (2) fossil forms in a region resembled modern forms in the same region (temporal continuity), (3) similar environments on different continents had different but ecologically similar species. These biogeographic patterns were inexplicable by special creation but consistent with descent with modification.

**ELI5:** Darwin noticed something strange: island animals looked like their mainland neighbors (not like animals on similar islands far away). And fossils in South America looked like modern South American animals. It's like how your accent sounds like your neighbors', not like people from a far-off country with similar weather. This made sense only if species come from nearby ancestors.

nodes.title: Natural Selection: The Four Ingredients
nodes.subtitle: Variation, heritability, differential reproduction → evolution (Lec 2 slides 13-23)

popup.heading: Lecture 2 — The Logic of Natural Selection

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Darwin drew on Thomas Malthus (1766-1834), who observed that populations reproduce exponentially while resources are finite. Reproductive potential examples: aphids could produce 524 billion offspring in 1 year; elephants could produce 19 million in 750 years; Staphylococcus would cover Earth 7 feet deep in 24 hours. Populations do NOT grow exponentially in reality because resources constrain survival — creating a struggle for existence.

sections.label: THE FIVE INGREDIENTS
sections.body:
1. High reproductive potential
2. Population size remains roughly stable
3. Resources are limited → struggle to survive
4. Phenotypic variation among individuals
5. Heritability of that variation. INFERENCE: survival and reproduction are therefore NONRANDOM with respect to phenotype. FITNESS = ability to survive and reproduce RELATIVE to others. ADAPTATION = a trait that increases fitness relative to individuals without it

sections.label: KEY TERMS
sections.body: Phenotypic variation, heritability, Malthusian overproduction, differential reproductive success, fitness (relative), adaptation.

examples: Variation: individuals in a population differ in traits (beak size, coat color, etc.)
Heritability: offspring resemble parents more than unrelated individuals.
Differential reproductive success: some variants leave more descendants.
Inference: the successful variants' traits become more common each generation.
Reproductive potential: Staphylococcus would cover Earth 7 feet deep in 24 hours if unconstrained; elephants could produce 19 million descendants in 750 years.
warnings: WATCH OUT: If ANY ingredient is missing, natural selection cannot occur. Non-heritable differences (e.g., sunburn) do not evolve.
WATCH OUT: Natural selection acts on PHENOTYPES, but evolution is measured as ALLELE FREQUENCY CHANGE at the genotype level.
popup.mnemonic: VHI-DRS = Variation + Heritability + Inference-from-Differential-Reproductive-Success = Evolution.

flashcard.front: What are the four necessary ingredients for evolution by natural selection, and what is the logical inference that follows from combining them?
flashcard.back: (1) Phenotypic variation among individuals; (2) Heritability of that variation (offspring resemble parents); (3) Differential survival and reproduction based on phenotype (i.e., fitness differences); (4) More offspring produced than can survive (Malthusian overproduction). INFERENCE: The phenotypes linked to higher fitness will become more common each generation — the population evolves. If ANY ingredient is missing, no evolution by natural selection occurs.

quiz.question: A population of plants shows variation in leaf shape, but leaf shape is entirely determined by soil chemistry and not passed from parent to offspring. Can natural selection act on this variation?
quiz.correct: No — without heritability, differential reproduction will not change allele frequencies
distractors: Yes — any phenotypic variation is sufficient for evolution by natural selection
Yes — but only if the variation produces differences in germination rate
No — but only because plants reproduce vegetatively rather than sexually

quiz.question: A zookeeper breeds the longest-necked giraffes each generation for 15 generations. Average neck length DOES increase. What does this ALONE confirm about natural selection's four ingredients?
quiz.correct: It confirms variation exists and that differential reproduction on neck length produced a response (R > 0) — but it does NOT confirm whether the force causing the change in the wild is predation, competition for high foliage, or sexual selection, which must be tested separately
distractors: It confirms all four ingredients of natural selection: variation, heritability, differential reproduction, and overproduction — because the response was observed, all must be present
It confirms only that neck length is heritable — the zookeeper's experiment is functionally equivalent to measuring parent-offspring regression, the gold standard for estimating h²

It does not confirm any ingredient of natural selection because artificial selection by a zookeeper does not count as natural selection — only wild populations experiencing predation or drought qualify

quiz.question: Which scenario violates the requirement for DIFFERENTIAL REPRODUCTIVE SUCCESS but satisfies variation and heritability?
quiz.correct: A population of beetles varies in shell color (heritable), but predatory birds hunt randomly and do not preferentially eat any color — survival and reproduction are equal across color variants
distractors: A population of bacteria varies in antibiotic resistance due to plasmid acquisition; the resistance trait cannot be passed to daughter cells because plasmids are lost during binary fission
A population of fish varies in body size (heritable), but all fish die at age 1 due to a sudden winter freeze before any reproduce — removing all selection pressure

A population of mice lives in a perfectly uniform environment with no predators and unlimited food; variation in fur color exists but individuals do not differ in reproductive success because there is nothing to select against

quiz.question: Darwin's inference from the four ingredients was: "the successful variants' traits will become more common." What makes this an INFERENCE rather than a direct observation, and what experiment would make it a direct observation?
quiz.correct: It is an inference because we do not directly observe allele frequencies changing — we observe phenotype-fitness correlations. A direct test requires measuring allele frequencies before and after selection in a marked population (like the Grant finch study) and confirming R = h²S predicts the observed change
distractors: It is an inference only because Darwin lacked microscopes — with modern sequencing we can directly observe the variation ingredient, making the whole argument observational rather than inferential

It is an inference because differential reproduction is unobservable in wild populations — only lab experiments can directly measure which variants reproduce more, so field evolution is always inferred, never observed
It is not an inference at all — observing a changed population after several generations IS direct observation of evolution by natural selection, requiring no additional experimental confirmation

quiz.question: Darwin drew on Malthus's observation about population growth for his fourth ingredient: overproduction. What is the logical role of "more offspring produced than can survive" in the natural selection argument?
quiz.correct: Overproduction ensures that not all offspring can survive — creating a selective filter that differentiates between those with advantageous traits and those without, which is what "differential survival" requires
distractors: Overproduction directly causes mutations — the pressure to reproduce rapidly forces DNA to mutate at higher rates in stressed populations
Overproduction is needed to satisfy Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, which requires a minimum offspring count per generation
Overproduction is irrelevant — natural selection operates equally well when exactly one offspring per parent survives

quiz.question: Natural selection acts on which level, and evolution is measured at which level?
quiz.correct: Natural selection acts on PHENOTYPES (individuals with their traits face environmental challenges), but evolution is measured as changes in ALLELE FREQUENCIES at the GENOTYPE level
distractors: Both selection and evolution act on alleles directly — phenotypes are irrelevant because DNA is what matters
Both selection and evolution act on whole populations simultaneously — individuals and their genes are never the unit of analysis
Selection acts on species, and evolution is measured as the rate of speciation across geological time

quiz.question: A student claims that natural selection "creates" new traits by designing them for the environment. Why is this a misconception?
quiz.correct: Natural selection only SORTS among existing variants — it cannot create new traits. The source of new variation is mutation (plus recombination and gene flow); selection just favors some pre-existing variants over others
distractors: The student is correct — selection can directly create new traits by modifying DNA in response to environmental pressure
The student is wrong only because selection creates traits slowly rather than quickly — otherwise the claim is correct
The student is correct when selection is strong, but not when it is weak — strong selection can induce new beneficial mutations

quiz.question: If a population has variation in a trait, that variation is heritable, AND there is differential reproductive success based on that trait, what is the INEVITABLE outcome according to Darwin's logic?
quiz.correct: The population will evolve — the variants with higher fitness will become more common each generation, shifting the population mean
distractors: The population will only evolve if natural selection is strong — weak selection produces no detectable change
The population will only evolve if the environment is changing — stable environments produce no evolution even with all four ingredients present
Nothing will happen — these three ingredients are necessary but not sufficient; a fourth requirement (mutation) must also be met

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: Which 18th-19th century economist's work on population growth directly influenced Darwin's thinking on the "struggle for existence"?
quiz.correct: Thomas Malthus (1766-1834)
distractors: Adam Smith
John Stuart Mill
Karl Marx

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: Which example is given in lecture to illustrate extreme reproductive potential — a bacterium that, unconstrained, would cover Earth 7 feet deep in 24 hours?
quiz.correct: Staphylococcus
distractors: E. coli
Streptococcus pneumoniae
Bacillus subtilis

quiz.question: L3 COMPARISON: How is FITNESS defined in the context of natural selection?
quiz.correct: The ability to survive and reproduce RELATIVE to others in the population — it is always a relative, not absolute, measure
distractors: The absolute number of offspring an individual produces in its lifetime
Physical strength and endurance, as measured by athletic performance tests
The longevity of an individual, independent of its reproductive success

visual.mnemonic: VHOD→N: Variation, Heritability, Overproduction, Differential fitness → Next generation changed.
visual.trap: Natural selection does NOT produce traits — it only sorts among existing variants. The source of variation is mutation + recombination.

diagram.title: Darwin — Five Ingredients of Natural Selection (Lec 2)

nodes.label: High reproductive potential
nodes.detail: Every species can produce far more offspring than can possibly survive. Lec 2 examples: Aphis fabae can generate 524 billion aphids in a single year; Staphylococcus aureus would blanket Earth 7 feet deep in 24 hours with no limits; from one elephant pair, Darwin calculated 19 million elephants in 750 years. Thomas Malthus (1766–1834) formalized the idea in his 1798 "Essay on the Principle of Population," which Darwin read in October 1838 and explicitly credited as the spark for natural selection.
nodes.mnemonic: 524B aphids/yr — no population actually reaches its reproductive ceiling.
nodes.watchOut: This is an OBSERVATION, not a cause. Overproduction alone produces no change.

nodes.label: Populations stable in size
nodes.detail: Despite enormous reproductive potential, populations do not grow without bound. Malthus argued that populations grow geometrically while food supply grows arithmetically — so most offspring must die before reproducing. Alfred Russel Wallace independently reached the same insight in 1858 while in the Malay Archipelago, also citing Malthus.
nodes.mnemonic: Malthus: geometric births meet arithmetic food → stability is enforced by death.
nodes.watchOut: Short-term booms happen, but long-term equilibrium is the rule.

nodes.label: Limited resources → struggle
nodes.detail: Food, mates, territory, water, and nest sites are finite. Individuals must compete — the "struggle for existence" (Darwin 1859, Ch. 3). The struggle is often passive: outgrowing a neighbor, tolerating drought, evading a parasite.
nodes.mnemonic: Struggle is mostly quiet, not bloody.
nodes.watchOut: Struggle does not require direct combat — differential survival is enough.

nodes.label: Phenotypic variation
nodes.detail: No two individuals are identical. Variation spans morphology, physiology, behavior, and performance. Darwin documented variation across pigeon breeds, barnacles, and Galápagos finches. Without phenotypic differences, selection has nothing to grip.
nodes.mnemonic: No variation → no selection, period.
nodes.watchOut: Non-heritable differences (scars, sunburn, learned tricks) don’t evolve.

nodes.label: Heritability
nodes.detail: Trait values must be transmitted from parents to offspring. Darwin had no mechanism — Mendel’s 1866 peas were unknown to him, and genetics was not rediscovered until 1900. Yet breeders had shown heritability empirically for centuries. Wallace, co-announcing the theory at the Linnean Society on 1 July 1858, also lacked a transmission mechanism.
nodes.mnemonic: Parents must "send" the trait to offspring for selection to stick.
nodes.watchOut: If heritability is zero, NS cannot occur even with perfect variation and DRS.

nodes.label: Differential reproductive success
nodes.detail: The logical inference: given overproduction, limited resources, heritable variation, and competition, individuals with advantageous heritable traits will leave disproportionately more offspring. This is the operational definition of natural selection — not "survival of the fittest" but "differential reproductive success conditional on phenotype."
nodes.mnemonic: VHI-DRS: Variation + Heritability + Inference → Differential Reproductive Success.
nodes.watchOut: Fitness = lifetime reproductive output, not just survival to adulthood.

nodes.label: Evolution by natural selection
nodes.detail: Over generations, allele frequencies shift toward variants that improve relative fitness in the current environment. Populations — not individuals — evolve. Joint Darwin/Wallace presentation was read at the Linnean Society on 1 July 1858, and Darwin’s "On the Origin of Species" followed on 24 November 1859. (Wallace mention: the theory is correctly called Darwin–Wallace natural selection, though Darwin’s evidence was more extensive.)
nodes.mnemonic: allele frequencies change — that IS evolution.
nodes.watchOut: Selection acts on phenotypes, but evolution is measured as allele frequency change.

diagram.mnemonic: VHI-DRS: Variation + Heritability + Inference → Differential Reproductive Success → Evolution.

flashcards.front: Who was Thomas Malthus, and how did he influence Darwin?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Thomas Robert Malthus (1766-1834) was an English cleric and economist who argued in An Essay on the Principle of Population (1798) that human populations grow geometrically while food supply grows arithmetically, inevitably leading to competition and suffering. Darwin read Malthus in 1838 and realized this 'struggle for existence' provided the selective pressure driving evolution.

**ELI5:** Malthus (1766-1834) was an economist who said populations always grow faster than their food supply, leading to a constant struggle. When Darwin read this, he had his 'aha!' moment: if there's always a fight for food, the organisms best suited to win that fight will survive and have more babies. Competition drives evolution.

flashcards.front: What are the five ingredients (conditions) required for natural selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) High reproductive potential -- organisms can produce more offspring than the environment can support. (2) Stable population size -- despite reproductive potential, populations remain roughly constant. (3) Limited resources -- food, space, mates, etc. are finite. (4) Phenotypic variation -- individuals within a population differ in observable traits. (5) Heritability -- at least some of that variation is genetically based and passed to offspring.

**ELI5:** Five things must be true for natural selection to work: (1) Animals can make lots of babies. (2) But the population stays about the same size. (3) Because there's not enough food/space for everyone. (4) Every individual is a bit different. (5) Those differences are passed down from parents to kids. If all five are true, evolution by natural selection is inevitable.

flashcards.front: Give three examples of high reproductive potential.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) A single pair of aphids could theoretically produce 524 billion descendants in one year. (2) A single pair of elephants (the slowest-reproducing land mammal) could theoretically produce 19 million descendants in 750 years. (3) A single Staphylococcus aureus bacterium could theoretically produce enough offspring to cover Earth 7 feet deep in just 24 hours.

**ELI5:** Nature overproduces like crazy: (1) Two aphids could make 524 billion babies in a year. (2) Even slow-breeding elephants could fill the earth in 750 years. (3) One bacterium could bury the planet 7 feet deep in a single day. Obviously this doesn't happen -- most die before reproducing. That's the point.

flashcards.front: What is the inference from the five ingredients of natural selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The logical inference: since more offspring are produced than can survive (ingredients 1-3), and individuals vary in heritable traits (ingredients 4-5), survival and reproduction are nonrandom -- individuals with traits better suited to the current environment will disproportionately survive and reproduce. This differential reproductive success IS natural selection, leading to adaptation.

**ELI5:** Put the five ingredients together and you get this: since not everyone can survive, and everyone's a little different, and those differences are inherited -- the ones best suited to their environment will win the survival lottery most often. Over time, the 'winning' traits become more common. That's natural selection.

flashcards.front: What is the precise definition of fitness in evolutionary biology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Fitness (Darwinian fitness, W) is the relative reproductive success of a genotype or phenotype compared to other genotypes/phenotypes in the same population and environment. It is measured as the proportional contribution of offspring to the next generation. Fitness is ALWAYS relative -- there is no absolute fitness. A fitness of W=1.0 is assigned to the most fit genotype; others are expressed relative to it.

**ELI5:** Fitness isn't about being strong or fast -- it's about having the most surviving babies compared to everyone else. A scrawny mouse that has 20 pups is 'fitter' than a buff mouse that has 2. And fitness is always a comparison -- you're only fit or unfit compared to your neighbors, not in absolute terms.

flashcards.front: Why is fitness described as RELATIVE rather than absolute?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Fitness is relative because natural selection acts through differential reproduction -- what matters is not how many offspring an individual produces in isolation, but how many it produces compared to others in the same population and environment. An organism producing 10 offspring is highly fit if others average 2, but poorly fit if others average 20. Fitness also changes when the environment changes.

**ELI5:** Imagine a race. Running a mile in 6 minutes is great if everyone else takes 8 minutes, but terrible if everyone else does it in 4 minutes. Fitness works the same way -- it's always relative to your competition. And when the competition or conditions change, who's 'fit' changes too.

flashcards.front: What is the definition of adaptation in evolutionary biology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** An adaptation is a heritable trait that increases the relative fitness of its bearer compared to individuals lacking that trait in a given environment. Adaptations arise through natural selection acting on heritable variation over generations. The term can also refer to the process of becoming adapted. Not all traits are adaptations (some arise by drift, constraint, or chance).

**ELI5:** An adaptation is any inherited feature that helps an organism survive and reproduce better than others. A cactus's thick skin that holds water is an adaptation for the desert. A polar bear's white fur is an adaptation for sneaking up on seals in the snow. It's a trait that gives you an edge.

flashcards.front: What was Malthus's specific contribution to the development of natural selection theory?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Malthus provided the key insight that populations inherently tend to grow exponentially while resources remain limited, creating an inevitable 'struggle for existence.' Darwin and Wallace both credited Malthus as the catalyst for understanding that this competition, combined with heritable variation, produces differential survival and reproduction -- the mechanism of natural selection.

**ELI5:** Malthus wasn't even a biologist -- he was an economist worried about too many people and not enough food. But both Darwin and Wallace read his work and thought, 'Wait -- if there's always a competition for survival, then the ones with the best traits will win!' An economics book accidentally helped solve the biggest question in biology.

flashcards.front: What happens if any one of the five ingredients is missing?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Without any single ingredient, natural selection cannot operate: (1) Without high reproductive potential, no overproduction and no competition. (2) Without stable population size, there's no evidence of mortality/selection. (3) Without limited resources, all survive equally (no selection). (4) Without phenotypic variation, selection has nothing to act on (all are equal). (5) Without heritability, even if some variants survive better, the advantage cannot be passed on and the population doesn't evolve.

**ELI5:** Remove any one ingredient and evolution breaks: No overproduction = no competition. No limited resources = everyone survives equally. No variation = everyone's the same, so there's nothing to select. No heritability = even if the best survive, their kids don't inherit the advantage. It's like a recipe -- leave out any ingredient and the cake doesn't rise.

flashcards.front: Does natural selection act on phenotypes or genotypes?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Natural selection acts directly on phenotypes (observable traits), not genotypes (genetic constitution). The environment 'sees' the phenotype -- e.g., beak size, running speed, coloration. However, evolution occurs only when phenotypic differences have a genetic (heritable) basis, because only genetic changes are transmitted to offspring. Selection on phenotypes with no genetic basis produces no evolutionary change.

**ELI5:** Nature doesn't read your DNA -- it sees what you look like and what you can do. A predator chasing zebras doesn't care about their genes; it catches the slowest one. BUT, for evolution to happen, that speed has to be inherited. If slow parents have fast kids randomly, being slow doesn't get 'selected out.' Natural selection judges the outside but needs the inside to make it stick.

flashcards.front: What is the distinction between phenotype and genotype in the context of selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Genotype = the specific set of alleles an organism carries. Phenotype = the observable characteristics resulting from genotype-environment interaction. Selection differential (S) acts on phenotypic variation. Evolutionary response (R) depends on heritability (h2): R = h2 x S. Without heritability, phenotypic selection produces no genotypic (evolutionary) change. The environment mediates the genotype-phenotype relationship.

**ELI5:** Your genotype is your recipe (DNA). Your phenotype is the finished dish (how you look and act). Nature tastes the dish (selects on phenotype), but only the recipe gets passed down (genotype). If the dish is great because of the recipe, the next generation gets the good recipe too. If the dish is great because of the oven (environment), the next generation doesn't benefit.

flashcards.front: What is the breeder's equation, and what does each variable mean?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** R = h2 x S, where R = response to selection (evolutionary change in trait mean between generations), h2 = narrow-sense heritability (proportion of phenotypic variance due to additive genetic effects, ranging 0-1), and S = selection differential (difference in mean trait value between the selected parents and the overall population). This equation predicts evolutionary response to selection.

**ELI5:** R = h2 x S is evolution's prediction formula. S = how much you push (how different are the survivors from the average). h2 = how much that push sticks (how heritable the trait is, 0 to 1). R = how much the next generation actually changes. Big push + high heritability = big evolution. Big push + low heritability = nothing much happens.

flashcards.front: How is the concept of 'struggle for existence' derived from ingredients 1-3?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Ingredient 1 (high reproductive potential) means populations can grow exponentially. Ingredient 2 (stable population size) means populations are not growing exponentially. Ingredient 3 (limited resources) explains WHY populations are stable despite reproductive potential -- most individuals die before reproducing or produce fewer offspring. This mortality is the 'struggle for existence' that creates the opportunity for differential survival.

**ELI5:** Animals can make way more babies than the environment can handle (ingredient 1), but the population stays about the same (ingredient 2), because there isn't enough food and space (ingredient 3). This means most babies die. That deadly competition is what Darwin called the 'struggle for existence' -- and it's where natural selection happens.

nodes.title: Galápagos Finches: Selection Case Study
nodes.subtitle: Grant & Grant beak size evolution (Lec 2 slides 24-31)

popup.heading: Lecture 2 — Evolution of Beak Shape in Galápagos Finches

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Peter and Rosemary Grant studied Geospiza fortis (medium ground finch) on Daphne Major in the Galápagos for decades. They directly measured all four conditions of natural selection on beak depth: (1) VARIATION — beak depth varied among individuals; (2) HERITABILITY — parent-offspring regression yielded h² ≈ 0.65-0.8; (3) DIFFERENTIAL SURVIVAL — during the 1977 drought, 89% of the population died before breeding; (4) NON-RANDOM SURVIVAL with respect to beak depth.

sections.label: MECHANISM
sections.body: The 1977 drought killed small-seed plants, leaving only large, hard seeds (Tribulus cistoides). Deeper-beaked birds survived because they could crack these seeds. The next generation had a larger average beak depth — directly measured evolution by natural selection. Sexual selection also contributes to beak evolution in finches.

sections.label: KEY FIGURES
sections.body: Peter Grant, Rosemary Grant.

examples: Peter and Rosemary Grant measured beak depth in Geospiza fortis on Daphne Major across decades.
1977 drought: small soft seeds disappeared; only large hard seeds (Tribulus cistoides) remained. 89% of the population died before breeding.
Next generation had significantly larger average beak size — directly measured evolution by natural selection.
Heritability of beak depth measured by parent-offspring regression: h² ≈ 0.65-0.8.
warnings: WATCH OUT: The Grant finch study showed selection REVERSED in wet years — larger beaks were selected AGAINST when small seeds dominated again. Selection direction tracks the environment, NOT unidirectional.
WATCH OUT: Heritability of beak depth (h² ≈ 0.65-0.8) was MEASURED by parent-offspring regression, not assumed. Without measuring heritability, you cannot confirm the R = h²S prediction.
popup.mnemonic: 4 conditions confirmed: Variable beaks, Heritable beaks, Survival differences, Beak size mattered.

flashcard.front: How did the Grants' Galápagos finch study provide direct empirical evidence for each ingredient of natural selection?
flashcard.back: (1) VARIATION: Beak depth varied among individual Geospiza fortis. (2) HERITABILITY: Parent-offspring regression showed beak depth is heritable (h² ≈ 0.65-0.8). (3) DIFFERENTIAL SURVIVAL: During the 1977 drought, birds with deeper beaks ate hard Tribulus seeds and survived while smaller-beaked birds died. (4) INFERENCE CONFIRMED: The next generation had significantly larger average beak depth — selection was measured, not inferred.

quiz.question: In the Grant finch study, what was the selective pressure that drove evolution of larger beaks in 1977?
quiz.correct: A drought eliminated small, soft seeds, leaving mostly large, hard seeds that only birds with deep beaks could crack
distractors: A new predator preferred small-beaked birds
Birds intentionally grew larger beaks during the drought
Climate cooling caused beaks to grow larger

quiz.question: After the 1977 drought the Grants observed that the Geospiza fortis population had larger average beaks. When wet years returned in the early 1980s, beak size started shifting BACK toward smaller. What does this pattern most directly demonstrate?
quiz.correct: Natural selection is not unidirectional — the direction of selection tracks the current environment. Larger beaks are favored only when hard seeds dominate; smaller beaks are favored when small soft seeds are abundant again
distractors: Genetic drift reversed the 1977 selection because the finch population was still small enough after the drought for random sampling to overcome directional selection
The birds that survived the drought mated with immigrants from nearby islands who had smaller beaks, and gene flow reversed the evolutionary change
Beaks regressed to their pre-drought size because heritability of beak depth is close to zero — the 1977 change was environmental, not genetic, and disappeared when the environment normalized

quiz.question: The Grant study measured beak heritability using parent-offspring regression and found h² ≈ 0.65-0.8. Why is measuring h² essential for confirming that the beak-size change across the 1977-78 drought was evolution by natural selection, rather than just environmental change?
quiz.correct: Without confirming h² > 0, the beak-size shift could reflect individual developmental plasticity (birds eating harder seeds grow deeper beaks) rather than a true change in allele frequencies — heritability measurement proves the response was genetic
distractors: Without h², we cannot calculate the selection differential S — and without S we cannot confirm that predation pressure was acting in the correct direction to drive larger beaks
Heritability measurement is not necessary to confirm natural selection — it is only needed if you want to predict the response in the next generation using the breeders equation R = h²S

Without h², we cannot distinguish natural selection from genetic drift — only by confirming h² > 0 can we rule out that the beak-size shift was caused by random sampling in the small post-drought population

quiz.question: During the 1977 drought, researchers observed that Tribulus cistoides (large-seeded plant) dominated the available food. If the Grants had NOT measured food supply, beak heritability, AND survival rates separately, which alternative explanation could NOT be ruled out?
quiz.correct: A phenotype-fitness correlation alone could be explained by an environmental confound — perhaps well-nourished birds are both bigger-beaked AND survive better, with diet (not beak size) causing the survival difference
distractors: Without measuring food supply, we could not rule out that beak size increased due to hybridization with the larger-beaked G. magnirostris arriving from another island during the drought

Without measuring heritability, we could not rule out that beak size evolved through sexual selection rather than food-based selection — females may have preferred larger-beaked males specifically during drought years

Without measuring survival rates, we could not rule out that all finches survived the drought equally and the apparent evolutionary change was caused entirely by immigration of larger-beaked birds from other Galápagos islands

quiz.question: The species of finch studied by Peter and Rosemary Grant on Daphne Major was:
quiz.correct: Geospiza fortis — the medium ground finch
distractors: Geospiza magnirostris — the large ground finch
Darwin's rosy finch — a Hawaiian species
Carduelis galapagensis — the Galápagos goldfinch

quiz.question: The Grants measured beak depth heritability using parent-offspring regression. What specific value range did they find for h² in Geospiza fortis?
quiz.correct: h² ≈ 0.65-0.8 — meaning the majority of variation in beak depth is additive genetic variance that will respond to selection
distractors: h² ≈ 0.1-0.2 — meaning beak depth is mostly environmentally determined
h² = 1.0 — meaning beak depth is completely determined by genetics with no environmental influence
h² ≈ 0.5 exactly — the default value assumed for all quantitative traits

quiz.question: The Grant finch study is often cited as the FIRST direct measurement of evolution by natural selection in a wild vertebrate population. What made it the first of its kind?
quiz.correct: All four ingredients of natural selection were measured in the same population simultaneously — variation, heritability, differential survival, and response across generations — with quantitative data for each
distractors: It was the first evolution study to use genetic sequencing to measure allele frequencies directly across generations
It was the first evolution study conducted on a vertebrate — all previous studies used insects and microorganisms exclusively
It was the first evolution study to be conducted on the Galápagos Islands since Darwin's original voyage

quiz.question: Which hard-seeded plant species is most directly associated with the 1977 drought selection event on beak size in G. fortis?
quiz.correct: Tribulus cistoides — whose large, hard seeds dominated the food supply during the drought and could only be cracked by birds with sufficiently deep beaks
distractors: Arabidopsis thaliana — a model plant with small soft seeds
Opuntia cactus — whose fruits are the main year-round food
Scalesia pedunculata — a soft-bodied daisy tree

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: What percentage of the Daphne Major finch population died before breeding during the 1977 drought?
L5 SYNTHESIS: In the Grant finch study, the measured value of h² was in what range — and what does this mean for response to selection?
quiz.correct: h² ≈ 0.65-0.8, meaning most of the phenotypic variance in beak depth is additive genetic variance — so selection produces a substantial response each generation (R = h²S)
distractors: h² ≈ 0.1-0.2, meaning there is almost no genetic contribution — most variation is environmental and no response to selection is expected
h² = 0, meaning beak depth is entirely environmentally determined and cannot evolve
h² = 1, meaning beak depth is entirely genetic with zero environmental influence

quiz.question: L2 MECHANISM: Besides food-mediated natural selection, which OTHER form of selection contributes to beak evolution in Geospiza finches, as discussed in lecture?
quiz.correct: Sexual selection — mate choice also influences which beak morphologies reproduce most successfully
distractors: Kin selection between siblings on the same nest
Group selection at the island-level
Meiotic drive that biases beak alleles in gamete production

visual.mnemonic: Drought → Hard seeds → Deep beaks win.
visual.trap: After the drought ended, selection sometimes reversed direction (wet years favored smaller beaks). Selection is not unidirectional — it tracks environment.

diagram.title: Peppered Moth — One Generation of Selection (Biston betularia)

nodes.label: Initial population
nodes.detail: Starting allele frequencies at the carbonaria (B) locus: p = 0.5, q = 0.5. Under HWE this yields genotype frequencies f(BB) = 0.25, f(Bb) = 0.50, f(bb) = 0.25. Kettlewell’s 1950s mark–release–recapture experiments in polluted Birmingham vs unpolluted Dorset woodlands made this the textbook case for natural selection in action.
nodes.mnemonic: Start at p=q=0.5 so selection signal is easy to see.
nodes.watchOut: Real pre-industrial frequencies of carbonaria were <1%, not 50% — this is a teaching setup.

nodes.label: bb survival (white)
nodes.detail: Homozygous recessive bb moths are light-colored ("typica" morph). On pollution-blackened bark in industrial Britain they are conspicuous to visually hunting birds. Empirical survival probability = 0.55.
nodes.mnemonic: White on black bark = bird food.
nodes.watchOut: Survival, not reproduction, is what this line measures — the two can diverge.

nodes.label: BB survival (dark)
nodes.detail: Homozygous dominant BB moths are dark (carbonaria). They are cryptic against soot-covered bark. Empirical survival probability = 0.90 in polluted woodlands.
nodes.mnemonic: Dark on black = hidden from birds.
nodes.watchOut: Reverse the pattern after 1956 Clean Air Act — BB became the visible morph.

nodes.label: Bb survival (dark)
nodes.detail: Heterozygotes are also dark because B is dominant. Empirical survival = 0.87, slightly lower than BB homozygotes due to incomplete dominance in some populations. Dominance here is at the pigmentation level, not fitness.
nodes.mnemonic: Dominance hides bb from selection through heterozygotes.
nodes.watchOut: Heterozygote survival (0.87) is slightly below BB (0.90) — not perfectly dominant.

nodes.label: w(BB) relative fitness
nodes.detail: Set the highest-surviving genotype to w = 1. BB has the highest survival (0.90), so w(BB) = 0.90 / 0.90 = 1.00. All other fitnesses are rescaled against this reference.
nodes.mnemonic: Highest survivor always gets w = 1.
nodes.watchOut: Relative fitness is unitless; absolute fitness drops out.

nodes.label: w(Bb) relative fitness
nodes.detail: w(Bb) = 0.87 / 0.90 = 0.9667 ≈ 0.96. Heterozygote selection coefficient s_Bb = 1 − 0.96 = 0.04, a modest disadvantage relative to BB.
nodes.mnemonic: w(Bb) = survival(Bb) ÷ survival(best).
nodes.watchOut: A 4% disadvantage per generation still causes rapid frequency shifts.

nodes.label: w(bb) relative fitness
nodes.detail: w(bb) = 0.55 / 0.90 = 0.6111 ≈ 0.61. Selection coefficient s_bb = 1 − 0.61 = 0.39, a huge fitness disadvantage. Historically matched by observed carbonaria frequency rising from <1% to 98% in Manchester by 1895.
nodes.mnemonic: s = 1 − (w / w_best).
nodes.watchOut: Very strong selection (s = 0.39) explains the observed rapid industrial melanism.

nodes.label: p' after selection
nodes.mnemonic: p' = (AA frequency + ½ Aa frequency) weighted by fitness.
nodes.watchOut: Do not forget the 1/2 factor for heterozygote contribution to the A allele pool.

nodes.label: Δp = +0.057
nodes.detail: Δp = p' − p = 0.557 − 0.500 = +0.057. In ONE generation the B allele has increased by 5.7 percentage points. Over 20 generations at similar intensity the frequency rises from ~0.01 to >0.9 — matching the Manchester data Kettlewell documented.
nodes.mnemonic: Positive selection on B allele → Δp > 0.
nodes.watchOut: Δp depends on current p — selection slows as favored allele approaches fixation.

diagram.mnemonic: s = 1 − (w / w_best). Higher fitness → higher frequency next generation.

flashcards.front: Who are Peter and Rosemary Grant, and what did they study?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Peter and Rosemary Grant are evolutionary biologists (Princeton University) who conducted a multi-decade field study of Darwin's finches (Geospiza spp.) on the island of Daphne Major in the Galapagos. Their work provided some of the most detailed, real-time documentation of natural selection and evolution in a wild population.

**ELI5:** Peter and Rosemary Grant are a husband-wife scientist team who spent decades on a tiny island in the Galapagos catching, measuring, and tagging finches. They basically watched evolution happen in real time -- like setting up a camera to film a flower blooming, except it was natural selection they captured.

flashcards.front: What species did the Grants primarily study, and on which island?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The Grants primarily studied the medium ground finch (Geospiza fortis) on the island of Daphne Major in the Galapagos archipelago. Daphne Major is small (~0.34 km2) and isolated, making it ideal for monitoring entire populations -- every individual could be banded, measured, and tracked.

**ELI5:** They studied a bird called the medium ground finch (Geospiza fortis) on a tiny volcanic island called Daphne Major. The island is so small they could catch and measure basically every single finch living there -- like doing a census of every person in a small town.

flashcards.front: What happened during the 1977 drought on Daphne Major?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A severe drought in 1977 caused ~89% mortality in the G. fortis population (from ~1,200 to ~180 individuals). Drought dramatically reduced seed availability, with small soft seeds depleted first. The remaining seeds were predominantly large, hard seeds (especially from Tribulus cistoides, caltrop plant). Birds with larger, deeper beaks could crack these hard seeds and survived at higher rates.

**ELI5:** In 1977, it barely rained on the island, and almost 9 out of 10 finches died. The small, easy-to-eat seeds ran out first, leaving only big, tough seeds that were like trying to crack a walnut. Finches with bigger, stronger beaks could crack those tough seeds and survived. The small-beaked birds starved.

flashcards.front: What seeds remained available during the 1977 drought?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The large, hard seeds of Tribulus cistoides (puncture vine/caltrop) persisted while smaller, softer seeds were quickly depleted. Tribulus seeds require substantial biting force to crack, creating strong directional selection for larger, deeper beaks in G. fortis.

**ELI5:** The tough seeds from a plant called Tribulus cistoides (basically little spiky balls) were the last food standing after the drought killed off all the easy-to-eat stuff. Only finches with big, strong beaks could crack them open. It was like the only food left in the house being inside a locked safe.

flashcards.front: What was the heritability (h2) of beak depth in Geospiza fortis?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The narrow-sense heritability (h2) of beak depth in G. fortis was approximately 0.65-0.80 (varies by study/year). This high heritability means 65-80% of the phenotypic variation in beak depth is attributable to additive genetic effects. This ensured that selection on beak depth during the drought would produce an evolutionary response in the next generation.

**ELI5:** Heritability of beak depth was about 0.65-0.80, meaning beak size is VERY strongly inherited from parents. If your parents have big beaks, you almost certainly will too. This is why the drought changed the population -- the big-beaked survivors passed their big beaks to their chicks.

flashcards.front: How does the breeder's equation (R = h2 x S) apply to the Grants' finch data?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** S (selection differential) = the difference in mean beak depth between surviving finches and the original population (survivors had ~0.5mm deeper beaks). h2 (heritability) = ~0.65-0.80. R (response) = h2 x S predicts the expected shift in mean beak depth in the next generation. The observed shift closely matched the prediction, providing powerful validation of quantitative genetics theory in a wild population.

**ELI5:** The formula R = h2 x S predicted exactly how much bigger the next generation's beaks would be. The survivors had beaks about 0.5mm bigger than average (that's S). Heritability was ~0.7 (that's h2). So the prediction was: next generation's beaks should be about 0.35mm bigger. And that's almost exactly what happened. Math predicted evolution!

flashcards.front: Did natural selection on finches only go in one direction?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** No. Selection reversed in subsequent wet years (e.g., 1984-85) when abundant rainfall increased the availability of small, soft seeds. Under these conditions, smaller beaks were favored (more efficient for handling small seeds), and mean beak depth decreased. This demonstrates that natural selection is responsive to current environmental conditions and has no fixed direction.

**ELI5:** Nope! When the rains came back and small seeds were plentiful again, the smaller-beaked finches actually did better. So beaks got smaller again. Evolution went in reverse! This proves evolution doesn't have a destination -- it just responds to whatever's happening right now.

flashcards.front: How did the Grants' study verify all five conditions for natural selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) High reproductive potential: finches can lay multiple clutches per year. (2) Stable to declining population: population didn't explode (89% died in drought). (3) Limited resources: drought eliminated most food. (4) Phenotypic variation: continuous variation in beak depth across the population. (5) Heritability: h2 = 0.65-0.80 for beak depth. All five conditions met, and the predicted evolutionary response was observed.

**ELI5:** The Grants checked every box: (1) Finches make lots of eggs. (2) But the population didn't grow (most died). (3) There wasn't enough food. (4) Every finch had a slightly different beak. (5) Big-beaked parents had big-beaked babies. All five conditions were met, and evolution happened exactly as predicted. It's like a perfect science experiment done by nature.

flashcards.front: What role does sexual selection play in the finch system?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In addition to natural selection via survival, sexual selection also contributed to beak evolution in G. fortis. Females preferentially mate with males whose songs and beak morphology match certain preferences. Beak shape affects song production (the beak acts as a resonating structure), creating a link between natural and sexual selection on beak morphology.

**ELI5:** Female finches are picky about who they mate with -- they prefer males with certain beak shapes and songs. Since beak shape actually affects how a finch sings (like how the shape of a trumpet affects its sound), beak evolution is driven by both survival AND attracting mates. It's a double whammy.

flashcards.front: What makes the Grants' study one of the best demonstrations of natural selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The study's strength lies in: (1) complete population monitoring (every individual tracked), (2) quantitative measurement of selection differentials, (3) known pedigrees allowing heritability estimation, (4) observed environmental cause (drought), (5) predicted and observed evolutionary response matching quantitative genetic theory, (6) multi-generational data, (7) demonstration of selection reversal. It is one of few studies showing all components of natural selection in a wild population.

**ELI5:** The Grants' study is special because they measured EVERYTHING: every bird, every beak, every parent-child pair, every seed. They saw the cause (drought), the selection (big beaks survived), the inheritance (big beaks passed on), and the result (bigger beaks next generation). They even saw it reverse. No other study has caught evolution so completely in the act.

flashcards.front: What is directional selection, as illustrated by the 1977 drought?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Directional selection occurs when individuals at one extreme of a phenotypic distribution have higher fitness than those at the other extreme, shifting the population mean in that direction. The 1977 drought imposed directional selection for larger beak depth -- the mean beak depth of the population shifted toward larger values after the selective event.

**ELI5:** Directional selection means the average shifts one way. During the drought, the average beak got bigger because all the small-beaked birds died. It's like if a basketball team cut everyone under 6 feet tall -- the average height of the remaining team would jump up. The whole bell curve shifted to the right.

flashcards.front: What was the approximate mortality rate during the 1977 drought?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Approximately 89% of the Geospiza fortis population on Daphne Major died during the 1977 drought. The population declined from approximately 1,200 individuals to roughly 180 survivors. This severe selective episode created an extremely strong selection differential for beak morphology.

**ELI5:** About 89% of the finches died -- almost 9 out of every 10. Out of about 1,200 birds, only about 180 survived. That's a brutal filter. The survivors weren't random -- they were disproportionately the ones with bigger, stronger beaks.

nodes.title: Descent with Modification & Common Ancestry
nodes.subtitle: Artificial selection, homology, tree-thinking (Lec 2 slides 32-41)

popup.heading: Lecture 2 — Descent with Modification

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Darwin's FIVE KEY POINTS about natural selection: (1) selection acts on individuals but consequences are seen in populations; (2) it acts on phenotypes, while evolution is measured as allele frequency change; (3) it is NOT forward-looking; (4) it results in descent with modification; (5) it is NOT progressive or linear — there is no higher or lower; (6) it does NOT lead to perfection.

sections.label: HOMOLOGY
sections.body: Shared structures inherited from a common ancestor. Forelimb homology: whale flipper, bat wing, human arm, and dog foreleg all have the same bone arrangement (humerus, radius, ulna, carpals, phalanges). Embryonic homologies: pharyngeal arches and notochord appear in all vertebrate embryos. Contrast with ANALOGY (bird wing vs insect wing — same function, different origins).

sections.label: ARTIFICIAL SELECTION
sections.body: Brassica oleracea gave rise to cabbage, broccoli, kale, Brussels sprouts, and kohlrabi — all one species, selected for different traits. Dogs came from wolves. Corn (maize) descends from teosinte.

sections.label: VESTIGIAL ORGANS
sections.body: Reduced but still present ancestral structures: whale pelvic bones, human appendix, cave-fish eye sockets, human arrector pili (goosebump) muscles.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body: COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS: The "March of Progress" iconography (ape → hominid → modern human in a linear sequence) seriously misrepresents evolution. It portrays evolution as a linear, directional progression toward a "higher" endpoint. In reality, evolution is a branching tree: lineages split, some go extinct, and there is no predetermined "goal." This misleading iconography reinforces the false idea that evolution is a ladder of progress rather than a bush of divergence.

examples: Artificial selection on dogs (from wolves), cabbage/broccoli/kale/Brussels sprouts/kohlrabi (all Brassica oleracea cultivars), corn (from teosinte) — proves phenotypes can be dramatically reshaped by selection in few generations.
Vertebrate forelimb homology: whale flipper, bat wing, human arm, dog foreleg — same bones in same arrangement.
Vertebrate embryonic homologies: pharyngeal arches, notochord — shared developmental stages reveal common ancestry.
Vestigial organs: whale pelvis, human appendix, cave-fish eyes, arrector pili muscles.
warnings: WATCH OUT: The "march of progress" image (ape → human) is MISLEADING — evolution is branching, not linear. Humans did NOT evolve FROM modern chimpanzees; we share a common ancestor.
popup.mnemonic: DHA-V = Descent + Homology + Artificial selection + Vestigial organs = evidence for common ancestry.

flashcard.front: How do vestigial organs provide evidence for descent with modification?
flashcard.back: Vestigial organs (e.g., whale hip bones, human appendix, flightless bird wings, cave fish eye sockets) are structures that have lost their original function but persist as reduced, functionless remnants. Their existence is only explicable under descent with modification: an ancestor used the structure, selection pressure for that function was relaxed, and the structure degraded over generations but was not fully eliminated. Under special creation, these "useless" structures would require ad-hoc explanations.

quiz.question: Which evidence most directly refutes the "march of progress" interpretation of evolution?
quiz.correct: Phylogenies show evolution is a branching tree, with modern species as tips — not a linear sequence
distractors: Mutations never happen in modern populations
All modern species are equally ancient
Fossils are rare and poorly preserved

quiz.question: All cultivated cabbages — broccoli, kohlrabi, kale, cauliflower, Brussels sprouts — are varieties of the single species Brassica oleracea, produced by artificial selection. What does this most directly demonstrate about evolution?
quiz.correct: Selection acting on standing genetic variation can produce dramatically different phenotypes within a species over relatively few generations — proving the raw material for evolution exists and responds to selection
distractors: Artificial selection proves that natural selection cannot produce such large morphological changes — humans are far more effective at directing evolution than natural forces could be
All Brassica cultivars are evidence of convergent evolution — each vegetable type independently evolved the same solution to the problem of being domesticated by humans
The Brassica example demonstrates Lamarckian inheritance — farmers selected plants they wanted, and those plants responded by producing offspring with the desired traits in the next generation

quiz.question: Whale pelvic bones are vestigial — they are present but non-functional remnants of hindlimb bones. Occasionally a whale is born with small external hind-flipper nubs. What does this phenotypic reappearance ("atavism") most directly show?
quiz.correct: The genetic pathways for hindlimb development still exist in the whale genome but are normally suppressed during development — vestigial organs can reappear when regulatory control breaks down, confirming descent from limbed ancestors
distractors: Atavisms disprove descent with modification — if whales truly descended from limbed ancestors, these developmental programs would have been completely deleted from the genome over millions of years
Whale hindlimb nubs are evidence of a Lamarckian reversion — the whale's body is reverting to an ancestral form because it perceives environmental pressure to walk on land again
Atavisms are purely random developmental errors with no evolutionary significance — the hindlimb nubs are caused by radiation-induced mutations during embryogenesis, unrelated to ancestry

quiz.question: The vertebrate forelimb bones (humerus, radius, ulna, carpals, phalanges) appear in bat wings, whale flippers, human arms, horse legs, and bird wings — all with the same set of bones in the same positional arrangement. This structural HOMOLOGY is BEST explained by:
quiz.correct: All tetrapods inherited the same forelimb bone developmental program from a common ancestor — the bones were modified by natural selection for different functions (flying, swimming, grasping, running) while the underlying developmental program remained conserved
distractors: Convergent evolution: all tetrapods independently evolved the same forelimb bone configuration because it is the optimal engineering solution for weight-bearing appendages
Parallel evolution: the same mutation in the same Hox gene occurred independently in all tetrapod lineages, producing identical forelimb configurations by chance
Natural selection is extremely constrained and can only produce one forelimb configuration — the homologous structure reflects a developmental constraint rather than shared ancestry

quiz.question: Vertebrate embryos share features like pharyngeal arches and notochord early in development, but these features are modified differently later in development across species. What does this pattern demonstrate?
quiz.correct: Developmental homologies reveal shared ancestry — the early developmental stages are conserved because they inherit the same basic body plan from a common ancestor, while later stages diverge to produce species-specific features
distractors: All vertebrates go through an adult "fish stage" at some point in their lives, supporting a strict Haeckelian recapitulation theory
Embryonic similarities are coincidences with no evolutionary meaning — different species independently develop similar structures at similar times by chance
Pharyngeal arches and notochord are still functional in adult humans and mammals, proving that vertebrates never really diverged from their aquatic ancestors

quiz.question: Humans and chimpanzees share approximately what proportion of their DNA?
quiz.correct: ~98.7% — but this does NOT mean humans evolved FROM modern chimps; instead, we share a common ancestor ~7 million years ago
distractors: ~98.7% — which means humans evolved directly from chimps and are therefore just highly evolved chimpanzees
~50% — making us more distant from chimps than from bananas
~100% — there is no genetic difference between humans and chimps, only environmental differences

quiz.question: The "march of progress" iconography (ape → hominid → modern human in a linear sequence) is popular but misleading. What aspect of evolutionary theory does it most seriously misrepresent?
quiz.correct: It portrays evolution as a linear, directional progression toward a "higher" endpoint, when in reality evolution is a branching tree with modern species at the tips — all equally "evolved" from ancestral lineages
distractors: It incorrectly shows apes as extinct — modern apes still exist and share a common ancestor with humans
It depicts humans walking upright, when in fact bipedalism evolved only recently and is not a defining trait of the human lineage
It shows too many transitional forms — fossil data actually show sudden, not gradual, transitions between hominid species

quiz.question: A researcher finds that the human appendix, whale pelvic bones, and cave-fish eye sockets are all described as "vestigial organs." What do these have in common evolutionarily?
quiz.correct: All are reduced, largely non-functional remnants of structures that were fully functional in ancestral populations — their persistence reveals descent from ancestors in which they served specific functions
distractors: All are completely useless and prove that evolution is imperfect at removing unnecessary structures
All will be completely eliminated within the next 100 generations as selection continues to remove them
All are examples of Lamarckian disuse — individuals who did not use these structures passed on shortened versions to their offspring

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: From which wild ancestor is domesticated corn (maize) descended?
quiz.correct: Teosinte — a wild Mexican grass that was artificially selected over thousands of years into modern corn
distractors: Wheat (Triticum aestivum)
Rice (Oryza sativa)
Sorghum — a related wild grass

quiz.question: L4 APPLICATION: A farmer notices his pig has vestigial "dew claws" on its feet — small, non-functional digits higher up on the leg. How should this be interpreted evolutionarily?
quiz.correct: The dew claws are vestigial remnants of ancestral digits that were functional in earlier ungulate ancestors — their persistence in reduced form is direct evidence of descent with modification
distractors: The dew claws arose by a recent mutation and will become fully functional digits in future generations if selected
The dew claws are evidence of special creation — they have no evolutionary explanation and must have been placed there intentionally
The dew claws are a Lamarckian response to walking on soft ground, where the pig's ancestors failed to use extra digits
