visual.description: Branching vs linear models of evolution
visual.mnemonic: Tree not ladder.
visual.trap: Humans are not descended from chimps — humans and chimps share a common ancestor ~7 million years ago.

diagram.title: Tree-Thinking — Components of a Phylogeny

nodes.label: Root / outgroup
nodes.detail: The base of the tree, chosen to polarize character states. The outgroup is a taxon known (from other evidence) to have diverged before the rest of the ingroup — it defines which states are ancestral and which are derived.

nodes.label: Internal node (speciation event)
nodes.detail: Represents an inferred common ancestor — NOT a direct fossil, just a logical split point where one lineage became two. Supported by shared derived characters (synapomorphies) in the descendants.

nodes.label: Branch
nodes.detail: A lineage persisting through time between splits. Length may represent time or amount of character change. A branch does NOT imply "primitive" or "advanced" — it just traces ancestry.

nodes.label: Tip (terminal taxon)
nodes.detail: A present-day species, population, or molecular sequence at the end of a branch. Tips are what we actually observe; internal nodes and roots are inferred.

nodes.label: Clade / monophyletic group
nodes.detail: An ancestor plus ALL of its descendants. The only valid type of taxonomic group in modern phylogenetic systematics. A clade can be cut from a tree with a single scissor snip.
nodes.mnemonic: clade = complete family tree branch

nodes.label: Sister taxa
nodes.detail: Two lineages sharing an immediate common ancestor — i.e., separated by exactly one internal node. Humans and chimps are sister taxa. Sisters share a more recent ancestor with each other than with any other taxon.

nodes.label: Robbins snail island example
nodes.detail: Population 1 colonizes island 2 (pink form), which then seeds island 3 (high-spire form), which in turn seeds island 4. On a phylogeny, the populations on islands 3 and 4 are sister taxa — more closely related to each other than either is to the population on island 1.
nodes.watchOut: Current island-1 snails are NOT the ancestors of islands 3 and 4 — modern tips are cousins, not ancestors. Every living taxon is at a tip, never at a node.

nodes.label: Node rotations are free
nodes.detail: Rotating any internal node around its pivot does not change the phylogeny. Only the TOPOLOGY (who shares an ancestor with whom) matters — not the left-right arrangement of tips on the page. Two trees that look different may be identical phylogenies.
nodes.watchOut: NEVER read trees left-to-right as "progress." There is no "highest" or "most evolved" tip. Every tip has had exactly the same amount of evolution since the root.

diagram.mnemonic: Tip, Branch, Node, Root, Clade — the five words that describe any phylogeny.

flashcards.front: What are Darwin's five key points about natural selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Natural selection acts on individuals but is seen at the population level. (2) It acts on phenotypes, not directly on genotypes. (3) It is not forward-looking or goal-directed (no teleology). (4) It produces descent with modification (organisms share common ancestors but diverge over time). (5) It does not produce perfection -- only 'good enough' solutions constrained by existing variation, history, and trade-offs.

**ELI5:** Five things to remember about natural selection: (1) Individual animals get selected, but evolution shows up in groups. (2) Nature judges how you look, not your genes directly. (3) There's no plan or goal -- just what works right now. (4) All life is related but changes over time. (5) Nothing comes out perfect -- just good enough.

flashcards.front: What is homology in evolutionary biology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Homology refers to similarity between structures in different organisms due to shared ancestry (inherited from a common ancestor). Homologous structures may differ in form and function (e.g., forelimbs of whales, bats, humans, dogs) but share underlying structural elements (same bones: humerus, radius, ulna, carpals, metacarpals, phalanges). Homology is evidence of common descent.

**ELI5:** Homology means 'same origin, different use.' Your arm, a whale's flipper, a bat's wing, and a dog's front leg all have the same bones arranged in the same basic pattern. They look different because they do different jobs, but they all started from the same ancestor's limb. It's like how English, Spanish, and French all came from Latin.

flashcards.front: Give the classic example of forelimb homology.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The vertebrate forelimb demonstrates homology across: human arm (manipulation), whale flipper (swimming), bat wing (flying), dog forelimb (running). All share the same skeletal elements -- humerus, radius, ulna, carpals, metacarpals, phalanges -- modified in proportion and shape for different functions. The underlying structural plan is conserved despite functional divergence.

**ELI5:** Look at these four arms: a human's hand for grabbing, a whale's flipper for swimming, a bat's wing for flying, a dog's leg for running. They all have the exact same bones inside! Humerus, radius, ulna, wrist bones, finger bones -- just stretched, shrunk, or fused differently. Same blueprint, different uses, one ancestor.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between homology and analogy?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Homology = structural similarity due to common descent (shared ancestry), regardless of function (e.g., bat wing and human arm). Analogy (convergent evolution) = functional similarity NOT due to common descent, but due to similar selective pressures (e.g., bat wing and insect wing). Analogous structures have different developmental origins and structural plans. Distinguishing them is crucial for phylogenetics.

**ELI5:** Homology = same ancestor, maybe different job (your arm and a whale flipper -- same bones, different use). Analogy = same job, different ancestor (bird wing and butterfly wing -- both fly, but built totally differently). Homology = genuine family resemblance. Analogy = strangers who happen to wear the same outfit.

flashcards.front: What is Brassica oleracea, and how does it demonstrate artificial selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Brassica oleracea is a single wild mustard species that humans have artificially selected into dramatically different cultivars: broccoli (selected for flower clusters), cauliflower (inflorescence), cabbage (terminal bud/leaves), Brussels sprouts (lateral buds), kale (leaves), and kohlrabi (stem). All are the same species, demonstrating the power of selection on existing variation.

**ELI5:** Broccoli, cauliflower, cabbage, Brussels sprouts, kale, and kohlrabi are ALL the same species -- just one wild mustard plant that humans bred in different directions. Want big leaves? You get kale. Want a big flower? Broccoli. It's like one dog breed split into Great Danes and Chihuahuas, except with vegetables.

flashcards.front: How do dogs demonstrate artificial selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** All domestic dog breeds (Canis lupus familiaris) are descended from wolves (Canis lupus) through ~15,000-40,000 years of artificial selection. The enormous morphological diversity -- from Chihuahuas to Great Danes -- arose from selection on standing genetic variation in the ancestral wolf population. This demonstrates how selection can produce dramatic phenotypic change relatively quickly.

**ELI5:** Every dog -- from a tiny Chihuahua to a giant Great Dane -- came from wolves. Humans just kept picking the ones they liked and breeding them together for thousands of years. The result is more physical variety in dogs than in almost any other species. If humans can do this much in a few thousand years, imagine what nature can do in millions.

flashcards.front: How does corn (maize) demonstrate artificial selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Modern maize (Zea mays) was domesticated from teosinte (Zea mays ssp. parviglumis) approximately 9,000 years ago in Mexico. Teosinte has small ears with few hard kernels and a shattering rachis. Artificial selection transformed it into modern corn with large cobs, hundreds of soft kernels, and a non-shattering rachis. Remarkably, only ~5 major genetic loci account for most morphological differences.

**ELI5:** Modern corn came from a scraggly grass called teosinte that had tiny 'ears' with maybe 5-12 hard kernels. Ancient farmers kept planting the biggest, tastiest seeds for thousands of years, and eventually got the big fat corn cobs we eat today. The two plants look nothing alike, but they're the same species -- just massively reshaped by human selection.

flashcards.front: What are vestigial structures, and how do they support evolution?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Vestigial structures are anatomical features that have lost most or all of their ancestral function through evolution. They are remnants of structures that were functional in ancestors. Examples: whale pelvic bones (remnant of terrestrial ancestor's hindlimbs), human appendix (reduced cecum), cave fish eyes (degenerated in darkness), arrector pili muscles (once raised fur for insulation/display). They are evidence of descent with modification.

**ELI5:** Vestigial structures are evolutionary leftovers -- body parts that used to do something useful in an ancestor but don't anymore. Whales have tiny hidden leg bones (from when their ancestors walked on land). Blind cave fish still grow eyes that don't work. It's like your phone still having a calculator app from a feature phone era -- it hints at the past.

flashcards.front: Give four examples of vestigial structures.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Whale pelvic/femur bones -- remnants of hindlimbs from terrestrial ancestors, now reduced to small internal bones with no locomotor function. (2) Human vermiform appendix -- reduced cecum, vestigial from herbivorous ancestors. (3) Cave fish eyes -- present but non-functional in permanently dark cave environments. (4) Human arrector pili muscles -- tiny muscles that erect body hair (goosebumps), vestigial from ancestors with dense fur where this aided thermoregulation and display.

**ELI5:** (1) Whales have tiny leg bones hidden inside their bodies -- from when their great-great-grandparents walked on land. (2) Your appendix used to help digest tough plants in your ancestors but now mostly just sits there. (3) Blind cave fish grow eyes that get covered with skin -- eyes are useless in total darkness. (4) Goosebumps: your muscles try to fluff up fur you don't have anymore.

flashcards.front: Why is the 'March of Progress' image misleading?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The iconic linear 'March of Progress' (ape-to-human sequence) is misleading because: (1) evolution is a branching tree, not a linear ladder; (2) it implies progression toward a 'goal' (humans), which contradicts the non-teleological nature of evolution; (3) modern apes are NOT ancestral to humans -- they are cousin lineages; (4) it implies improvement/directionality, which evolution does not have; (5) it represents only one of millions of evolutionary lineages.

**ELI5:** That famous picture of an ape slowly turning into a man walking upright is WRONG in almost every way. Evolution isn't a straight line heading toward humans. It's more like a bush with millions of branches going in all directions. Chimps aren't our ancestors -- they're our cousins. We're not the 'end goal' of evolution. We're just one twig on an enormous tree.

flashcards.front: Why doesn't natural selection produce perfection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Natural selection cannot produce perfection because: (1) it can only work with existing variation (constrained by mutation supply), (2) genetic constraints and trade-offs limit optimization (e.g., pleiotropy), (3) environments constantly change (what's optimal now may not be tomorrow), (4) historical contingency (evolution modifies existing structures, doesn't design from scratch), (5) genetic drift can fix suboptimal alleles, (6) time lags between environmental change and adaptive response.

**ELI5:** Evolution can't build from scratch -- it can only modify what already exists, like remodeling a house instead of tearing it down and rebuilding. You're stuck with the original floor plan. Plus, the 'rules' keep changing (environments shift), and sometimes random luck matters more than quality. That's why humans have back pain (our spine was designed for four legs) and why we choke on food (breathing and eating share a tube).

flashcards.front: What does 'descent with modification' mean?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Descent with modification is Darwin's central concept: all organisms share common ancestors (descent), and lineages change over time as they adapt to different environments (modification). This explains both the unity of life (shared features due to common ancestry) and its diversity (accumulated modifications across diverging lineages). It predicts nested hierarchies of relatedness.

**ELI5:** 'Descent with modification' means all living things are related (we all share great-great-great-grandparents) AND they change over time. It's like a family tree where every generation looks slightly different from the last. Over millions of years, some branches became fish, some became trees, some became you. Same family, different modifications.

flashcards.front: What types of evidence support common ancestry?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Evidence for common ancestry includes: (1) homologous structures (shared skeletal plans), (2) embryological similarities (pharyngeal arches, similar early development across vertebrates), (3) vestigial structures, (4) molecular homology (universal genetic code, shared genes), (5) biogeographic patterns, (6) fossil record (transitional forms), (7) artificial selection demonstrating modification potential, (8) observed speciation events.

**ELI5:** Many clues prove all life is related: (1) Same arm bones in whales, bats, and humans. (2) Embryos of fish, chickens, and humans look eerily similar early on. (3) Leftover body parts (whale leg bones). (4) ALL life uses the same DNA code. (5) Island animals look like nearby mainland ones. (6) Fossils show species between groups (like fish-to-land-animal transitions). It's like a detective case with overwhelming evidence.

flashcards.front: What is embryological homology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Embryological homology refers to similarities in developmental stages across different species, reflecting shared ancestry. For example, all vertebrate embryos develop pharyngeal arches (gill slits in fish, modified into jaw/ear structures in mammals), notochords, and similar body plans during early development, even when adult forms are radically different. These shared developmental programs are inherited from common ancestors.

**ELI5:** In the early weeks of development, a human embryo, a chicken embryo, and a fish embryo look almost identical -- they all have little gill-like slits and tails. As they grow, they become very different. But that early similarity is like finding the same baby photo in three different people's albums -- it proves they're related.

nodes.title: Genes, Proteins & Gene Expression
nodes.subtitle: DNA-RNA-protein and regulatory networks (Lec 3 slides 1-9)

popup.heading: Lecture 3 — Genes and Heritable Variation

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: The CENTRAL DOGMA of molecular biology: DNA → RNA → Protein. Transcription is carried out by RNA polymerase; translation is carried out by the ribosome. The universal use of DNA, RNA, and protein across all three domains of life (bacteria, archaea, eukaryotes) is itself a homology — strong evidence of common ancestry.

sections.label: KEY EXAMPLE: HUMAN CHROMOSOME 2
sections.body: Humans have 23 chromosome pairs; great apes have 24. Human chromosome 2 is the FUSION product of two ancestral ape chromosomes — it contains two centromeres (one vestigial) and fused telomeric sequences internally, confirming the fusion.

sections.label: REGULATION
sections.body: Gene expression is regulated by histones (chromatin state), transcription factors, hormones, and environmental signals. Dolphin hindlimb genes are STILL PRESENT in the genome but transcriptionally SILENCED — showing that evolutionary loss of a trait can occur via regulation rather than gene deletion.

examples: DNA is a universal homology across all life — the code itself is evidence of common ancestry.
Human chromosome 2 is the fusion of two ancestral ape chromosomes (has 2 centromeres, one vestigial).
Dolphin hindlimb genes are still present but transcriptionally silenced — regulation, not gene loss, explains limb absence.
Gene expression is regulated by complex networks: enhancers, transcription factors, histones, hormones, environment.
warnings: WATCH OUT: The central dogma (DNA → RNA → Protein) does NOT mean every gene is always expressed. Regulation determines which genes are ON or OFF.
WATCH OUT: Reverse transcription (retroviruses, retrotransposons) does NOT contradict the central dogma — the dogma describes the direction of information flow, not an absolute prohibition.
popup.mnemonic: DNA → RNA → Protein, but REGULATION determines phenotype.

flashcard.front: Why are dolphin hindlimb genes a surprising illustration of gene expression regulation?
flashcard.back: Dolphins are limbless in hindquarters, but genomic sequencing reveals the genes for building hindlimbs are still present in their DNA. Their hindlimbs do not develop because the regulatory pathway controlling limb outgrowth is suppressed during development — not because the genes were deleted. This shows that evolution can "lose" traits not by removing genes but by changing expression patterns. Occasionally, a dolphin is born with vestigial hindlimbs, revealing the dormant genetic capacity.

quiz.question: The observation that DNA is used as the hereditary molecule in all domains of life (bacteria, archaea, eukaryotes) is evidence for:
quiz.correct: Common ancestry of all extant life
distractors: Independent origins of each domain
Convergent molecular evolution
Horizontal gene transfer eliminating other molecules

quiz.question: Dolphins do not have hindlimbs, yet their genomes contain the full set of hindlimb developmental genes. Occasionally, a dolphin is born with small external flipper nubs at the hindquarter. What does this most directly demonstrate about the relationship between genes and phenotype?
quiz.correct: The same genome can produce different phenotypes depending on WHICH genes are expressed — evolution can "silence" traits by changing regulatory control without deleting the underlying genes
distractors: It demonstrates that dolphin hindlimb genes have accumulated enough mutations to become pseudogenes — they are present but non-functional, and the rare flipper nubs represent random developmental noise
It proves that acquired traits can be inherited — dolphins lost their hindlimbs through Lamarckian disuse, but the genes were never fully eliminated, causing occasional reversions in offspring
It demonstrates that genomic drift is the primary cause of limb loss in cetaceans — the hindlimb genes are present by chance, not because they were actively conserved under any selective pressure

quiz.question: The "central dogma" states DNA → RNA → protein. A student claims retroviruses like HIV violate the central dogma because they convert RNA back to DNA using reverse transcriptase. Is the student correct?
quiz.correct: The student is incorrect — the central dogma refers to the direction of SEQUENCE INFORMATION in normal protein synthesis. Reverse transcription does not violate it because the dogma also explicitly allows RNA → DNA as a special transfer; what it prohibits is protein → nucleic acid information flow
distractors: The student is correct — reverse transcriptase is a major exception to the central dogma because DNA was supposed to be the starting point for all information flow, not RNA

The student is incorrect — HIV is not a true retrovirus because it lacks ribosomes and cannot perform its own protein synthesis; it relies entirely on host cell machinery, so the central dogma applies only to host cell processes

The student is correct — the central dogma was specifically designed to describe eukaryotic cells; viral systems like HIV use completely different information flow rules that operate independently of the dogma

quiz.question: Two organisms have identical DNA sequences at a protein-coding gene, yet one has much higher levels of the encoded protein than the other. What type of variation is responsible, and does this violate the central dogma?
quiz.correct: Regulatory variation — differences in transcription factor binding sites, enhancers, or silencers upstream of the gene cause different transcription rates. This does NOT violate the central dogma, which describes directional information flow, not expression levels
distractors: Post-translational variation — different organisms degrade the protein at different rates through the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway; this is still consistent with the central dogma because the sequence information flows normally

Epigenetic variation — one organism has methylated the protein-coding region itself, changing the nucleotide sequence at the phenotypic level without altering the underlying DNA; this is an exception to the central dogma

Translational variation — ribosomes in one organism read the same mRNA twice per transcript, doubling protein output; this violates the central dogma because the protein output exceeds what the mRNA should encode

quiz.question: Gene expression is controlled by multiple regulatory elements. Which of the following is NOT a mechanism of transcriptional regulation mentioned in Lecture 3?
quiz.correct: Ribosomal decoding speed — how fast ribosomes read mRNA codons during translation
distractors: Enhancers — DNA sequences that increase transcription when bound by activator proteins
Transcription factors — proteins that bind DNA to activate or repress genes
Chromatin state — DNA accessibility controlled by histone modifications

quiz.question: A human liver cell and a human neuron have the same DNA but produce very different proteins and perform very different functions. How is this possible?
quiz.correct: Gene regulation determines which genes are expressed in each cell type — the same genome can produce hundreds of different cell types by turning different sets of genes ON and OFF
distractors: The two cells have different DNA sequences due to somatic mutations that accumulated during development
The two cells use different genetic codes — liver cells use a modified version of the DNA code compared to neurons
Only liver cells contain the full genome; neurons discard most of their DNA during maturation and retain only the genes they need

quiz.question: The universality of DNA as the hereditary molecule across bacteria, archaea, and eukaryotes is explained by what evolutionary principle?
quiz.correct: Common ancestry — all extant life descended from a single ancestral lineage that already used DNA, and this fundamental feature has been conserved across all descendants
distractors: Convergent molecular evolution — each domain independently arrived at DNA because it is the optimal hereditary molecule, without any shared ancestry
Horizontal gene transfer — DNA has been passed between unrelated domains so many times that all life now shares DNA despite having different origins
Environmental pressure — all cellular environments on Earth happen to favor DNA-based heredity, so all life independently evolved it

quiz.question: The dolphin hindlimb example illustrates that phenotypic change during evolution can occur by:
quiz.correct: Changes in gene REGULATION rather than gene deletion — dolphin genomes still contain the hindlimb developmental genes, but these genes are transcriptionally silenced during development, so the limbs do not form
distractors: Complete deletion of hindlimb genes from the dolphin genome
Replacement of dolphin DNA with fish DNA through horizontal gene transfer
Lamarckian loss — dolphin ancestors stopped using their hindlimbs, which caused the genes to disappear across generations

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: How many chromosome pairs do humans have, and how many do most great apes have?
quiz.correct: Humans have 23 pairs; great apes have 24. The difference is because human chromosome 2 is a fusion of two ancestral ape chromosomes
distractors: Humans have 24 pairs; great apes have 23 (the reverse)
Both humans and great apes have 23 pairs — no difference
Humans have 46 pairs; great apes have 48 pairs

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: What molecular evidence specifically supports the claim that human chromosome 2 arose from a fusion of two ancestral ape chromosomes?
quiz.correct: Human chromosome 2 contains two centromeres (one vestigial) and internal telomeric DNA sequences — signatures of a head-to-head fusion of two ancestral chromosomes
distractors: Human chromosome 2 is twice as long as any ape chromosome
Human chromosome 2 lacks a centromere entirely because it was formed by a deletion
Human chromosome 2 contains only coding genes with no regulatory elements

visual.mnemonic: Genes code, regulation decides.
visual.trap: Having a gene does NOT mean the trait is expressed. Regulation is just as important as sequence.

diagram.title: Hox and the Regulatory Toolkit of Evo-Devo

nodes.label: Ancestral Hox cluster
nodes.detail: The single Hox cluster present in the last common bilaterian ancestor. Encodes transcription factors that pattern the anterior-posterior body axis. Everything downstream — flies, mice, humans — descended from this one toolkit.
nodes.mnemonic: One toolkit to build them all.

nodes.label: Drosophila Hox
nodes.detail: Fruit flies carry one Hox cluster split into two complexes (Antennapedia and Bithorax) containing 8 homeotic genes. Classic mutants like Antennapedia (legs where antennae should be) and Ultrabithorax (four wings) first revealed Hox function.
nodes.mnemonic: Fly Hox made the field.

nodes.label: Mouse Hox
nodes.detail: Vertebrate Hox was duplicated TWICE during early vertebrate evolution, producing four clusters (HoxA, HoxB, HoxC, HoxD) with roughly 10 genes each. Redundancy among paralogs allowed evolutionary flexibility without losing essential functions.
nodes.mnemonic: Two whole-genome duplications = 4 Hox clusters.

nodes.label: Colinear arrangement
nodes.detail: Hox genes are arranged on the chromosome in the same order in which they are expressed along the anterior-posterior body axis. 3-prime genes pattern the head, 5-prime genes pattern the tail. This spatial colinearity is conserved from flies to humans and is unique to Hox clusters.
nodes.mnemonic: Chromosome order = body order.

nodes.label: Pitx1 regulatory change
nodes.detail: Freshwater threespine sticklebacks (Gasterosteus aculeatus) lost their pelvic spines repeatedly in different lakes. David Kingsley's lab showed this was driven by deletion of a pelvic ENHANCER of the Pitx1 gene, not a change in the coding sequence. The protein is fine — only its deployment changed.
nodes.mnemonic: Same gene, different switch = dropped pelvis.
nodes.watchOut: Regulatory vs coding evolution are NOT mutually exclusive — both contribute to morphology.

nodes.label: Bmp4 + calmodulin expression
nodes.detail: Abzhanov, Tabin, and Grant showed that Galapagos finch beak diversity is produced by changes in the expression LEVELS of just two proteins: Bmp4 (beak depth and width) and calmodulin (beak length). Small regulatory tweaks produced the adaptive radiation Darwin famously documented.
nodes.mnemonic: Two knobs, 14 beaks.

nodes.label: Distalless (Dll)
nodes.detail: Distalless is an appendage-identity gene deployed to build limbs across phyla: insect legs, crustacean antennae, sea urchin tube feet, vertebrate limbs, annelid parapodia. A shared developmental logic used wherever the body needs to grow a projecting structure.
nodes.mnemonic: Dll = "build an outgrowth here".

diagram.mnemonic: Small regulatory changes produce big morphological differences. Same toolkit, different deployment.

flashcards.front: What is the central dogma of molecular biology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The central dogma states that genetic information flows from DNA to RNA to protein: DNA is transcribed into mRNA by RNA polymerase, and mRNA is translated into protein by ribosomes. DNA serves as the heritable information store; RNA is the intermediate messenger; proteins are the functional molecules that carry out cellular processes. Information does not flow from protein back to nucleic acid.

**ELI5:** The central dogma is biology's production line: DNA is the master blueprint (locked in the vault), RNA is the photocopy that goes to the factory floor, and protein is the finished product that does the actual work. Information flows one way: blueprint -> photocopy -> product. You don't reverse-engineer the blueprint from the product.

flashcards.front: What is transcription, and what enzyme carries it out?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Transcription is the synthesis of a complementary RNA strand from a DNA template, catalyzed by RNA polymerase. The enzyme reads the template DNA strand 3' to 5' and synthesizes mRNA in the 5' to 3' direction. In eukaryotes, transcription occurs in the nucleus and the pre-mRNA is processed (5' cap, 3' poly-A tail, intron splicing) before export to the cytoplasm.

**ELI5:** Transcription is photocopying the DNA. An enzyme called RNA polymerase unzips the DNA, reads one side, and makes an RNA copy of the gene. It's like a scribe in a library who can't take the original book out, so they copy the relevant page (RNA) and carry that copy to the workshop (ribosome).

flashcards.front: What is translation, and what structure carries it out?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Translation is the synthesis of a polypeptide (protein) from an mRNA template, carried out by the ribosome. The ribosome reads mRNA codons (three-nucleotide sequences) and, with the aid of tRNAs carrying specific amino acids, assembles the polypeptide chain according to the genetic code. Translation occurs in the cytoplasm (on free ribosomes or rough ER).

**ELI5:** Translation is reading the RNA photocopy and building the actual product. The ribosome is like a little factory robot that reads the RNA instructions three letters at a time and snaps together the correct amino acid building blocks to build a protein. It's assembling LEGO pieces following coded instructions.

flashcards.front: What is the evidence from human chromosome 2 for common ancestry with apes?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Humans have 23 chromosome pairs; great apes have 24. Human chromosome 2 shows evidence of an ancient fusion of two ancestral chromosomes: (1) it contains two centromeric regions (one functional, one vestigial), (2) it has interstitial (internal) telomeric sequences at the fusion site (telomeres normally only occur at chromosome ends), (3) its banding pattern matches two separate ape chromosomes (2A and 2B). This is a molecular 'fossil' of the fusion event.

**ELI5:** Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes but apes have 24. Scientists found that our chromosome 2 is actually two ape chromosomes glued together! The evidence: it has two center points (centromeres) when it should have one, and it has end-cap sequences (telomeres) stuck in the MIDDLE where two chromosomes fused. It's like finding the seam where two books were taped together into one.

flashcards.front: What are four mechanisms of gene regulation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Chromatin remodeling/histone modification -- acetylation, methylation, etc. alter DNA accessibility. (2) Transcription factors (TFs) -- proteins that bind DNA regulatory regions (promoters, enhancers) to activate or repress transcription. (3) Hormonal regulation -- hormones (e.g., steroid hormones) enter cells and activate nuclear receptors that function as TFs. (4) Environmental regulation -- external signals (temperature, nutrition, light) influence gene expression through signal transduction pathways.

**ELI5:** Genes aren't just 'on' or 'off' -- they're controlled like a dimmer switch by: (1) Histones -- proteins that DNA wraps around, which can be loosened or tightened to hide or expose genes. (2) Transcription factors -- helper proteins that sit on DNA and tell RNA polymerase 'go' or 'stop.' (3) Hormones -- chemical messengers that travel through your blood and flip gene switches. (4) Environment -- things like temperature and food can change which genes are active.

flashcards.front: What is the evolutionary significance of dolphin hindlimb genes?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Dolphins possess the same hindlimb developmental genes (e.g., Sonic hedgehog, Tbx4) as terrestrial mammals, but these genes are silenced during development through regulatory changes (altered enhancer activity). The genes are intact but not expressed at the right time/place. This demonstrates that evolution often works by modifying gene regulation rather than eliminating genes, and provides molecular evidence of dolphins' terrestrial ancestry.

**ELI5:** Dolphins still have the genes for making legs -- they just turned off! The instruction manual still has the 'build legs' chapter, but the switch is flipped to 'skip.' This is amazing evidence that dolphins descended from animals that walked on land. Evolution didn't delete the leg genes; it just silenced them.

flashcards.front: Why does the universality of the genetic code support common ancestry?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Nearly all living organisms (from bacteria to humans) use the same genetic code: the same 64 codons specify the same 20 amino acids and stop signals. This universality is most parsimoniously explained by inheritance from a single common ancestor (LUCA -- Last Universal Common Ancestor). If life arose independently multiple times, different genetic codes would be expected. Minor code variations in some mitochondria and microorganisms represent derived modifications.

**ELI5:** Every living thing on Earth -- from bacteria to mushrooms to whales to you -- uses the exact same DNA language. The 'word' ATG always means the same amino acid everywhere. The chances of this happening by coincidence are essentially zero. It's like discovering that every book ever written uses the same alphabet -- the simplest explanation is they all came from the same source.

flashcards.front: Does reverse transcription (RNA to DNA) violate the central dogma?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** No. Reverse transcription (catalyzed by reverse transcriptase, as in retroviruses like HIV) converts RNA back to DNA, but this does not violate the central dogma. The dogma specifically states that information cannot flow from protein to nucleic acid. RNA-to-DNA is a nucleic acid to nucleic acid conversion. Crick's original formulation explicitly allowed for RNA-to-DNA transfer.

**ELI5:** No! The central dogma says information can't go from protein BACK to DNA. Going from RNA back to DNA is fine -- it's still nucleic acid talking to nucleic acid (like translating between two similar languages). The 'forbidden' direction would be protein back to DNA, which never happens. Retroviruses going RNA -> DNA is perfectly compatible.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between DNA, RNA, and protein in terms of information flow?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** DNA is the permanent information repository (double-stranded, stable, replicated). RNA is the transient intermediate (single-stranded, less stable, diverse functions: mRNA, tRNA, rRNA, regulatory RNA). Protein is the functional endpoint (enzymes, structural components, signaling molecules). The flow DNA -> RNA -> Protein represents increasing functional specificity and decreasing informational permanence. DNA replication (DNA -> DNA) and reverse transcription (RNA -> DNA) are also possible.

**ELI5:** Think of it as: DNA = the original recipe book kept safe in a library. RNA = the handwritten copy you take to the kitchen. Protein = the actual cake you bake. The recipe book is permanent and precious. The handwritten copy is temporary. The cake does the actual job of being delicious. Information flows from book to copy to cake, not backwards.

flashcards.front: Why is gene regulation important for understanding evolution?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Gene regulation is a major mechanism of evolutionary change. Many evolutionary differences between species result from changes in WHEN, WHERE, and HOW MUCH genes are expressed (cis-regulatory evolution) rather than changes in the protein-coding sequences themselves. Humans and chimpanzees share ~99% coding DNA; many differences are regulatory. Evo-devo research shows that morphological diversity often arises from modifications in developmental gene regulation.

**ELI5:** Here's a mind-blowing fact: humans and chimps have almost identical genes, but we look very different. The difference is mostly in which genes are turned on, when, and how much -- like having the same ingredients but following different recipes. Evolution often works by changing the recipe (regulation) rather than the ingredients (genes).

flashcards.front: What are histones, and how do they affect gene expression?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Histones are alkaline proteins around which DNA winds to form nucleosomes (the fundamental unit of chromatin). Chemical modifications to histone tails (acetylation, methylation, phosphorylation, ubiquitination) alter chromatin structure. Acetylation generally loosens chromatin (euchromatin), increasing gene accessibility and transcription. Methylation can either activate or repress, depending on the specific residue. This is a key mechanism of epigenetic regulation.

**ELI5:** DNA is super long, so it wraps around little protein spools called histones, like thread around bobbins. When the thread is wound tight, the cell can't read the DNA (gene is OFF). When it's loosened, the gene becomes readable (gene is ON). Chemical tags on the histones control how tight or loose the winding is -- it's like adjusting how tightly you roll up a poster to hide or show the image.

nodes.title: Mutations: Raw Material of Evolution
nodes.subtitle: Types, rates, and effects (Lec 3 slides 10-17)

popup.heading: Lecture 3 — Mutations

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Mutations are the ultimate source of new genetic variation. Humans (and Neanderthals) have 23 chromosome pairs; other great apes have 24 — reflecting the #2 fusion. Most mutations are NEUTRAL or SLIGHTLY DELETERIOUS; strongly beneficial mutations are rare. Mutation rate examples: yeast ~3×10⁻⁸ per site; Arabidopsis ~7×10⁻⁹ per site per generation.

sections.label: SOMATIC vs GERMLINE
sections.body: SOMATIC mutations occur in body cells and die with the individual — they are not inherited. GERMLINE mutations occur in cells that give rise to gametes and CAN be passed to offspring — they are the raw material of evolution.

sections.label: POINT MUTATION EXAMPLES
sections.body: Albinism (FGFR3 mutations); thumb polydactyly (LMBR1 C→T substitution); Hutchinson-Gilford progeria syndrome (mutation in the Lamin A gene, causing premature aging).

sections.label: KEY TERMS
sections.body: Point mutation, silent/missense/nonsense substitution, frameshift (insertion/deletion), germline vs somatic.

examples: Point mutations: substitution (silent / missense / nonsense), insertion, deletion.
Yeast mutation rate: ~3 × 10⁻⁸ per site per generation.
Arabidopsis thaliana mutation rate: ~7 × 10⁻⁹ per site per generation.
Human germ-line mutation rate: ~70 new mutations per generation (of ~3 billion bp).
Albinism caused by FGFR3 mutations; polydactyly by LMBR1 C→T substitution; Hutchinson-Gilford progeria by Lamin A mutation.
Mutations in multicellular organisms: only germline mutations are heritable; somatic mutations affect only the individual.
warnings: WATCH OUT: Most mutations are NEUTRAL or slightly deleterious. Beneficial mutations are rare.
WATCH OUT: Mutation rate is NOT determined by need — mutation is a STOCHASTIC process that happens independently of whether the organism would benefit from the change.
popup.mnemonic: MRS-BE = Mutations are Random, Stochastic, Beneficial-rarely, Essential.

flashcard.front: Compare the evolutionary consequences of somatic versus germline mutations.
flashcard.back: SOMATIC mutations occur in non-reproductive cells (skin, liver, etc.). They affect only the individual and die with that individual — they cannot be inherited, so they do not contribute to population-level evolution. GERMLINE mutations occur in cells that give rise to gametes (eggs and sperm). These can be passed to offspring and become the raw material of evolution. Cancer is typically caused by accumulated somatic mutations; evolution is driven by germline mutations.

quiz.question: A woman develops a BRCA1 mutation in breast tissue during adulthood. This mutation is:
quiz.correct: Somatic — it will not be inherited by her children
distractors: Germline — her children will inherit it with 50% probability
Both somatic and germline — all cell mutations are heritable
Not a true mutation because it occurred after birth

quiz.question: The human germline mutation rate is approximately 70 new mutations per generation across ~3 billion base pairs. What is the evolutionary significance of this number?
quiz.correct: It means each new human carries about 70 novel variants not present in either parent — providing a constant, low-level input of new alleles into the population that are the ultimate source of genetic variation for selection and drift to act on
distractors: 70 mutations per generation is dangerously high — it means natural populations should accumulate mutational load faster than selection can remove it, eventually driving populations extinct through mutational meltdown
70 mutations per generation proves that Arabidopsis thaliana (with ~7 × 10⁻⁹ per site) evolves faster than humans, because 70 out of 3 billion is a lower per-site rate than Arabidopsis

70 mutations per generation means that after just 10 generations, a lineage will have accumulated 700 mutations — enough to make the descendants a different species from the founders by Hardy-Weinberg standards

quiz.question: A point mutation in exon 5 of a gene changes codon GAA (Glu) to GAG (still Glu). This mutation is described as "silent." What is the most important caveat about assuming it has NO fitness effect?
quiz.correct: Silent mutations can affect mRNA secondary structure, splicing enhancers, or codon usage bias (some codons are translated faster), potentially altering protein production rate or folding — a truly zero-effect assumption requires experimental testing
distractors: Silent mutations always have exactly zero fitness effect by definition — that is what "silent" means; any fitness effect would re-classify the mutation as a missense or regulatory variant
The caveat is that codon GAG is found only in introns in most species, so this mutation likely falls in a non-coding region and cannot be considered silent because exon/intron boundaries vary
Silent mutations can become non-silent if the amino acid they encode is later changed by a second mutation — the fitness effect is latent and only appears in double-mutant backgrounds

quiz.question: Arabidopsis thaliana has a mutation rate of ~7 × 10⁻⁹ per site per generation. If a population of Arabidopsis contains 1,000 plants and each plant has 120,000,000 base pairs in its genome, approximately how many new mutations does the entire population experience each generation?
quiz.correct: About 840 new mutations per generation (7 × 10⁻⁹ × 1.2 × 10⁸ bp ≈ 0.84 mutations/individual × 1,000 plants), providing the continuous raw material for evolution even in this small population
distractors: About 7,000 mutations per generation — one must multiply the mutation rate directly by population size alone, without accounting for genome size, to get the population-level mutation supply
Zero new mutations per generation — the mutation rate of 7 × 10⁻⁹ is so close to zero that in populations smaller than 10,000 individuals, no new mutations are expected in any given generation
About 120,000 new mutations per generation — the mutation rate applies to each individual gene rather than each base pair, and with ~120 million genes in the genome the total is very large

quiz.question: Which statement about the RANDOMNESS of mutations is CORRECT?
quiz.correct: Mutations occur randomly with respect to need — a population under antibiotic pressure does NOT mutate preferentially in genes that would confer resistance; resistance mutations occur at the same rate in stressed and unstressed populations, and selection simply favors the pre-existing variants
distractors: Mutations occur in response to environmental need — when a population experiences a new selective pressure, the mutation rate increases specifically at genes relevant to that pressure
Mutations are never truly random — each mutation occurs where the cell "knows" it would be most beneficial, which is why populations can adapt quickly
Mutations occur at the same rate at all sites equally — all base pairs have identical probabilities of mutation regardless of their chromosomal position or sequence context

quiz.question: A frameshift mutation is caused by:
quiz.correct: An insertion or deletion of a number of nucleotides that is not a multiple of 3, shifting the reading frame and typically producing a completely altered (and usually truncated) protein downstream
distractors: A single nucleotide substitution that changes one amino acid to another
A reversal of the DNA helix orientation that inverts the reading direction
A chromosomal translocation that moves a gene to a new chromosome

quiz.question: A nonsense mutation creates:
quiz.correct: A premature stop codon — causing protein translation to terminate early and producing a truncated (and usually non-functional) protein
distractors: An amino acid that has no biological meaning, which is why the mutation is called "nonsense"
A silent mutation with no protein-level effect
A codon that codes for an amino acid not present in any other protein in the organism

quiz.question: Among mutation types, which generally has the LEAST protein-level effect?
quiz.correct: Silent mutations — DNA changes that produce the same amino acid due to the redundancy of the genetic code (though some silent mutations can still affect mRNA stability, splicing, or codon usage)
distractors: Nonsense mutations — they create premature stop codons and have minimal effect because the remaining protein still folds correctly
Frameshift mutations — they shift only a few positions and rarely affect protein function
Missense mutations — they always produce a functional protein with just a different amino acid

quiz.question: Most new mutations in a population are:
quiz.correct: Neutral or slightly deleterious — strongly beneficial mutations are rare, which is why adaptive evolution is generally slow and depends on accumulating the rare beneficial variants over many generations
distractors: Strongly beneficial — which is why populations adapt quickly to changing environments
Lethal — which is why mutation rates are kept low to prevent population collapse
Reversible — meaning that any bad mutation can easily be undone by a second mutation at the same site

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: Which gene was identified as the site of a C→T point mutation causing thumb polydactyly (extra digits) in humans?
quiz.correct: LMBR1
distractors: FGFR3
Lamin A
BRCA1

quiz.question: L3 COMPARISON: A single point mutation in the Lamin A gene causes Hutchinson-Gilford progeria. What does this syndrome illustrate about point mutations?
quiz.correct: A single nucleotide change in one gene can produce a dramatic whole-body phenotype — in this case, premature aging — showing that mutations with large phenotypic effects are possible (though rare)
distractors: Point mutations are always silent and never produce measurable phenotypes
Lamin A mutations are Lamarckian and only affect individuals who experience aging stress
Progeria proves that mutations accumulate linearly across cell divisions regardless of DNA sequence context

visual.mnemonic: SMNF: Silent, Missense, Nonsense, Frameshift — ordered by typical severity.
visual.trap: A "silent" mutation is not always silent — some affect mRNA stability, splicing, or codon usage and can have phenotypic effects.

flashcards.front: Why are mutations considered the 'raw material' of evolution?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Mutations are the ultimate source of all new genetic variation in populations. Without mutation, there would be no new alleles for natural selection, genetic drift, or gene flow to act upon. While recombination reshuffles existing variation, only mutation generates novel alleles. All evolutionary change ultimately traces back to mutational events.

**ELI5:** Mutations are like typos in the DNA recipe book -- and they're the ONLY way to get truly new recipes. Without mutations, evolution would have nothing new to work with. Natural selection is the taste-tester, but mutation is the chef who invents new dishes. No new dishes = no evolution.

flashcards.front: How many chromosome pairs do humans have vs. great apes?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes (2n = 46). Great apes (chimpanzees, gorillas, orangutans) have 24 pairs (2n = 48). The difference is explained by the fusion of two ancestral ape chromosomes into human chromosome 2, as evidenced by vestigial centromeric and telomeric sequences at the fusion site.

**ELI5:** Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes, but chimps, gorillas, and orangutans all have 24 pairs. We're the odd ones out! Our chromosome 2 is actually two ape chromosomes that got stuck together long ago. It's like finding out everyone else has a 24-volume encyclopedia set and yours has 23 volumes because someone bound two into one.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between somatic and germline mutations?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Somatic mutations occur in non-reproductive (body) cells and are NOT passed to offspring. They can cause cancer and mosaicism within an individual but have no evolutionary significance. Germline mutations occur in reproductive cells (eggs, sperm, or their precursors) and ARE transmitted to offspring. Only germline mutations contribute to heritable variation and evolutionary change.

**ELI5:** Somatic mutations are like scratches on YOUR copy of a book -- they affect only you and disappear when you die. Germline mutations are like typos in the MASTER copy that gets photocopied for your kids -- they get passed down forever. Evolution only cares about germline mutations because those are the ones that get inherited.

flashcards.front: What is a silent (synonymous) mutation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A silent mutation is a point mutation in a protein-coding region that changes a codon but does NOT change the amino acid it specifies, due to the degeneracy (redundancy) of the genetic code. For example, GCU, GCC, GCA, GCG all encode alanine. A change from GCU to GCC is silent. These mutations are often selectively neutral at the protein level, though they can affect mRNA folding, splicing, or translation efficiency.

**ELI5:** A silent mutation is like changing a word's spelling without changing its meaning -- like 'colour' vs 'color.' The DNA letter changes, but the protein stays exactly the same because multiple DNA codes can produce the same amino acid. It's a change nobody notices.

flashcards.front: What is a missense mutation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A missense mutation is a point mutation that changes a codon to specify a DIFFERENT amino acid. Effects range from benign (conservative substitution, e.g., leucine to isoleucine) to severe (non-conservative substitution affecting protein structure/function, e.g., sickle cell: glutamic acid to valine in hemoglobin). The impact depends on the chemical properties of the substituted amino acid and its location in the protein.

**ELI5:** A missense mutation swaps one amino acid for a different one -- like replacing one LEGO brick with a different color. Sometimes it doesn't matter (replacing a red brick with an orange one in a big wall). Sometimes it's catastrophic (replacing the keystone piece that holds the whole structure together). Sickle cell anemia comes from just one missense mutation.

flashcards.front: What is a nonsense mutation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A nonsense mutation is a point mutation that converts an amino acid codon into a premature stop codon (UAG, UAA, or UGA), resulting in a truncated (shortened) protein. The truncated protein is usually nonfunctional and is often degraded by nonsense-mediated mRNA decay. Nonsense mutations are typically deleterious.

**ELI5:** A nonsense mutation is like a period appearing in the middle of a sentence. The ribosome reads 'build this protein... STOP' way too early, and you get a half-finished protein. Imagine building a bridge but stopping halfway -- it's useless. That's what a nonsense mutation does.

flashcards.front: What is a frameshift mutation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A frameshift mutation results from an insertion or deletion of nucleotides that is NOT a multiple of three, shifting the reading frame of all downstream codons. This typically produces an entirely different amino acid sequence downstream of the mutation and often introduces a premature stop codon. Frameshifts are almost always severely deleterious because they garble the entire protein from the mutation point onward.

**ELI5:** DNA is read three letters at a time: THE CAT ATE THE RAT. Delete one letter: THE CA TAT ETH ERA T. Every word after the deletion is scrambled. That's a frameshift -- adding or removing a letter shifts the 'reading frame' and turns the rest of the protein into gibberish.

flashcards.front: What are typical mutation rates in different organisms?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Per-base-pair per-generation mutation rates vary: Yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) ~3 x 10^-8 per bp per generation. Arabidopsis thaliana ~7 x 10^-9 per bp per generation. Humans ~1.0-1.5 x 10^-8 per bp per generation, resulting in approximately 70 new mutations per individual per generation across the ~3.2 billion bp genome.

**ELI5:** Different organisms make typos at different rates: Yeast makes about 3 typos per hundred million letters each generation. A tiny plant (Arabidopsis) makes about 7 per billion. Humans make about 70 brand-new typos per baby (out of 3.2 billion DNA letters total). Most typos don't matter, but occasionally one changes something important.

flashcards.front: Approximately how many new mutations does each human carry?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Each human individual carries approximately 70 de novo (new) mutations not present in either parent. This is derived from the per-nucleotide mutation rate (~1.0-1.5 x 10^-8 per bp/generation) multiplied by the diploid genome size (~6.4 x 10^9 bp). Most of these fall in non-coding regions and are selectively neutral.

**ELI5:** Every baby born has about 70 brand-new mutations that neither parent had. Out of 6.4 billion DNA letters, 70 are 'new typos.' Most of those typos are in the boring parts of the manual that nobody reads (non-coding DNA), so they don't cause any problems. But occasionally, one lands somewhere important.

flashcards.front: Why are most mutations neutral or slightly deleterious?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Most mutations are neutral because: (1) ~98.5% of the human genome is non-coding, so most mutations hit non-functional regions; (2) the genetic code is degenerate, so many coding mutations are synonymous (silent); (3) many amino acid substitutions are conservative (similar chemical properties). Of mutations that do affect fitness, most are deleterious because organisms are already reasonably well-adapted -- random changes to a functional system are more likely to break it than improve it.

**ELI5:** Imagine randomly changing one part of a working car engine. Most changes will either do nothing (neutral -- you changed a non-essential bolt) or break something (deleterious -- you swapped a fuel line). Very rarely will a random change make the car go faster (beneficial). Same with mutations -- random changes to a well-tuned system usually don't help.

flashcards.front: What is albinism, and what gene is associated with it?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Albinism involves mutations in genes required for melanin biosynthesis, resulting in reduced or absent pigmentation. The question references FGFR3, though classical oculocutaneous albinism is typically associated with TYR (tyrosinase) gene mutations. FGFR3 mutations are more commonly associated with achondroplasia (dwarfism). The key principle: single-gene mutations can produce dramatic phenotypic effects.

**ELI5:** Albinism happens when a mutation breaks the gene that makes skin/hair/eye pigment. The body can't make the color chemical (melanin), so the animal or person has very pale skin, white hair, and light eyes. One tiny change in one gene can have a huge visible effect.

flashcards.front: What causes polydactyly, and what gene is involved?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Polydactyly (extra fingers or toes) is associated with mutations in the LMBR1 gene (limb development membrane protein 1), specifically in its regulatory element (ZRS -- Zone of Polarizing Activity Regulatory Sequence) that controls Sonic hedgehog (SHH) expression during limb development. Point mutations in this enhancer cause ectopic SHH expression, leading to extra digit formation. It demonstrates how regulatory mutations can cause morphological changes.

**ELI5:** Polydactyly (being born with extra fingers or toes) is caused by a mutation in a gene called LMBR1 that controls how fingers develop. The mutation makes the 'build more fingers' signal turn on where it shouldn't. It's like a typo in the blueprint that says 'build 6 rooms' instead of 5 -- the builder just follows the (wrong) instructions.

flashcards.front: What causes progeria, and what gene is involved?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Hutchinson-Gilford progeria syndrome is caused by a de novo point mutation in the LMNA gene (Lamin A/C). The most common mutation (C1824T) creates an abnormal splice site, producing a truncated protein called progerin. This disrupts the nuclear lamina, causing premature aging, cardiovascular disease, and death typically in the teenage years. It demonstrates how a single nucleotide change can have devastating systemic effects.

**ELI5:** Progeria makes children age super fast -- a 10-year-old can look 70. It's caused by just ONE letter change in ONE gene (LMNA) that makes a protein for the cell's skeleton. The broken protein makes cells fall apart, like a building with a cracked foundation. One tiny typo in 3 billion letters causes catastrophic premature aging.

flashcards.front: Are mutations directed toward the needs of the organism?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** No. Mutations are stochastic (random) with respect to fitness -- they occur regardless of whether they would be beneficial or harmful. The probability of a particular mutation occurring is unrelated to its potential fitness effects. Mutations arise from replication errors, chemical damage, and radiation, not from organismal 'need.' This was demonstrated by the Luria-Delbruck fluctuation test (1943) and later by replica plating experiments. Lamarck's idea of 'directed' variation is incorrect.

**ELI5:** Mutations don't happen because an organism needs them. A bacterium doesn't 'decide' to mutate to resist antibiotics -- random mutations are happening all the time, and the ones that happen to help survival get passed on. It's like lottery tickets: you don't win because you need money, you just get lucky. Mutations are random; selection is not.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between point mutations and chromosomal mutations?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Point mutations affect single nucleotides (substitutions: transitions/transversions; small insertions/deletions). Chromosomal mutations are large-scale changes affecting chromosome structure or number: duplications, deletions, inversions, translocations, aneuploidy (extra/missing chromosomes), polyploidy (extra chromosome sets). Chromosomal mutations affect many genes simultaneously and can have major evolutionary consequences (e.g., polyploidy in plant speciation).

**ELI5:** Point mutations are like changing one letter in a word. Chromosomal mutations are like rearranging entire chapters of a book -- copying a chapter twice, deleting one, flipping one backwards, or moving it to a different book. Point mutations are small and surgical; chromosomal mutations are big and dramatic.

flashcards.front: How does mutation relate to natural selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Mutation generates the raw heritable variation upon which natural selection acts. Mutation is RANDOM with respect to fitness (provides variation without direction). Natural selection is NONRANDOM (provides direction by differentially favoring certain variants). Together, they produce adaptive evolution: mutation proposes, selection disposes. Without mutation, there are no new variants. Without selection, mutations just accumulate randomly (genetic drift).

**ELI5:** Mutation and natural selection are a team. Mutation is like a cook throwing random ingredients into pots. Natural selection is the judge who tastes everything and keeps only the good dishes. Neither works alone -- without new ingredients (mutations), there's nothing new to taste. Without the judge (selection), you just get random mush. Together, they produce great recipes (adaptations).

flashcards.front: What is a transition vs. a transversion?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Transitions are point mutations that substitute a purine for a purine (A<->G) or a pyrimidine for a pyrimidine (C<->T). Transversions substitute a purine for a pyrimidine or vice versa (A<->C, A<->T, G<->C, G<->T). Transitions are more common than transversions (~2:1 ratio) because they involve less structural disruption to the DNA helix. There are 4 possible transversions but only 2 possible transitions, yet transitions still predominate.

**ELI5:** DNA bases come in two sizes: big ones (A, G = purines) and small ones (C, T = pyrimidines). Transitions swap big for big or small for small -- like trading a basketball for a soccer ball (both round). Transversions swap big for small -- like trading a basketball for a golf ball. Transitions happen more often because it's easier to swap similar-sized things.

nodes.title: Sex Cells & Meiotic Variation
nodes.subtitle: How meiosis generates diversity (Lec 3 slides 18-19)

popup.heading: Lecture 3 — Meiotic Sources of Variation

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Meiosis generates genetic variation through three mechanisms: (1) CROSSING OVER between homologous chromosomes during PROPHASE I (synaptic exchange at chiasmata); (2) INDEPENDENT ASSORTMENT of homologs during METAPHASE I — 2^n combinations for n chromosome pairs, which is 2^23 ≈ 8 million different gamete types in humans; (3) RANDOM FERTILIZATION, where any sperm can fuse with any egg, multiplying variation combinatorially.

sections.label: KEY TERMS
sections.body: Meiosis I, prophase I, metaphase I, chiasmata, crossing over, recombination, independent assortment, random fertilization.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body:
• Sex cells introduce new genetic variation
• Sexually-reproducing organisms do not produce genetic clones because parental chromosomal complements are not genetically identical
• Allele: One of several alternative forms of the DNA sequence of the same locus
• Allele differences between chromosome pairs in each generation create variation among the gamete cells produced
• Meiosis generates variation
• Genetic recombination: exchange of genetic material between paired chromosomes during meiosis
• Independent assortment
• Sexual reproduction then shuffles up combinations of alleles even more

examples: Meiotic crossing over (recombination) during prophase I — shuffles alleles between homologs.
Independent assortment of homologs during metaphase I — 2^n possible combinations for n chromosome pairs (2^23 ≈ 8 million in humans).
Random fertilization: any sperm can meet any egg, multiplying variation further.
warnings: WATCH OUT: Crossing over occurs during PROPHASE I (when homologs are synapsed), NOT during anaphase. Independent assortment is the METAPHASE I process.
WATCH OUT: Meiosis generates variation; mitosis does NOT — mitosis produces genetically identical daughter cells.
popup.mnemonic: CIR = Crossing over + Independent assortment + Random fertilization.

flashcard.front: How many genetically distinct gametes can a single human potentially produce, ignoring new mutations?
flashcard.back: Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes. Through independent assortment alone, 2²³ ≈ 8.4 million different combinations of homologs are possible. Adding crossing over (recombination) between homologs dramatically increases this number to effectively infinite (~10^1000+ depending on crossover positions). Random fertilization then multiplies this by the same factor in the partner — so each human offspring is effectively genetically unique (identical twins excepted).

quiz.question: Which meiotic process contributes to genetic variation by shuffling alleles between homologous chromosomes?
quiz.correct: Crossing over (recombination) during prophase I
distractors: Independent assortment during metaphase I
Mitotic division of germ cells
Sister chromatid separation during anaphase II

quiz.question: A plant reproduces ONLY by clonal vegetative propagation (no sex, no meiosis). Compared to a sexually reproducing relative, which statement about genetic variation in the clonal plant's population is MOST accurate?
quiz.correct: New variation in the clonal lineage accumulates ONLY through new mutations — it cannot recombine existing alleles; the population will be far more genetically uniform and will track environmental change more slowly than the sexual relative
distractors: Clonal plants have MORE variation than sexual plants because every mutation is automatically passed to all offspring without being diluted by recombination with a second parent's genome

Clonal and sexual plants have equal genetic variation over evolutionary time because mutation rates are the same in both, and recombination only shuffles existing variation rather than creating new alleles
Clonal plants have zero genetic variation because without meiosis, no new chromosomal combinations can arise; all individuals in a clonal population are genetically identical regardless of mutation

quiz.question: Independent assortment of chromosomes during meiosis can produce 2^23 ≈ 8.4 million different gamete combinations in humans. If a human couple each produces gametes via independent assortment alone (no crossing over), how many genetically distinct offspring are THEORETICALLY possible?
quiz.correct: About 70 trillion (8.4 million × 8.4 million) — because each parent independently produces any of ~8.4 million gamete types, and fertilization combines one from each parent
distractors: About 8.4 million — because independent assortment applies only to the egg, while the sperm's chromosomes are fixed in sequence once spermatogenesis begins
About 46 — because humans have 46 chromosomes and independent assortment produces one unique combination per chromosome pair, summing to 46 possible outcomes
Infinite — because crossing over and mutation add unlimited combinations on top of independent assortment, making the 2^23 calculation meaningless as a practical limit

quiz.question: Two genes on the SAME chromosome (linked genes) do NOT assort completely independently. However, crossing over can still produce recombinant gametes carrying new allele combinations. How does recombination frequency between two linked genes reflect their physical distance apart?
quiz.correct: Genes that are far apart on a chromosome cross over more often between them — recombination frequency increases with physical distance, up to a maximum of 50% (appearing to assort independently when genes are far apart on the same chromosome)
distractors: Genes close together recombine MORE often because the chromosome is more flexible near the centromere where nearby genes cluster during prophase I, increasing chiasmata frequency
Recombination frequency is unrelated to physical distance — crossing-over hotspots are randomly distributed and equally likely at any chromosomal position regardless of gene location
Two genes on the same chromosome NEVER produce recombinant gametes because crossing over only occurs between NON-homologous chromosomes during independent assortment at metaphase I

quiz.question: Random fertilization is the THIRD component of meiotic variation. How does it multiply the diversity produced by independent assortment and crossing over?
quiz.correct: Any sperm can meet any egg — pairing one unique gamete from each parent multiplies the gametic diversity combinatorially, so total offspring possibilities equal (gamete types from parent 1) × (gamete types from parent 2)
distractors: Random fertilization adds nothing to diversity — the egg specifically selects the sperm with the most compatible genes, limiting the possible combinations
Random fertilization creates new mutations — the union of sperm and egg DNA damages both genomes, producing novel alleles not present in either parent
Random fertilization only affects species with external fertilization — in internal fertilizers, the process is deterministic and cannot add variation

quiz.question: Mitosis and meiosis differ in their contributions to genetic variation. Which statement is CORRECT?
quiz.correct: Mitosis produces genetically IDENTICAL daughter cells (aside from rare mutations), while meiosis produces genetically DIVERSE gametes through crossing over and independent assortment
distractors: Both mitosis and meiosis generate variation through crossing over — meiosis does it once, mitosis does it every cell division
Mitosis generates MORE variation than meiosis because mitotic divisions are more frequent in an organism's lifetime
Meiosis produces identical daughter cells while mitosis produces diverse ones — this is why sexual reproduction creates clones

quiz.question: An asexual lineage (e.g., bdelloid rotifers, some aphid clones) lacks the genetic reshuffling of meiosis. What is the predicted long-term consequence?
quiz.correct: New beneficial mutations cannot be combined with each other — they must accumulate sequentially in the same lineage, and deleterious mutations cannot be purged via recombination (Muller's ratchet), generally limiting long-term adaptation capacity
distractors: Asexual lineages adapt FASTER than sexual ones because they do not waste time producing males and can pass on all beneficial mutations to all offspring
Asexual lineages have no mutations because meiosis is the source of all genetic variation
Asexual and sexual lineages have identical long-term evolution because mutation rates are the same in both

quiz.question: During which phase of meiosis does crossing over occur, and why is this phase specifically important?
quiz.correct: Prophase I — this is when homologous chromosomes synapse (pair up tightly) with their chromatids aligned, allowing physical exchange of DNA segments at chiasmata. Without this synapsis, no crossing over could occur
distractors: Anaphase I — when homologs separate, they exchange segments as they pull apart
Metaphase II — when sister chromatids align at the equator, they swap segments
Telophase I — during cytokinesis, DNA is exchanged between the two daughter cells

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: How many different gamete combinations can a single human theoretically produce from independent assortment alone (ignoring crossing over)?
quiz.correct: Approximately 2^23 ≈ 8 million (8.4 million) — one combination for each of the 23 chromosome pairs
distractors: Approximately 46 — one per chromosome
Approximately 2^46 — one per chromatid
Approximately 23 million — multiplying the pairs by independent assortment directly

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: Which mnemonic captures the three meiotic mechanisms that generate variation?
quiz.correct: CIR — Crossing over, Independent assortment, Random fertilization
distractors: CPI — Crossing over, Polyploidy, Inbreeding
VHD — Variation, Heritability, Differential reproduction
MGP — Mitosis, Gametogenesis, Polyploidy

visual.mnemonic: Three levels of mixing in sex.
visual.trap: Mitosis does not generate variation — only meiosis + fertilization do. This is why asexual lineages accumulate mutations linearly while sexual lineages can recombine them.

flashcards.front: What are the THREE mechanisms that generate genetic variation during sexual reproduction?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Crossing over (recombination) at chiasmata during Prophase I of meiosis, (2) Independent assortment of homologous chromosomes at Metaphase I, (3) Random fertilization (which sperm meets which egg). Together these ensure virtually every gamete is genetically unique.

**ELI5:** Imagine making a sandwich: (1) you swap fillings between two sandwiches (crossing over), (2) you randomly pick bread from different loaves (independent assortment), (3) someone randomly picks which sandwich to eat (random fertilization). Three layers of randomness.

flashcards.front: What is crossing over and WHEN does it occur in meiosis?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Crossing over is the reciprocal exchange of DNA segments between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes. It occurs during Prophase I of meiosis at structures called chiasmata (singular: chiasma). This produces recombinant chromosomes that carry allele combinations not present in either parent.

**ELI5:** Two matching chromosomes line up and literally swap chunks of their DNA, like two friends trading sleeves on their jackets. This happens in Prophase I, the very first major stage of meiosis.

flashcards.front: What is a chiasma (plural: chiasmata)?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A chiasma is the physical point of contact where crossing over occurs between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes during Prophase I. It is the cytologically visible manifestation of recombination. Multiple chiasmata can form per chromosome pair.

**ELI5:** It's the spot where two chromosomes are physically stuck together while swapping DNA -- like the point where two people are mid-handshake, each passing something to the other.

flashcards.front: What is independent assortment? When does it happen and how much variation does it produce in humans?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Independent assortment is the random orientation of homologous chromosome pairs at the metaphase plate during Metaphase I. Each pair aligns independently of all other pairs. In humans (n=23), this produces 2^23 = 8,388,608 (approximately 8.4 million) possible gamete combinations from one individual, WITHOUT counting crossing over.

**ELI5:** You have 23 pairs of chromosomes, and at each pair you flip a coin to decide which version goes into the egg/sperm. That's 23 coin flips = over 8 million possible outcomes per person.

flashcards.front: Calculate: How many genetically unique zygotes can two human parents theoretically produce from independent assortment alone?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Each parent can produce 2^23 different gamete types via independent assortment. Random fertilization combines one gamete from each parent: 2^23 x 2^23 = 2^46 = approximately 70 trillion (7 x 10^13) unique zygote combinations. This is BEFORE adding crossing over variation.

**ELI5:** Mom can make 8 million different eggs, Dad can make 8 million different sperm. Any egg can meet any sperm: 8 million times 8 million = about 70 trillion unique babies possible. And that's the conservative number.

flashcards.front: What is random fertilization and why does it matter for genetic variation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Random fertilization is the chance union of any one sperm with any one egg during sexual reproduction. Because each gamete is genetically unique (due to crossing over and independent assortment), and which sperm fertilizes which egg is essentially random, this adds a third multiplicative layer of genetic variation.

**ELI5:** Out of millions of different sperm and thousands of different eggs, which two actually meet is pure luck -- like pulling one random marble from each of two enormous bags, each containing millions of unique marbles.

flashcards.front: What is an allele?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** An allele is a specific variant form of a gene at a particular locus (position) on a chromosome. Diploid organisms carry two alleles per locus (one from each parent), which may be identical (homozygous) or different (heterozygous). Different alleles arise by mutation.

**ELI5:** If a gene is a recipe, an allele is a specific version of that recipe. You got one version from Mom and one from Dad -- they might be the same version or different.

flashcards.front: Name TWO key differences between meiosis and mitosis regarding genetic variation.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Meiosis includes crossing over between homologs in Prophase I; mitosis does not involve recombination between homologs. (2) Meiosis has independent assortment at Metaphase I (homologs separate randomly); mitosis does not separate homologs at all. Additionally, meiosis produces 4 haploid cells (genetically unique), while mitosis produces 2 diploid cells (genetically identical to parent).

**ELI5:** Mitosis is a photocopy machine -- it makes identical copies. Meiosis is a shuffle-and-deal card game -- it mixes everything up and deals out four unique hands.

flashcards.front: COMMON MISTAKE: A student says 'Independent assortment happens during Metaphase II.' Why is this wrong?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Independent assortment occurs at Metaphase I, not Metaphase II. It is the random orientation of HOMOLOGOUS pairs at the metaphase plate. By Metaphase II, homologs have already been separated -- only sister chromatids remain. Metaphase II is analogous to mitotic metaphase and does not contribute to combinatorial variation.

**ELI5:** The big shuffle happens in round 1 (Metaphase I) when matching pairs decide which side to go to. By round 2 (Metaphase II), the pairs are already split up -- there's nothing left to independently assort.

flashcards.front: COMMON MISTAKE: 'Crossing over produces new alleles.' True or false?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** FALSE. Crossing over produces new COMBINATIONS of existing alleles on chromosomes, but it does not create new alleles. Only mutation creates entirely new alleles. Crossing over recombines alleles that already exist in the population by swapping segments between homologous chromosomes.

**ELI5:** Crossing over is like reshuffling a deck of cards -- you get new hands, but no new cards are created. Only mutation can add a brand new card to the deck.

flashcards.front: During which phase of meiosis do homologous chromosomes physically pair up (synapsis)?
flashcards.back:
