**TECHNICAL:** Synapsis occurs during Prophase I of meiosis. Homologous chromosomes pair up to form bivalents (tetrads), connected by the synaptonemal complex. This pairing is a prerequisite for crossing over to occur at chiasmata.

**ELI5:** In Prophase I, matching chromosomes find each other and zipper together side-by-side, like partners lining up for a dance. They need to be paired up before they can trade pieces.

flashcards.front: Why is sexual reproduction considered more evolutionarily costly than asexual reproduction, yet so widespread?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Sexual reproduction has the 'two-fold cost of sex': only females produce offspring, so a sexual population grows half as fast as an asexual one. Yet sex persists because the three mechanisms of variation (crossing over, independent assortment, random fertilization) generate enormous genetic diversity, allowing populations to adapt to changing environments, parasites (Red Queen hypothesis), and purge deleterious mutations more efficiently.

**ELI5:** Making babies with a partner is slower and harder than cloning yourself, but mixing genes creates variety. Variety is insurance -- some offspring will survive whatever new threat comes along.

flashcards.front: Put the three mechanisms of meiotic variation in order from MOST to LEAST new combinations generated.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Random fertilization generates the most combinations (2^23 x 2^23 = 2^46 in humans), (2) Crossing over generates enormous variation (theoretically unlimited recombinant types per chromosome), (3) Independent assortment generates 2^n combinations per individual (2^23 in humans). However, all three are multiplicative, so ranking is somewhat context-dependent. The standard lecture emphasis is: independent assortment (2^23) < random fertilization (2^46), with crossing over adding variation on top.

**ELI5:** All three multiply together like a combo lock. Independent assortment gives millions, random fertilization squares that to trillions, and crossing over adds even more on top by remixing individual chromosomes.

nodes.title: Genotype-Phenotype Map: Polygenic Traits & Environment
nodes.subtitle: How genes + environment shape phenotypes (Lec 3 slides 20-29)

popup.heading: Lecture 3 — Linking Genotype and Phenotype

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Genotype does not map 1:1 to phenotype. There are three distinct flavors of variation: (1) GENETIC POLYMORPHISM (discrete classes) — e.g., morning glory leaf shape, where T = lobed dominant and t = entire; (2) POLYGENIC TRAITS (continuous) — Edward East's 1916 tobacco corolla length: 2 loci → 5 classes; 6 loci → 13 classes; (3) PHENOTYPIC PLASTICITY — Ambystoma (salamander) cannibalistic morphs develop in response to environment.

sections.label: VARIANCE PARTITIONING
sections.body: V_P = V_G + V_E. If V_G = 0, no evolution can occur (nothing to select on). If V_G > V_E, rapid change is possible.

sections.label: KEY FIGURES
sections.body: Edward East (1916) — classic tobacco corolla experiments demonstrating polygenic inheritance.

examples: Genetic polymorphism: morning glory leaf shape — T (lobed) dominant, t (entire) recessive. Discrete classes from one or few loci.
Polygenic traits: Edward East 1916 tobacco corolla — 2 loci produces 5 phenotypic classes; 6 loci produces 13 classes.
Phenotypic plasticity: Ambystoma cannibalistic morphs develop in response to environmental cues.
V_P = V_G + V_E; if V_G = 0 → no evolution possible; if V_G > V_E → rapid change.
warnings: WATCH OUT: Continuous variation does NOT mean environmental. Human height is ~80% heritable but still continuous because many genes contribute (polygenic). Heritability and continuous-vs-discrete are independent dimensions.
WATCH OUT: A "polymorphism" is a POPULATION-level phenomenon (two or more forms coexist). An individual has alleles or a genotype, not a polymorphism.
popup.mnemonic: PPE = Polymorphism (discrete) vs Polygenic (continuous) vs Environmental (plasticity).

flashcard.front: What is the difference between a genetic polymorphism and a polygenic trait? Give an example of each.
flashcard.back: A POLYMORPHISM is the presence of two or more DISCRETE phenotypic variants in a population maintained by genetic variation at one or a few loci — e.g., human ABO blood types (3 alleles → 4 phenotypes), Mendelian pea flower color. A POLYGENIC trait is continuous variation controlled by MANY genes each contributing a small amount, usually producing a bell-curve distribution — e.g., human height, skin color, IQ, disease risk (BMI, hypertension). Polygenic traits can also be influenced by environment, producing gene × environment effects.

quiz.question: Human height is considered a polygenic trait because:
quiz.correct: It is influenced by many genes each contributing a small amount, producing continuous variation
distractors: It is controlled by a single dominant/recessive gene pair
It is determined entirely by environment and nutrition
It does not vary within populations

quiz.question: A study reports that height heritability is ~80% in wealthy countries but lower in developing countries. How should this difference be interpreted?
quiz.correct: Heritability is population- and environment-specific: when environmental variation (nutrition) is large and variable, V_E is high and h² = V_A/V_P decreases even if V_A is unchanged — it does not mean genes matter less in poorer countries
distractors: Lower heritability in developing countries means height genes are less functional under nutritional stress — malnutrition causes mutations that reduce additive genetic variance for height
Higher heritability in wealthy countries means wealthy populations have more height alleles due to positive selection for tall individuals during industrialization over the past 200 years
The difference proves that height is primarily environmental in developing countries and primarily genetic in wealthy countries — two different mechanisms are operating in different populations

quiz.question: A genetic polymorphism for human ABO blood type has alleles I^A, I^B, and i at one locus. Three blood groups (A, B, AB, O) exist simultaneously. Which statement about this polymorphism and its fitness effects is MOST likely correct?
quiz.correct: ABO blood type is probably maintained as a balanced polymorphism — possibly by pathogen-driven selection (different blood types confer different resistance profiles to different pathogens), though the exact mechanism is still debated
distractors: ABO blood type has zero fitness effect and persists only through mutation-selection balance — all three alleles are selectively neutral and drift freely in human populations

ABO blood type represents a transient polymorphism: I^A is selectively neutral, I^B is slightly deleterious, and i is favored; given enough time, all human populations will eventually fix the i allele

Because ABO blood type is codominant (no dominance effects), it cannot be maintained by natural selection — only additive traits respond to selection, so blood type variation is maintained entirely by random genetic drift

quiz.question: A trait shows continuous variation in a population. A researcher measures heritability as h² = 0.0. Which explanation is CORRECT?
quiz.correct: All phenotypic variation in the population is due to environmental differences — there is no additive genetic variance to respond to selection, so the trait cannot evolve even with strong selection differential S
distractors: A trait with h² = 0.0 has no genetic basis at all and is entirely plastic — individuals can change their phenotype at will in response to the environment without any genetic change

h² = 0.0 means the trait is not polygenic — it must be controlled by a single gene pair, which produces discrete categories rather than continuous variation, so the continuous distribution is a measurement error

h² = 0.0 is impossible for a continuous trait — if variation is measurable, some of it must be genetic; a measured value of 0.0 indicates the researcher made an error in the parent-offspring regression

quiz.question: Which of the following is an example of a GENETIC POLYMORPHISM rather than a polygenic trait?
quiz.correct: ABO blood type — discrete categories (A, B, AB, O) controlled by a small number of alleles at one locus
distractors: Human height — continuous variation determined by many genes
Human skin color — continuous variation with many contributing loci
Human IQ — continuous distribution shaped by both genes and environment

quiz.question: Which of the following is an example of a POLYGENIC TRAIT rather than a discrete polymorphism?
quiz.correct: Human body mass index (BMI) — continuous variation controlled by hundreds of loci, each contributing a small amount
distractors: Mendelian pea flower color (purple vs white)
Human ABO blood type
Presence or absence of a tail in certain mammals

quiz.question: A trait shows a perfect bell-curve distribution in a population. What can you CONCLUDE from this alone?
quiz.correct: Very little — a bell curve is consistent with many possible scenarios: a highly heritable polygenic trait, a purely environmental trait, or any mixture of the two. The shape alone does not reveal whether genes or environment dominate
distractors: The trait must be polygenic with high heritability — bell curves only arise from many additive genetic effects
The trait must be environmentally determined — bell curves arise only from normally-distributed environmental factors
The trait must be neutral — bell curves are characteristic of traits under no selective pressure

quiz.question: An individual is described as having a "polymorphism." Why is this terminology INCORRECT?
quiz.correct: Polymorphism is a POPULATION-level concept — it refers to the coexistence of two or more discrete variants in a population. An individual has ALLELES (or a GENOTYPE), not a polymorphism
distractors: The terminology is correct in all usages — individuals, populations, and species can all "have polymorphisms" interchangeably
Individuals cannot have any genetic variation — only populations have genetic differences, so the word "polymorphism" should never apply anywhere
Polymorphism only applies to molecular traits like protein electrophoresis bands, not to phenotypic traits

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: In Edward East's 1916 tobacco corolla experiments, how many phenotypic classes were observed when TWO loci contributed to corolla length?
quiz.correct: 5 classes (2 loci → 2n+1 = 5)
distractors: 2 classes — one per locus
13 classes (the number seen for 6 loci)
10 classes — twice the number of loci squared

quiz.question: L2 MECHANISM: If a population has V_P = 10, V_G = 0, and V_E = 10 for a quantitative trait, what happens if strong selection is applied?
quiz.correct: No evolutionary response — with V_G = 0, there is no heritable component to select on, so the trait mean cannot change across generations regardless of selection strength
distractors: Rapid evolution because V_E is large and environmental effects are directly selected
Slow but steady evolution because selection operates on phenotypes regardless of heritability
Evolution in the opposite direction of selection, because V_E = V_P creates feedback reversing the response

visual.mnemonic: Discrete vs Continuous vs Plastic.
visual.trap: Continuous variation does NOT mean purely environmental — many continuous traits are highly heritable.

diagram.title: Reaction Norms & G×E Interaction — V_P = V_G + V_E + V_GxE

nodes.label: Genotype 1 at env A
nodes.detail: Reaction norm value for Genotype 1 in environment A (e.g. Achillea millefolium yarrow clone grown at low elevation in the Mather/Stanford/Timberline common garden experiment, Clausen, Keck & Hiesey 1940s). Genotype 1 performs well at low elevation: phenotypic value is high.
nodes.mnemonic: Low elevation is this clone’s sweet spot.
nodes.watchOut: The phenotype is a plastic response, not a new genotype.

nodes.label: Genotype 1 at env B
nodes.detail: Same Genotype 1 clone transplanted to environment B (e.g. high-elevation Timberline garden). Performance drops sharply — stem height, leaf number, and biomass all fall. Still the identical genotype; the environment alone shifts the phenotypic value.
nodes.mnemonic: Same DNA, harsher conditions → lower phenotype.
nodes.watchOut: Transplanting clones is how you prove variation is V_E, not V_G.

nodes.label: Genotype 1 reaction norm shape
nodes.detail: The line connecting Genotype 1’s phenotype across environments A and B slopes downward (good at low elevation, bad at high). Reaction norm SHAPE is a heritable property of the genotype — the plastic response itself is not, but the capacity for it is.
nodes.mnemonic: Reaction norm = the genotype’s response curve.
nodes.watchOut: Reaction norm shape is heritable; single phenotype values are not.

nodes.label: Genotype 2 at env A
nodes.detail: Genotype 2 clone in environment A performs poorly — low elevation is not its niche. In the Clausen/Keck/Hiesey Achillea common-garden study, montane ecotypes were shorter than lowland ecotypes at low elevation.
nodes.mnemonic: This clone prefers altitude.
nodes.watchOut: A poor phenotype in one environment does not mean a poor genotype overall.

nodes.label: Genotype 2 at env B
nodes.detail: Genotype 2 clone in environment B (high elevation) now performs well — taller, more vigorous than Genotype 1 at the same location. The ranking of genotypes has FLIPPED between environments. This crossover is the signature of G×E interaction.
nodes.mnemonic: Rank order flipped = G×E, not just G + E.
nodes.watchOut: Crossover interaction means "best genotype" is environment-dependent.

nodes.label: Genotype 2 reaction norm shape
nodes.detail: The line connecting Genotype 2’s phenotype across environments slopes upward (bad at low elevation, good at high). Because this line crosses Genotype 1’s line, the interaction term V_GxE is non-zero.
nodes.mnemonic: Crossing lines = G×E interaction.
nodes.watchOut: Parallel reaction norms produce V_E but no V_GxE.

nodes.label: V_G (between-genotype)
nodes.detail: Variance in phenotype attributable to genetic differences among individuals, averaged across environments. Estimated from clonal or half-sib comparisons. V_G drives heritability (h² = V_A / V_P).
nodes.mnemonic: V_G = "nature" component of the variance.
nodes.watchOut: V_G alone does not predict response to selection — only additive V_A does.

nodes.label: V_E (environmental)
nodes.detail: Variance in phenotype attributable to environmental differences, averaged across genotypes. In a common-garden experiment, V_E is estimated by comparing the same clone across sites.
nodes.mnemonic: V_E = "nurture" component of the variance.
nodes.watchOut: Uncontrolled micro-environmental variation can inflate V_E estimates.

nodes.label: V_GxE interaction
nodes.detail: Variance arising when genotypes respond DIFFERENTLY to environmental changes — the non-parallelism of reaction norms. V_GxE > 0 implies no single genotype is universally best, and local adaptation is possible. Detected as a significant G×E term in a two-way ANOVA on clone × environment data.
nodes.mnemonic: Crossing reaction norms → V_GxE > 0.
nodes.watchOut: Plasticity is NOT evolution. The reaction norm SHAPE is heritable; the plastic response itself is not allele frequency change.

nodes.label: Classic G×E cases
nodes.detail: Richard Woltereck coined "Reaktionsnorm" in 1909 studying Daphnia head shape across food concentrations. Daphnia cucullata grow neck teeth when they detect Chaoborus midge-larvae chemical cues — an inducible defense. Ambystoma tigrinum salamander larvae develop cannibalistic morphs at high density and prey availability. Clausen, Keck & Hiesey’s Achillea millefolium common garden across Mather (low), Stanford, and Timberline (high) showed crossing reaction norms in stem height.
nodes.mnemonic: Woltereck 1909, Achillea Mather/Stanford, Daphnia, Ambystoma cannibals.
nodes.watchOut: Inducible defenses are plasticity, not evolution — no allele frequency change.

diagram.mnemonic: V_P = V_G + V_E + V_GxE. Crossing reaction norms = G×E interaction.

flashcards.front: What are the THREE types of genotype-to-phenotype variation discussed in lecture?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Genetic polymorphism -- discrete phenotypic classes controlled by one or few loci (e.g., morning glory flower color T/t). (2) Polygenic inheritance -- continuous or near-continuous phenotypic variation controlled by many loci (e.g., East 1916 tobacco corolla length). (3) Phenotypic plasticity -- a single genotype producing different phenotypes in different environments (e.g., Ambystoma salamander morphs).

**ELI5:** Traits come in three flavors: (1) simple switches (one gene, few options), (2) many-dial blending (lots of genes, smooth spectrum), (3) same genes but the environment changes the result (like how the same plant grows differently in sun vs. shade).

flashcards.front: What is genetic polymorphism? Give the lecture example.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Genetic polymorphism is the occurrence of two or more discrete phenotypic forms in a population, controlled by alleles at one or a few loci. The lecture example is morning glory (Ipomoea) flower color: a single locus with alleles T (dominant, producing pigmented flowers) and t (recessive, producing white flowers). Genotypes TT and Tt produce colored flowers; tt produces white.

**ELI5:** It's like a light switch -- one gene flips between distinct options (colored or white flowers). No in-between.

flashcards.front: Describe East's 1916 tobacco experiment and what it demonstrated about polygenic inheritance.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** East crossed two pure-breeding tobacco lines differing in corolla length. The F1 was intermediate and relatively uniform. The F2 showed much greater variation, forming a roughly continuous distribution. East showed that with just 2 loci (each with 2 alleles contributing additively), you get 5 phenotypic classes; with 6 loci, you get 13 classes. As locus number increases, the distribution approaches a continuous bell curve even though the underlying genetics are discrete Mendelian loci.

**ELI5:** East crossed short-flower and long-flower tobacco plants. Their kids were medium. Their grandkids ranged from short to long in a smooth spread. He proved you don't need 'blending inheritance' -- just a handful of genes each adding a little bit creates a smooth-looking spectrum.

flashcards.front: In East's tobacco experiment, how many phenotypic classes result from 2 additive loci? From 6 loci?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** For n additive loci each with 2 alleles, the number of phenotypic classes = 2n + 1. So: 2 loci produce 2(2)+1 = 5 phenotypic classes; 6 loci produce 2(6)+1 = 13 phenotypic classes. As locus number increases, the distribution becomes increasingly continuous and bell-shaped.

**ELI5:** With 2 genes, you get 5 possible heights (like a 5-step staircase). With 6 genes, you get 13 steps. The more genes involved, the more steps, until the staircase looks like a smooth ramp.

flashcards.front: What is phenotypic plasticity? Give the lecture example.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Phenotypic plasticity is the ability of a single genotype to produce different phenotypes depending on environmental conditions. The lecture example is Ambystoma (tiger salamander): the same genotype can develop into either a typical morph or a cannibal morph depending on environmental cues like population density and food availability. This is NOT genetic change.

**ELI5:** Same DNA, different result depending on surroundings. Like how the same salamander can grow up normal OR become a big-headed cannibal depending on how crowded its pond is. The genes didn't change -- the environment flipped a switch.

flashcards.front: Write the equation that partitions total phenotypic variance. Define each term.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** VP = VG + VE, where VP = total phenotypic variance (all observable variation), VG = genetic variance (variation due to genetic differences among individuals), VE = environmental variance (variation due to environmental differences among individuals). A more complete model includes VGxE (genotype-by-environment interaction), but the basic partition is VP = VG + VE.

**ELI5:** How different people look (VP) = differences in their genes (VG) + differences in their environments (VE). It's nature + nurture adding up.

flashcards.front: COMMON MISTAKE: 'Continuous phenotypic variation must be caused by environmental effects, not genes.' Why is this wrong?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** This is false. Continuous variation can be entirely genetic in origin if the trait is polygenic (controlled by many loci). East's tobacco experiment demonstrated that even with purely genetic differences (no environmental variation in the inbred lines), multiple additive loci produce continuous-looking distributions. The key insight is: continuous distribution does NOT imply environmental causation.

**ELI5:** Just because heights form a smooth bell curve doesn't mean it's all about nutrition. Even if everyone ate the same food, many genes each adding a little would still produce a smooth range of heights.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a genotype and a phenotype?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Genotype is the genetic constitution of an organism -- the specific alleles it carries at one or more loci. Phenotype is the observable characteristics of an organism (morphology, physiology, behavior), which result from the interaction of genotype with the environment. The same genotype can produce different phenotypes in different environments (plasticity), and different genotypes can produce similar phenotypes.

**ELI5:** Genotype is the recipe written in your DNA. Phenotype is the actual dish that comes out of the oven -- which depends on the recipe AND the cooking conditions.

flashcards.front: In the morning glory example, what are the genotypes and phenotypes for the T locus?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Genotype TT (homozygous dominant) = pigmented flowers. Genotype Tt (heterozygous) = pigmented flowers (T is dominant). Genotype tt (homozygous recessive) = white flowers. This is a case of complete dominance at a single locus producing a genetic polymorphism with two discrete phenotypic classes.

**ELI5:** TT or Tt = colored flowers (the T allele is bossy and dominates). tt = white flowers (without any T, the recessive white shows through). Two gene copies, but only two possible looks.

flashcards.front: How does increasing the number of loci affect the phenotypic distribution in a polygenic trait?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** As the number of additive loci increases, the number of phenotypic classes increases (2n+1), the differences between adjacent classes become smaller, and the overall distribution becomes more continuous and approaches a normal (bell-shaped) distribution. Even with just 6 loci, the distribution is nearly indistinguishable from continuous. Adding environmental variance further smooths the distribution.

**ELI5:** Imagine stacking blocks of different colors. With 2 types, you see clear groups. With 20 types, each adding tiny differences, the groups blur into a smooth rainbow. More genes = smoother spectrum.

flashcards.front: Can phenotypic plasticity be considered evolution? Why or why not?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** No. Phenotypic plasticity is NOT evolution because it does not involve a change in allele frequencies in the population. It is a within-generation phenotypic response to the environment by individuals with the same genotype. However, the CAPACITY for plasticity (the reaction norm) is itself genetically determined and can evolve by natural selection.

**ELI5:** A plant growing taller in more sunlight isn't evolution -- its DNA didn't change. But if some plant families are BETTER at adjusting to sunlight than others, that ability to adjust can itself evolve.

flashcards.front: What is a qualitative trait vs. a quantitative trait?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A qualitative trait shows discrete, distinct phenotypic classes (e.g., flower color, blood type) and is typically controlled by 1-2 loci. A quantitative trait shows continuous variation across a range (e.g., height, weight, corolla length) and is typically polygenic (many loci). The distinction is about the number of loci and whether the phenotypic distribution is discrete or continuous.

**ELI5:** Qualitative = categories you can sort into buckets (red vs. white flowers). Quantitative = a sliding scale with every shade in between (how tall you are). One has a few genes calling the shots, the other has a whole committee.

nodes.title: Sources of Evolutionary Change
nodes.subtitle: The 5 mechanisms that change allele frequencies (Lec 4 slides 1-3)

popup.heading: Lecture 4 — Sources of Evolutionary Change

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: A population is a group of interbreeding individuals sharing a common gene pool. From the population-genetics view, evolution = change in allele frequencies across generations. There are FIVE mechanisms that change allele frequencies: natural selection, genetic drift, mutation, gene flow (migration), and non-random mating. The absence of all five forces = Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (HWE), which serves as the NULL HYPOTHESIS for detecting evolution.

sections.label: WHY IT MATTERS
sections.body: These five mechanisms constitute the complete explanatory toolkit for all population-level genetic change. Any observed allele frequency change must be attributable to one or a combination of these forces.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body:
• Genetic Evolution in Populations
• Mendelian genetics in populations
• Population: A group of inter-breeding individuals that exist together in space and time (share a distinct gene pool)
• Population genetics: Study of the distribution of alleles within populations and the mechanisms that cause allele frequencies to change over time
• Track inherited genes over generations by following allele and genotype frequencies
• From population genetic perspective – Evolution is the change in allele frequency across generations
• Sources of evolutionary change

examples: Five evolutionary forces: mutation, genetic drift, gene flow, natural selection, non-random mating.
In a Hardy-Weinberg "null" population, all five forces are absent and allele frequencies do not change.
warnings: WATCH OUT: Non-random mating (inbreeding, assortative) changes GENOTYPE frequencies, NOT allele frequencies directly.
WATCH OUT: Only MUTATION creates NEW variation; the other four (selection, drift, gene flow, non-random mating) only redistribute existing variation.
popup.mnemonic: MUD-GAS = Mutation, Unequal drift, Drift, Gene flow, Assortative mating, Selection.

flashcard.front: Name the five evolutionary forces that can change allele frequencies in a population, and briefly describe each.
flashcard.back: (1) MUTATION — new alleles appear via DNA replication errors. (2) GENETIC DRIFT — random sampling causes allele frequencies to fluctuate between generations (stronger in small populations). (3) GENE FLOW (migration) — alleles move between populations when individuals immigrate/emigrate. (4) NATURAL SELECTION — differential reproductive success based on heritable traits. (5) NON-RANDOM MATING (assortative or inbreeding) — affects genotype frequencies (but technically not allele frequencies). All five disrupt Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium.

quiz.question: A population that satisfies Hardy-Weinberg assumptions will:
quiz.correct: Have allele frequencies that do not change from generation to generation
distractors: Evolve rapidly due to random mutation
Lose all genetic variation within a few generations
Exhibit directional natural selection

quiz.question: A population shows genotype frequencies of AA = 0.49, Aa = 0.42, aa = 0.09. With allele frequencies p = 0.7, q = 0.3, expected HWE frequencies are p² = 0.49, 2pq = 0.42, q² = 0.09. The observed and expected frequencies match. What can be CORRECTLY concluded?
quiz.correct: The locus is in Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium — we cannot conclude that NO evolutionary forces are acting on the population, only that NONE of the five HWE-violating forces are detectably affecting THIS LOCUS AT THIS MOMENT
distractors: The population is not evolving at all — HWE at one locus proves all five assumptions are satisfied across the entire genome
The population is evolving by genetic drift — HWE is only expected in very large populations, so matching HWE frequencies must indicate drift is actively maintaining the equilibrium
The population is under balancing selection — only frequency-dependent or heterozygote-advantage selection can produce the exact HWE frequencies at both allele frequencies simultaneously

quiz.question: In a population, inbreeding (mating between relatives) increases the frequency of homozygotes. Which statement about inbreeding and Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium is CORRECT?
quiz.correct: Inbreeding changes GENOTYPE frequencies (increases homozygotes, decreases heterozygotes) but does NOT change ALLELE frequencies — so inbreeding violates HWE assumptions but is not itself a force that drives allele frequency change
distractors: Inbreeding changes allele frequencies by increasing the probability that deleterious recessive alleles are exposed to selection, which causes natural selection to remove them more efficiently

Inbreeding both changes genotype frequencies AND allele frequencies, because homozygotes produce different gametes than heterozygotes — so inbreeding is fully equivalent to selection in its evolutionary effects

Inbreeding maintains Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium as long as it is consistent across generations, because the ratio of homozygotes to heterozygotes stabilizes at a new equilibrium value that still satisfies the HWE equation

quiz.question: Among the five forces that can change allele frequencies, which one CREATES new alleles that did not previously exist in the population?
quiz.correct: Mutation — all other forces (drift, gene flow, selection, non-random mating) only change the frequencies of alleles that are already present; mutation is the only source of truly novel genetic variation
distractors: Gene flow — migration introduces alleles from other populations that may never have existed locally before, creating new variation in the recipient population
Genetic drift — in small populations, drift can randomly create new allele combinations that function as novel variants by rearranging existing alleles into new haplotypes

Natural selection — positive selection for a heterozygous advantage can maintain two alleles that would otherwise disappear, effectively "creating" genetic diversity that would not persist without selection

quiz.question: Gene flow (migration) is one of the five evolutionary forces. What is its direct effect on allele frequencies in the recipient population?
quiz.correct: It moves alleles between populations — immigrants bring in alleles that may not be present locally, and emigrants remove alleles from the source; over time gene flow tends to HOMOGENIZE allele frequencies between connected populations
distractors: Gene flow only adds new mutations — it has no effect on existing allele frequencies unless the migrants carry a novel mutation
Gene flow increases genetic drift because more individuals means more random sampling error
Gene flow reduces allele frequencies uniformly — every migration event reduces all allele frequencies by equal amounts

quiz.question: Why is non-random mating listed as a force that disrupts Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium even though it does NOT directly change allele frequencies?
quiz.correct: It changes GENOTYPE frequencies (e.g., increases homozygotes under inbreeding) without changing allele frequencies — but this still represents a deviation from HWE predictions and can indirectly set the stage for selection to act differently on the redistributed genotypes
distractors: Non-random mating always changes allele frequencies directly by reducing heterozygote fertility
Non-random mating is not actually one of the five forces — it was added by mistake to modern textbooks
Non-random mating only disrupts HWE when combined with mutation — by itself it has no effect on the equation

quiz.question: A Hardy-Weinberg null population has allele frequencies that do not change across generations. Which of the following must be true of such a population?
quiz.correct: No mutation, no selection, no migration, random mating, AND infinite population size — all five assumptions must hold simultaneously for HWE to be maintained
distractors: Only natural selection and mutation must be absent — drift, gene flow, and mating patterns do not affect HWE
Only population size must be sufficiently large; the other four assumptions are irrelevant to HWE
At least three of the five assumptions must hold, but any two can be violated without disrupting the equilibrium

quiz.question: Genetic drift is most appropriately described as:
quiz.correct: Random sampling error that causes allele frequencies to fluctuate between generations, with effects inversely proportional to population size — strongest in small populations
distractors: Directional change in allele frequencies caused by environmental pressure
The rate at which new mutations appear in a population
The movement of alleles between isolated populations via migration

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: What is the name of the theoretical state in which all five evolutionary forces are absent, producing unchanging allele frequencies across generations?
quiz.correct: Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium (HWE)
distractors: Fisher equilibrium
Neutral drift equilibrium
Mutation-selection balance

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: Which of the five evolutionary forces is the ONLY source of truly NEW genetic variation in a population?
quiz.correct: Mutation — the other four (natural selection, genetic drift, gene flow, non-random mating) only redistribute or filter existing variation, while mutation introduces novel alleles
distractors: Gene flow — because migrants can bring alleles never before seen in the recipient population
Genetic drift — because random sampling can create new allele combinations
Natural selection — because strong selection can induce beneficial mutations by pressure

visual.mnemonic: Five forces disrupt H-W equilibrium.
visual.trap: Only mutation creates NEW variation. The others sort and redistribute existing variation.

diagram.title: Hardy-Weinberg — Five Assumptions and Their Violators

nodes.label: Infinite population size
nodes.detail: Assumption 1: N is effectively infinite, so sampling variance in allele frequencies across generations is zero. Real populations are finite and allele counts sample from a multinomial distribution each generation.
nodes.mnemonic: Infinite N = no sampling error in the gamete draw.
nodes.watchOut: Even N = 10,000 shows measurable drift over hundreds of generations.

nodes.label: No selection
nodes.detail: Assumption 2: every genotype has equal fitness (w_AA = w_Aa = w_aa = 1). Any fitness differential changes the gamete pool before the next generation is sampled.
nodes.mnemonic: No selection = survival is a fair coin across genotypes.
nodes.watchOut: Selection is the ONLY force that consistently produces adaptation.

nodes.label: No mutation
nodes.detail: Assumption 3: alleles do not change identity. Real mutation rates are small but nonzero: ~3×10⁻⁸ per site per generation in yeast and ~7×10⁻⁹ in Arabidopsis thaliana. Mutation is the ultimate source of new variation.
nodes.mnemonic: μ ≈ 10⁻⁸ — small but the only source of new alleles.
nodes.watchOut: Mutation is directionally weak but creates variance that other forces act on.

nodes.label: No migration (gene flow)
nodes.detail: Assumption 4: the population is closed — no immigrants (m = 0). Any inflow of alleles from a source with different frequencies shifts p and q toward the source population at rate proportional to m.
nodes.mnemonic: Closed population = no new alleles arriving.
nodes.watchOut: Gene flow homogenizes populations and opposes local adaptation.

nodes.label: Random mating
nodes.detail: Assumption 5: mating partners are chosen independently of genotype at the locus of interest. Inbreeding, assortative mating, and spatial clustering all violate this — but interestingly, non-random mating changes genotype frequencies without changing allele frequencies at all.
nodes.mnemonic: Random mating = panmixia; any structure breaks it.
nodes.watchOut: Non-random mating is the odd one out — it violates HWE without changing p or q.

nodes.label: Genetic drift
nodes.detail: Violates "infinite N". Allele frequencies change randomly due to finite sampling. Buri 1956 ran 107 Drosophila populations (8 M + 8 F per generation) for 19 generations at the brown-eye bw locus starting at p = 0.5 and showed 28 populations fixed for bw and 30 lost it by generation 19 — pure drift.
nodes.mnemonic: Buri 107 pops, 19 gens → drift goes to fixation or loss randomly.
nodes.watchOut: Drift is strongest in small populations and acts on all alleles, neutral or not.

nodes.label: Natural selection
nodes.detail: Violates "no selection". Differential survival/reproduction shifts allele frequencies. Classic examples: Kettlewell’s peppered moth (Biston betularia) industrial melanism and the Drosophila ADH locus where allozyme frequencies track environmental alcohol tolerance.
nodes.mnemonic: Selection is the only force that builds adaptation.
nodes.watchOut: Selection coefficients as small as s = 0.01 can still drive fixation in ~700 generations.

nodes.label: Mutation
nodes.detail: Violates "no mutation". Creates new alleles via base substitution, indel, or larger structural change. Yeast germline mutation rate ≈ 3×10⁻⁸ per site per generation; Arabidopsis thaliana ≈ 7×10⁻⁹. Slow but the only generator of truly new variation.
nodes.mnemonic: Yeast 3×10⁻⁸, Arabidopsis 7×10⁻⁹ per site per generation.
nodes.watchOut: Mutation alone produces negligible frequency change; it feeds other forces.

nodes.label: Gene flow (migration)
nodes.detail: Violates "no migration". Movement of individuals (or gametes) between populations with different allele frequencies redistributes existing alleles. A migrant rate m = 0.1 per generation fully homogenizes two populations within ~20 generations.
nodes.mnemonic: Gene flow = allele frequency laundering between populations.
nodes.watchOut: Gene flow only redistributes alleles; it doesn’t create new ones.

nodes.label: Non-random mating
nodes.detail: Violates "random mating". Inbreeding increases homozygosity by a factor F (the inbreeding coefficient). Assortative mating by phenotype similarly concentrates genotypes. Crucially, non-random mating changes GENOTYPE frequencies (more homozygotes) but NOT allele frequencies.
nodes.mnemonic: Inbreeding boosts homozygotes without changing p or q.
nodes.watchOut: Changes GENOTYPE frequencies but NOT allele frequencies by itself — violates HWE but is not an allele frequency change mechanism.

diagram.mnemonic: MUD-GAS: Mutation / Drift / Gene flow / Assortative mating / Selection. Only mutation creates new variation; the others redistribute existing variation.

flashcards.front: What is a population in evolutionary biology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A population is a group of interbreeding individuals of the same species that share a common gene pool (the total set of alleles at all loci in the population). It is the fundamental unit of evolution because evolution occurs through changes in allele frequencies within populations, not within individuals.

**ELI5:** A population is a neighborhood of animals (or plants) that actually mate with each other and share the same pool of gene versions. Evolution happens to the neighborhood, not to any single individual.

flashcards.front: What is the precise definition of evolution at the population level?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Evolution is a change in allele frequencies within a population over generations. If the frequency of any allele at any locus changes from one generation to the next, evolution has occurred. This is the microevolutionary definition, distinct from the broader concept of descent with modification.

**ELI5:** Evolution = the recipe book of a population changing over time. If blue-eye genes go from 30% to 35% of the gene pool in one generation, that's evolution -- even if nobody notices.

flashcards.front: List ALL FIVE mechanisms (forces) that can cause evolution.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Natural selection -- differential survival/reproduction based on phenotype. (2) Genetic drift -- random changes in allele frequencies due to finite population size. (3) Mutation -- introduction of new alleles. (4) Gene flow (migration) -- movement of alleles between populations. (5) Non-random mating -- preferential mating based on genotype/phenotype (e.g., assortative mating, inbreeding).

**ELI5:** Five ways to change a population's gene mix: (1) some do better than others (selection), (2) random luck (drift), (3) typos in DNA (mutation), (4) newcomers arrive or locals leave (gene flow), (5) picky mating (non-random mating).

flashcards.front: What is the Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium and what role does it play in evolutionary biology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium (HWE) is the null hypothesis of population genetics. It states that allele and genotype frequencies remain constant across generations in the absence of evolutionary forces. If a population deviates from HWE expectations, one or more of the five evolutionary mechanisms must be acting. It serves as a baseline for detecting evolution.

**ELI5:** HWE is the 'nothing is happening' scenario -- like assuming a coin is fair until proven otherwise. If gene frequencies change when they shouldn't, something (selection, drift, etc.) must be causing it.

flashcards.front: Which of the five evolutionary mechanisms is the ONLY one that can create genuinely NEW genetic variation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Mutation is the only mechanism that creates new alleles (new genetic variants that did not previously exist). Natural selection, genetic drift, gene flow, and non-random mating can change the frequencies of existing alleles or redistribute them, but they cannot generate novel alleles. Recombination creates new combinations of existing alleles but not new alleles themselves.

**ELI5:** Mutation is the inventor -- it creates brand new gene versions. Everything else just shuffles, spreads, or filters the versions that mutation already invented.

flashcards.front: What is gene flow and how does it affect evolution?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Gene flow (migration) is the movement of alleles between populations, typically through the immigration or emigration of individuals (or gametes, like pollen). It can introduce new alleles to a population, change allele frequencies, and homogenize allele frequencies between populations, counteracting divergence due to selection or drift.

**ELI5:** Gene flow is when someone from another town moves in and has kids with the locals. They bring gene versions that might be rare or absent in the new town, mixing the gene pools together.

flashcards.front: How does non-random mating differ from the other four evolutionary forces?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Non-random mating changes GENOTYPE frequencies (e.g., increasing homozygosity through inbreeding) but does NOT by itself change ALLELE frequencies. The other four forces (selection, drift, mutation, gene flow) all change allele frequencies. Non-random mating is sometimes excluded from lists of evolutionary forces for this reason, though it is still considered a departure from HWE.

**ELI5:** Picky mating reshuffles who pairs with whom (more like-with-like, or relatives together), changing how gene versions are packaged into individuals. But the overall fraction of each gene version in the population stays the same.

flashcards.front: What is a gene pool?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The gene pool is the complete set of all alleles at all loci present in a population at a given time. It represents the total genetic diversity available in that population. Allele frequencies within the gene pool are what change during evolution.

**ELI5:** The gene pool is like a giant bag of marbles where each marble is a copy of a gene variant. All the individuals in a population contribute their gene copies to this shared bag, and babies draw from it.

flashcards.front: Natural selection requires heritable variation in fitness. What does this mean in terms of the five mechanisms?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Natural selection requires: (1) phenotypic variation among individuals, (2) that variation must be heritable (genetic basis), and (3) variation must cause differential fitness (survival and/or reproduction). Mutation provides the raw material (new alleles), recombination creates new combinations, and selection then acts on the resulting phenotypic variation. Without heritable variation, selection has nothing to act on.

**ELI5:** For natural selection to work, individuals must differ, those differences must be passed to offspring, and some versions must help you survive/reproduce better. No differences = nothing to select.

flashcards.front: Can evolution occur without natural selection? Explain.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Yes. Genetic drift alone can change allele frequencies (especially in small populations), causing evolution without any fitness differences. Gene flow can change allele frequencies regardless of selection. Mutation pressure can shift frequencies. Non-random mating changes genotype frequencies. Natural selection is just ONE of five mechanisms; evolution can occur through any combination of them.

**ELI5:** Absolutely. A storm killing random members of a small island population (drift) changes the gene mix without any gene being 'better.' Evolution just means gene frequencies changed -- it doesn't require survival of the fittest.

flashcards.front: EXAM TRAP: A student writes 'Evolution is when organisms change to adapt to their environment.' What is wrong with this definition?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** This definition is too narrow. It describes only adaptive evolution via natural selection. The correct population genetics definition is: evolution is any change in allele frequencies within a population over generations. This includes non-adaptive changes due to drift, gene flow, mutation pressure, and non-random mating. Also, individuals do not evolve -- populations do.

**ELI5:** That definition only describes one of five ways evolution happens and wrongly implies it's always purposeful improvement. Evolution includes random changes (drift) and is about populations, not individual organisms changing during their lifetime.

flashcards.front: Why is mutation considered a weak evolutionary force on its own?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Mutation rates are extremely low (typically 10^-5 to 10^-9 per locus per generation), so mutation alone changes allele frequencies very slowly. It would take thousands of generations for mutation pressure alone to significantly shift an allele frequency. However, mutation is critical as the ULTIMATE source of all new genetic variation that the other forces then act upon.

**ELI5:** Mutation is like a very slow drip filling a pool. By itself, it barely changes anything per generation. But it's the only faucet in the room -- without it, there would be no new gene versions for selection or drift to work with.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a mechanism of evolution and the raw material of evolution?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Mutation and recombination provide the raw material of evolution (genetic variation). The five evolutionary mechanisms (selection, drift, mutation, gene flow, non-random mating) are the processes that change allele or genotype frequencies. Mutation plays a dual role: it is both a source of variation AND a (weak) evolutionary force. Recombination generates new allele combinations but is not itself a mechanism that changes allele frequencies.

**ELI5:** Raw material = the different ingredients available (created by mutation). Mechanisms = the processes that change the recipe (selection picks favorites, drift removes some randomly, etc.).

nodes.title: Hardy-Weinberg Theorem
nodes.subtitle: The null model of population genetics (Lec 4 slides 4-12)

popup.heading: Lecture 4 — Hardy-Weinberg Principle

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: The Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium (HWE) is the NULL HYPOTHESIS of population genetics. Its five assumptions are: infinite population, no selection, no migration (gene flow), no mutation, and random mating. The equations are p + q = 1 (allele frequencies) and p² + 2pq + q² = 1 (genotype frequencies: AA, Aa, aa).

sections.label: WORKED EXAMPLE
sections.body: Drosophila ADH locus, 500 flies, p=0.7, q=0.3. EXPECTED frequencies: AA = 245 (p² × 500), Aa = 210 (2pq × 500), aa = 45 (q² × 500). OBSERVED: AA = 287, Aa = 126, aa = 87. Chi-square P = 0.02 → excess of homozygotes → population is NOT in HWE → some force (e.g., inbreeding, selection) is acting on this locus.

sections.label: WHY IT MATTERS
sections.body: HWE gives us a mathematical standard against which to detect evolution. Any deviation from HWE expectations indicates one or more of the five evolutionary forces is operating on the tested locus.

examples: Hardy-Weinberg equations: p + q = 1, p² + 2pq + q² = 1.
Five assumptions: (1) no mutation, (2) no selection, (3) no migration, (4) random mating, (5) infinite population (no drift).
Drosophila ADH example: 500 flies, p=0.7, q=0.3. Expected AA=245, Aa=210, aa=45. Observed AA=287, Aa=126, aa=87. Chi-square P=0.02 → NOT in HWE, excess homozygotes.
warnings: WATCH OUT: HWE is a NULL hypothesis — we test whether a population is in equilibrium to DETECT evolution.
WATCH OUT: A population can be in HWE for one locus and not others — each locus must be tested independently.
popup.mnemonic: p² + 2pq + q² = 1. If observed ≠ expected → evolution is happening.

flashcard.front: State the five assumptions of the Hardy-Weinberg principle and explain what happens if each one is violated.
flashcard.back: (1) NO MUTATION — if violated, new alleles enter the population and allele frequencies drift. (2) NO NATURAL SELECTION — if violated, fitness differences skew genotype frequencies. (3) NO MIGRATION (GENE FLOW) — if violated, alleles move in/out and frequencies change. (4) RANDOM MATING — if violated (e.g., assortative mating or inbreeding), genotype frequencies deviate from p² + 2pq + q² even without allele frequency change. (5) INFINITE POPULATION SIZE — if violated, genetic drift randomly changes allele frequencies (stronger in smaller populations). HWE gives us a NULL model: if observed genotype frequencies deviate from the predictions, one of these five assumptions is being violated.

quiz.question: In a population with alleles A and a at frequencies p = 0.6 and q = 0.4, what is the expected frequency of heterozygotes under Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium?
distractors: 0.50 (always one-half)

quiz.question: A recessive autosomal disorder occurs at a frequency of 1 in 10,000 in a large randomly mating population. What is the CARRIER (heterozygote) frequency?
quiz.correct: q² = 0.0001, so q = 0.01 and p ≈ 0.99; carrier frequency = 2pq ≈ 2 × 0.99 × 0.01 ≈ 0.0198 (about 1 in 50) — far more common than affected individuals, which is why recessive diseases persist
distractors: 1 in 10,000 — the carrier frequency equals the disease frequency because heterozygotes pass one copy of the allele just as affected homozygotes do, so both are equally common
1 in 5,000 — exactly twice the disease frequency, because for every one affected individual there must be two carriers who contributed the two q alleles observed in the affected person
1 in 100,000,000 — because carriers must carry BOTH a dominant and a recessive allele, and the probability of inheriting both is q × p, which is much smaller than q² for rare alleles

quiz.question: A population geneticist observes that at a particular locus, the observed frequency of heterozygotes is LOWER than expected under HWE. Which of the following is the MOST likely explanation?
quiz.correct: Inbreeding or assortative mating — both cause individuals to mate with genetically similar partners, increasing homozygosity and reducing heterozygote frequency relative to HWE predictions
distractors: Directional selection favoring the dominant allele — selection increases p and decreases q, but at any given p and q the HWE prediction is still 2pq; selection on a dominant allele would not specifically reduce heterozygote frequency below 2pq

Genetic drift in a large population — drift decreases allele frequencies uniformly across all genotype classes, so it reduces both homozygote and heterozygote frequencies proportionally and cannot produce a heterozygote deficit specifically
Mutation pressure creating new alleles — new mutations increase allelic diversity, which tends to increase the frequency of heterozygotes above the HWE prediction, not below it

quiz.question: The Hardy-Weinberg principle is called a "null model" for population genetics. What does this mean in practice when a researcher detects a deviation from HWE at a locus?
quiz.correct: A deviation from HWE is a SIGNAL that one or more evolutionary forces (selection, drift, mutation, gene flow, non-random mating) are affecting that locus — it does not identify WHICH force, only that something is operating
distractors: A deviation from HWE proves that natural selection is acting on that specific locus — it is the most sensitive test for selection available and rules out all other evolutionary forces when positive
A deviation from HWE means the Hardy-Weinberg equation was applied incorrectly — the null model is mathematically exact, so any apparent deviation must reflect a calculation or sampling error

A deviation from HWE is expected in all real populations because no population truly has infinite size and zero mutation — HWE deviations are universal and provide no information about specific evolutionary mechanisms

quiz.question: In the Hardy-Weinberg equations, what does "p + q = 1" represent, and what does "p² + 2pq + q² = 1" represent?
quiz.correct: The first equation describes ALLELE frequencies (p = frequency of A, q = frequency of a); the second describes GENOTYPE frequencies (p² = AA, 2pq = Aa, q² = aa) expected at equilibrium
distractors: The first describes genotype frequencies and the second describes allele frequencies — the two equations are interchangeable
Both equations describe allele frequencies; the second is just a more detailed version of the first
Both equations describe genotype frequencies; p + q refers to homozygous genotypes only

quiz.question: A researcher using Hardy-Weinberg to estimate the frequency of a recessive allele in a population should calculate q from:
quiz.correct: q² — the frequency of homozygous recessive individuals — because dominant alleles mask recessive ones in heterozygotes, so you cannot calculate q directly from the frequency of dominant phenotypes
distractors: p² — because the frequency of dominant homozygotes is always equal to the square of q
2pq — the heterozygote frequency, which directly gives q without needing a square root
The frequency of dominant phenotypes — which is always equal to 1-q by definition

quiz.question: The ADH locus in fruit flies is often cited as an HWE-tractable example because:
quiz.correct: Allele frequencies can be estimated from electrophoresis phenotypes — different ADH alleles produce differently migrating protein bands, and the observed phenotype frequencies can be directly compared against HWE predictions
distractors: Fruit flies are the only organism in which Hardy-Weinberg applies — the equation fails in other species due to different inheritance patterns
ADH is a single-allele locus with no variation, which makes it a perfect null for HWE tests
ADH is a lethal gene that kills carriers, which is why HWE predictions are easy to verify

quiz.question: If a population is in HWE at ONE locus, can you conclude the population is in HWE at ALL loci?
quiz.correct: No — a population can be in HWE at one locus and not others. Each locus must be tested independently, because selection, drift, or other forces may affect some loci more strongly than others
distractors: Yes — HWE is a whole-population property; if one locus satisfies it, all loci must
Yes — because HWE requires random mating across the whole population, any HWE-consistent locus implies random mating for all loci
No — but only if you can demonstrate explicit selection on other loci; without that evidence, HWE at one locus does imply HWE at all

quiz.question: L4 APPLICATION: At the Drosophila ADH locus, 500 flies were sampled with p=0.7 and q=0.3. Under HWE, how many AA homozygotes would be EXPECTED?
distractors: 287 (the observed count)
210 (which is 2pq × 500, the heterozygote count)
350 (which is 0.7 × 500, the allele count)

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: In a Drosophila ADH study, observed genotypes were AA=287, Aa=126, aa=87 when HWE predicted AA=245, Aa=210, aa=45. A chi-square test gave P=0.02. What does this pattern (excess homozygotes) most likely indicate?
quiz.correct: Inbreeding or positive assortative mating — both increase homozygote frequency relative to HWE expectation without necessarily changing allele frequencies
distractors: Heterozygote advantage — which would increase the number of heterozygotes, not decrease them
Random mutation at the ADH locus — which would not specifically produce a homozygote excess
Random genetic drift — which affects allele frequencies but does not specifically produce excess homozygotes

visual.mnemonic: p² + 2pq + q² = 1 always works when population is at equilibrium.
visual.trap: Rare dominant alleles hide rarer recessive alleles. Always calculate q from q² (homozygous recessives), not from a count of dominant phenotypes.

diagram.title: Hardy-Weinberg Punnett Square — Drosophila ADH (Lec 4, n=500)

nodes.label: A (p=0.7)
nodes.detail: Row header: maternal gamete carrying allele A at frequency p = 0.7. Under random mating, 70% of eggs carry A. p is simply the count of A alleles divided by the total number of alleles (2N) in the population.
nodes.mnemonic: p = frequency of the A allele in the egg pool.
nodes.watchOut: p is an ALLELE frequency, not a genotype frequency. Do not conflate p with f(AA).

nodes.label: a (q=0.3)
nodes.detail: Row header: maternal gamete carrying allele a at frequency q = 0.3. Because only two alleles segregate at this ADH locus, p + q = 1, so q = 1 − p = 0.3.
nodes.mnemonic: q = 1 − p, always.
nodes.watchOut: q drifts upward when the A allele is selected against — not when a is "good".

nodes.label: A (p=0.7)
nodes.detail: Column header: paternal gamete carrying allele A at frequency p = 0.7. Random mating means egg A meets sperm A with probability p × p.
nodes.mnemonic: Column = sperm, row = egg. Random mating lets us multiply.
nodes.watchOut: Only valid if mating is random with respect to this locus.

nodes.label: a (q=0.3)
nodes.detail: Column header: paternal gamete carrying allele a at frequency q = 0.3. Sperm a has the same population frequency as egg a because gametes are drawn from the same allele pool.
nodes.mnemonic: Sperm and egg frequencies equal if both sexes share allele frequencies.
nodes.watchOut: Sex-linked loci break this symmetry — X and Y pools differ.

nodes.label: AA = p² = 0.49
nodes.detail: Homozygous dominant cell. Expected frequency under HWE = p × p = 0.7 × 0.7 = 0.49. In the Lec 4 Drosophila ADH sample of n = 500 flies the expected count is 245, but Robbins reported observed AA = 287 — an excess of homozygotes.
nodes.mnemonic: p² = AA share of the population.
nodes.watchOut: Excess AA with deficient Aa is the signature of inbreeding or Wahlund effect.

nodes.label: Aa = pq = 0.21
nodes.detail: First heterozygous cell: egg A meets sperm a. Frequency = p × q = 0.7 × 0.3 = 0.21. Combined with its mirror cell (aA), the total heterozygote frequency is 2pq = 0.42.
nodes.mnemonic: pq is half of 2pq — don’t forget the mirror cell.
nodes.watchOut: Students sometimes report heterozygote frequency as pq instead of 2pq.

nodes.label: aA = qp = 0.21
nodes.detail: Mirror heterozygous cell: egg a meets sperm A. Genotype is identical to Aa_1 but with reversed parental origin. Adding both halves gives 2pq = 0.42, the expected Aa frequency. Expected count in n=500 is 210; observed only 126.
nodes.mnemonic: 2pq = combined heterozygote share.
nodes.watchOut: Heterozygote deficit (obs 126 vs exp 210) points to non-random mating or drift.

nodes.label: aa = q² = 0.09
nodes.detail: Homozygous recessive cell. Expected frequency = q × q = 0.3 × 0.3 = 0.09. Expected count in n=500 is 45; Robbins reported observed aa = 87 — nearly double. Chi-square on AA=287, Aa=126, aa=87 vs expected 245/210/45 gives P = 0.02, so the ADH locus is NOT in HWE and at least one assumption is violated.
nodes.mnemonic: q² = the "recessive disease" fraction you can see.
nodes.watchOut: Chi-square P = 0.02 means we reject HWE for ADH. Excess homozygotes at both ends plus a heterozygote deficit — classic inbreeding signature, not drift alone.

diagram.mnemonic: p² + 2pq + q² = 1. If observed ≠ expected → some force is acting.

flashcards.front: State the Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium equations for a two-allele system.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Allele frequencies: p + q = 1, where p = frequency of allele A (or dominant), q = frequency of allele a (or recessive). Genotype frequencies: p^2 + 2pq + q^2 = 1, where p^2 = freq(AA), 2pq = freq(Aa), q^2 = freq(aa). These hold when no evolutionary forces are acting.

**ELI5:** If p is the fraction of 'A' alleles and q is the fraction of 'a' alleles (and p + q = 1), then randomly pairing them gives: AA = p times p, Aa = 2 times p times q, aa = q times q. It's just the math of random combinations.

flashcards.front: List ALL FIVE assumptions of Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Infinitely large population (no genetic drift), (2) No natural selection (all genotypes have equal fitness), (3) No migration/gene flow, (4) No mutation, (5) Random mating (no assortative mating or inbreeding). If ANY assumption is violated, the population may deviate from HWE.

**ELI5:** HWE works only in a fantasy world where: the population is huge, nobody's better at surviving, nobody moves in/out, no DNA typos happen, and everyone mates randomly. Break any rule and the math won't match reality.

flashcards.front: HWE PROBLEM: In a population of 500 Drosophila, you observe 287 AA, 126 Aa, and 87 aa at the ADH locus. Calculate p and q.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Count alleles: Total alleles = 2 x 500 = 1000. A alleles = 2(287) + 126 = 574 + 126 = 700. So p = 700/1000 = 0.70. a alleles = 2(87) + 126 = 174 + 126 = 300. So q = 300/1000 = 0.30. Check: p + q = 0.70 + 0.30 = 1.0.

**ELI5:** Each fly has 2 gene copies. Count all the A copies: each AA fly gives 2, each Aa gives 1. Divide by total copies (1000). You get 70% A and 30% a.

flashcards.front: HWE PROBLEM: With p=0.7 and q=0.3 in 500 flies, what are the EXPECTED genotype numbers under HWE?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Expected frequencies: AA = p^2 = (0.7)^2 = 0.49, Aa = 2pq = 2(0.7)(0.3) = 0.42, aa = q^2 = (0.3)^2 = 0.09. Expected numbers (x500): AA = 0.49 x 500 = 245, Aa = 0.42 x 500 = 210, aa = 0.09 x 500 = 45. Check: 245 + 210 + 45 = 500.

**ELI5:** If mating were truly random: 49% should be AA (245 flies), 42% should be Aa (210 flies), 9% should be aa (45 flies).

flashcards.front: HWE PROBLEM: Observed = 287 AA, 126 Aa, 87 aa. Expected = 245, 210, 45. The chi-square test gives P=0.02. What do you conclude?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Since P = 0.02 < 0.05 (significance threshold), we REJECT the null hypothesis that the population is in Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium. The observed genotype frequencies differ significantly from HWE expectations. One or more evolutionary forces must be acting on this locus (excess of homozygotes suggests possible non-random mating, selection, or population substructure/Wahlund effect).

**ELI5:** The math says the real numbers don't match what random mating would predict. Something's going on -- maybe flies prefer mating with similar types, or some genotypes survive better.

flashcards.front: How do you calculate the chi-square statistic for an HWE test?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Chi-square = Sum of [(Observed - Expected)^2 / Expected] for each genotype class. For the Drosophila example: (287-245)^2/245 + (126-210)^2/210 + (87-45)^2/45 = (42)^2/245 + (-84)^2/210 + (42)^2/45 = 7.2 + 33.6 + 39.2 = 80.0. With 1 degree of freedom (3 classes - 2 estimated parameters), compare to chi-square table. The critical value at P=0.05 is 3.84; our value far exceeds this.

**ELI5:** For each genotype, take (actual minus expected), square it, divide by expected. Add them up. If the total is big (bigger than 3.84), the difference is real, not just random noise.

flashcards.front: Why does a chi-square HWE test have only 1 degree of freedom for a 2-allele system?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Degrees of freedom = (number of genotype classes) - (number of estimated parameters) - 1. With 3 genotype classes (AA, Aa, aa) and 1 estimated parameter (p, since q = 1-p), df = 3 - 1 - 1 = 1. We lose one df for estimating the allele frequency from the data and one for the constraint that frequencies sum to 1.

**ELI5:** You have 3 categories but you used the data to estimate one number (p), and the total must equal your sample size. So only 1 number is truly free to vary.

flashcards.front: If a population IS in Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium, what can you conclude about evolutionary forces?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** If a population is in HWE at a given locus, you can conclude that the NET effect of evolutionary forces at that locus is negligible. However, failing to reject HWE does NOT prove no forces are acting -- forces could be weak, opposing each other, or the test may lack statistical power. HWE is assessed locus by locus; a population may be in HWE at one locus but not another.

**ELI5:** It means everything looks 'normal' at that gene -- like a coin that appears fair after 100 flips. But it doesn't guarantee nothing's happening; the test might just not be sensitive enough to catch it.

flashcards.front: Can a population be in HWE at one locus but not another? Why?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Yes. Each locus is tested independently. Different evolutionary forces may act on different loci. For example, natural selection might act strongly on a locus coding for disease resistance while a nearby neutral locus remains in HWE. Genetic drift, selection, and gene flow can have locus-specific effects.

**ELI5:** Each gene is its own story. One gene might be under heavy selection (not in HWE) while the gene next door is ignored by selection and looks perfectly random (in HWE).

flashcards.front: A population has q^2 = 0.04 for a recessive condition. What are q, p, and the carrier frequency?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** q^2 = 0.04, so q = sqrt(0.04) = 0.20. p = 1 - q = 0.80. Carrier frequency (heterozygotes) = 2pq = 2(0.80)(0.20) = 0.32 = 32%. Note: carriers (32%) are far more common than affected individuals (4%), which is why recessive alleles persist in populations.

**ELI5:** 4% of people show the condition (aa). Square-root to get the 'a' frequency: 20%. The 'A' frequency is 80%. Carriers (Aa) = 2 x 80% x 20% = 32%. One in three people carries the hidden allele!

flashcards.front: COMMON MISTAKE: 'A dominant allele will eventually reach a frequency of 100%.' Why is this wrong under HWE?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Under HWE, allele frequencies do not change across generations regardless of dominance relationships. Dominance affects phenotype ratios, not allele frequency dynamics. In the absence of selection, a dominant allele at 30% will remain at 30% indefinitely. Dominance is about the phenotypic expression of heterozygotes, not about evolutionary advantage.

**ELI5:** Being dominant just means you show your effect when you're paired with a recessive. It doesn't mean you spread faster. A dominant allele at 30% stays at 30% forever if nothing else is going on -- being loud doesn't make you more popular.

flashcards.front: Why does HWE require random mating? What happens with non-random mating?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** HWE requires random mating to produce genotype frequencies that match p^2, 2pq, q^2 predictions. Non-random mating (e.g., inbreeding, assortative mating) changes GENOTYPE frequencies -- typically increasing homozygosity (excess of AA and aa, deficit of Aa) -- without changing allele frequencies. The population departs from HWE genotype predictions even though p and q remain the same.

**ELI5:** If tall people only marry tall people, you'll see more extreme tall and short people (more homozygotes) than random mixing would predict. But the overall fraction of 'tall' and 'short' gene copies hasn't changed.

flashcards.front: In the Drosophila ADH example, the observed data shows excess homozygotes (both AA and aa) and deficit of heterozygotes. What evolutionary force might explain this?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Excess homozygosity with deficient heterozygosity is a classic signature of: (1) Non-random mating (inbreeding or positive assortative mating), (2) Population substructure (Wahlund effect -- sampling from two differentiated subpopulations), or (3) Disruptive selection favoring homozygotes over heterozygotes. Further data would be needed to distinguish these.

**ELI5:** Too many 'purebreds' (AA and aa) and too few 'mixes' (Aa) means either: flies mate with similar flies, the sample is actually two separate groups mixed together, or middle-of-the-road flies do worse than the extremes.

flashcards.front: A recessive allele has q = 0.01. What fraction of recessive alleles are 'hidden' in carriers vs. expressed in homozygotes?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Alleles in homozygotes (aa) = 2 x q^2 = 2(0.01)^2 = 0.0002 per person on average. Alleles in carriers (Aa) = 2pq = 2(0.99)(0.01) = 0.0198. Ratio of carrier alleles to homozygote alleles: 0.0198/0.0002 = 99. So 99% of recessive alleles are hidden in carriers and only 1% are in homozygous individuals. This is why selection against rare recessives is extremely slow.

**ELI5:** When a bad recessive allele is rare (1%), almost all copies of it are hiding in healthy carriers (99 out of 100 copies). Selection can only 'see' the 1% that show up in affected individuals -- the other 99% are invisible.

flashcards.front: Write out a step-by-step protocol for testing whether a population is in HWE.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Count observed genotype numbers. (2) Calculate observed allele frequencies (p, q) from genotype counts. (3) Calculate expected genotype frequencies under HWE: p^2, 2pq, q^2. (4) Multiply expected frequencies by total N to get expected numbers. (5) Compute chi-square statistic: sum of (O-E)^2/E. (6) Compare to chi-square distribution with 1 df. (7) If P < 0.05, reject HWE.

**ELI5:** Count your genotypes, figure out allele percentages, predict what random mating should give you, compare prediction to reality using chi-square math, and if the mismatch is too big, something evolutionary is happening.

nodes.title: Genetic Drift, Bottlenecks & Founder Effects
nodes.subtitle: Random allele frequency change in finite populations (Lec 4 slides 13-22)

popup.heading: Lecture 4 — Genetic Drift

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Genetic drift = RANDOM change in allele frequencies due to finite sampling. Drift is NOT directional and is STRONGEST in SMALL populations. It can fix or lose alleles regardless of fitness.

sections.label: KEY EXPERIMENT: BURI 1956
sections.body: Peter Buri established 107 Drosophila populations, each starting with bw/bw75 heterozygotes (8 males and 8 females per population per generation). By generation 19, the bw75 allele had either gone to fixation or been lost at random in each line — dramatically illustrating drift in small populations.

sections.label: BOTTLENECK vs FOUNDER EFFECT
sections.body: BOTTLENECK: a pre-existing population crashes to very small size. Northern elephant seals were hunted to only 10-20 individuals; they have recovered to over 100,000 but show very low diversity. FOUNDER EFFECT: a few individuals colonize a new area. Examples: Amish populations (elevated Ellis-van Creveld syndrome); Pingelap islanders (colorblindness); Galápagos colonization.

examples: Drift in Drosophila (Buri 1956): 107 populations, 8M + 8F per generation; by generation 19, bw75 allele randomly fixed or lost.
Genetic bottleneck: Northern elephant seals hunted to 10-20 individuals, now >100,000 but very low diversity.
Founder effect: Amish (Ellis-van Creveld syndrome), Pingelap (colorblindness), Galápagos colonization events.
warnings: WATCH OUT: Drift is NOT directional — it is random. Its effects are strongest in SMALL populations.
WATCH OUT: Bottleneck effects persist for many generations even after the population recovers, because LOST alleles are gone permanently.
popup.mnemonic: BFS = Bottleneck, Founder effect, Small population = strong drift.

flashcard.front: Explain how the founder effect differs from a genetic bottleneck, and give an example of each.
flashcard.back: A GENETIC BOTTLENECK occurs when an existing population is dramatically reduced in size (disease, disaster, overhunting), eliminating many alleles by chance. The survivors rebuild the population from a non-representative sample — example: cheetahs (~10,000 years ago bottleneck reduced genetic diversity to near-clonal levels; northern elephant seals reduced to ~20 individuals in the 1890s). A FOUNDER EFFECT occurs when a small number of individuals colonize a new habitat, bringing only a subset of the source population's alleles. The new population is genetically distinct from the source not by selection but by sampling chance — example: Amish populations in Pennsylvania show elevated frequencies of recessive disorders (e.g., polydactyly) because founders happened to carry those alleles. Both reduce genetic diversity through drift.

quiz.question: Genetic drift has the strongest effect on allele frequency change in which type of population?
quiz.correct: Small populations
distractors: Large populations with equal sex ratios
Populations at Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium
Populations with high gene flow

quiz.question: Northern elephant seals were hunted to ~20 individuals in the 1890s. Today the population has recovered to ~100,000. Despite this recovery, the population shows almost no genetic variation. What best explains this?
quiz.correct: A severe bottleneck eliminates alleles by chance regardless of their fitness — the surviving 20 individuals carried only a small, non-representative sample of the original allelic diversity, and no amount of subsequent population growth can restore alleles that were lost
distractors: Strong directional selection during the hunting era removed all alleles that reduced resistance to human predation, leaving only the fittest alleles in the recovered population
Inbreeding among the 20 survivors fixed recessive alleles to homozygosity, and these recessive alleles are now expressed in all individuals, reducing measured heterozygosity

Genetic drift can reduce diversity only over millions of generations — the ~130 years since the bottleneck is too short to have measurably reduced variation, so the low diversity must predate the bottleneck

quiz.question: The Old Order Amish community in Pennsylvania has an unusually high frequency of Ellis-van Creveld syndrome (extra fingers, short stature), caused by a rare recessive allele. The population was founded by a small group of immigrants from Europe. Which evolutionary mechanism BEST explains this elevated frequency?
quiz.correct: Founder effect — the founding immigrants happened to carry the Ellis-van Creveld allele at higher-than-average frequency by chance, and subsequent population growth within the community amplified that initially elevated frequency
distractors: Natural selection favoring the Ellis-van Creveld phenotype in the agricultural Pennsylvania environment — individuals with extra fingers may have had manual dexterity advantages in farm work

Mutation accumulation — the isolated Amish community has a higher mutation rate at the Ellis-van Creveld locus due to inbreeding, which increases the probability that the same mutation arises repeatedly in each generation

Gene flow from neighboring Pennsylvania Dutch populations who share a similar recessive allele — intermarriage between the Amish and their neighbors introduced the allele and elevated its frequency above the European baseline

quiz.question: Buri's 1956 classic Drosophila experiment started 107 populations, each of 16 flies (8 males, 8 females), all heterozygous at the bw75 locus. After 19 generations, most populations had FIXED one allele or the other. What would happen if the same experiment were run with 1,600 flies per population instead of 16?
quiz.correct: Allele fixation would be much slower and fewer populations would reach fixation by generation 19, because drift is proportional to 1/(2N) — larger populations have less sampling error and allele frequencies change less per generation
distractors: The result would be identical — genetic drift is determined solely by the starting heterozygosity (50/50), not by population size, so fixation time is the same regardless of N

Larger populations would fix alleles FASTER because natural selection for or against bw75 becomes more efficient in large populations and overrides drift, deterministically driving allele frequencies to fixation

In populations of 1,600, both alleles would reach 50% frequency and be maintained there indefinitely by heterozygote advantage, because large populations always converge on the heterozygote-maximizing equilibrium

quiz.question: Cheetahs have extraordinarily low genetic diversity compared to other big cats. Which evolutionary event MOST likely explains this?
quiz.correct: A severe genetic bottleneck approximately 10,000 years ago reduced the population to a small number of individuals — the surviving lineage carried only a subset of the original genetic variation, and today's population is essentially descended from this narrow founding group
distractors: Strong purifying selection has eliminated all but the fittest alleles over the past 10,000 years — cheetahs are at the local fitness peak and have no room for additional variation
Cheetahs reproduce asexually, which prevents genetic recombination from generating new variation each generation
Cheetahs have the lowest mutation rate of any mammal, which explains why they accumulate very few novel alleles compared to other species

quiz.question: Is genetic drift directional?
quiz.correct: No — drift is random. An allele may increase or decrease in frequency by chance, with no consistent direction. Over time, drift tends to eliminate variation (either fix or lose alleles), but the direction of change in any particular generation is unpredictable
distractors: Yes — drift always favors the dominant allele, which is why drift and selection often act in the same direction
