distractors: All snakes and all lizards universally
Only mammals, specifically in monotremes
Only birds, whose body temperatures vary during incubation

quiz.question: Is TSD an ancestral or derived trait in reptiles?
quiz.correct: TSD is DERIVED — it evolved multiple times independently in different reptile lineages, and in some cases has been lost and replaced by genetic sex determination. It is not a primitive feature of vertebrates
distractors: TSD is ancestral to all vertebrates — all mammals, birds, and reptiles originally used TSD before some lineages evolved genetic sex determination
TSD evolved once in reptiles and has never been lost since — it is a single evolutionary innovation
TSD is ancestral only in birds, where it still persists in some species

quiz.question: A Type II TSD pattern (e.g., in crocodiles) produces:
quiz.correct: Males at extreme temperatures (both high and low) and females in the intermediate temperature range — producing a more complex bimodal relationship between temperature and sex
distractors: Only males at high temperatures with no temperature sensitivity at low temperatures
Only females at all temperatures — crocodiles are all-female species
Random sex ratios independent of temperature

quiz.question: Climate change is a conservation threat to TSD species like sea turtles because:
quiz.correct: Rising temperatures may push nest incubation beyond the range that produces balanced sex ratios — in some turtle species, elevated beach temperatures are already producing almost exclusively female hatchlings, threatening long-term population viability
distractors: Climate change directly damages the DNA of TSD species, preventing reproduction
Climate change eliminates all predators of sea turtles, causing overpopulation and collapse
Climate change increases sea level and drowns all nests regardless of temperature

quiz.question: L2 MECHANISM: Warner & Shine (2008, Nature) tested the Charnov-Bull model in jacky dragons by using aromatase inhibitor. What did this chemical allow them to do?
quiz.correct: Override the natural temperature-sex relationship, producing males and females at all three incubation temperatures (23°C, 27°C, 33°C) so that the fitness of each sex × temperature combination could be measured independently
distractors: Kill all female embryos so only male sex ratios could be measured
Sequence the sex-determining gene in each embryo directly without DNA extraction
Induce mutations in the sex-determination pathway to test for genetic sex determination

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: In the gallfly extended phenotype example, why is gall size under STABILIZING selection?
quiz.correct: Parasitoid wasps preferentially attack SMALL galls while birds preferentially attack LARGE galls — these two opposing predators select against both extremes, favoring intermediate gall size
distractors: Only parasitoid wasps are predators, so small galls are selected against in one direction only — this is directional selection
Stabilizing selection means no selection at all — gall size drifts randomly
Gall size has no heritable component, so no selection can act on it

visual.mnemonic: Three temperature-sex curve shapes across reptiles.
visual.trap: TSD is NOT a primitive trait — it evolved multiple times and can be lost again. Genetic sex determination is ancestral in most vertebrates.

diagram.title: Temperature-Dependent Sex Determination — Charnov-Bull Model

nodes.label: Egg incubation temperature
nodes.detail: In TSD species the temperature experienced by the egg during a critical developmental window sets the phenotypic sex of the hatchling. Warner & Shine used three treatments: 23 degC, 27 degC, and 33 degC across six outdoor enclosures.
nodes.mnemonic: Nest thermometer decides sex.
nodes.watchOut: The "pivotal temperature" differs by species and even by population.

nodes.label: Sex determined at pivotal temp
nodes.detail: Crossing the pivotal temperature during the thermosensitive window flips the developmental switch that produces ovaries vs testes. No sex chromosomes required — the gonad path is chosen by thermal input to aromatase expression.
nodes.mnemonic: Pivotal temp = the tipping point.

nodes.label: TSD vs GSD
nodes.detail: TSD = temperature-dependent sex determination (reptiles, some fish). GSD = genotypic sex determination (mammals XY, birds ZW). Both produce sexed offspring but GSD is insensitive to nest temperature while TSD is entirely driven by it.
nodes.mnemonic: TSD thermometer vs GSD genome.
nodes.watchOut: Some species have BOTH — genotype can be overridden by extreme temperatures.

nodes.label: Charnov-Bull model
nodes.detail: Charnov & Bull (1977) predicted that TSD is adaptive when egg environment differentially affects MALE vs FEMALE fitness. If warm nests produce fitter males and cool nests produce fitter females (or vice versa), matching sex to temperature beats random assignment.
nodes.mnemonic: Temperature matters DIFFERENTLY for sons vs daughters.

nodes.label: Jacky dragon experiment
nodes.detail: Warner & Shine 2008 Nature tracked reproductive success of jacky dragons (Amphibolurus muricatus) hatched at 23, 27, and 33 degC. High-temperature males and low-temperature females had the highest lifetime reproductive success — the first direct test confirming the Charnov-Bull prediction.
nodes.mnemonic: Jacky dragon = Charnov-Bull vindicated.

nodes.label: Aromatase inhibitor override
nodes.detail: Warner & Shine dosed eggs with an aromatase inhibitor (fadrozole) that blocks the temperature-driven conversion of androgens to estrogens. This decoupled sex from temperature, allowing the team to produce "wrong-temperature males" and "wrong-temperature females" and measure their fitness directly.
nodes.mnemonic: Drug breaks the temp-sex link so we can test it.
nodes.watchOut: Without the aromatase trick, temperature and sex are perfectly correlated — cannot separate their effects.

nodes.label: Climate change sex-ratio skew
nodes.detail: In TSD species like green sea turtles (Chelonia mydas), rising sand temperatures have already pushed northern Great Barrier Reef nests above 99% female. Similar feminization threatens crocodilians, tuatara, and many lizards — climate-driven sex ratios can collapse populations faster than any other effect.
nodes.watchOut: Rising temperatures in TSD species like sea turtles can produce all-female nests and cause population collapse.

diagram.mnemonic: Charnov-Bull: TSD is adaptive when temperature x sex-specific fitness interaction differs. TSD is a DERIVED trait — evolved independently multiple times.

flashcards.front: What is Temperature-Dependent Sex Determination (TSD)?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** TSD is a system of environmental sex determination in which the incubation temperature of eggs during a critical developmental window determines the sex of offspring, rather than sex chromosomes (GSD). The temperature affects expression of sex-determining genes such as aromatase.

**ELI5:** Instead of XX/XY chromosomes deciding boy or girl, the temperature the egg sits at while developing picks the sex -- like a thermostat dial for gender.

flashcards.front: How does TSD differ from GSD (Genetic Sex Determination)?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In GSD, sex is determined at fertilization by sex chromosomes (e.g., XX/XY or ZW/ZZ). In TSD, sex is determined post-fertilization by environmental temperature during a thermosensitive period of embryonic development. GSD is fixed at conception; TSD is labile and environmentally contingent.

**ELI5:** GSD is like a coin flip at conception -- heads = male, tails = female, done forever. TSD is like baking -- the oven temperature while the cake is rising decides what kind of cake you get.

flashcards.front: In leopard geckos, what is the relationship between incubation temperature and offspring sex?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Leopard geckos exhibit a male-biased sex ratio at warm incubation temperatures. Higher temperatures produce predominantly males, while cooler temperatures produce predominantly females. This is a Pattern II TSD system (males at intermediate temperatures in some classifications, but in leopard geckos specifically warm = male-biased).

**ELI5:** Turn up the heat on gecko eggs and you get more boys; keep them cool and you get more girls.

flashcards.front: What is the Charnov-Bull model of TSD, and what does it predict?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The Charnov-Bull (1977) model predicts that TSD is adaptive when incubation temperature differentially affects the fitness of males and females. If environmental conditions (temperature) correlate with sex-specific fitness -- meaning one sex benefits more from developing at a particular temperature -- then TSD will be favored over GSD.

**ELI5:** If being born in the warm season makes you a better male but a worse female, it pays to let temperature pick your sex so each kid develops under conditions that suit their gender best.

flashcards.front: Describe the Warner & Shine (2008) jacky dragon experiment and its key finding.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Warner & Shine tested the Charnov-Bull model using jacky dragons (Amphibolurus muricatus) incubated at 23C, 27C, and 33C. They used an aromatase inhibitor to override TSD and produce males at all temperatures and females at all temperatures. Males produced at warm temperatures and females produced at cool temperatures (matching natural TSD outcomes) had the highest reproductive success (RS), directly supporting the Charnov-Bull prediction.

**ELI5:** They chemically forced some lizards to be boys and others to be girls at every temperature. Boys born hot and girls born cold did best in life -- proving the natural system was getting it right all along.

flashcards.front: What role did the aromatase inhibitor play in the Warner & Shine jacky dragon experiment?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The aromatase inhibitor blocked the enzyme aromatase, which converts testosterone to estrogen and is critical for female sex determination in TSD species. By inhibiting aromatase, researchers could produce males at temperatures that would normally produce females, effectively decoupling sex from temperature and allowing a direct test of temperature-sex-fitness correlations.

**ELI5:** Aromatase is the chemical switch that says 'make this one female.' The inhibitor jammed that switch so researchers could make males at any temperature they wanted, like overriding the thermostat.

flashcards.front: What three temperatures were used in the Warner & Shine jacky dragon study, and what sex did each naturally produce?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** 23C (cool) naturally produced females, 27C (intermediate) produced a mix, and 33C (warm) naturally produced males. The experiment showed males from 33C and females from 23C had the highest lifetime reproductive success, confirming temperature-sex-fitness correlations.

**ELI5:** Cool (23C) = girls, medium (27C) = mix, hot (33C) = boys. The naturally-assigned kids outperformed the chemically-switched ones at every temperature.

flashcards.front: What is the gallfly extended phenotype example, and what type of selection does it illustrate?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Goldenrod gallflies (Eurosta solidaginis) induce galls on goldenrod plants. Gall size is an extended phenotype of the fly. Small galls are preferentially attacked by parasitoid wasps (Eurytoma gigantea), while large galls are preferentially attacked by downy woodpeckers. This dual selective pressure from two natural enemies creates stabilizing selection favoring intermediate gall sizes.

**ELI5:** The fly makes a bump on a plant to live in. Too small a bump and wasps find you; too big and birds find you. The Goldilocks-sized bump -- not too big, not too small -- is just right for survival.

flashcards.front: What is an 'extended phenotype' in the context of the gallfly?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** An extended phenotype (concept from Dawkins 1982) is a phenotypic effect of an organism's genes that extends beyond the organism's body. The gall is not part of the fly's body, but its size and structure are influenced by the fly's genotype, making the gall an extended phenotype of the gallfly subject to natural selection.

**ELI5:** Your phenotype is usually your body -- height, eye color. But the gallfly's genes also control the size of the house it builds on the plant. That house IS part of its phenotype, even though it's made of plant tissue.

flashcards.front: What is stabilizing selection, and how does the gallfly gall size example demonstrate it?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Stabilizing selection favors intermediate phenotypes and acts against extremes, reducing phenotypic variance without shifting the mean. In gallflies, wasps impose selection against small galls (directional toward larger) while birds impose selection against large galls (directional toward smaller). The combined effect is stabilizing selection maintaining intermediate gall diameter.

**ELI5:** Imagine a game where you lose points for being too tall OR too short. Over time, everyone converges toward medium height. Gallflies converge on medium gall size because both extremes get eaten.

flashcards.front: Why does climate change pose a particular threat to species with TSD?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Climate change alters incubation temperatures, potentially skewing sex ratios dramatically. If temperatures consistently exceed or fall below thresholds, populations may produce predominantly one sex, leading to demographic collapse. The rate of climate change may exceed the capacity for evolutionary rescue (shifting reaction norms). Species with long generation times (e.g., sea turtles) are especially vulnerable because they cannot adapt quickly enough.

**ELI5:** If the planet gets hotter and your baby's sex depends on temperature, you might end up with all girls and no boys (or vice versa). The species can't make babies with only one sex, so it could go extinct.

flashcards.front: How could TSD species potentially adapt to climate change? What are the limitations?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Potential adaptations include: (1) shifting nesting phenology (nesting earlier/later), (2) evolving new thermal reaction norms for sex determination, (3) changing nest-site selection to cooler/warmer microhabitats. Limitations include slow generation times in many TSD species (sea turtles: 20-30 years), limited genetic variation in threshold temperatures, and the rate of environmental change potentially exceeding adaptive capacity.

**ELI5:** Animals could move their nests to shadier spots or start nesting at different times of year. But many TSD animals like sea turtles take decades to grow up and have babies, so evolution moves way too slowly to keep up with rapid warming.

flashcards.front: What enzyme is central to TSD mechanisms in many reptiles, and what does it do?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Aromatase (CYP19A1) is the key enzyme. It converts androgens (testosterone) to estrogens (estradiol). At feminizing temperatures, aromatase expression is upregulated in the developing gonad, producing estrogens that drive ovarian differentiation. At masculinizing temperatures, aromatase expression is suppressed, allowing testicular development.

**ELI5:** Aromatase is like a converter that turns 'boy hormone' into 'girl hormone.' When eggs are at female-making temperatures, this converter runs on high. At male-making temperatures, it shuts off.

flashcards.front: What evidence would support the Charnov-Bull model for a TSD species?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Supporting evidence requires demonstrating that: (1) offspring sex varies with incubation temperature, (2) developmental temperature affects sex-specific fitness differently (males do better at one temperature, females at another), and (3) the natural TSD pattern matches the sex to the temperature that maximizes its fitness. Ideally, experiments decouple sex from temperature (e.g., hormone manipulation) to test fitness at mismatched temperature-sex combinations.

**ELI5:** You need to show three things: temperature picks sex, temperature affects how well each sex does in life differently, and the natural system assigns each sex to the temperature where it thrives. The jacky dragon experiment nailed all three.

flashcards.front: If only one species in an interaction is evolving in response to the other, is that coevolution? Relate this to TSD.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** No -- that is adaptation, not coevolution. Coevolution requires reciprocal evolutionary change in both interacting species. In the gallfly example, flies evolve gall size in response to wasps and birds, but this is adaptation to predation pressure. True coevolution would require wasps/birds to evolve counter-adaptations to gall size changes, creating an arms race.

**ELI5:** Coevolution is like two tennis players improving because of each other. If only one player gets better while the other stays the same, that's just practice (adaptation), not a rivalry (coevolution).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Pattern I and Pattern II TSD?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Pattern I (TSD Ia): females produced at high temperatures, males at low temperatures (e.g., many turtles). Pattern II (TSD II): males produced at intermediate temperatures, females at both extremes (e.g., some crocodilians, some lizards). Some classifications include TSD Ib (males at high, females at low). The leopard gecko pattern (male-biased warm) fits a variant classification.

**ELI5:** Pattern I: hot = girls, cold = boys (like sea turtles). Pattern II: medium = boys, extremes = girls (like some crocs). Different species wired the thermostat differently.

flashcards.front: Why is the gallfly gall example relevant to a lecture on TSD?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Both examples illustrate how environmental factors shape phenotypic outcomes and fitness through selection. The gallfly demonstrates stabilizing selection on an extended phenotype driven by multiple selective agents, while TSD demonstrates how environmental conditions (temperature) can be co-opted as a sex-determination mechanism when they correlate with sex-specific fitness. Both illustrate organism-environment interactions driving adaptation.

**ELI5:** Both stories are about the environment shaping what organisms become. Temperature shapes whether a lizard is male or female; the combined pressure of wasps and birds shapes how big a fly's home grows. Nature's context matters enormously.

nodes.title: Fitness Landscapes
nodes.subtitle: Wright's metaphor for evolutionary dynamics (Lec 7 slides 23-26)

popup.heading: Lecture 7 — Fitness Landscape

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Introduced by Sewall Wright in 1932. A fitness landscape is a metaphorical surface where X and Y axes represent genotypes or trait values, and Z (height) represents fitness. Populations evolve by climbing up the landscape.

sections.label: DYNAMICS
sections.body: ADAPTIVE PEAKS are high-fitness genotype combinations. VALLEYS are low-fitness regions between peaks. Natural selection moves populations locally UPHILL and can trap them on suboptimal local peaks. GENETIC DRIFT (or gene flow) can randomly push a population across a valley to reach a higher peak. EPISTASIS creates RUGGEDNESS in the landscape.

sections.label: EXAMPLES
sections.body: Northwestern garter snakes: different morphology × behavior combinations form distinct peaks. Gallfly gall-size evolution: multivariate selection with OPPOSING agents (wasps vs birds) creates a stabilizing peak between two valleys.

sections.label: KEY FIGURE
sections.body: Sewall Wright (1932) — introduced the fitness landscape metaphor and later the shifting balance theory.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body: HIGH-DIMENSIONAL LANDSCAPES & NEUTRAL THEORY: Real fitness landscapes are >10,000-dimensional (one axis per segregating locus). In high-dimensional space, most local peaks visible in 2D projections are actually connected through ridges or neutral networks — paths of equal fitness that allow populations to traverse landscapes without crossing deep valleys. Neutral theory (Kimura, 1968) proposes that most molecular evolution occurs through neutral mutations fixed by genetic drift rather than natural selection. In high-dimensional fitness landscapes, neutral networks connect genotypes of equal fitness, enabling populations to explore sequence space through drift alone, potentially reaching new adaptive peaks without requiring selection to cross fitness valleys.

examples: Sewall Wright (1932): fitness landscape — a metaphorical surface where x,y axes are genotypes/traits and z (height) is fitness.
Adaptive peaks: high-fitness genotype combinations.
Valleys: low-fitness genotype combinations between peaks.
Populations tend to climb local peaks via natural selection, but may get stuck on suboptimal peaks (can't cross valleys).
Drift or gene flow can help populations cross valleys and reach higher peaks.
Northwestern garter snake morphology × behavior combinations produce distinct peaks.
Gallfly gall-size: wasp + bird selection create multivariate stabilizing peak.
warnings: WATCH OUT: The fitness landscape metaphor is a SIMPLIFICATION — real genotype space is thousands-dimensional, not 2D or 3D.
WATCH OUT: Fitness landscapes CHANGE over time when environments change — a local peak in one environment may be a valley in another.
popup.mnemonic: Peaks & Valleys: Selection → local peak; Drift/flow → cross valley to higher peak.

flashcard.front: What is a fitness landscape and why can selection alone get "stuck" on a local peak?
flashcard.back: A FITNESS LANDSCAPE (Wright 1932) is a metaphorical surface where axes represent allele combinations (or trait values) and the height at each point is the fitness of that combination. Populations evolve by "climbing" up the landscape — selection pushes them toward higher fitness. However, natural selection only moves a population LOCALLY UPHILL. If a population reaches a LOCAL PEAK, any small change reduces fitness — selection prevents the population from leaving. To reach a HIGHER peak across a valley, the population must temporarily pass through lower-fitness states, which selection opposes. GENETIC DRIFT or GENE FLOW can help populations cross valleys by randomly moving them off the current peak — a classic example of how drift and flow can actually accelerate adaptation under some conditions.

quiz.question: A population is stuck on a local fitness peak while a much higher peak exists across a fitness valley. Which process is most likely to help the population move to the higher peak?
quiz.correct: Genetic drift temporarily pushing the population off the local peak
distractors: Stronger stabilizing selection
Elimination of all mutations
Reduced population size to zero

quiz.question: Sewall Wright proposed that large populations are subdivided into small semi-isolated demes to facilitate crossing fitness valleys. What is his "shifting balance theory" and what is its main critique?
quiz.correct: Wright proposed that small demes drift off local peaks → selection then drives them to higher peaks → gene flow then spreads the superior genotype to the whole population. Critics argue the gene-flow phase is theoretically unlikely because migrants from the high peak would swamp the deme back to the local peak before spreading the beneficial combination
distractors: Wright proposed that all evolution occurs in large homogeneous populations via directional selection alone — drift is too random to produce adaptive change and is only relevant in artificially small laboratory populations

Wright's theory proposed that fitness valleys do not actually exist — the apparent valleys in 2D representations disappear in high-dimensional genotype space where all genetic combinations can be connected by paths of equal fitness

Wright's shifting balance theory was confirmed by the Drosophila bw75 experiment: Buri showed that small demes shift allele frequencies faster than large populations, with the shifted demes then exporting their alleles to neighboring populations via gene flow

quiz.question: A fitness landscape metaphor describes evolution as "hill-climbing." In which scenario does this metaphor BREAK DOWN most seriously?
quiz.correct: When the environment changes, the landscape itself shifts — a genotype sitting on a high peak in last year's environment may be in a valley this year. Directional environmental change means populations must track a moving target, and the hill-climbing metaphor implies a static landscape
distractors: When mutation rates are very high — many simultaneous mutations prevent populations from moving in a single direction, and the hill-climbing metaphor fails because populations jump randomly across the landscape rather than climbing steadily

When populations are very large — in populations of millions, all possible genotypes are present simultaneously, so the concept of a single population "position" on the landscape is meaningless and hill-climbing does not apply

When selection coefficients are greater than 0.1 — strong selection causes populations to move faster than the landscape can accommodate, so populations overshoot fitness peaks and oscillate without ever stabilizing at the optimum

quiz.question: The fitness landscape is described as >10,000-dimensional in reality (one axis per segregating locus). If this is true, what important consequence does it have for interpreting real evolutionary trajectories?
quiz.correct: In high-dimensional space, most local peaks in 2D are connected through ridges of equal or higher fitness in other dimensions — populations may not be as "trapped" as the 2D metaphor implies, because alternative evolutionary paths exist that avoid fitness valleys by moving through other dimensions
distractors: High dimensionality means natural selection is impossible in real organisms — with more than 100 loci under selection simultaneously, fitness interactions are too complex for selection to maintain any coherent direction across generations

High dimensionality proves that the neutral theory of molecular evolution is correct — in >1000-dimensional space, most mutations are equally neutral and drift, not selection, explains the majority of evolutionary change

High-dimensional fitness landscapes are identical to 2D landscapes for practical purposes because all organisms with more than 10 chromosomes experience independent assortment, which effectively collapses the high-dimensional space into 23 independent 1D selection problems

quiz.question: Who introduced the fitness landscape metaphor in 1932?
quiz.correct: Sewall Wright — his landscape concept visualized evolutionary dynamics as populations climbing a surface defined by genotype-fitness relationships
distractors: Charles Darwin — in his original Origin of Species
Ronald Fisher — in his Fundamental Theorem of Natural Selection
J.B.S. Haldane — in his population genetics work

quiz.question: In Wright's fitness landscape metaphor, what do the AXES and HEIGHT represent?
quiz.correct: The horizontal axes represent genotypes or trait values (allele combinations), and the height (z-axis) represents fitness — so a point on the landscape corresponds to a specific genotype's expected fitness
distractors: The axes represent time and population size, while height represents genetic variation
The axes represent latitude and longitude, while height represents species abundance
The axes represent mutation rate and gene flow, while height represents the number of individuals

quiz.question: Why does natural selection alone tend to get a population stuck on a local peak?
quiz.correct: Selection moves populations only LOCALLY UPHILL — any small change that reduces fitness is opposed, so a population at a local peak cannot cross a fitness valley even if a higher peak exists on the other side
distractors: Selection actively prevents populations from reaching any peaks, always pushing them toward the valleys
Selection randomly chooses between peaks regardless of their height
Selection moves populations downhill to conserve energy

quiz.question: Which evolutionary forces can help a population CROSS a fitness valley to reach a higher peak?
quiz.correct: Genetic drift (especially in small populations) or gene flow can randomly push a population off its current peak, providing the opportunity to reach higher peaks that selection alone could not access
distractors: Only stronger natural selection can help a population escape a local peak
Only Lamarckian inheritance can bridge fitness valleys
Only mutation can move populations across valleys, and only when mutation rates exceed 10% per generation

quiz.question: Why can fitness landscapes CHANGE over time?
quiz.correct: When environments change, the fitness of each genotype changes — a combination that was a high peak in one environment may become a valley in another. The landscape itself is not static; it shifts as conditions change, re-starting the evolutionary climb
distractors: Fitness landscapes never change — they are fixed by the laws of physics and biochemistry
Landscapes change only when mutation rates increase, producing new peaks
Landscapes change only during speciation events

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: In what year did Sewall Wright introduce the fitness landscape metaphor?
distractors: 1859 (Darwin Origin)
1900 (Mendel rediscovery)
1959 (Modern Synthesis)

quiz.question: L3 COMPARISON: What does EPISTASIS contribute to the structure of a fitness landscape?
quiz.correct: Epistasis makes the landscape RUGGED — interactions between loci create multiple peaks and valleys instead of a single smooth surface, which is why selection can get stuck on local optima
distractors: Epistasis smooths the landscape by averaging fitness across loci
Epistasis eliminates all peaks, producing a flat surface
Epistasis creates only one global peak and no local peaks

visual.mnemonic: Selection is blind to height beyond its immediate neighborhood.
visual.trap: Real fitness landscapes are high-dimensional (thousands of genes). 2D visualizations are radical simplifications.

diagram.title: Wright 1932 Adaptive Landscape — Peaks, Valleys, and Shifting Balance

nodes.label: Low fitness valley
nodes.detail: A low point on the fitness surface: genotype combinations with poor reproductive success. In Wright’s (1932) framing, every point in genotype space has an associated mean fitness; valleys are low-fitness regions bounded by higher ground on all sides. Populations trapped in valleys cannot climb out by selection alone.
nodes.mnemonic: Valley = mean fitness minimum.
nodes.watchOut: Selection pushes populations uphill locally — valleys are barriers, not destinations.

nodes.label: Local adaptive peak
nodes.detail: A region where any small genetic change lowers fitness — a local optimum. Populations on a local peak are stable under selection even though better peaks exist elsewhere in genotype space. Sewall Wright’s central question (1932 Sixth International Congress of Genetics talk) was how populations ever escape such local peaks.
nodes.mnemonic: Local peak = any step downhill.
nodes.watchOut: Selection cannot distinguish local from global peaks — it just climbs whatever slope it sits on.

nodes.label: Global/higher peak
nodes.detail: A taller region of the landscape with higher mean fitness than the local peak. To reach it, a population must CROSS the fitness valley separating the two peaks — a feat impossible for selection acting alone. Wright argued a subdivided population structure (many small demes connected by limited gene flow) is the ideal architecture for exploring multiple peaks.
nodes.mnemonic: Global peak = the best attainable fitness combination.
nodes.watchOut: "Global" is only global within the landscape you’ve modeled — real landscapes are high-dimensional.

nodes.label: Selection climbs local peak
nodes.detail: Natural selection acts as a hill-climbing algorithm: each generation, the population shifts toward higher fitness by fixing beneficial alleles. The climb is always LOCAL — selection has no look-ahead and cannot see over the ridge into a valley.
nodes.mnemonic: Selection is a greedy uphill algorithm.
nodes.watchOut: Selection cannot see beyond adjacent genotypes — it is blind to distant peaks.

nodes.label: Drift can cross valley
nodes.detail: In small populations, random genetic drift can push allele frequencies temporarily DOWNHILL, allowing the population to traverse a fitness valley and enter the basin of attraction of a higher peak. Wright’s Shifting Balance Theory (1932) proposed exactly this: drift crosses valleys, selection climbs peaks, gene flow spreads successful genotypes.
nodes.mnemonic: Drift crosses valleys because it ignores fitness.
nodes.watchOut: Selection alone cannot cross fitness valleys — drift is required in small populations. Wright’s shifting balance requires ALL three forces.

nodes.label: Gene flow spreads successful genotypes
nodes.detail: Once a sub-population drifts across a valley and selection fixes a higher-peak genotype, migration exports the successful allele combination to neighboring demes. The whole metapopulation can then shift toward the higher peak. This is the third leg of Wright’s Shifting Balance Theory.
nodes.mnemonic: Gene flow distributes peak discoveries across the metapopulation.
nodes.watchOut: Too much gene flow swamps local adaptation; too little prevents spread.

nodes.label: Epistasis creates rugged landscapes
nodes.detail: When alleles at one locus modify the fitness effects of alleles at others, single-locus optima no longer suffice — the landscape becomes rugged with many local peaks. Epistasis was Wright’s (1932) main motivation for treating fitness as a multi-locus surface rather than an additive sum of allele effects.
nodes.mnemonic: Epistasis turns smooth hills into jagged terrain.
nodes.watchOut: Epistasis is invisible if you measure single-locus effects in isolation.

nodes.label: Gallfly: multivariate selection
nodes.detail: Goldenrod (Solidago altissima) gall flies (Eurosta solidaginis) face opposing selective pressures on gall size: parasitoid wasps (Eurytoma gigantea) favor large galls (can’t enter small ones) while downy woodpeckers favor small galls (can’t see small ones) → stabilizing selection at intermediate gall size (Weis & Abrahamson 1986; Weis et al. 1992). A real multivariate fitness peak documented in the field, similar to the garter snake color × antipredator behavior example from the Pacific Northwest that Wright-style landscapes predict.
nodes.mnemonic: Big galls die to wasps; small galls die to birds; intermediate survives.
nodes.watchOut: Stabilizing selection carves a single peak; it does not create multiple peaks.

diagram.mnemonic: Peaks & Valleys: Selection → local peak; Drift/flow → cross valley to higher peak.

flashcards.front: What is a fitness landscape, and who introduced the concept?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A fitness landscape, introduced by Sewall Wright in 1932, is a conceptual/mathematical model where the X and Y axes represent genotypic or phenotypic dimensions (allele frequencies, trait values) and the Z axis represents fitness (mean population fitness or individual fitness). Populations are visualized as occupying positions on this surface, with natural selection driving them uphill toward adaptive peaks.

**ELI5:** Imagine a hilly terrain where height = how well you survive. Your genes determine where you stand on the map. Evolution is like water flowing uphill (weird, but true here) -- populations climb toward the hilltops where fitness is highest.

flashcards.front: What are adaptive peaks and valleys in a fitness landscape?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Adaptive peaks are local fitness maxima -- genotype/phenotype combinations with higher fitness than neighboring combinations. Valleys are fitness minima separating peaks. A population at a peak cannot move to a higher peak via natural selection alone because doing so would require passing through lower-fitness intermediate states (the valley), which selection opposes.

**ELI5:** Peaks are the hilltops where life is good. Valleys are the low spots between hills where life is bad. Once you climb a hill, you're stuck -- you'd have to go downhill (get worse) before you could climb a taller hill.

flashcards.front: Why can natural selection trap a population on a local adaptive peak?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Natural selection is a hill-climbing algorithm -- it can only increase fitness in the short term. If a population sits on a local peak (a fitness maximum that is not the global maximum), selection will resist any movement away because all neighboring genotypes have lower fitness. There is no selective path through the fitness valley to reach a higher peak. Selection is 'myopic' -- it cannot plan ahead or sacrifice short-term fitness for long-term gain.

**ELI5:** Natural selection is like a blindfolded hiker who only steps uphill. Once you reach the top of a small hill, every direction is down, so you stop -- even if there's a mountain nearby. You can't see it, and you refuse to go downhill to get there.

flashcards.front: How can genetic drift help populations cross fitness valleys?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Genetic drift, being random and not dependent on fitness, can move a population away from a local peak into a valley by chance. In small populations, drift is strong enough to overcome selection and push the population's allele frequencies through low-fitness intermediate states. Once across the valley, natural selection can then drive the population uphill to a new (potentially higher) peak. This is Wright's shifting balance theory.

**ELI5:** Drift is like a random gust of wind that can blow you off your small hill. If the wind happens to blow you toward a bigger hill and you land on its slope, you can then climb to a higher peak. Randomness occasionally helps by doing what careful planning cannot.

flashcards.front: How can gene flow help populations navigate fitness landscapes?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Gene flow introduces alleles from populations that may occupy different adaptive peaks. This influx of genetic variation can push a population off its local peak into a valley, potentially allowing it to climb a different (possibly higher) peak. Gene flow disrupts local adaptation but can facilitate exploration of the fitness landscape by increasing genetic diversity and connecting otherwise isolated evolutionary trajectories.

**ELI5:** Immigrants bring new genes from a different hilltop. Mixing those genes in can knock your population off its current hill, but you might land near a taller one and start climbing.

flashcards.front: How does epistasis affect the shape of a fitness landscape?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Epistasis (gene-gene interactions where the fitness effect of one allele depends on alleles at other loci) creates ruggedness in the fitness landscape -- many peaks and valleys of varying heights rather than a single smooth peak. Strong epistasis means that beneficial mutations at one locus may be deleterious in a different genetic background, creating multiple local optima. Without epistasis, the landscape would be smooth with a single global peak (Mt. Fuji landscape).

**ELI5:** Epistasis means genes affect each other -- gene A might be helpful only if you also have gene B. This creates a bumpy, complicated terrain with lots of little hills and valleys instead of one big smooth mountain.

flashcards.front: What is the garter snake morphology x behavior example on fitness landscapes?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Garter snakes demonstrate that morphology and behavior interact on a multivariate fitness landscape. Striped snakes that flee in a straight line (making the stripe a motion-camouflage illusion) have high fitness. Blotched/spotted snakes that freeze and rely on crypsis also have high fitness. But mismatched combinations (striped + freeze, or blotched + flee) have low fitness. This creates two adaptive peaks with a valley between them.

**ELI5:** Stripy snakes that run straight survive (the stripes blur and hide them). Spotty snakes that hold still survive (they blend in). But a stripy snake that freezes or a spotty snake that runs is easy prey. Two winning strategies, and mixing them up is losing.

flashcards.front: How does the gallfly example illustrate a multivariate fitness landscape?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Gallfly fitness depends on multiple traits simultaneously -- gall size interacts with other traits (gall shape, wall thickness, position on plant) to determine survival against multiple selective agents (wasps, birds). The fitness landscape is multivariate, with the optimal phenotype representing a peak in multi-dimensional trait space where the balance of selection pressures is resolved.

**ELI5:** The fly's survival depends on several things at once -- not just how big the gall is, but how thick, what shape, where on the plant. It's like needing the right combination on a multi-dial lock, not just getting one number right.

flashcards.front: What happens to a fitness landscape when the environment changes?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Environmental change alters the fitness function, reshaping the landscape. Peaks can become valleys, valleys can become peaks, and new peaks can emerge. A population previously at an adaptive peak may suddenly find itself in a valley or on the slope of a different peak. This means populations must continuously track shifting optima, and what was once an optimal genotype may become suboptimal or maladaptive.

**ELI5:** Imagine the hills and valleys constantly rearranging like a living terrain. The hilltop you stood on sinks, and a new hill pops up elsewhere. You have to keep chasing moving targets.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a 'smooth' (Fujiyama) and a 'rugged' fitness landscape?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A smooth/Fujiyama landscape has a single global peak with a monotonically increasing fitness surface leading to it -- any uphill path leads to the optimum. There is no risk of being trapped on local peaks. A rugged landscape has multiple peaks and valleys due to epistasis, frequency-dependence, or environmental heterogeneity. Populations can get stuck on suboptimal local peaks. Most real biological landscapes are rugged.

**ELI5:** Smooth landscape = one big mountain, any path up gets you to the top. Rugged landscape = a mountain range with dozens of peaks -- you might get stuck on a foothill when the real summit is across a deep valley.

flashcards.front: In Wright's shifting balance theory, what three phases allow populations to explore fitness landscapes?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Phase 1: Random genetic drift in small subpopulations moves them off local peaks and through fitness valleys. Phase 2: Natural selection drives subpopulations that have crossed a valley up the slope of a new (potentially higher) peak. Phase 3: Interdeme selection -- subpopulations on higher peaks grow faster and export migrants, spreading their superior genotypes to other demes via gene flow.

**ELI5:** Step 1: Random luck bumps small groups off their little hills. Step 2: If they land near a bigger hill, natural selection pushes them up. Step 3: The groups on the tallest hills grow biggest and spread their winning genes to neighbors.

flashcards.front: Why is the concept of fitness landscapes useful despite being a simplification?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Fitness landscapes provide intuitive visualization of key evolutionary concepts: adaptive peaks, evolutionary constraints, the roles of selection vs. drift vs. gene flow, effects of epistasis, and why evolution doesn't always reach global optima. They help explain why organisms appear 'imperfectly adapted' and why evolutionary outcomes are historically contingent. The simplification (low-dimensional representation of high-dimensional reality) is acknowledged but the conceptual framework remains powerful.

**ELI5:** It's a useful mental picture even though real evolution happens in millions of dimensions we can't draw. It helps explain why nature often seems 'good enough' rather than perfect -- populations get stuck on local hills.

flashcards.front: How do the axes of a fitness landscape differ between genotype space and phenotype space representations?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In genotype space, axes represent allele frequencies or specific genotypic states -- each point is a different genetic combination. In phenotype space, axes represent measurable trait values (body size, beak depth, etc.). Genotype-space landscapes are discrete and very high-dimensional. Phenotype-space landscapes are continuous and may show different topography because the genotype-phenotype map is many-to-one (many genotypes can produce similar phenotypes).

**ELI5:** Genotype space: axes are gene versions (which DNA letters you have). Phenotype space: axes are body features (how tall, how fast). Same idea, different maps -- like plotting a city by street addresses vs. by GPS coordinates.

nodes.title: Adaptations: Trait vs Process
nodes.subtitle: Defining adaptation and novel traits (Lec 8 slides 1-6)

popup.heading: Lecture 8 — Complex Adaptations

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Adaptation has TWO meanings: (1) as a TRAIT — a heritable, inherited aspect of an organism that increases its competitive fitness; (2) as a PROCESS — the ongoing evolutionary change produced by natural selection that generates fitness-enhancing traits. NOVEL TRAITS arise de novo (eyes, limbs, wings, teeth) — they did not exist in the ancestor in their current form. A COMPLEX ADAPTATION is a suite of co-expressed traits working together (e.g., camera eye: cornea + lens + retina + optic nerve). Not every trait is adaptive: SPANDRELS (Gould & Lewontin 1979) are byproducts of other adaptations, VESTIGIAL structures are reduced remnants of once-functional ancestral traits, and NEUTRAL traits have no fitness effect. Adaptation is always relative to a specific environment — sickle cell is adaptive in malaria zones but deleterious elsewhere.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body:
• Explain how mutations in a regulatory network may affect development
• Explain how changes in the timing and location of the expression of developmental genes can affect the appearance of complex traits
• All organisms are adapted to the environment they live in
• There are no ‘higher’ or ‘lower’ species
• Adaptation: viewed as both a trait and a process
• Inherited aspects of an individual that allow it to outcompete other members of a population that lack the trait (or have a different variant of the trait)

sections.label: VESTIGIAL ORGANS
sections.body: CASE STUDY — CAVE FISH: Cave-dwelling fish (e.g., Astyanax mexicanus) illustrate multiple adaptation categories simultaneously. Reduced eyes = vestigial (ancestral functional structure now diminished because selection maintaining eyes was relaxed in total darkness). Elongated tactile barbels = adaptation (enhanced touch sensitivity currently maintained by selection for navigating dark environments). Pale/depigmented skin may be vestigial (loss of protective melanin no longer needed without UV) or a byproduct of developmental changes in neural crest cells.

examples: Adaptation as TRAIT: a heritable feature that increases fitness in a particular environment.
Adaptation as PROCESS: the ongoing evolutionary change driven by natural selection.
Novel traits: arise de novo (not inherited) — e.g., limbs in tetrapods, wings in birds.
Complex traits: require coordination of multiple genes and developmental modules.
warnings: Not every trait is an adaptation — spandrels, vestigial, neutral traits exist. Gould & Lewontin (1979) warned against the "adaptationist programme" of assuming every feature is adaptive without evidence.
Adaptation is always relative to an environment. Sickle cell in malaria-free regions is deleterious — a trait adaptive in one environment can be neutral or harmful in another.
popup.mnemonic: Trait vs Process — "adaptation" carries both meanings. Always specify which.

flashcard.front: What is the difference between "adaptation" as a trait and "adaptation" as a process?
flashcard.back: As a TRAIT (noun): an adaptation is a specific heritable feature shaped by past selection that increases fitness — e.g., the woodpecker's long tongue, the giraffe's neck, antifreeze proteins in Antarctic fish. As a PROCESS (verb): adaptation is the ongoing evolutionary mechanism by which populations become better matched to their environment through natural selection. These two meanings are related but distinct: the process produces the traits. In scientific papers, careful writers specify which they mean.

quiz.question: Which statement best describes a "novel trait" in evolution?
quiz.correct: A trait that arises de novo and is not directly inherited from an ancestor
distractors: Any point mutation that appears in a germ cell, regardless of phenotypic effect
A trait that results from phenotypic plasticity in response to a new environment
A trait shared by two distantly related species due to convergent evolution

quiz.question: A biologist observes that humans have a blind spot in their retina but octopuses do not, and that the human spine causes back pain during bipedal walking. She argues these are evidence for evolution rather than intelligent design. Which reasoning is MOST correct?
quiz.correct: These are "imperfect" designs that only make sense as evolutionary compromises — the vertebrate blind spot is an ancestral consequence of inverted photoreceptors in early vertebrates, and the spine was shaped by selection for quadrupedal locomotion before bipedalism, not engineered for walking upright
distractors: These flaws disprove natural selection because selection should have eliminated suboptimal traits — if selection were real, the blind spot and back pain would have been corrected millions of years ago

These flaws support creationism — an intelligent designer would intentionally include imperfections to allow the organism to adapt through learned behavior rather than relying entirely on biological structure

These features are actually optimal designs — the blind spot provides a useful location for the optic nerve to exit without vision loss, and lumbar curvature in the spine is a biomechanical advantage for absorbing impact during walking

quiz.question: Antagonistic pleiotropy proposes that some genes beneficial early in life are maintained despite causing harm later. Which specific example from human biology BEST illustrates this principle?
quiz.correct: APOE ε4 allele — associated with faster brain development and potentially better immune function in youth, but dramatically increases Alzheimer's risk after age 60; selection on early-life benefits maintained this allele despite its late-life cost
distractors: The CCR5-Δ32 mutation — this allele confers resistance to HIV infection in homozygotes, conferring a survival benefit throughout life; it is a simple case of positive selection rather than antagonistic pleiotropy because no late-life cost is known

Sickle cell heterozygosity — HbA/HbS individuals have malaria resistance early in life but suffer reduced oxygen transport in old age, which is a textbook example of antagonistic pleiotropy because the cost appears decades after the benefit

The BRCA1 wildtype allele — maintains functional DNA repair throughout life and protects against cancer in youth, but becomes a liability in post-reproductive age when damaged alleles accumulate; this is pure mutation accumulation, not antagonistic pleiotropy

quiz.question: A researcher says that sickle cell trait (heterozygous HbA/HbS) in malaria-endemic regions is NOT an adaptation in the strict evolutionary sense. What would be the most rigorous response?
quiz.correct: Sickle cell heterozygosity IS an adaptation — it is a heritable trait (specific allele) that increases fitness (malaria resistance) in a specific environment (high malaria burden), even though HbS causes disease in homozygotes; the distinction is that adaptation is always environment-specific
distractors: The researcher is correct — adaptation requires that the trait was directly shaped BY natural selection FOR malaria resistance. Since the HbS allele arose from a sickle-cell anemia mutation, it was selected against, not for; the malaria resistance is a coincidental byproduct

Sickle cell trait is a SPANDREL, not an adaptation — the malaria protection is an incidental byproduct of reduced red blood cell rigidity that evolved for other reasons, similar to how the space between arches in a building serves a purpose not intended by the architect

Sickle cell trait cannot be an adaptation because it causes disease — by definition, adaptations only have positive fitness effects; any trait that causes harm in any genotype is classified as a deleterious mutation maintained by mutation-selection balance

quiz.question: Which of the following traits would most likely be classified as a SPANDREL rather than an adaptation?
quiz.correct: The reddish color of vertebrate blood — hemoglobin evolved for oxygen transport (the adaptation), and its red color is an incidental chemical byproduct that was not itself selected for
distractors: The camouflage coloration of a stick insect — cryptic coloration clearly provides a direct fitness advantage through reduced predation
The thick fur of an arctic fox — insulation is a direct thermoregulatory adaptation shaped by cold-climate selection
The echolocation ability of bats — a specialized sensory system shaped by selection for nocturnal foraging

quiz.question: A trait is described as "vestigial." Which statement BEST captures what vestigial means in evolutionary biology?
quiz.correct: A vestigial trait is a reduced remnant of a structure that was functional in an ancestor but has lost most or all of its original function — e.g., the human appendix, whale pelvic bones, or cave fish eyes
distractors: Vestigial traits are entirely useless and provide no function whatsoever — the moment a structure retains any function, it is no longer vestigial by definition
Vestigial traits are new structures that have only recently begun to evolve and have not yet reached their final adaptive form
Vestigial traits are traits found only in one sex of a sexually dimorphic species, reflecting the ancestral state of the opposite sex

quiz.question: A novel trait in tetrapods is the limb. Which statement MOST accurately describes what "novel" means in this context?
quiz.correct: The tetrapod limb arose from the modification of ancestral fish fin structures — "novel" means the trait as currently constituted did not exist before, even though it evolved from pre-existing parts
distractors: Novel means "arose from scratch with no ancestral precursor" — tetrapod limbs have no evolutionary ancestry and first appeared in the fossil record without transitional forms
Novel means "unique to one species" — a trait found in multiple species cannot be novel because true novelty requires species-specific origin
Novel means "beneficial" — any trait that increases fitness is by definition novel, regardless of its evolutionary ancestry

quiz.question: A student confuses "adaptation" with "acclimation." Which statement correctly distinguishes these two concepts?
quiz.correct: Adaptation is heritable genetic change across generations driven by selection; acclimation is reversible phenotypic change within an individual's lifetime (e.g., tanning, altitude acclimatization) that is NOT heritable
distractors: Adaptation and acclimation are the same process at different timescales — both involve genetic change, but adaptation happens faster
Acclimation is heritable and adaptation is not — acclimated phenotypes pass to offspring via Lamarckian inheritance
Adaptation occurs only in animals while acclimation occurs only in plants — the distinction is taxonomic rather than mechanistic

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: Who coined the term "spandrel" in the evolutionary biology context, and in what landmark paper?
quiz.correct: Stephen Jay Gould and Richard Lewontin, in their 1979 paper "The Spandrels of San Marco and the Panglossian Paradigm" — they used architectural spandrels as a metaphor for non-adaptive byproducts
distractors: Charles Darwin, in "On the Origin of Species" (1859) — Darwin introduced spandrels as an alternative to his own theory of natural selection
Ernst Mayr, in "Systematics and the Origin of Species" (1942) — Mayr used spandrels to describe speciation byproducts
Richard Dawkins, in "The Selfish Gene" (1976) — Dawkins defined spandrels as gene-level byproducts

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: A newly discovered cave-dwelling fish has (1) reduced eyes, (2) elongated tactile barbels, (3) pale body color, and (4) high metabolic efficiency. Classify each trait as adaptation, vestigial, spandrel, or neutral, and defend your reasoning using the principles of this lecture.
quiz.correct: Reduced eyes = VESTIGIAL (ancestral functional structure now diminished because selection for vision relaxed in darkness); elongated barbels = ADAPTATION (enhanced tactile sensing directly increases fitness in sensory-limited environment); pale body = likely NEUTRAL or vestigial byproduct of relaxed selection on pigmentation; high metabolic efficiency = ADAPTATION to low-food cave ecology. Each classification depends on whether the trait is currently maintained by selection, is a constrained byproduct, or is a remnant of past function
distractors: All four are adaptations to cave life because any trait found in a cave species must have been selected for by the cave environment — the cave is the selective regime and every derived trait is an adaptive response
All four are vestigial because cave environments reduce selection pressure on all traits — all modifications in cave animals represent loss of ancestral function

All four are spandrels because the fish's basic body plan was set by its ancestor and any modifications since then are mere byproducts of a single master regulatory gene change that coincidentally produces all four changes together

quiz.question: L3 APPLICATION: A trait is labeled "exaptation" (Gould & Vrba 1982). Which example BEST illustrates exaptation as opposed to adaptation?
quiz.correct: Bird feathers were originally evolved for thermoregulation or display in ancestral theropods and were later co-opted for flight — the trait evolved for one function and acquired a new function without being originally selected for flight
distractors: The human hand evolved directly for tool use — the grasp is an adaptation, not an exaptation, because tools were the selective agent from the very beginning of hominid hand evolution
The giraffe neck evolved for reaching high foliage — it is an adaptation because its current function (feeding) matches its original function
The cheetah's fast running muscles evolved for chasing prey — this is a pure adaptation because the current use (running down prey) matches the selective environment that shaped it

quiz.question: L4 ANALYSIS: A researcher claims a particular bird song feature is an adaptation for mate attraction. What evidence would MOST RIGOROUSLY support this adaptive hypothesis rather than alternatives (spandrel, drift, developmental byproduct)?
quiz.correct: Demonstrating heritability of the trait, showing that individuals with the trait have higher reproductive success, confirming that the trait affects mate choice in controlled playback experiments, and ruling out correlation with non-reproductive fitness components — together establishing that the trait was shaped by directional selection FOR mate attraction specifically
distractors: Showing that the trait is present in many related species — widespread presence proves adaptive function
Showing that the trait is complex — complexity alone rules out drift and byproduct explanations
Showing that the trait differs between populations — any between-population variation proves the trait is adaptive because drift would produce uniform traits

visual.mnemonic: Trait = what. Process = how.
visual.trap: Not every trait is an adaptation. Some are byproducts of other adaptations, some are vestigial, some are selectively neutral.

flashcards.front: What are the two meanings of 'adaptation' in evolutionary biology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Adaptation as a trait (noun): A heritable phenotypic feature that increases the fitness of its bearer relative to individuals lacking the trait, shaped by natural selection. (2) Adaptation as a process (verb): The evolutionary process by which natural selection generates and refines traits that improve fitness in a given environment. The trait definition is static; the process definition is dynamic.

**ELI5:** 'Adaptation' means two things: (1) a useful feature an organism has (like a polar bear's thick fur), and (2) the process of evolving that feature over generations. It's both the finished product and the factory that made it.

flashcards.front: What criteria must a trait meet to be considered an adaptation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A trait must be: (1) heritable -- genetically transmitted across generations, (2) the product of natural selection -- not drift, mutation, or other non-selective forces, and (3) associated with increased fitness (survival and/or reproductive success) relative to alternative trait variants. Additionally, the trait must have been shaped by selection for its current function (not incidentally beneficial).

**ELI5:** To count as an adaptation, a trait must: be passed from parents to kids through genes, have been built by natural selection (not luck), and actually help the organism survive or reproduce better than the alternatives.

flashcards.front: What is a spandrel in evolutionary biology, and who introduced the concept?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Gould & Lewontin (1979) introduced the concept of spandrels (borrowing from architecture) -- traits that are byproducts of selection on other traits rather than direct targets of selection themselves. In architecture, spandrels are the triangular spaces between arches, not designed as features but inevitable geometric consequences of arch design. Similarly, biological spandrels are phenotypic features that arise as side effects of developmental or structural constraints, not because selection favored them directly.

**ELI5:** When you build arches, you automatically get triangular spaces between them -- nobody designed those spaces, they're just leftovers. Some biological traits are like that: byproducts of building other things, not things evolution specifically aimed for.

flashcards.front: Give an example of a biological spandrel.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The human chin is often cited as a possible spandrel -- it may not have been directly selected for but instead arose as a byproduct of differential growth rates of two bone fields in the mandible. The redness of blood is another example: it's a consequence of hemoglobin's chemistry, not a trait selected for its color. The belly button is a developmental byproduct of the umbilical cord attachment.

**ELI5:** Your belly button isn't 'designed' by evolution to do anything -- it's just a leftover scar from where the umbilical cord was. It came along for free as a side effect of how mammals develop.

flashcards.front: What are vestigial structures, and how do they relate to adaptation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Vestigial structures are reduced or rudimentary features that were functional adaptations in ancestors but have lost most or all of their original function in the descendant lineage due to changed selective pressures. Examples: human appendix (reduced cecum), whale pelvic bones (reduced hindlimbs), flightless bird wings. They demonstrate that adaptations are environment-specific and can become non-functional when selection relaxes or shifts.

**ELI5:** Vestigial structures are leftovers from ancestors who needed them. Whales have tiny leg bones buried inside because their ancestors walked on land. It's like keeping a flip-phone charger in your drawer -- it was useful once but not anymore.

flashcards.front: What is a complex adaptation, and why is it significant?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** A complex adaptation is a trait composed of multiple interacting components that function together as an integrated system, where removing any component significantly impairs function. Examples: the vertebrate eye, the immune system, bacterial flagella. Complex adaptations are significant because they require explanation of how multiple parts were assembled incrementally by selection, each step being independently beneficial. They were historically used as arguments against evolution but are well-explained by gradual modification.

**ELI5:** A complex adaptation is like a machine with many parts that all need to work together -- like an eye with a lens, retina, and optic nerve. It seems impossible to build in one step, but evolution builds it piece by piece, each piece slightly helpful on its own.

flashcards.front: Why are adaptations environment-specific? Use sickle cell as an example.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Adaptations are environment-specific because fitness is context-dependent. The sickle cell allele (HbS) is a classic example: in malaria-endemic regions, HbS heterozygotes have higher fitness than either homozygote (balancing selection via heterozygote advantage) because HbS confers resistance to Plasmodium falciparum. In non-malarial regions, HbS is purely deleterious (homozygotes suffer sickle cell disease with no compensating benefit). The same allele is adaptive or maladaptive depending on environment.

**ELI5:** Sickle cell protects against malaria, so in Africa it's helpful (in one copy). In places without malaria, it just causes disease. Whether a trait is 'good' depends entirely on where you live -- there's no universal best.

flashcards.front: What are novel traits in the context of adaptation, and give two examples.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Novel traits are evolutionary innovations -- qualitatively new structures, functions, or body plans that arise through modification of pre-existing features (not de novo). Examples: (1) Eyes evolved from simple photoreceptor patches through progressive elaboration, (2) Tetrapod limbs evolved from lobe-finned fish fins via modification of skeletal and muscular elements. Each represents a major evolutionary innovation built by co-opting and modifying ancestral structures.

**ELI5:** Novel traits are brand-new inventions of evolution -- like eyes or legs. But they're never truly 'from scratch.' Eyes started as simple light-sensitive spots, and legs started as sturdy fins. Evolution is a tinkerer, not an inventor.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between an adaptation and a neutral trait?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** An adaptation is a trait shaped by natural selection that confers a fitness advantage. A neutral trait has no effect on fitness (selection coefficient s approximately 0) and its frequency in the population is governed by genetic drift rather than selection. Neutral traits are not adaptations even if they are heritable. Examples of neutral traits: synonymous DNA substitutions, some morphological variation within populations.

**ELI5:** Adaptations help you survive and were built by natural selection. Neutral traits don't help or hurt -- they just drift around randomly in the population like a coin flip. Your earlobes being attached vs. free probably doesn't affect your survival at all.

flashcards.front: What is the 'adaptationist programme' that Gould & Lewontin (1979) criticized?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The adaptationist programme (or Panglossian paradigm) is the tendency to assume every trait is an optimal adaptation shaped by natural selection, generating 'just-so stories' (post-hoc adaptive explanations) without rigorously testing alternatives. Gould & Lewontin argued that evolutionary biologists should also consider constraints (developmental, phylogenetic, physical), pleiotropy, drift, historical contingency, and spandrels as explanations for trait existence before defaulting to adaptation.

**ELI5:** Gould & Lewontin said: 'Stop assuming everything has a purpose!' Not every feature is perfectly designed by evolution. Some things are side effects, leftovers, or constrained by physics and development. Don't invent stories about why every bump and wrinkle is 'for' something.

flashcards.front: How would you distinguish a true adaptation from a spandrel experimentally?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** To distinguish adaptation from spandrel: (1) Demonstrate the trait increases fitness (comparative or experimental fitness measures), (2) Show the trait has heritable genetic variation, (3) Demonstrate historical selection by showing the trait evolved independently in similar environments (convergence), (4) Rule out that the trait is a necessary structural/developmental byproduct of a different adaptive trait, (5) Use phylogenetic comparative methods to test whether trait evolution correlates with ecological variables.

**ELI5:** Test if the trait actually helps survival, check if it's genetic, see if unrelated species in similar environments evolved the same thing, and make sure it's not just a side effect of something else. If it passes all those tests, it's likely a real adaptation.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between natural selection and the process of adaptation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Natural selection is the primary (though not sole) mechanism driving adaptation. Adaptation as a process occurs when: (1) heritable phenotypic variation exists in a population, (2) that variation causes differential fitness, and (3) selection acts directionally over generations, increasing the frequency of fitness-enhancing alleles. Adaptation requires selection, but not all selection leads to adaptation (e.g., stabilizing selection maintains existing adaptations rather than generating new ones).

**ELI5:** Natural selection is the engine; adaptation is the journey. Selection picks winners each generation, and over many generations those winners' traits accumulate and refine. It's like how a river carves a canyon -- water (selection) does the work, the canyon (adaptation) is the result.

nodes.title: Evo-Devo & Regulatory Networks
nodes.subtitle: How gene networks build body plans (Lec 8 slides 7-22)

popup.heading: Lecture 8 — Evolutionary Developmental Biology

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Evo-devo = the fusion of developmental biology with evolution. Early ideas (Meckel, Haeckel) proposed "ontogeny recapitulates phylogeny" — now only partly accepted. HOX GENES control body plan and are conserved across all bilaterians (the same Hox genes work in flies and mice despite 570 million years of separation). Vertebrates have FOUR SETS of Hox clusters due to two whole-genome duplications. Other conserved patterning genes: Distalless (Dll), Homothorax (hth). PROMISCUOUS PROTEINS can bind multiple targets, enabling evolutionary tinkering. GENE DUPLICATION produces PARALOGS that can diverge in function (neofunctionalization). 50–70% of flowering plants are polyploid. GENE RECRUITMENT co-opts existing genes for new developmental roles. Classic examples: (1) STICKLEBACKS — Pitx1 REGULATORY change (not coding change) reduces pelvic spine in freshwater populations; (2) GALÁPAGOS FINCHES — Bmp4 and calmodulin expression differences (not new genes) produce the beak diversity Darwin observed. Key insight: SMALL REGULATORY CHANGES → BIG MORPHOLOGICAL DIFFERENCES.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body:
• German anatomist J.F. Meckel (1781-1833): developmental stages paralleled evolutionary history of a species
• Earnst Hackel (1834-1919): Theory of recapitulation-Ontogeny is a precise and compressed recapitulation of phylogeny
• The developmental progress of an organism (ontogeny) replays (recapitulation) its evolutionary history (phylogeny)
• Evolutionary processes produced new species by tacking on something new and novel to the terminal part of development of the ancestral species
• Those traits that appear early in development are resistant to evolutionary change
• Hackel – evolution produced a new species by tacking something new on the terminal part of development of the ancestral species

sections.label: KEY EXPERIMENT
sections.body: HOX GENE INTERCHANGEABILITY: If the same Hox gene "toolkit" is conserved across all animals, toolkit genes should be substantially interchangeable between species. The famous Halder, Callaerts & Gehring (1995) experiment demonstrated this: the mouse Pax6 gene, when expressed in Drosophila, induced ectopic eye formation — a mouse gene triggering eye specification in a fly. This confirms that the master regulatory gene is functionally conserved despite >500 million years of divergence. The downstream targets differ (compound eye vs. camera eye), but the upstream switch is interchangeable.

examples: Evo-devo: the study of how developmental processes evolve and how gene regulation shapes morphology.
Hox genes: conserved master regulators of body plan across animals. Small changes in Hox expression → large morphological changes.
Limb development: same gene network (Hox, Tbx, Sonic hedgehog) is used by all tetrapods to build limbs.
Galapagos finches: beak size differences are controlled by Bmp4 and calmodulin expression differences, not new genes.
warnings: Regulatory and coding evolution are NOT mutually exclusive. Evo-devo emphasizes regulatory changes for large morphological differences, but coding changes (e.g., beach mouse Mc1r) also drive adaptation.
Hox gene SEQUENCE conservation does NOT equal Hox FUNCTION conservation. Changing WHEN or WHERE a Hox gene is expressed can dramatically change body plan without altering the protein sequence.
popup.mnemonic: Small regulatory changes → Big morphological differences. Same toolkit, different deployment.

flashcard.front: Why do evolutionary biologists emphasize regulatory evolution over protein-coding evolution for explaining body plan differences?
flashcard.back: Most animals share the SAME toolkit of developmental genes — Hox, Pax, Tbx, BMP, Wnt, Sonic hedgehog. These genes are nearly identical in fruit flies, mice, and humans. Major morphological differences (legs vs. no legs, big beak vs. small beak, limbs vs. fins) arise mostly from CHANGES IN WHEN, WHERE, AND HOW MUCH these genes are expressed — not from new protein-coding genes. A single mutation in a cis-regulatory element can shift the domain of expression, producing large phenotypic effects. This is why "small changes in networks lead to new complex traits."

quiz.question: The striking morphological differences in beak size among Galápagos finches are produced primarily by:
quiz.correct: Differences in the expression levels of regulatory genes like Bmp4 and calmodulin during beak development
distractors: Completely different sets of genes in each species
New proteins that evolved only in finches
Environmental sculpting of beak bone after hatching

quiz.question: In evo-devo, it is often said that "small regulatory changes lead to large morphological differences." Hox genes are the classic example. If a mutation shifts the anterior expression boundary of Hox gene Hox-c6 two vertebrae forward in a snake lineage, what macroscopic effect would you PREDICT?
quiz.correct: The thoracic-to-lumbar boundary shifts anteriorly — ribs grow on vertebrae that previously had none, extending the ribbed trunk region further toward the tail, as seen in highly elongated snake body plans with hundreds of vertebrae
distractors: The snake would develop limbs at the anterior expression boundary of Hox-c6 because Hox genes specify limb position, and shifting the boundary anteriorly repositions where limb buds are induced

The mutation would be lethal because Hox genes are perfectly conserved across all vertebrates; any change to their expression domain disrupts the basic body plan so severely that embryonic development arrests
The snake's scale pattern would shift anteriorly because Hox gene expression domains solely specify surface integument patterns, not internal skeletal organization in reptiles

quiz.question: Cis-regulatory elements (enhancers and silencers) are non-coding DNA sequences that control when and where genes are expressed. Why are mutations in cis-regulatory elements often BETTER candidates for evolutionary change than mutations in protein-coding sequences?
quiz.correct: Changing a protein-coding sequence affects the protein in ALL tissues and contexts simultaneously (pleiotropic), often causing collateral damage. Cis-regulatory mutations can alter expression in ONE tissue or developmental stage without affecting the protein's function elsewhere — enabling modular evolution with lower pleiotropic costs
distractors: Cis-regulatory mutations are better candidates because they occur at higher rates than coding mutations — non-coding DNA has no proofreading during replication, so mutations accumulate there faster than in coding regions

Protein-coding mutations are always deleterious because proteins are already optimally shaped by billions of years of selection — the only available evolutionary change left in modern species is through non-coding regulatory variation

Cis-regulatory elements are directly translated into short peptides that modulate RNA polymerase binding — because these peptides are rapidly degraded, their mutations have lower fitness effects than coding mutations that alter stable structural proteins

quiz.question: The same Sonic hedgehog (SHH) signaling pathway specifies digit identity in mouse feet, tooth development in sharks, and eye patterning in flies. If a developmental biologist says "evolution uses a common toolkit," what does this imply about how new body plans evolve?
quiz.correct: New body plans arise not by inventing new genes but by redeploying the same conserved toolkit genes in different spatial and temporal contexts — small changes in when/where toolkit genes are expressed can produce radically different morphologies with minimal protein sequence change
distractors: It implies that all multicellular animals evolved from a single common ancestor that had a complex body plan with all modern structures already present — the toolkit is conserved because all modern structures were present in the ancestor

It implies that evolution is impossible beyond the toolkit genes — all possible phenotypic variation in animals is already encoded by the limited set of toolkit genes, and no truly novel traits can arise without acquiring new genes via horizontal transfer

It implies that natural selection acts primarily on toolkit gene sequences — the extreme conservation of SHH, Hox, and Wnt sequences shows that coding-sequence changes in these genes have the highest fitness effects and are therefore the primary targets of evolution

quiz.question: A key insight of evo-devo is that Hox gene SEQUENCES are highly conserved across animals (nearly identical between flies and humans), yet body plans are radically different. What is the explanation for this apparent paradox?
quiz.correct: Changing WHEN and WHERE a Hox gene is expressed (via cis-regulatory mutations in enhancers and silencers) produces body plan differences without altering the Hox protein itself — conservation of protein sequence does NOT mean conservation of expression pattern
