**ELI5:** A lek is like a talent show where all the guys perform on stage and the girls watch from the audience, then pick their favorite. No prizes except the guys themselves. Sage grouse do chest-puffing dances on a big open field, and manakins do backflip routines in the jungle. In both cases, the top few performers get almost all the attention.

flashcards.front: Explain the sensory bias (sensory exploitation) hypothesis for mate choice, with examples.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Sensory bias proposes that female preferences evolve before the male traits they favor -- females have pre-existing sensory biases (from foraging, predator detection, etc.) that males subsequently exploit. Examples: (1) Female guppies prefer orange food items (fruits), and males evolved orange spots that exploit this foraging preference. (2) Female swordtails (Xiphophorus) prefer males with elongated sword-like tail extensions, yet closely related species whose males lack swords still show the preference -- the bias predates the trait.

**ELI5:** Females already liked certain things before males started showing off. Lady guppies loved orange because orange fruit = yummy food. Then male guppies evolved orange spots to hack that preference. It's like if women already loved the color red, and then guys started wearing red ties. The preference came first; the fashion followed.

flashcards.front: What is the handicap principle (Zahavi 1975)?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The handicap principle, proposed by Amotz Zahavi in 1975, states that reliable (honest) signals of quality must be costly to produce or maintain, so that only genuinely high-quality individuals can afford them. The costliness ensures the signal cannot be faked by low-quality individuals. For example, a peacock's enormous tail is a survival handicap -- it attracts predators, is expensive to grow -- but precisely because it is costly, surviving with such a tail honestly signals the male's superior genetic quality to choosy females.

**ELI5:** Zahavi said the peacock's ridiculous tail is the point, not a mistake. It's like driving a Lamborghini -- only rich people can afford one, so it honestly signals wealth. A cheap knockoff would break down. The peacock's tail screams 'I'm so fit I can survive even with this absurd handicap.' Fakers can't keep up the act.

flashcards.front: Explain Fisherian runaway selection.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Fisherian runaway (proposed by R.A. Fisher) occurs when a female preference gene and a male ornament gene become genetically correlated through linkage disequilibrium. Initially, females prefer a slightly exaggerated male trait (for any reason). Sons inherit both the ornament alleles and, because their mothers were choosy, the preference alleles come along. This creates a positive feedback loop: the preference makes the ornament spread, and the ornament makes the preference spread. The trait and preference coevolve explosively until opposed by natural selection (survival cost).

**ELI5:** Imagine girls start liking boys with slightly longer hair. Boys with long hair get more dates, so they have more babies. Their daughters inherit the 'I like long hair' gene AND their sons inherit the 'grow long hair' gene. Next generation, girls like EVEN LONGER hair, boys grow EVEN LONGER hair. It spirals out of control -- runaway! -- until the hair gets so long it's actually dangerous, and natural selection puts on the brakes.

flashcards.front: How does the handicap principle differ from Fisherian runaway as explanations for elaborate male ornaments?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The handicap principle (Zahavi) posits that ornaments are honest signals of genetic quality -- only high-quality males can bear the cost, so females gain indirect 'good genes' benefits. Preference is maintained because choosy females produce fitter offspring. Fisherian runaway (Fisher) posits that ornaments become exaggerated through a self-reinforcing genetic correlation between preference and trait -- the ornament need not signal quality; it spreads simply because it is preferred. Handicap = signal of quality; Fisherian = arbitrary but self-amplifying fashion.

**ELI5:** Handicap: 'His giant tail proves he's genuinely tough -- I want those genes for my kids.' Fisherian: 'His giant tail is popular, so my sons will be popular too -- I don't care if it means anything.' One is like marrying a doctor because doctors are smart (quality signal). The other is like marrying a celebrity because fame breeds fame (popularity spiral).

flashcards.front: Who conducted the side-blotched lizard RPS study, when was it published, and what was the key finding?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Barry Sinervo and Curtis Lively published the study in 1996. They documented that the three male throat-color morphs (orange, blue, yellow) of Uta stansburiana cycle in frequency with approximately 12-year periodicity. This represents one of the clearest empirical demonstrations of rock-paper-scissors dynamics maintained by negative frequency-dependent selection in nature, where each strategy beats one alternative but loses to the other.

**ELI5:** Sinervo and Lively (1996) watched these lizards for years and proved they really do play rock-paper-scissors with their mating strategies. Every ~12 years the 'winner' rotates. It was one of the best real-life examples of a game-theory cycle happening in actual animals, not just math on a chalkboard.

flashcards.front: How does the redback spider's suicidal mating behavior represent an extreme form of male reproductive strategy?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** During mating, the male redback spider (Latrodectus hasselti) performs a somersault that positions his abdomen over the female's fangs. She typically consumes him during copulation. This 'sexual cannibalism' benefits the male in two ways: (1) it extends copulation duration, increasing sperm transfer, and (2) cannibalized males sire proportionally more offspring because the female is less likely to remate soon (she's full). Given the male's low probability of finding another mate, self-sacrifice maximizes his lifetime reproductive success.

**ELI5:** The male redback spider literally flips onto the female's mouth and lets her eat him while they mate. Sounds crazy, but: he'd probably never find another date anyway, getting eaten keeps her busy (so she doesn't mate with someone else), and the longer mating pumps in more sperm. He's trading his life for maximum babies -- the ultimate 'all in' bet.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a 'good genes' model and a 'sexy sons' model of indirect benefits?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In good genes models, females choose males whose ornaments honestly signal high viability (disease resistance, foraging ability, etc.) -- offspring of both sexes benefit from inheriting superior survival alleles. In sexy sons models, females choose attractive males so that sons will be attractive and achieve high mating success -- the benefit is purely through future mating advantage, not survival quality. Good genes = offspring survive better; sexy sons = male offspring mate more.

**ELI5:** 'Good genes': 'I picked your dad because he's healthy, and now you're healthy too -- boy or girl, you benefit.' 'Sexy sons': 'I picked your dad because he's hot, and now YOU'RE hot, son -- you'll get tons of dates.' One gives survival perks to all kids; the other gives dating perks specifically to sons.

flashcards.front: Why do female sage grouse on leks typically mate with only one or a few males out of dozens displaying?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Leks create conditions for extreme directional intersexual selection. Females assess many males simultaneously, and small differences in display vigor, timing, or position are amplified by direct comparison. Female copying (mate-choice copying) further concentrates mating on top performers. The result is a highly skewed mating distribution where 1-2 males may sire 80%+ of offspring. This extreme choosiness is thought to provide indirect (good genes or sexy sons) benefits, since lek males provide no direct resources.

**ELI5:** When all the guys are on the same stage doing the same dance, small differences become obvious. The best dancer gets picked by one girl, then other girls copy her choice because 'she probably knows something I don't.' Before you know it, one or two guys get all the dates and the rest get zero. It's the American Idol effect -- millions audition, but everyone picks the same winner.

flashcards.front: In the swordtail fish example of sensory bias, what evidence suggests the female preference predates the male trait?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Phylogenetic analysis shows that females of Xiphophorus species that LACK swords (e.g., platyfish) still prefer males with artificial swords attached experimentally. Because the preference exists in lineages whose males never evolved swords, the preference must have evolved before the sword itself -- likely as a byproduct of sensory processing or preference for larger apparent body size. This is strong evidence for sensory exploitation rather than coevolution of trait and preference.

**ELI5:** Scientists glued fake swords onto fish that naturally don't have swords. The females of those swordless species STILL preferred the guys with fake swords! This proves the preference was already there, waiting. It's like discovering that people who've never seen ice cream still prefer sweet things -- the taste preference existed before the treat was invented.

flashcards.front: What keeps the three morphs cycling in side-blotched lizards rather than one strategy permanently winning?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Negative frequency-dependent selection prevents any morph from fixing. When a morph becomes common, the morph it is vulnerable to gains a fitness advantage and increases in frequency. For example, if orange (aggressive territory holders) becomes common, yellow (sneakers) thrives by infiltrating their large territories. As yellow rises, blue (mate-guarders) thrives because pair-bonding defeats sneaking. As blue rises, orange beats them through aggression. No morph can reach fixation because success decreases as frequency increases.

**ELI5:** Whichever lizard type is winning creates the perfect opportunity for its counter. Lots of bullies? Sneaks thrive. Lots of sneaks? Faithful guarders catch them. Lots of guarders? Bullies steamroll them. It's exactly like rock-paper-scissors -- no hand signal wins every time because the best move depends on what everyone else is doing.

flashcards.front: How does female guppy preference for orange coloration support the sensory bias hypothesis?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Female guppies exhibit a foraging preference for orange objects (e.g., ripe fruits that fall into streams). This pre-existing sensory bias for orange was co-opted by sexual selection: males with more orange coloration exploit the female's food-detection system and receive more matings. Experiments show that populations with more orange food items in the environment have females with stronger orange preferences and males with more orange spots, consistent with the sensory bias being linked to foraging ecology.

**ELI5:** Female guppies already loved the color orange because orange = tasty fallen fruit in their streams. Male guppies 'figured out' (evolved) that wearing orange gets female attention. It's like how fast-food restaurants use red and yellow because those colors already make humans hungry -- the males are hacking an existing preference that had nothing to do with dating.

nodes.title: Sperm Competition & Sexual Conflict
nodes.subtitle: When females mate with multiple males (Lec 10-11 slides 38-45)

popup.heading: Lecture 10-11 — Sperm Competition and Sexual Conflict

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: When females mate with multiple males, sperm from different males compete to fertilize eggs. FOUR MALE STRATEGIES: (1) REMOVE RIVAL SPERM — seed beetles have penile spines that scrape rival sperm from the female reproductive tract; dragonflies do the same. (2) PRODUCE MORE SPERM — chimpanzees (highly polyandrous) have very LARGE testes relative to body size, gorillas (harem-holding) have small testes, humans are intermediate; testes-size/body-size ratio predicts mating system. (3) SPERM COOPERATION — Peromyscus maniculatus (promiscuous mice) sperm form cooperative "brother sperm" swimming trains, while P. polionotus (monogamous) show indiscriminate aggregation with no selective cooperation. (4) PREVENT FEMALE REMATING — mate guarding, toxic seminal proteins (Drosophila accessory gland proteins), giant sperm, copulatory plugs. SEXUAL CONFLICT: Drosophila seminal fluid proteins increase female egg-laying but reduce female lifespan; females evolve defensive proteins in reciprocal response. A 40-generation forced-monogamy experiment in Drosophila reduced male toxicity AND reduced female defenses — showing rapid reciprocal evolution within a species.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body:
• Sexual selection does not end at mating
• Post-copulatory selection
• Sperm competition: Sperm compete for fertilization of a female’s eggs after mating
• Many different strategies to increase fertilization success
• Most commonly seen in polyandrous mating systems: females mate with multiple males
• Strategy 1: Spines can remove sperm from other males
• Strategy 2: Larger testes produce more sperm
• Strategy – produce more sperm when there is high competition

examples: Sperm competition: when multiple males' sperm compete within the female reproductive tract to fertilize eggs.

Male strategies: (1) Spines remove rival sperm (Odonata), (2) Larger testes produce more sperm (primates with promiscuous females), (3) Sperm cooperate by forming swimming trains (some rodents), (4) Mate guarding / mating plugs / copulatory prolongation.
Drosophila seminal fluid contains chemicals that suppress female remating and reduce sperm of previous males — BUT also shorten female lifespan (sexually antagonistic).
Sexual conflict: what is optimal for males may harm females.
warnings: Sexual conflict drives rapid reciprocal evolution — an intersexual arms race WITHIN a single species, producing rapid coevolution of male offense and female defense.
popup.mnemonic: Remove, Out-produce, Cooperate, Prevent remating = 4 sperm strategies.

flashcard.front: What is sexual conflict and how does it manifest in Drosophila seminal fluid proteins?
flashcard.back: SEXUAL CONFLICT arises when what is optimal for male reproductive success is HARMFUL to female fitness (or vice versa). Because males and females share most of their genomes, evolution of male-beneficial traits can reduce female fitness and drive counter-adaptations in females — a sexually antagonistic coevolutionary arms race WITHIN a species. DROSOPHILA EXAMPLE: Male seminal fluid contains proteins (ACPs) that (1) suppress female remating with other males, (2) increase female egg-laying rate, and (3) kill sperm of previously mated males. These adaptations BENEFIT the male (by monopolizing paternity and accelerating reproduction). HOWEVER, the same chemicals ALSO SHORTEN FEMALE LIFESPAN and REDUCE HER LIFETIME REPRODUCTIVE SUCCESS. Females experience selection to resist the harmful effects, potentially by evolving detoxifying enzymes or refusing to mate. This drives reciprocal evolution between male offense and female defense — a form of intragenomic arms race. Sexual conflict is one of the most important insights of modern sexual selection theory.

quiz.question: A species where females mate with many males during one estrus period is most likely to have evolved:
quiz.correct: Relatively large testes to produce more sperm for competition
distractors: Elaborate male display ornaments for female choice
Complete monogamy
Reduced sexual dimorphism

quiz.question: Dragonfly males have penile structures that can scrape rival sperm from the female's reproductive tract before depositing their own sperm. Which aspect of sperm competition theory MOST directly predicts this adaptation?
quiz.correct: When females commonly mate with multiple males before a single fertilization event, the last-male advantage hypothesis predicts selection for mechanisms that reduce the success of rival sperm already present — scraping is one strategy to achieve sperm precedence
distractors: The dragonfly structure evolved to prevent physical injury to the female during mating — sperm scraping is a cooperative adaptation that protects the female's reproductive tract from damage by rival males' sperm, which would otherwise cause inflammation

The sperm-scraping structure is evidence of sexual conflict, where males have evolved a device that HARMS females by removing their option to use stored sperm from chosen previous mates — females have co-evolved resistance by reducing the efficiency of scraping with modified sperm-storage morphology

The scraping structure evolved through Müllerian mimicry between dragonfly species — multiple species converged on the same penile design because it optimally transfers sperm in the shared aquatic environment where all dragonflies breed

quiz.question: Drosophila seminal fluid proteins (ACPs) increase female egg-laying rate AND suppress female remating BUT also shorten female lifespan. From the FEMALE's perspective, what is the evolutionarily optimal response to receiving these proteins?
quiz.correct: Females should evolve resistance to the harmful components of ACPs (e.g., enzymes to degrade lifespan-shortening compounds) while retaining sensitivity to beneficial components — this is an intrasexual arms race where males evolve more effective ACPs and females evolve countermeasures
distractors: Females should evolve to maximize their uptake of ACPs because the increased egg-laying rate more than compensates for the shortened lifespan — from a lifetime fitness standpoint, more eggs per day always outweighs a shorter reproductive period

Females should evolve to REJECT sperm from males whose ACPs are most harmful — by co-evolving receptors that detect harmful ACPs, females can choose mates whose proteins increase reproduction without the lifespan cost

Females have no evolutionary recourse because ACPs are delivered internally during mating — natural selection cannot act on a trait that is only expressed internally after mating has already occurred, so female resistance to ACPs cannot evolve

quiz.question: In gorillas (highly polygynous, one male per many females), testes are relatively SMALL compared to body size. In chimpanzees (promiscuous, females mate with many males), testes are very LARGE relative to body size. Humans fall between the two. What mating system does human testes size SUGGEST our evolutionary history included?
quiz.correct: Moderate polygyny or mild promiscuity — human testes size (intermediate between gorilla and chimpanzee) suggests our lineage experienced sperm competition at moderate levels, consistent with a mating system where females occasionally mated with more than one male but not at the high frequency seen in chimpanzees
distractors: Strict monogamy throughout our evolutionary history — human testes are LARGER than gorilla testes, which is only consistent with a monogamous system where sperm competition is zero; gorilla testes are small because the single dominant male wastes no resources on sperm competition

Strict polygyny with no sperm competition — humans evolved in harem systems like gorillas; the slight increase over gorilla testes size reflects recent cultural changes from polygyny to serial monogamy, not ancient sperm competition

Testes size has no evolutionary significance for mating system inference because testes size is primarily determined by developmental temperature regulation, not by any sperm-competition-related selection pressure

quiz.question: In some rodent species (e.g., wood mice), sperm physically form "sperm trains" that swim faster as a group than individual sperm. What selective pressure drives this cooperation between sperm from the same male?
quiz.correct: All sperm in a single ejaculate are genetically related (they all carry copies of the same male's genome), so cooperation among them increases the inclusive fitness of each sperm — faster group swimming gives the cooperating male's sperm a competitive advantage over sperm from rival males
distractors: Sperm trains evolve to reduce energy expenditure — sperm save ATP by drafting behind each other like cyclists, not for any competitive advantage
Sperm trains are accidental clumping caused by seminal fluid viscosity, not an adaptation — there is no reproductive benefit
Sperm cooperation requires sperm to carry different alleles so they can recognize each other as distinct — this only works in highly outbred populations

quiz.question: Mating plugs (gelatinous secretions that block a female's reproductive tract after mating) are found in many species. What is the adaptive function from the male's perspective?
quiz.correct: Mating plugs prevent the female from receiving sperm from subsequent males, ensuring the plugging male's sperm sire the offspring — a form of post-copulatory mate guarding that reduces sperm competition
distractors: Mating plugs protect the female from infection during vulnerable post-mating periods — they are a cooperative adaptation that benefits both sexes
Mating plugs serve as nutrition for the developing embryos — the male provides post-fertilization resources through the plug
Mating plugs signal to other males that the female is unavailable, preventing wasteful mating attempts that would harm the male-female bond

quiz.question: What is the KEY concept distinguishing SEXUAL CONFLICT from regular sexual selection?
quiz.correct: Sexual conflict occurs when the evolutionary optimum for males differs from the optimum for females — traits beneficial to one sex can reduce the fitness of the other, driving an arms race WITHIN a species. Regular sexual selection can involve mutual benefit or one-sided choice without such intrasexual conflict
distractors: Sexual conflict occurs only between species (e.g., interspecific hybridization attempts), while sexual selection occurs within species
Sexual conflict is about physical aggression between individuals, while sexual selection is purely about mate choice without combat
Sexual conflict is a hypothetical concept that has not been experimentally verified, while sexual selection has extensive experimental support

quiz.question: A female Drosophila that has received ACPs (accessory gland proteins) shows increased egg-laying and reduced interest in re-mating. If she could evolutionarily resist ACPs, what conflict does this illustrate?
quiz.correct: Intersexual (sexual) conflict — male ACPs benefit the male by monopolizing paternity and accelerating egg-laying, but harm the female by shortening her lifespan. Female counter-adaptations (e.g., enzymes that degrade ACPs) represent her evolutionary response to the male strategy
distractors: Intrasexual conflict — ACPs are male-male competition tools that do not affect the female directly; the female is a passive vehicle
Parent-offspring conflict — ACPs affect the offspring's development at the expense of the mother, creating a dispute over resource allocation
Kin conflict — ACPs reduce the inclusive fitness of the female's relatives who share her genes

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: In what Peromyscus species does sperm form cooperative "brother sperm" swimming trains, and what is the mating system?
quiz.correct: Peromyscus maniculatus — a PROMISCUOUS species where female mice mate with multiple males. Sperm from a single ejaculate form cooperative swimming trains that out-race rival sperm from competing males
distractors: Peromyscus polionotus — a monogamous species where sperm cooperation evolved first and was later lost in promiscuous relatives
Peromyscus leucopus — a species with no sperm cooperation
Peromyscus californicus — the only known primate-like Peromyscus species

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: In the forced-monogamy Drosophila experiment (40 generations), what changes occurred in male seminal fluid toxicity and female defenses?
quiz.correct: After 40 generations of enforced monogamy, male seminal fluid became LESS toxic (no longer needed because no sperm competition) AND female defensive proteins DECREASED (no longer needed because males stopped harming them) — demonstrating rapid reciprocal de-escalation in sexual conflict
distractors: Males became more toxic and females more defensive — enforced monogamy intensified the arms race
No detectable changes occurred within 40 generations — sexual conflict evolves too slowly
Males became more toxic and females became less defensive, leading to female extinction in the experimental lines

quiz.question: L3 APPLICATION: Gorillas have small testes relative to body size; chimpanzees have very large testes. What does testes size ratio PREDICT about the mating system that shapes a species?
quiz.correct: Small testes relative to body size indicate LOW sperm competition (monogamous or harem systems where a single male monopolizes matings); large testes indicate HIGH sperm competition (promiscuous systems where multiple males mate with each female and sperm compete in the female tract). The predictive relationship is robust across primates
distractors: Testes size ratio is random and has no predictive value for mating system
Large testes predict monogamy because fewer matings require each ejaculate to be larger
Small testes predict promiscuity because smaller sperm are faster swimmers

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: Integrate the four sperm-competition strategies (remove, out-produce, cooperate, prevent remating) into a unified framework explaining how mating-system promiscuity predicts which strategies dominate. Provide specific examples.
quiz.correct: Mating system dictates sperm competition intensity which shapes strategy selection: (1) REMOVE — favored when females store sperm and last-male precedence is common (dragonflies, seed beetles). (2) OUT-PRODUCE — favored when multiple males ejaculate sequentially (chimps vs gorillas testes size ratio). (3) COOPERATE — favored when sperm from one ejaculate must out-swim rivals (Peromyscus maniculatus brother-sperm trains). (4) PREVENT REMATING — favored when monopolizing paternity is possible (Drosophila ACPs, mating plugs). Species commonly deploy multiple strategies; the relative importance depends on whether competition happens within the female or between ejaculate volumes
distractors: All four strategies are equally effective in all species and their distribution is random
Only one strategy can evolve in a given lineage because they are mutually exclusive
Species use whichever strategy their ancestors used regardless of current mating system — phylogenetic inertia dominates

visual.mnemonic: RCOP: Remove, Cooperate, Out-produce, Prevent.
visual.trap: Testis size (relative to body mass) is a strong predictor of mating system — promiscuous species have large testes, monogamous species have small ones.

diagram.title: Sperm Competition Strategies Under Polyandry

nodes.label: Remove rival sperm
nodes.detail: Physically evict or destroy sperm from previous males. Morphological weapons on the genitalia scrape, scoop, or displace sperm already stored in the female reproductive tract.
nodes.mnemonic: Scrape before you score.

nodes.label: Seed beetle penile spines
nodes.detail: Callosobruchus maculatus males have hardened penile spines that damage the female reproductive tract while scraping out rival sperm. Causes harm to the female — a canonical sexual conflict system.

nodes.label: Out-produce rivals
nodes.detail: Numerical advantage: more sperm from you = higher fertilization share. Drives the evolution of enlarged testes relative to body mass in species with high polyandry.
nodes.mnemonic: Flood the zone.

nodes.label: Chimp vs gorilla testes
nodes.detail: Chimpanzees (polyandrous — many males mate with one female) have large testes relative to body mass. Gorillas (harem structure, one silverback) have small testes because sperm competition is rare. Humans are intermediate — a clue about our mating history.

nodes.label: Cooperate (brother sperm)
nodes.detail: Related sperm from one male aggregate and swim together, reaching the egg faster than sperm from a different male. Kin selection operating at the gamete level — Hamilton-style rB > C in miniature.
nodes.mnemonic: Brothers swim together.

nodes.label: Peromyscus sperm trains
nodes.detail: Peromyscus maniculatus (promiscuous deer mouse) sperm form aggregates — "sperm trains" — that swim faster than single sperm. P. polionotus (monogamous) shows NO such aggregation, because there is no rival sperm to out-race. Direct phylogenetic test of the hypothesis.

nodes.label: Prevent remating
nodes.detail: Stop the female from mating again. Ranges from behavioral mate guarding to chemical warfare in the ejaculate to physical plugs that block the reproductive tract.
nodes.mnemonic: Lock the door.

nodes.label: Drosophila seminal toxins + plugs
nodes.detail: Drosophila melanogaster seminal fluid contains "Acps" (accessory gland proteins) that reduce female receptivity to remating — but also shorten female lifespan. Other species use copulatory plugs or giant sperm that fill the female storage organ.

nodes.label: Sexual conflict
nodes.detail: Drosophila forced-monogamy experiment: after 40 generations of enforced monogamy (no sperm competition), seminal fluid became LESS toxic and females LOST defensive proteins. Confirms ongoing antagonistic coevolution — each sex is constantly evolving counter-measures against the other.
nodes.watchOut: Sexual conflict drives rapid reciprocal evolution — an arms race WITHIN a species, not between species.

nodes.label: Testes-mass ratio
nodes.detail: Testes size relative to body mass tracks degree of sperm competition. A high ratio indicates the mating system is polyandrous; a low ratio indicates harem or monogamy. Humans sit in the middle — interpreted as evidence of mild but non-zero sperm competition in our ancestry.

diagram.mnemonic: Remove, Out-produce, Cooperate, Prevent remating — four sperm competition strategies.

flashcards.front: Name the four major male strategies for sperm competition.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Remove rival sperm -- physical displacement or destruction of previously deposited sperm (e.g., dragonflies, seed beetles). (2) Produce more sperm -- overwhelm rivals numerically with greater ejaculate volume (e.g., chimpanzees' large testes). (3) Sperm cooperation -- sperm from the same male form cooperative groups that outcompete rival sperm (e.g., Peromyscus maniculatus sperm trains). (4) Prevent female remating -- mate guarding, copulatory plugs, anti-aphrodisiac pheromones, or toxic seminal proteins that reduce female receptivity.

**ELI5:** Four ways guys try to win the sperm wars: (1) Scrape out the last guy's contribution (dragonflies literally scoop). (2) Flood the zone with more swimmers than the competition. (3) Have your sperm team up like a relay team. (4) Block the entrance so nobody else can get in (plugs, guarding, or chemicals that make her lose interest in other guys).

flashcards.front: How do dragonflies and seed beetles exemplify the 'remove rival sperm' strategy?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Male dragonflies (Odonata) have specialized penile structures with backward-pointing spines or inflatable bulbs that physically scoop out or compact sperm deposited by previous males before depositing their own. Male seed beetles (Callosobruchus) have spiny genitalia that scrape the female reproductive tract, displacing rival sperm. Both represent direct mechanical removal/displacement of competitor sperm to achieve last-male sperm precedence.

**ELI5:** Male dragonflies have a built-in sperm scoop on their reproductive organ -- they literally shovel out the previous guy's sperm before adding their own. Seed beetles use spiky equipment that scrapes the walls clean. It's like erasing someone else's work on a whiteboard before writing your own answer.

flashcards.front: Compare testes size in chimpanzees, gorillas, and humans. What does relative testes size indicate about mating systems?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Chimpanzees have the largest testes relative to body size among great apes -- reflecting their promiscuous, multi-male mating system where sperm competition is intense. Gorillas have very small relative testes because a single silverback monopolizes mating (low sperm competition). Humans have intermediate relative testes size, suggesting moderate ancestral sperm competition consistent with a mostly, but not exclusively, pair-bonded mating system. Larger testes produce more sperm, an advantage when multiple males' ejaculates compete.

**ELI5:** Chimp guys have huge testes (for their body) because multiple males mate with the same female -- it's a sperm arms race. Gorilla guys have tiny testes because one big boss gets all the females, so no competition. Human guys are in between -- suggesting our ancestors weren't strictly monogamous but weren't having all-out sperm battles either. Testes size = a record of your species' mating history.

flashcards.front: What are sperm 'trains' in Peromyscus maniculatus and why do they form?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In deer mice (Peromyscus maniculatus), sperm from the same male aggregate by hooking their heads together, forming cooperative 'trains' or clusters that swim faster through the female reproductive tract than individual sperm. Critically, sperm from different males do NOT cooperate -- they can discriminate self from non-self. This kin-selected cooperation (r=0.5 between sperm sharing a father) gives the cooperating male's sperm a competitive advantage in the race to the egg.

**ELI5:** Deer mouse sperm from the same dad link up head-to-head like a relay team and swim faster together. Sperm from different dads refuse to team up -- they can somehow tell who's family and who's not. It's like brothers forming a rowing team that crushes the competition. Since all brothers share DNA, even the ones who don't fertilize the egg still helped their genes win.

flashcards.front: List three ways males can prevent female remating after copulation.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Mate guarding -- physically staying near the female to ward off rival males (common in many insects, birds, mammals). (2) Copulatory plugs -- depositing a hardened secretion that blocks the female's reproductive tract (seen in many rodents, snakes, insects). (3) Toxic/manipulative seminal proteins -- chemical compounds in seminal fluid that reduce female receptivity to further mating or accelerate egg-laying (e.g., Drosophila Acp proteins).

**ELI5:** Three ways to lock the door after yourself: (1) Stand guard -- physically block other guys from getting close. (2) Plug it up -- leave a physical barrier (like putting gum in a lock). (3) Chemical warfare -- pump in substances that make the female not want to mate again or lay eggs immediately before she can. Nature's version of 'no solicitors' signs.

flashcards.front: What is sexual conflict? How does Drosophila seminal fluid illustrate it?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Sexual conflict occurs when the fitness optima of males and females differ regarding a shared trait or interaction. In Drosophila melanogaster, male seminal fluid contains accessory gland proteins (Acps, especially the 'sex peptide' SP) that increase egg-laying rate and decrease female remating -- both beneficial to the male's paternity. However, these same proteins are toxic to females, reducing their lifespan. Males benefit from maximizing short-term egg production at the female's expense; females would benefit from living longer and mating multiply.

**ELI5:** Sexual conflict is when what's good for the guy is bad for the girl, and vice versa. Male fruit flies inject chemicals with their sperm that make the female lay more eggs faster (good for him) but also poison her, shortening her life (terrible for her). It's like a business partner who makes you work overtime -- great for their profits, but you burn out.

flashcards.front: Describe the Drosophila 40-generation forced monogamy experiment and its results.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Researchers enforced monogamy (one male, one female per generation) for ~40 generations, removing selection for sperm competition and sexual conflict. After 40 generations, males evolved to be less harmful to females (reduced seminal fluid toxicity), and females evolved to be less resistant to male-induced harm (reduced costly counter-adaptations). This demonstrated that sexual conflict is an ongoing coevolutionary arms race: when the conflict is removed, both sides 'disarm.'

**ELI5:** Scientists forced fruit flies into arranged marriages (one boy, one girl, no competition) for 40 generations. Result: the boys became nicer (less toxic sperm chemicals) AND the girls dropped their defenses (no need for armor anymore). This proves both sides were locked in an arms race -- the boys were poisoning and the girls were developing antidotes. Take away the war, and both sides relax.

flashcards.front: How does the Drosophila forced-monogamy experiment provide evidence that female resistance traits are costly?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** After 40 generations of monogamy, females lost their resistance to male seminal toxins. If resistance were cost-free, there would be no reason to lose it under relaxed selection. The fact that resistance was lost indicates it carries a fitness cost (energy, physiological trade-offs) that is only worth paying when sexual conflict is active. This is consistent with antagonistic coevolution: both offense (male toxicity) and defense (female resistance) are maintained only because each is a response to the other.

**ELI5:** After generations of peace, the girls stopped building shields. If shields were free, why drop them? Because shields are heavy and expensive -- they only make sense when someone's actually shooting at you. The fact that girls lost their defenses when the attacks stopped proves that defending against toxic males costs energy they'd rather spend on something else.

flashcards.front: Why do gorillas have relatively small testes compared to their body size?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Gorillas live in single-male harem groups where one dominant silverback has near-exclusive mating access to all females. Because females rarely mate with multiple males, there is minimal sperm competition. Without selection pressure to outcompete rival ejaculates, there is no advantage to large testes and high sperm production. The silverback's reproductive success depends on his ability to fight off rival males (body size, weaponry) rather than on sperm volume.

**ELI5:** The gorilla king already won the fight -- he's the only guy with access to the ladies. Since no other male's sperm is in the race, he doesn't need a big sperm factory. It's like being the only store in town: you don't need a huge inventory because you have no competition. His muscles got big instead -- that's what keeps rivals away.

flashcards.front: How do copulatory plugs function as a male anti-competition strategy?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Copulatory plugs are physical barriers deposited by males in the female's reproductive tract after mating. Composed of coagulated seminal fluid, accessory gland secretions, or specialized structures, they physically obstruct subsequent males from inseminating the female or mechanically displace the plug-forming male's sperm. Plugs have been documented in diverse taxa including rodents, snakes, insects, and spiders. Their effectiveness varies -- in some species, subsequent males can remove plugs.

**ELI5:** After mating, some males literally leave a biological cork in the female so no other male can get in. It's like putting a 'closed' sign on the door after you walk in. It works great unless the next guy is strong enough to yank the cork out -- which sometimes happens, leading to an arms race of better corks vs. better cork-pullers.

flashcards.front: How does sexual conflict drive coevolutionary arms races between males and females?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** When male adaptations that increase their fitness reduce female fitness (or vice versa), each sex evolves counter-adaptations, creating an ongoing antagonistic coevolution. Males evolve more effective manipulation (toxic seminal proteins, traumatic insemination), and females evolve resistance (detoxification, morphological defenses). Each adaptation selects for counter-adaptation in the other sex. This can escalate indefinitely, with both sexes investing heavily in offense/defense without any net fitness gain -- a Red Queen dynamic between the sexes.

**ELI5:** It's like a never-ending spy vs. spy battle between boys and girls of the same species. Boys develop sneakier tricks, girls develop better countermeasures, boys escalate, girls respond, on and on forever. Neither side wins permanently -- they just keep one-upping each other, spending more and more energy on the battle. The 40-generation monogamy experiment showed what happens when you declare a ceasefire: both sides immediately start saving energy.

flashcards.front: What evidence suggests that sperm from different males of Peromyscus can discriminate kin from non-kin?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** When sperm from different Peromyscus maniculatus males are mixed in vitro, sperm preferentially aggregate with other sperm from the same male, forming cooperative trains with brothers (r=0.5) rather than with unrelated sperm. This discriminatory behavior suggests cell-surface recognition molecules (possibly MHC-related or other polymorphic markers) enable self/non-self recognition among sperm, facilitating kin-selected cooperation.

**ELI5:** When you mix sperm from two different mouse dads in a dish, each team finds its own teammates and links up -- like players on a sports field recognizing their own team's jerseys. They won't team up with the other dad's sperm. Scientists think sperm have some kind of molecular ID badge that lets them tell 'us' from 'them.' It's kin recognition at the cellular level.

flashcards.front: Why is human testes size considered 'intermediate' and what does this imply about ancestral human mating systems?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Human relative testes size falls between highly promiscuous chimpanzees (large testes) and harem-holding gorillas (small testes). This intermediate size suggests moderate levels of ancestral sperm competition -- consistent with a primarily pair-bonded mating system with some extra-pair mating (not strict monogamy, not extreme promiscuity). The testes-size data aligns with other evidence (concealed ovulation, moderate sexual dimorphism) suggesting flexible mating strategies in human evolutionary history.

**ELI5:** Human testes aren't huge like chimps' (total free-for-all mating) or tiny like gorillas' (one guy monopolizes all the ladies). We're in the middle, suggesting our ancestors were mostly coupled up but occasionally cheated. Our body carries the receipt of our ancestors' dating habits -- and it says 'mostly faithful, sometimes not.'

flashcards.front: In Drosophila, what specific molecule in seminal fluid is most associated with the toxic effects on females?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The 'sex peptide' (SP, also called Acp70A) is the best-characterized molecule responsible for seminal fluid toxicity in Drosophila. SP binds to receptors in the female reproductive tract and nervous system, triggering increased egg-laying (oviposition), reduced remating receptivity, altered feeding behavior, and decreased sleep. While these effects benefit male reproductive success, the cumulative physiological burden reduces female lifespan. Other Acps (accessory gland proteins) also contribute to toxicity.

**ELI5:** The main 'poison' is called 'sex peptide' -- it's a tiny protein that hijacks the female's brain and body. It forces her to lay more eggs, eat differently, reject other males, and sleep less. Great deal for the male (more of HIS babies, no competition), terrible deal for the female (burns out faster). It's like a remote control for the female's reproductive system that the male operates for his own benefit.

flashcards.front: How do seed beetle genital spines relate to sexual conflict?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Male seed beetles (Callosobruchus maculatus) possess spiny genitalia that damage the female reproductive tract during mating. These spines may function in sperm competition by removing rival sperm or anchoring during copulation, but they reduce female longevity and fitness. Experimental evolution studies show that populations evolving under high sperm competition develop males with longer spines but also females with thicker reproductive tract walls -- a classic sexually antagonistic coevolution.

**ELI5:** Male seed beetles have spiked equipment that hurts the female during mating. It probably helps scrape out other males' sperm or hold on longer, but it injures her. Over generations, females evolve thicker armor in response, and males evolve even spikier equipment. It's a painful arms race where both sides keep escalating -- he gets spikier, she gets tougher, nobody really wins.

nodes.title: Life History Strategies & Trade-offs
nodes.subtitle: r-K strategies, energy and time trade-offs (Lec 12 slides 1-11)

popup.heading: Lecture 12 — Life History Evolution

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Energy and time are FINITE — every organism faces trade-offs between growth, maintenance, immune defense, and reproduction. Extreme life-history examples: Adactylidium mites are born already sexually mature, are inseminated by their own brothers (who die shortly after) inside the mother, then consume the mother from within; adults live ~4 days. Kiwis lay a SINGLE huge egg relative to body size. Mayflies live only hours as adults but can lay thousands of eggs. Elephants take 10–12 years to reach reproductive maturity. Bristlecone pines can live over 4000 years. The r/K-SELECTION continuum characterizes these strategies: r-selected = fast growth (rabbits, insects, weeds) with many small offspring, little parental care, short lifespan; K-selected = slow, stable growth (elephants, humans, long-lived trees) with few large offspring, heavy parental investment, long lifespan. Two polar strategies are MAXIMIZE SIZE (grow big to dominate at carrying capacity) vs MINIMIZE TIME (reproduce fast before you die). Opossums have been shown to shift energy allocation between reproduction and maintenance as they age.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body:
• Life History Evolution
• 1) To understand why trade-offs may limit the number of offspring produced
• 2) To explain why extrinsic mortality can influence the evolution of life-history traits
• 3) To understand two leading hypotheses for the evolution of aging
• 4) To explain the different types and benefits of flexible allocation strategies
• Reproductive strategies after conception are extremely diverse
• Organism that is perfectly adapted for reproduction

sections.label: WORKED EXAMPLE
sections.body: PACIFIC SALMON — INTEGRATING TRADE-OFFS: Pacific salmon illustrate how the finite-energy constraint, r/K continuum, and semelparity interact. Salmon face extremely high adult mortality from the arduous upstream migration (predators, rapids, exhaustion). With survival probability near zero after spawning, natural selection favors pouring ALL remaining energy into a single massive reproductive event (semelparity) — optimizing egg number and quality rather than holding reserves for future reproduction. This is an extreme K-selected semelparous strategy: few, large, well-provisioned offspring, one lethal reproductive bout.

examples: Life history: the schedule of birth, maturation, reproduction, and death.
r-strategy: many small offspring, little parental care, fast maturation (insects, weeds).
K-strategy: few large offspring, heavy parental care, slow maturation (elephants, humans, oak trees).
Trade-offs: energy spent on reproduction CANNOT be spent on growth or survival. Time spent finding a mate cannot be spent foraging.
warnings: r/K is a CONTINUUM, not a binary. Most species fall somewhere in between — do not treat r vs K as mutually exclusive categories.

r and K are POPULATION GROWTH parameters. r-selected species thrive when population is below carrying capacity (high r); K-selected species are competitive at carrying capacity. The strategy is about environment stability, not just offspring number.
popup.mnemonic: r vs K: rabbits (r) vs K-apes. Live fast die young OR slow and steady.

flashcard.front: What are the classic r vs K life history strategies, and what trade-off underlies the distinction?
flashcard.back: The r/K strategy framework describes two ends of a continuum of life-history strategies. r-SELECTED species (named for r in population growth models) live in unstable or unpredictable environments. They evolve FAST reproduction: many small offspring, rapid maturation, little parental care, short lifespan. Examples: insects, dandelions, mice. Each offspring has low survival probability, but sheer numbers compensate. K-SELECTED species (K = carrying capacity) live in stable, competitive environments. They evolve SLOW reproduction: few large offspring, long maturation, heavy parental investment, long lifespan. Examples: elephants, humans, oak trees. Each offspring has high survival probability. The UNDERLYING TRADE-OFF is that energy/time devoted to reproduction cannot be used for growth or maintenance, and vice versa. The r/K framework is now considered a simplification — modern life history theory uses continuous traits and optimization models — but it remains a useful conceptual starting point.

quiz.question: An r-selected species is most likely to:
quiz.correct: Produce many small offspring with little parental care and mature quickly
distractors: Produce few large offspring with heavy parental care
Have very long lifespans
Live in highly stable environments

quiz.question: A dandelion produces ~15,000 seeds per plant with no parental care. A giant tortoise produces 1-4 eggs per year and takes 30 years to mature. According to life-history theory, what underlying trade-off explains this difference?
quiz.correct: Energy and time devoted to reproduction cannot simultaneously be invested in offspring quality, growth, or maintenance — the dandelion allocates all reproductive investment into quantity, while the tortoise invests heavily in each offspring's quality and in its own survival to reproduce repeatedly
distractors: The difference is purely taxonomic — dandelions are angiosperms and tortoises are reptiles; life-history differences between plants and animals cannot be explained by the same trade-off logic because they occupy fundamentally different ecological roles

The difference reflects mutation accumulation — dandelions are annuals with high generation turnover, allowing mutations to accumulate faster and driving faster evolution toward high fecundity; tortoises accumulate few mutations per unit time due to long lifespan

The difference is primarily driven by body size — larger organisms always evolve lower reproductive rates because metabolic costs scale with body mass, not because of any evolutionary optimization of investment trade-offs

quiz.question: A conservationist is comparing two endangered species: Species A matures at age 2 and produces 200 eggs per year (r-selected). Species B matures at age 20 and produces 1 offspring per year (K-selected). If both species have had populations reduced to 50 individuals by habitat loss, which faces greater extinction risk, and why?
quiz.correct: Species B faces greater extinction risk because its slow recovery rate (1 offspring/yr, 20-yr maturation delay) means the population cannot rebound quickly even if habitat is restored — r-selected Species A can recover much faster from low population size because reproductive rate is high relative to generation time
distractors: Species A faces greater extinction risk because r-selected species naturally experience boom-bust population cycles — with only 50 individuals, Species A is in a bust phase and may crash to zero before the next boom, while Species B's stable K-strategy makes it resilient at low population densities

Both face identical extinction risk because extinction probability depends only on population SIZE, not on life-history strategy — any population below 50 individuals has the same probability of going extinct regardless of reproductive rate or generation time

Species B faces less extinction risk because K-selected species evolved in stable environments where population crashes are rare — their slow reproduction is an evolved response to near-zero extinction risk, so being reduced to 50 individuals is an anomalous condition they are evolutionarily prepared for

quiz.question: Iteroparous organisms (reproduce multiple times) vs. semelparous organisms (reproduce once and die, like Pacific salmon). Under what environmental conditions should semelparity be evolutionarily favored over iteroparity?
quiz.correct: When adult survival rate is low (high extrinsic mortality) AND a single large reproductive episode produces dramatically more offspring than multiple small ones — in high-mortality environments, investing everything in a single massive reproductive effort has higher expected fitness than surviving to reproduce again (which is unlikely)
distractors: Semelparity is favored when environments are stable and predictable — semelparous organisms evolved to match their single reproductive event to a predictable resource peak, which requires environmental stability, while unpredictable environments always favor iteroparity

Semelparity is favored when juveniles have HIGH survival rates — if offspring are very likely to survive, producing large numbers at once is beneficial; when juvenile survival is low, iteroparity is favored to continuously try again with each small brood

Semelparity is always favored in aquatic organisms because water buoyancy reduces the metabolic cost of egg production — the high mass of Pacific salmon eggs is only possible underwater, making semelparity an ecological constraint of aquatic life rather than an evolved life-history strategy

quiz.question: What do the letters "r" and "K" stand for in r/K selection theory?
quiz.correct: r = the intrinsic rate of population increase in logistic growth models (dN/dt = rN(1-N/K)); K = carrying capacity, the maximum sustainable population size of the environment
distractors: r = reproductive rate per year; K = number of offspring per clutch
r = random mating; K = selective (non-random) mating — the distinction is about mating system
r = rapid; K = slow — these are descriptive terms for life history pace, not population parameters

quiz.question: Why is the r/K framework considered a "continuum" rather than a binary classification?
quiz.correct: Most real species show a mix of r and K traits and fall somewhere between the extremes — e.g., a medium-sized mammal with moderate offspring numbers and moderate parental care is neither fully r nor fully K. Treating r/K as a binary misrepresents the gradient of life history strategies
distractors: The framework is a continuum because all species cycle between r and K strategies seasonally — every species is r-selected in spring and K-selected in fall
r/K is a continuum because the framework has been replaced by numerical rates (0-100 scale) rather than qualitative categories — there is no meaningful distinction between r and K anymore
The r/K framework is a strict binary, not a continuum — each species is definitively classified as one or the other based on reproductive rate alone

quiz.question: Identify which species is r-selected and which is K-selected: (a) a field mouse with 4 litters per year of 6 pups each, 1-year lifespan; (b) an elephant with 1 calf every 4-6 years, 60-year lifespan.
quiz.correct: The field mouse is r-selected (high reproductive rate, short lifespan, many small offspring); the elephant is K-selected (low reproductive rate, long lifespan, few large offspring with heavy parental investment)
distractors: Both are K-selected because they are mammals — all mammals are K-selected by definition
The mouse is K-selected because it reproduces multiple times per year (iteroparous); the elephant is r-selected because it has few offspring
Neither is r or K selected — both fall in the middle of the continuum and the distinction does not apply to vertebrates

quiz.question: Which ONE of these is NOT a typical trait of an r-selected species?
quiz.correct: Heavy parental investment per offspring — r-selected species typically provide LITTLE parental care (the "r" strategy relies on producing many offspring with low individual survival rather than investing heavily in each)
distractors: Fast maturation time — r-selected species reach reproductive age quickly
High fecundity (many offspring) — r-selected species produce many offspring per reproductive event
Thrives in unstable or unpredictable environments — r-selection is favored where population crashes and recoveries are frequent

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: The Adactylidium mite is cited as an extreme life-history case. What is unusual about its reproductive biology?
quiz.correct: Adactylidium females are born already mature; their brothers inseminate them while still inside the mother; the developing offspring then consume the mother from within, emerging as adults after only about 4 days
distractors: Adactylidium females carry eggs for 10 years before laying them
Adactylidium females live for decades and reproduce only once
Adactylidium is a hermaphroditic species that self-fertilizes every hour

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: Bristlecone pines are cited as an extreme longevity example. Approximately how long can they live?
quiz.correct: Over 4000 years — some individual bristlecone pines (Pinus longaeva) are among the oldest known non-clonal organisms on Earth, representing the extreme K-selected end of the continuum
distractors: About 500 years
About 100 years
About 50,000 years

quiz.question: L3 APPLICATION: A plant species produces exactly 1 large, well-provisioned seed per year and lives for 200 years before dying. A second species produces 10,000 tiny seeds per year and lives for only 1 year. Which is r-selected and which is K-selected, and what trade-off does the difference illustrate?
quiz.correct: The 1-seed 200-year plant is K-selected (low fecundity, long life, high per-offspring investment); the 10,000-seed 1-year plant is r-selected (high fecundity, short life, low per-offspring investment). The trade-off is between OFFSPRING NUMBER and OFFSPRING QUALITY — finite resources cannot simultaneously maximize both
distractors: Both are K-selected because both are plants and all plants are K-selected by taxonomy
The 10,000-seed plant is K-selected because it has more total offspring
The 1-seed plant is r-selected because it invests the same resources in one large seed

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: Integrate the finite-energy constraint, the r/K continuum, and iteroparity vs semelparity into an explanation of why semelparous Pacific salmon (single massive spawning event, then death) are evolutionarily stable despite "wasting" their adult body in one reproductive event.
quiz.correct: Pacific salmon face EXTREMELY HIGH ADULT MORTALITY from the arduous upstream migration — surviving to a second reproductive event is very unlikely. Because finite energy must be allocated, and because future reproduction is nearly impossible, optimizing CURRENT reproduction by pouring all energy into one massive spawning event produces more lifetime offspring than reserving energy for a second unlikely attempt. Iteroparity would be favored if adult survival were higher. Semelparity + maximum per-event investment is the ESS when adult survival is ~0
distractors: Pacific salmon are K-selected species that happen to die after reproduction due to arbitrary developmental programming unrelated to life-history theory
Semelparity is an evolutionary mistake that will eventually be corrected in Pacific salmon lineages via selection for iteroparity
Salmon semelparity is driven by group selection for population control

visual.mnemonic: r = reckless; K = careful.
visual.trap: r/K is a continuum, not a binary. Most species are somewhere between the extremes.

diagram.title: r-selected vs K-selected Life History Strategies

nodes.label: r-selected (fast lane)
nodes.detail: Selection maximizes intrinsic growth rate r. Thrives when the population sits BELOW carrying capacity so density-independent mortality dominates. Unstable, disturbed, or unpredictable environments reward this strategy.
nodes.mnemonic: r = rabbits — live fast, die young.
nodes.watchOut: r is a population growth parameter; the strategy is defined by the ENVIRONMENT (below K), not offspring count alone.

nodes.label: K-selected (slow lane)
nodes.detail: Selection maximizes competitive ability at carrying capacity K. Stable, predictable environments at density-dependent equilibrium reward heavy investment per offspring.
nodes.mnemonic: K = K-apes — slow and steady.
nodes.watchOut: K-selected does NOT mean "smart" or "advanced." It means competitive at K.

nodes.label: Many small cheap offspring
nodes.detail: Produce enormous numbers of tiny offspring with almost no energy invested per individual. A single mayfly female lays hundreds to thousands of eggs. Most die; a few survive by luck.
nodes.mnemonic: quantity beats quality

nodes.label: Few large expensive offspring
nodes.detail: Produce a small number of large, well-provisioned offspring. Kiwi lays one egg that is ~20% of the female's body mass. African elephants produce one calf every 4-5 years.
nodes.mnemonic: quality beats quantity

nodes.label: Minimal parental care
nodes.detail: Zero or near-zero investment after laying/birth. Energy saved is re-routed into producing more offspring. Offspring survive or die based on luck, not parenting.

nodes.label: Heavy prolonged parental care
nodes.detail: Extended parenting, defense, and teaching. Human childhood is ~18 years — the most extreme case. Offspring survival depends on continuous parental investment.

nodes.label: Early maturation
nodes.detail: Reach reproductive age quickly. Critical under high extrinsic mortality — if you will probably die young, reproduce first. Mice mature in ~6 weeks.

nodes.label: Late maturation
nodes.detail: Slow growth and delayed reproduction. Bristlecone pines take decades; elephants reach maturity at 10-12 years. Low extrinsic mortality makes waiting a viable strategy.

nodes.label: Short lifespan; often semelparous
nodes.detail: One reproductive event then death (semelparous), or short iteroparous life. Aging is fast because selection on late-life traits is weak under high extrinsic mortality.

nodes.label: Long lifespan; iteroparous
nodes.detail: Reproduce repeatedly over many years. Bristlecone pines live >4000 years. Iteroparity is favored when adult survival is high and single reproductive events are risky.

nodes.label: Unstable variable environment; below K
nodes.detail: Disturbance-prone, unpredictable, density-independent mortality dominates. Population usually sits below carrying capacity, so rapid growth pays off.

nodes.label: Stable environment; competitive at K
nodes.detail: Predictable, saturated, density-dependent mortality dominates. Competition for limited resources at carrying capacity favors competitive ability over raw reproductive rate.
nodes.watchOut: r/K is a CONTINUUM, not a binary. r and K are population growth parameters — the strategy is about ENVIRONMENT, not just offspring number.

nodes.label: r-selected examples
nodes.detail: Mayflies, mice, weeds, rabbits, dandelions, cane toads, most insects, bacteria. Short generations, boom-and-bust population dynamics.

nodes.label: K-selected examples
nodes.detail: Elephants, humans, bristlecone pine, kiwi, albatross, whales, redwoods. Long generations, stable population dynamics at carrying capacity.

nodes.label: Reznick guppy transplant
nodes.detail: 11 years: Trinidad guppies from high-predation pools were transplanted to low-predation streams. The transplanted populations evolved LATER maturation and LARGER offspring — a measurable r-to-K shift in 11 years of natural selection.
nodes.mnemonic: 11 years = evolution in real time

nodes.label: Austad opossum study
nodes.detail: Mainland Virginia opossums (high predation) vs Sapelo Island, Georgia opossums (5000 years isolated, no predators). Island opossums age significantly slower — senescence evolved in just 5000 years under relaxed predation.
nodes.mnemonic: no predators → slower aging

diagram.mnemonic: r = rabbits. K = K-apes. Live fast die young, or slow and steady.

flashcards.front: What is the fundamental principle underlying life history trade-offs?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Energy and time are finite resources, so allocation to one life-history function (growth, reproduction, survival, maintenance) necessarily reduces allocation to others. Organisms cannot simultaneously maximize all fitness components; instead, natural selection optimizes the allocation strategy given ecological conditions. These trade-offs shape every aspect of an organism's schedule of survival and reproduction.

**ELI5:** You only have so many hours and so much energy per day. Spend it all on a big party (reproduction) and you can't study (survive). Spend it all on studying and you miss the party. Life history is about how nature decides the best budget split between staying alive and making babies.

flashcards.front: What is remarkable about the life history of Adactylidium mites?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Adactylidium mites represent an extreme life history strategy. The mother's body contains developing offspring that consume her from the inside. Offspring (mostly female, one male) mate inside the mother's body before she dies and they emerge. The male dies shortly after mating with his sisters inside the mother. The entire life cycle is compressed to an extraordinary degree, with maternal death as an obligate part of reproduction.

**ELI5:** Adactylidium mites live inside their mother, eating her alive. The one brother mates with all his sisters while still inside mom, then dies. The sisters burst out and repeat the cycle. It's the most extreme example of 'giving everything for your kids' -- literally everything, including your life and your body as food.

flashcards.front: Describe the r/K selection continuum. What traits characterize each end?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** r-selected organisms (high r, intrinsic growth rate) are adapted to unstable/unpredictable environments: many small offspring, little or no parental care, rapid maturation, short lifespan, and high mortality. K-selected organisms (near carrying capacity K) are adapted to stable/competitive environments: few large offspring, heavy parental care, slow maturation, long lifespan, and low mortality. Importantly, r/K selection is a continuum, not a dichotomy -- most organisms fall somewhere in between.

**ELI5:** r-strategy: 'Have a million babies, hope some survive' -- like dandelions or mosquitoes. K-strategy: 'Have one or two babies, invest everything in them' -- like elephants or humans. Most animals are somewhere in between on this sliding scale. It's the difference between buying 1000 lottery tickets or investing all your money in one blue-chip stock.

flashcards.front: Give two examples each of extreme r-selected and extreme K-selected organisms.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Extreme r-selected: (1) Mayflies -- adult lifespan of hours to days, mass reproduction, no parental care. (2) Dandelions -- produce thousands of wind-dispersed seeds with no investment per seed. Extreme K-selected: (1) Elephants -- 22-month gestation, one calf, extensive maternal care, 60+ year lifespan. (2) Bristlecone pines -- grow extremely slowly, live 4,000+ years, invest heavily in structural defense and longevity.

**ELI5:** r-extreme: Mayflies live for one day and mate frantically; dandelions scatter thousands of seeds on the wind. K-extreme: Elephants are pregnant for almost 2 years and raise one baby for decades; bristlecone pines grow at a snail's pace but live for thousands of years. Quantity vs. quality taken to the max.

flashcards.front: Why is it incorrect to treat r/K selection as a strict binary classification?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** r/K selection is a continuum, not a dichotomy, because organisms exhibit intermediate combinations of traits. Many species have life-history strategies that blend elements of both ends: sea turtles produce many eggs (r-like) but are long-lived (K-like); some fish are short-lived but provide parental care. The framework is a useful heuristic for understanding life-history variation but oversimplifies the multidimensional nature of life-history evolution. Modern frameworks (e.g., Stearns' pace-of-life) offer more nuanced alternatives.

**ELI5:** Calling something 'r' or 'K' is like calling someone 'tall' or 'short' -- most people are in between. Sea turtles lay hundreds of eggs (seems r) but live 80 years (seems K). Real life is a sliding scale, not two boxes. The r/K labels help you think about trade-offs, but nature doesn't read the textbook.

flashcards.front: How does the kiwi egg illustrate extreme parental investment?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The kiwi lays an egg that is approximately 15-20% of the female's body mass -- the largest egg-to-body-size ratio of any bird. This represents an enormous energetic investment in a single offspring, reflecting a K-selected strategy within birds. The large egg provides extensive yolk reserves, and the chick hatches highly precocial (well-developed, covered in feathers, able to feed independently within days). The mother essentially front-loads parental investment into the egg itself.

**ELI5:** A kiwi egg is about 1/5th of the mom's entire body weight. Imagine being pregnant with a baby that weighs 30 pounds if you weigh 150. The egg is so huge and packed with nutrition that the chick hatches basically ready to go -- like being born already a toddler. The kiwi puts all its parenting effort into one mega-egg instead of many small ones.

flashcards.front: What does opossum energy allocation illustrate about life history trade-offs?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Opossums exemplify the trade-off between current reproduction and future survival. As marsupials with relatively short lifespans (2-3 years), they invest heavily in early and frequent reproduction at the cost of longevity and somatic maintenance. They show rapid senescence, high reproductive output per attempt, and relatively poor immune function and wound healing compared to similarly sized placentals. Their allocation favors reproduction over self-maintenance, consistent with high-extrinsic-mortality environments.

**ELI5:** Opossums are the 'live fast, die young' rock stars of the mammal world. They pour all their energy into making babies early and often, and barely spend anything on body upkeep. It's like spending your whole paycheck on a party and never putting money into home repairs -- the house falls apart fast, but you had a great time while it lasted.

flashcards.front: How do mayflies represent an extreme r-selected life history?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Adult mayflies (Ephemeroptera) live for only hours to a few days. They emerge as adults with vestigial, non-functional mouthparts (they cannot eat), exist solely to reproduce in mass synchronized mating swarms, then die. All energy for adult activity comes from reserves accumulated during the aquatic larval stage (which can last months to years). This represents an extreme allocation of the adult phase entirely to reproduction with zero investment in somatic maintenance.

**ELI5:** Mayflies spend months as underwater babies eating and growing, then emerge as adults that literally cannot eat -- they don't even have working mouths. Their entire adult life is: fly, find a mate, make babies, die -- all in about 24 hours. They're the ultimate 'one job and done' animal. Every calorie goes to making the next generation.

flashcards.front: What are bristlecone pines and why are they considered extreme K-strategists?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Bristlecone pines (Pinus longaeva) are among Earth's oldest living organisms, with some individuals exceeding 4,800 years. They grow extremely slowly in harsh alpine environments, investing heavily in dense, resin-rich wood that resists decay, insects, and fire. Reproduction is slow and modest. Their strategy maximizes longevity and structural persistence over reproductive rate -- the epitome of K-selected allocation toward survival and maintenance rather than rapid reproduction.

**ELI5:** Bristlecone pines are the tortoises of the plant world, but even slower. Some have been alive since before the pyramids were built. They grow a tiny bit each year, build super-tough wood, and reproduce leisurely. Their strategy is basically: 'I'm not in a hurry. I'll outlast everything.' They trade speed for an incredibly long life -- the ultimate K-strategy.

flashcards.front: A population of fish lives in a lake with heavy predation. Would you predict r-selected or K-selected traits, and why?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** You would predict more r-selected traits: earlier maturation, higher fecundity (more offspring per reproductive event), smaller offspring size, and shorter lifespan. High extrinsic mortality (predation) means that the probability of surviving to reproduce again is low, so natural selection favors maximizing early and current reproductive output rather than investing in longevity or future reproduction. This prediction has been confirmed in classic studies such as Reznick's guppy experiments.

**ELI5:** If fish keep getting eaten, the winners are the ones who grow up fast and have tons of babies ASAP -- because there might not be a tomorrow. Investing in a long life makes no sense if you're probably lunch before next year. It's like living in a neighborhood with lots of robberies: you'd spend your money now rather than save for a retirement you might not reach.

flashcards.front: Explain why 'energy allocation' is central to understanding life history strategies.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Organisms acquire a finite energy budget from their environment. This budget must be partitioned among competing demands: growth, maintenance/repair (somatic investment), immune function, and reproduction. Increasing allocation to one function necessarily decreases allocation to others (the principle of allocation). Life history theory predicts that natural selection optimizes this allocation to maximize lifetime reproductive success given the organism's ecological context (predation regime, resource availability, environmental stability).

**ELI5:** Every organism gets a daily energy allowance -- like an allowance of $100. You can spend it on growing bigger ($30), fixing injuries ($20), fighting off sickness ($20), or making babies ($30). But you can't spend $150. Life history is about how evolution decides the best way to split that $100 for each species based on their lifestyle and dangers.

flashcards.front: How does the concept of 'current vs. future reproduction' capture the core life history trade-off?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The fundamental trade-off in life history theory is between investing in current reproduction (immediate offspring production) vs. future reproduction (survival and growth that enable later breeding). Organisms that invest heavily in current reproduction (semelparity, high fecundity now) risk higher mortality and reduced future reproductive potential. Those that invest in survival/growth (iteroparity, restraint now) may produce more total offspring over a longer lifetime but risk dying before reproducing again. The optimal balance depends on the probability of surviving to future breeding opportunities.

**ELI5:** It's the ultimate 'spend now vs. save' question. Have 10 babies this year but maybe die from exhaustion? Or have 2 babies this year, stay healthy, and have 2 more for the next 8 years? If the world is dangerous (high mortality), spend now. If the world is safe, invest for the future. Every organism's life is shaped by this one decision.

nodes.title: Extrinsic Mortality, Aging, & Senescence
nodes.subtitle: Why organisms age — Medawar and Williams (Lec 12 slides 12-27)

popup.heading: Lecture 12 — Why Organisms Age

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body:
Extrinsic mortality (random death from predation, accident, disease) causes the FORCE OF SELECTION to decline with age — older individuals are a shrinking fraction of the population so selection on late-life traits is weak. MUTATION ACCUMULATION (Medawar): mutations with only late-life deleterious effects are not efficiently purged by selection because they express only in individuals who have largely already reproduced. ANTAGONISTIC PLEIOTROPY (Williams 1957): a gene with early benefit and late cost is FAVORED because the early benefit is selected strongly while the late cost experiences weak selection. AUSTAD'S OPOSSUM STUDY: Sepelo Island (Georgia, USA) opossums have lived on a predator-free island for ~5000 years, evolving slower aging than mainland opossums. GARTER SNAKE STUDY at Eagle Lake: "meadow" populations (low predation) mature slowly, produce ~4 babies, and live 8 years; "lake" populations (high predation) mature fast, produce ~8 babies, and live 4 years. REZNICK'S GUPPY 11-year transplant experiment in Trinidad directly confirmed these predictions: transplanted guppies evolved faster life histories within ~30 generations. About 100,000 people die daily worldwide from age-related diseases.

MEDAWAR vs. WILLIAMS — COMPLEMENTARY THEORIES: Medawar (mutation accumulation) explains why deleterious late-acting mutations accumulate unpurged — selection is too weak to remove mutations whose effects appear after most reproduction is done (the "selection shadow"). These mutations are sheltered from purging by timing alone. Williams (antagonistic pleiotropy) explains why some genes ACTIVELY cause aging — alleles that boost early reproduction but damage late survival are favored because early fitness benefits outweigh late costs. Together: Medawar explains passive accumulation of damage; Williams explains active trade-offs. Both are complementary, not competing — aging is an emergent property of both processes.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body:
• Extrinsic mortality – mortality due to external events
• Extrinsic mortality rate – rate at which external events lead to the death of individuals in a population
• Probability of survival to any given age declines with time
• Timing of mutation expression across life span
• Early life effect of deleterious mutation
• Very strong effect on fitness
• odds of being alive are greater at a young age

examples: High extrinsic mortality (predators, disease) favors fast reproduction and short lifespan — there's no point "saving" resources for old age if you probably won't reach it.
Low extrinsic mortality favors slow development and longer lifespan — energy investment in maintenance pays off.
