**TECHNICAL:** An ESS (Maynard Smith & Price 1973) is a strategy that, when adopted by most members of a population, cannot be invaded by any alternative (mutant) strategy when the alternative starts at low frequency. Formally, strategy I is an ESS if: for all alternative strategies J, either E(I,I) > E(J,I), or E(I,I) = E(J,I) and E(I,J) > E(J,J), where E(X,Y) is the payoff to strategy X against strategy Y. An ESS is resistant to invasion, not necessarily optimal for the population.

**ELI5:** An ESS is a strategy that, once everyone adopts it, no rare rebel can do better by being different. It's like a fashion trend that can't be disrupted: if everyone wears blue and a few rebels try red, the red-wearers don't get any advantage. The ESS is 'stable' because no mutant strategy can sneak in and take over.

flashcards.front: Why are fitness payoffs in ESS models frequency-dependent?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In game-theoretic models, an individual's fitness depends not just on its own strategy but on the strategies of others in the population (the frequency of each strategy). A hawk does well when opponents are mostly doves but poorly when opponents are mostly hawks. This frequency-dependence creates dynamic fitness landscapes where the optimal strategy changes as population composition shifts, preventing any single strategy from being universally 'best.'

**ELI5:** Your success depends on what everyone ELSE is doing. Being tough is great when everyone's a pushover, but terrible when everyone's tough (you keep getting injured). It's like playing rock-paper-scissors -- there's no 'best move,' only the best move given what your opponent plays. That's why fitness depends on frequency: the rarer you are, the more (or less) valuable your strategy becomes.

flashcards.front: Describe the Hawk-Dove game (Maynard Smith & Price 1973). What are the strategies?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The Hawk-Dove game models conflict over a resource of value V. Hawks always escalate fights and only retreat when injured; Doves always display but retreat if the opponent escalates. Payoffs: Hawk vs. Hawk = (V-C)/2 each (win or lose with equal probability, C = cost of injury). Hawk vs. Dove = V for Hawk, 0 for Dove. Dove vs. Dove = V/2 each (share after display). The ESS depends on V/C: if V>C, always-Hawk is ESS; if V<C, a mixed ESS emerges with proportion p(Hawk) = V/C.

**ELI5:** Two strategies: Hawks fight to win or get hurt; Doves show off but run away from real fights. Hawk meets Dove = Hawk takes everything. Hawk meets Hawk = bloody fight, both might lose. Dove meets Dove = they posture and split evenly. If the prize is worth more than the injury cost, everyone becomes a Hawk. If injuries are costly, you get a mix of Hawks and Doves where neither can take over completely.

flashcards.front: In the Hawk-Dove game, what is the mixed ESS when V < C, and how is the proportion of Hawks calculated?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** When V < C (injury cost exceeds resource value), pure Hawk is not an ESS (too many injuries). The mixed ESS has a proportion p of Hawks where p = V/C. At this frequency, the average payoff for Hawk equals the average payoff for Dove, so neither strategy can invade. If Hawks become too common, their average payoff drops (too many costly fights); if too rare, their payoff rises (easy wins against Doves). This can be interpreted as each individual playing Hawk with probability V/C, or as a polymorphic population.

**ELI5:** When fights are more costly than the prize, you don't want EVERYONE to be a fighter (too many injuries). The stable mix is: fraction of fighters = prize value / fight cost. If the prize is worth $50 and a fight costs $200, then 50/200 = 25% fighters, 75% wimps. At this balance, both strategies earn the same average payoff. Too many fighters? Bad deal (too many hospital bills). Too few? Great deal (easy wins). It self-corrects.

flashcards.front: How do side-blotched lizards illustrate ESS and frequency-dependent selection in nature?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The three male morphs of Uta stansburiana (orange, blue, yellow) represent a real-world intransitive competitive hierarchy (rock-paper-scissors). No single morph is an ESS because each is vulnerable to one alternative: orange beats blue, blue beats yellow, yellow beats orange. Fitness is strictly frequency-dependent -- the rarest morph has the highest fitness. The population cycles through morph frequencies with ~12-year periodicity (Sinervo & Lively 1996). This demonstrates that ESSs can be dynamic (cycling) rather than static equilibria.

**ELI5:** These lizards literally play rock-paper-scissors in real life. No strategy permanently wins because each one loses to one of the others. Whichever color is rare gets an advantage, so the 'winner' keeps rotating in ~12-year cycles. It's a living, breathing example of game theory where there's no single best move -- just an endless cycle of temporary advantages.

flashcards.front: Is an ESS necessarily the strategy that maximizes total population fitness? Why or why not?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** No. An ESS is resistant to invasion by rare mutants, but it does not necessarily maximize average population fitness. For example, in the Hawk-Dove game when V < C, the mixed ESS includes some Hawks, and Hawk-Hawk encounters waste resources on fighting. An all-Dove population would have higher average fitness (no fighting costs), but it is not an ESS because a rare Hawk would invade and dominate. The ESS reflects individual selection, which may lead to outcomes suboptimal for the group.

**ELI5:** An ESS is the strategy that can't be beaten, not the one that makes everyone happy. A world of all-Doves (everyone shares peacefully) would be great for everyone, but one bully (Hawk) would exploit them and take over. So the stable outcome includes some bullies, even though that's worse for the group. Individual selfishness beats group harmony -- that's why the ESS isn't always the 'best' answer for everyone.

flashcards.front: What does it mean for an ESS to 'resist invasion at low frequency'?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** An ESS resists invasion by any alternative strategy that appears as a rare mutant (low frequency). The key is that fitness payoffs are frequency-dependent: the mutant's payoff is evaluated against a population composed almost entirely of the resident ESS strategy. If the mutant cannot achieve higher fitness than the resident when rare, it will be eliminated by selection. The ESS is stable not because alternatives can't arise, but because they can't spread from rarity.

**ELI5:** Imagine everyone in town uses Strategy A. A few weirdos try Strategy B. If Strategy B can't do better than A when almost everyone else is doing A, the weirdos fail and disappear. That's what 'resist invasion at low frequency' means -- the occasional rebel can't get a foothold. It's not that rebels never show up; it's that they can never grow from a tiny minority into the majority.

flashcards.front: In the Hawk-Dove game, what happens when V > C?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** When V > C (the resource value exceeds the cost of injury), always-Hawk is the pure ESS. Even though Hawk-Hawk encounters incur costs, the expected payoff (V-C)/2 is positive, making it always worthwhile to fight. A Dove in this population gets 0 against Hawks, so Dove cannot invade. Every individual should escalate because the prize is worth the risk of injury. This represents scenarios where the contested resource is extremely valuable relative to fighting costs.

**ELI5:** When the prize is bigger than the hospital bill, it pays to fight every time. Even if you lose half your fights, the wins are worth more than the injuries cost. In this world, being a wimp gets you nothing because every bully takes your stuff. So everyone becomes a bully -- and that's actually the stable strategy because the jackpot is just too good to walk away from.

flashcards.front: Who formalized the concept of ESS and in what year?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** John Maynard Smith and George Price formalized the concept of the Evolutionarily Stable Strategy (ESS) in 1973, publishing the foundational paper 'The Logic of Animal Conflict.' They applied game theory (originally from economics, von Neumann & Morgenstern) to evolutionary biology, providing a framework for analyzing frequency-dependent selection in behavioral strategies. The ESS concept revolutionized behavioral ecology and became a cornerstone of evolutionary game theory.

**ELI5:** John Maynard Smith and George Price invented the ESS idea in 1973. They borrowed math from economics (game theory -- the stuff about poker strategies and business competition) and applied it to animal behavior. Before them, people struggled to explain why animals don't always fight to the death. Their framework showed that fighting behavior follows mathematical rules, just like poker.

flashcards.front: Why does individual selection typically beat group selection in determining which strategies persist?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Individual selection acts on variation within groups every generation, whereas group selection requires differential extinction/reproduction of entire groups -- a much slower and weaker process. A selfish strategy that increases individual fitness within a group will spread even if it harms the group's total fitness, because the individual advantage accrues immediately. Group-beneficial strategies are undermined by selfish mutants within groups long before groups can differentially go extinct. This is why ESSs reflect individual advantage, not group optima.

**ELI5:** Individual selection is like a pickpocket working every day -- small, constant, immediate gains. Group selection is like hoping your whole neighborhood wins the lottery -- rare and slow. The pickpocket always beats the lottery because he acts faster. That's why selfish strategies win over group-friendly ones: by the time 'good' groups would outcompete 'bad' ones, the selfish individuals have already taken over from within.

flashcards.front: Can a population have more than one ESS simultaneously?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Yes, multiple ESSs can exist for the same game. Which ESS a population reaches depends on initial conditions (the starting frequency of strategies). Once a population settles on one ESS, it cannot be invaded by strategies from another ESS equilibrium at low frequency, but a large perturbation could shift the population to the alternative ESS. Multiple ESSs mean the evolutionary outcome is historically contingent -- different populations of the same species could stabilize on different strategies.

**ELI5:** Yes! A game can have multiple stable outcomes, like how a ball can rest in either of two valleys. Which valley you end up in depends on where you started rolling. Once you're in one valley (ESS), a small nudge won't push you to the other. But a big enough kick could. That's why different populations of the same species sometimes behave completely differently -- they landed in different 'valleys.'

flashcards.front: Calculate the mixed ESS proportion of Hawks when V=50 and C=200.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The mixed ESS proportion of Hawks = V/C = 50/200 = 0.25, or 25%. At this equilibrium, 25% of the population plays Hawk and 75% plays Dove (or equivalently, each individual plays Hawk with probability 0.25). At this frequency, the expected payoff for Hawk equals the expected payoff for Dove: E(Hawk) = 0.25 * (50-200)/2 + 0.75 * 50 = 0.25 * (-75) + 37.5 = -18.75 + 37.5 = 18.75. E(Dove) = 0.25 * 0 + 0.75 * 25 = 18.75. Equal payoffs confirm the ESS.

**ELI5:** Prize = $50, fight cost = $200. Hawks fraction = 50/200 = 25%. So 1 in 4 individuals is a fighter. At this mix, fighters and non-fighters earn exactly the same on average ($18.75). If there were more fighters, fighting wouldn't pay (too many expensive brawls). If fewer, fighting would pay too well (easy pickings). 25% is the sweet spot where neither strategy has an edge.

nodes.title: Levels of Selection: Group vs Individual
nodes.subtitle: Why selection mostly acts on individuals (Lec 13 slides 8-13)

popup.heading: Lecture 13 — Levels of Selection

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Classical group selection usually FAILS because a selfish mutant can invade any cooperative group and take over. Within-group selection favoring selfish individuals typically acts faster than between-group selection favoring cooperative groups. Apparent "group benefits" are almost always explained by individual-level fitness gains. BENEFITS OF GROUP LIVING operate through INDIVIDUAL advantages: (1) DILUTION effect — your odds of being picked off by a predator decrease as group size grows; (2) VIGILANCE — more eyes watching means earlier threat detection; (3) COOPERATIVE DEFENSE against predators; (4) COOPERATIVE FORAGING — African wild dogs in larger packs each get more energy per day than solo hunters; (5) CLIFF SWALLOWS form colonies where predator vigilance benefits each individual. GROUP SIZE COSTS: conspicuousness to predators, within-group food and mate competition, and disease transmission. The correct framing is always to ask "how does this behavior increase the INDIVIDUAL's fitness?" — not "how does it help the species?"

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body:
• Selection on groups or individuals?
• Group selection logic
• Adaptations minimize the chances that the species or populations as a whole would go extinct
• Individuals cooperate to enhance group success
• …But models and empirical evidence suggest
• Individuals with mutations that make them behave selfishly will take over a group of cooperators
• Birds lay 6 eggs per
• Population rapidly increases and wipes out food resources quickly

sections.label: KEY EXAMPLES
sections.body: LEMMING MYTH DEBUNKED: The myth that lemmings deliberately commit mass suicide was popularized by a 1958 Disney documentary ("White Wilderness") that staged lemming mass drownings for dramatic footage — crews literally threw lemmings off cliffs. In reality, lemming population crashes are driven by food shortages, predation, and accidental drowning during migrations across bodies of water. The myth misrepresents lemmings as self-sacrificing for group benefit — a textbook example of naive group selectionism. Individual selection cannot favor "suicide for the good of the species" because sacrificers would be eliminated while refusers reproduce, quickly purging any suicide allele from the population. This is directly addressed by Williams (1966) in his critique of group selection.

examples: Classical group selection (Wynne-Edwards) proposed that traits "good for the species" evolve because groups with those traits outcompete others.
Williams' critique (1966): naive group selection usually fails because individual-level selection is stronger — selfish individuals in a group invade and destroy group traits.
Group hunting in African wild dogs works not because it helps the species but because each individual hunter gets more food than hunting alone.
Cliff swallows: colonies provide anti-predator vigilance, but each individual benefits from being in the group.
warnings: "This behavior evolved for the good of the species" is almost always WRONG on an evolution exam. You must explain how individual-level fitness benefits maintain the trait.

Lemmings do NOT intentionally leap off cliffs to control population — this is a myth popularized by the 1958 Disney documentary. Mass deaths are accidental panic-flight, not deliberate sacrifice. It is a classic example of falsely invoking group selection.
popup.mnemonic: Selection acts on the INDIVIDUAL. "Good for the species" = wrong framing.

flashcard.front: Why is "group selection" controversial and why does most modern evolutionary biology emphasize individual-level selection?
flashcard.back: GROUP SELECTION hypothesizes that traits evolve because they benefit the GROUP (or species), even if they reduce individual fitness. CLASSICAL EXAMPLE: "Lemmings leap off cliffs to prevent overpopulation." PROBLEM (Williams 1966): individual selection is much STRONGER than group selection in most cases, because the fitness differences between individuals WITHIN a group are larger and change faster than fitness differences between groups. A "selfish" individual who does NOT cooperate gains an immediate fitness advantage within the group — their genes spread. Within a few generations, the cooperative trait is replaced by the selfish one. For group selection to work, groups must have very restricted gene flow, very high extinction/founding rates, and the group-beneficial trait must be strongly heritable at the group level. Modern biology generally explains "group-like" behavior through (1) KIN SELECTION (helping relatives who share your genes), (2) RECIPROCAL ALTRUISM (I help you expecting future help), or (3) MUTUALISTIC BENEFIT (cooperation that also helps the cooperator directly). MODERN MULTI-LEVEL SELECTION theory accepts some group selection under narrow conditions but emphasizes that most apparent "group selection" is really selection at lower levels.

quiz.question: A population of wolves hunts in packs. What level of selection best explains pack hunting?
quiz.correct: Individual selection — each wolf gets more food per hunt when hunting as a pack than alone
distractors: Group selection only — for the good of the pack
Kin selection only — because wolves are related
Pure altruism with no individual benefit

quiz.question: George Williams (1966) argued that naive group selection fails. What is the core reason invading selfish individuals destroy a group-beneficial cooperative trait?
quiz.correct: Within-group selection favoring selfish individuals is stronger than between-group selection favoring cooperative groups — selfish cheaters spread faster than cooperative groups go extinct
distractors: Groups with cooperative traits go extinct faster because they use more resources
Selfish individuals always have lower fitness than cooperators within the same group
Gene flow between groups is too high for group-level selection to act, but too low for individual-level selection to work

quiz.question: Lemmings supposedly leap off cliffs to control population density — a classic "group selection" story. Why do biologists reject this explanation?
quiz.correct: The deaths are accidental (panic-flight behavior, not intentional sacrifice), and even if they were deliberate, individual-level selection would immediately favor any lemming that refused to leap — eliminating the suicidal trait within generations
distractors: Lemmings are solitary animals and would have no mechanism to coordinate mass behavior, so group selection cannot apply
Group selection could work in lemmings if relatedness within groups is high enough — the real problem is that r values have never been measured
The lemming behavior does evolve by group selection, but only because lemming groups have very high extinction rates which satisfies the special conditions Williams required

quiz.question: Modern multi-level selection theory accepts group selection under narrow conditions. Which combination of conditions makes group selection most plausible?
quiz.correct: High heritability of group-level traits, very low gene flow between groups (small founding groups), and high between-group extinction/replacement rates — so group composition is stable long enough for between-group fitness differences to matter
distractors: Large group sizes with high internal relatedness, so Hamilton's rule is satisfied and kin selection amplifies group-level effects
Frequency-dependent selection within each group, so cheaters are penalized by their own success and cooperation is maintained without any group-level component
Strong sexual selection within groups, so cooperators attract more mates and outreproduce cheaters before cheating can spread through the group

quiz.question: Who proposed the strong critique of group selection in 1966 that shifted evolutionary biology toward individual-level thinking?
quiz.correct: George C. Williams, in his book "Adaptation and Natural Selection" — Williams argued that appeals to group selection should be used only when individual-level explanations fail, and most "group-for-the-good-of-the-species" claims could be explained by individual selection
distractors: V.C. Wynne-Edwards, 1962 — Wynne-Edwards actually supported group selection with examples like clutch size regulation
Richard Dawkins, 1976 — Dawkins wrote "The Selfish Gene" but did not formally critique group selection until later
E.O. Wilson, 1975 — Wilson later revived group selection theory rather than critiquing it

quiz.question: The cliff swallow (Petrochelidon pyrrhonota) forms dense colonies that appear to provide group-level benefits such as predator vigilance. Why is this NOT a clean example of group selection?
quiz.correct: Each individual swallow benefits directly by being in the colony — it has earlier warning of predators, more eyes to spot threats, and can benefit from others' alarm calls. This is explained by individual-level selection, not group selection; the colony is a byproduct of individual benefits
distractors: Cliff swallows are actually solitary and do not form colonies — the textbook example is incorrect
Colonies cannot evolve by group selection because swallows are asexual and group selection requires sexual reproduction
The colony is a clear case of group selection because no single swallow can defend against predators alone, proving that cooperation is for the group's benefit

quiz.question: The myth that lemmings deliberately commit mass suicide was popularized by what?
quiz.correct: A 1958 Disney documentary ("White Wilderness") staged lemming mass drownings for dramatic effect — the footage created a cultural myth that real lemmings do not actually exhibit. Actual lemming population crashes are due to food shortages and dispersal accidents, not intentional suicide
distractors: Wynne-Edwards documented intentional lemming suicide in his 1962 book, providing the original scientific basis for the myth
Historical reports from medieval Scandinavia accurately described lemming suicide behavior, which has since declined due to climate change
The myth originated from scientific papers in the 1970s that mis-measured lemming mortality rates as deliberate rather than accidental

quiz.question: Which of the following is MOST likely to be correctly explained by group selection under modern multi-level selection theory?
quiz.correct: Reduced virulence in pathogens that depend on host survival for transmission — pathogens that kill hosts too quickly go extinct with their hosts, so between-host selection favors less virulent strains even when within-host selection favors more virulent ones
distractors: Altruistic alarm calls in ground squirrels — modern explanation is kin selection (Hamilton's rule), not group selection
Pack hunting in wolves — this is individual-level selection because each wolf benefits
Human cooperation in large societies — this is explained by reciprocal altruism and cultural group selection, not naive group selection

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: In African wild dog packs, what observed empirical pattern supports an individual-fitness explanation for group hunting?
quiz.correct: Each individual dog obtains MORE energy per day in larger packs than when hunting alone or in smaller packs — cooperative hunting is not altruistic sacrifice but a behavior that directly increases per-capita food intake for every participant
distractors: Individual dogs receive less food per hunt in larger packs — they cooperate despite personal cost
Pack size has no effect on individual food intake
Only dominant individuals benefit from larger packs while subordinates starve

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: Name at least two benefits of group living at the INDIVIDUAL (not group) level.
quiz.correct: Dilution effect (lower individual predation probability), increased vigilance (more eyes detecting threats), cooperative defense, cooperative foraging with higher per-capita yield, and shared information about food or predators — each of these directly benefits the individual in the group
distractors: Altruistic self-sacrifice and willingness to die for the group
Group-level resource sharing that equalizes fitness across all members
Population-level regulation of reproduction to prevent overpopulation

quiz.question: L3 APPLICATION: Cliff swallows (Petrochelidon pyrrhonota) form dense nesting colonies. A critic argues this "proves group selection because colony members reduce predation for everyone." Refute this with an individual-level explanation.
quiz.correct: Colony formation evolved because EACH INDIVIDUAL benefits from: (1) dilution (predation risk is distributed across many birds, so any individual bird is less likely to be the victim); (2) vigilance (more eyes see predators earlier); (3) information transfer about food sources. These individual benefits are sufficient to explain colony evolution without invoking group selection — each swallow gains fitness by joining the colony regardless of what others do
distractors: The critic is correct — cliff swallow coloniality is solid evidence for group selection
Colony formation is a byproduct with no adaptive explanation — neither individual nor group selection applies
Colony formation is maintained only by phylogenetic inertia from ancestral solitary behaviors

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: Explain why "lemmings throw themselves off cliffs to control population" is BOTH an empirical error (the behavior does not occur as described) AND a conceptual error (even if it occurred, it could not evolve by group selection).
quiz.correct: EMPIRICAL ERROR: real lemming mass deaths are accidental drowning during migration panic, not intentional population control. The cliff-jumping myth was staged for the 1958 Disney documentary "White Wilderness" and is not a real behavior. CONCEPTUAL ERROR: even if lemmings DID intentionally sacrifice themselves, group selection could not maintain this because a mutant lemming who refused to jump would have higher fitness than the sacrificing ones; selfish refusers would rapidly replace self-sacrificers within a few generations. Both errors must be addressed to correctly reject the claim
distractors: The claim is correct on both levels — lemmings do jump and this is a stable group-selected trait
The empirical claim is false but the conceptual claim is valid — lemmings could theoretically evolve such a trait if they actually existed
Only the conceptual error matters because the empirical claim is irrelevant to evolutionary theory

visual.mnemonic: Gene → Individual → Kin → Rarely group.
visual.trap: Do NOT say a trait evolved "for the good of the species" unless you can rule out individual-level explanations.

flashcards.front: Why does group selection usually fail as an explanation for altruistic behavior?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Group selection fails because selfish mutants within cooperative groups gain an individual fitness advantage and increase in frequency faster than groups can differentially go extinct. A group of altruists may outperform a group of selfish individuals, but WITHIN any mixed group, selfish individuals always do better than altruists (they reap benefits without paying costs). Since within-group selection acts every generation while between-group selection operates over much longer timescales, individual selfishness typically overwhelms group-level cooperation.

**ELI5:** Imagine a classroom where everyone agrees to share homework answers (group cooperation). One cheater copies everyone's work but never shares their own. The cheater gets the best grade in the class every time. Before the 'cheater class' can lose to the 'honest class' in some big competition, cheaters have already taken over from within. That's why group selection usually loses to individual selfishness.

flashcards.front: What was George Williams' (1966) key critique of group selection thinking?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In 'Adaptation and Natural Selection' (1966), George Williams argued that adaptations should be explained by the simplest sufficient level of selection -- typically individual selection. He criticized the then-common practice of invoking group selection ('for the good of the species') to explain behaviors like restraint in reproduction. Williams showed that such behaviors can almost always be better explained by individual-level selection and that group selection requires very restrictive conditions (small groups, low migration, strong group-level variation) that rarely hold in nature.

**ELI5:** Williams told biologists to stop being lazy with their explanations. Instead of saying 'animals do X for the good of the species,' he demanded: prove it's not just selfish individuals doing what benefits THEMSELVES. He showed that almost every 'group benefit' behavior has a simpler selfish explanation. It was like telling scientists: 'Don't assume magic when physics works just fine.'

flashcards.front: Why is 'for the good of the species' typically wrong framing in evolutionary biology?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Natural selection primarily acts on individuals (and their genes), not species. Traits spread because they benefit the individuals carrying them, not because they benefit the species. Group selection is theoretically possible but requires stringent conditions rarely met in nature. The 'good of the species' framing (naive group selectionism) incorrectly predicts that organisms will restrain reproduction or self-sacrifice for species persistence, when individual selection predicts -- and observations confirm -- that organisms maximize their own reproductive success.

**ELI5:** Saying animals do things 'for the good of the species' is like saying employees work for free 'for the good of the company.' They don't -- they work for their own paycheck (genes). Evolution doesn't care about species survival; it rewards individuals who leave the most copies of their own DNA. The species benefits or suffers as a side effect, not as a goal.

flashcards.front: Name five individual-level benefits of group living.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Dilution effect -- larger groups reduce each individual's probability of being the one caught by a predator. (2) Enhanced vigilance -- more eyes detect predators sooner, allowing each individual to spend less time scanning and more time foraging. (3) Cooperative defense -- groups can mob or deter predators that no individual could repel alone. (4) Cooperative foraging -- groups can locate, drive, or capture prey more efficiently (e.g., African wild dogs). (5) Information sharing -- cliff swallows follow successful foragers to food sources.

**ELI5:** Why hang out in a group? (1) Safety in numbers -- the lion eats 1 zebra, not all 100. (2) More eyes watching for danger -- you can eat while your neighbor scans for hawks. (3) Team defense -- a mob of birds can scare off a hawk that one bird couldn't. (4) Team hunting -- like wolves working together to bring down prey none could catch alone. (5) Following the smart one to dinner -- if your neighbor found food, follow them.

flashcards.front: Name three costs of group living.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Conspicuousness -- larger groups are more detectable by predators, potentially attracting more attacks overall even if per-individual risk is diluted. (2) Increased competition -- group members compete for limited food, mates, nesting sites, and other resources. (3) Disease transmission -- higher density facilitates the spread of parasites and pathogens within groups. Each of these costs can be explained through individual-level selection, without invoking group-level disadvantages.

**ELI5:** Groups have downsides: (1) A big crowd is easier to spot -- like a giant bonfire vs. a candle. (2) Everyone's fighting over the same food and mates -- too many mouths at the same table. (3) Diseases spread fast in crowds -- just like how colds rip through a crowded school. These costs are why not ALL animals live in groups; it's a trade-off.

flashcards.front: How do African wild dogs illustrate cooperative foraging as an individual-level benefit of group living?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** African wild dogs (Lycaon pictus) hunt cooperatively in packs, taking turns chasing prey over long distances (relay pursuit). Individual dogs could not sustain the pace needed to exhaust large prey, but the pack's cooperative strategy allows them to capture animals much larger than themselves. Each individual benefits from the shared kill -- obtaining more food than it could from solo hunting. Pack hunting increases per-capita foraging success, making cooperation individually advantageous without requiring group selection.

**ELI5:** Wild dogs take turns chasing the same antelope, like a relay race. No single dog could run down the prey alone, but together they exhaust it. Everyone gets a share of a big meal that none could have caught solo. Each dog joins the pack because it personally gets more food, not because it's 'helping the group.' It's selfishly smart, not selflessly noble.

flashcards.front: How do cliff swallows demonstrate individual benefits from group living?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Cliff swallows (Petrochelidon pyrrhonota) nest colonially and benefit from information sharing about food sources. When a bird discovers an ephemeral insect swarm, others in the colony can observe its successful return and follow it on subsequent foraging trips. Individual birds in larger colonies have higher foraging success because they can exploit the reconnaissance of many colony-mates. This information parasitism is an individual-level benefit of group living.

**ELI5:** Cliff swallows living in big apartment complexes spy on their neighbors. When one bird comes home with a full belly, others follow it next time to find the same food jackpot. Living in a group means you have dozens of scouts searching for food, and you just follow whoever finds it first. Each bird benefits individually by mooching off everyone else's search efforts.

flashcards.front: What is the 'lemming myth' and what actually happened (1958 Disney documentary)?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The popular myth that lemmings commit mass suicide 'for the good of the species' (to reduce overpopulation) was reinforced by the 1958 Disney documentary 'White Wilderness,' in which filmmakers actually pushed lemmings off a cliff to create dramatic footage. In reality, lemmings do experience population cycles and mass dispersal, but they do not intentionally commit suicide. Their occasional mass deaths during migration (drowning while swimming) are accidental, not adaptive group-level behavior. The myth is a textbook example of naive group selectionist thinking.

**ELI5:** Lemmings do NOT throw themselves off cliffs to save their species. That myth came from a fake Disney movie in 1958 where the filmmakers literally shoved them off a ledge for the camera. In real life, lemmings sometimes die while migrating across rivers (they're bad swimmers), but it's accidental, not noble sacrifice. The whole story is a perfect example of the wrong way to think about evolution -- animals don't kill themselves 'for the good of the species.'

flashcards.front: How does the dilution effect explain why individual prey animals benefit from living in herds?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The dilution effect states that in a group of N individuals, each individual's probability of being the one captured by a predator during an attack is 1/N (assuming the predator takes only one victim). As group size increases, the per-individual risk of predation decreases. Importantly, this is an individual-level benefit: each animal joins or stays in the group because doing so reduces its personal predation risk, not because herding benefits the species.

**ELI5:** If a lion attacks a herd of 100 zebras, your chance of being the one it catches is 1 in 100. If you were alone, it would be 1 in 1. So every zebra personally benefits from hiding in the crowd -- the bigger the crowd, the less likely YOU are the unlucky one. It's purely selfish math, not teamwork. You're basically using everyone else as a shield.

flashcards.front: Under what restrictive conditions could group selection theoretically work?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Group selection could theoretically work when: (1) groups are small (increasing random variation between groups), (2) migration between groups is very low (preventing selfish genes from spreading between groups), (3) group extinction/reproduction rates are high relative to individual generation time, and (4) there is strong between-group variation in the altruistic trait. These conditions are rarely satisfied simultaneously in nature, which is why group selection is generally considered a weak evolutionary force compared to individual selection.

**ELI5:** Group selection could work if: (1) groups are tiny (so random chance creates groups of mostly altruists), (2) nobody moves between groups (so cheaters can't immigrate), (3) whole groups die or reproduce quickly (so bad groups disappear fast), and (4) groups are really different from each other in niceness level. In the real world, these conditions almost never all hold at once, which is why group selection rarely wins.

flashcards.front: How does enhanced vigilance in groups represent individual rather than group-level selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In a group, each individual can reduce its personal scanning time (and increase foraging time) because other group members are also watching for predators. The probability that at least one individual detects an approaching predator increases with group size ('many-eyes' effect). Each individual benefits by trading some predator detection to others in exchange for more feeding time. This is individually advantageous: the decision to be in a group increases each member's personal survival and feeding efficiency.

**ELI5:** In a group, you can eat while your neighbors watch for danger. The more neighbors, the safer you can feel spending less time looking around and more time eating. You're not being generous by 'taking a shift' -- you're being selfish by letting others do the watching while you chow down. Everyone benefits individually, which is why the group stays together. No noble sacrifice required.

flashcards.front: A population biologist argues that deer limit their reproduction 'to prevent overgrazing for the good of the herd.' What is wrong with this argument?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** This is naive group selectionism. Individual deer that restrain reproduction would be outcompeted by 'selfish' deer that reproduce maximally -- the restraining lineage would shrink while the selfish lineage grows, within the same group. Any apparent reproductive restraint is better explained by individual-level trade-offs: females that produce too many fawns suffer higher mortality, poorer offspring quality, or reduced future reproduction. The optimal individual reproductive rate is set by life-history trade-offs, not species-level self-regulation.

**ELI5:** If one deer 'selflessly' has fewer babies while her neighbor has tons, the neighbor's genes take over. There's no reward for being responsible -- evolution doesn't give gold stars for self-restraint. Any limits on reproduction are because having too many babies hurts the MOTHER (she gets exhausted, her babies are weaker), not because she's worried about overgrazing for future deer. It's always about personal consequences, not species welfare.

nodes.title: Kin Selection, Inclusive Fitness & Altruism
nodes.subtitle: Hamilton's rule and the evolution of helping (Lec 13 slides 14-21)

popup.heading: Lecture 13 — Kin Selection and Altruism

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: An individual's total fitness has TWO components: DIRECT FITNESS (own reproduction) + INDIRECT FITNESS (through helped relatives' reproduction, weighted by relatedness) = INCLUSIVE FITNESS. COEFFICIENT OF RELATEDNESS (r) = probability two individuals share an allele identical by descent: offspring r=0.5, full siblings r=0.5, half-siblings r=0.25, grandchildren r=0.25, first cousins r=0.125. HAMILTON'S RULE (1964): altruism evolves when rB > C, where r=relatedness of actor to recipient, B=fitness benefit to recipient, C=fitness cost to actor. Note: ONLY benefit is discounted by relatedness; cost is not. HALDANE'S JOKE: "I would lay down my life for two brothers or eight cousins" — exactly satisfies rB ≥ C (2×0.5 = 1 and 8×0.125 = 1). Classic examples: meerkats share sentry duty among kin; Peromyscus brother-sperm cooperation. HAPLODIPLOIDY (Hymenoptera — ants, bees, wasps): females diploid, males haploid, so full sisters share r=0.75 (higher than parent-offspring r=0.5); this MAY HAVE FACILITATED the evolution of eusociality, though it is not sufficient alone — many haplodiploid species are NOT eusocial.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body:
• Altruism: When a helping individual behaves in a way that benefits another individual at a cost to its own fitness
• Poses a problem for individual selection
• Cost of group living: help raise offspring of dominant individuals
• Benefit of group living: less risk of being killed by a predator
• Selection arising from the indirect fitness benefits of helping relatives
• Direct fitness: Results from individual’s own success in transmitting alleles to next generation
• Indirect fitness: Results from reproductive success of other individuals that carry the same alleles

examples: Coefficient of relatedness (r): the probability that two individuals share an allele by descent. Full siblings: r = 0.5. Half siblings: r = 0.25. First cousins: r = 0.125.
Hamilton's rule: altruism evolves when rB > C, where r = relatedness, B = benefit to recipient, C = cost to actor.
Inclusive fitness: an individual's total fitness = direct fitness (own reproduction) + indirect fitness (from helping relatives reproduce).
Haldane: "I would lay down my life for two brothers or eight cousins" — illustrates rB > C logic.
Haplodiploidy in Hymenoptera: sisters are more related to each other (r = 0.75) than to their own offspring (r = 0.5), which may have facilitated eusociality in ants, bees, wasps.
warnings: Hamilton's rule is rB > C, NOT B > rC or rB > rC. ONLY the benefit is discounted by relatedness; the cost is NOT discounted. Getting the formula wrong is a common exam error.

Haplodiploidy (r = 0.75 for sisters) FACILITATES but does NOT automatically cause eusociality. Many haplodiploid species are not eusocial — haplodiploidy is one contributing factor among several (ecological constraint, nest defense, iteroparity).
popup.mnemonic: Hamilton's rule: rB > C. Relatedness × Benefit > Cost.

flashcard.front: State Hamilton's rule and use it to explain why an alarm call that alerts relatives to a predator can evolve despite putting the caller at risk.
flashcard.back: HAMILTON'S RULE (1964): an altruistic behavior can evolve whenever rB > C, where r = the COEFFICIENT OF RELATEDNESS between actor and recipient (probability of sharing an allele by descent), B = the FITNESS BENEFIT received by the beneficiary, and C = the FITNESS COST to the altruist. APPLICATION TO ALARM CALLS: Suppose a ground squirrel spots a hawk. Calling out attracts the hawk's attention (COST = increased risk of predation on the caller). But the alarm alerts nearby squirrels who escape to burrows (BENEFIT to them). If the nearby squirrels are RELATIVES (mother, siblings, cousins), they share many alleles with the caller — so genes for alarm-calling behavior benefit copies of themselves in the bodies of saved relatives. When rB > C (where r is typically averaged across all beneficiaries), alarm-calling spreads even though individual callers suffer. This is INCLUSIVE FITNESS: the actor's fitness = own direct reproduction + indirect reproduction through helped relatives. HALDANE QUOTE: "I would lay down my life for two brothers [r = 0.5, 2 × 0.5 = 1.0] or eight cousins [r = 0.125, 8 × 0.125 = 1.0]" — the math of kin selection in one joke.

quiz.question: According to Hamilton's rule, an altruistic behavior is favored when:
quiz.correct: rB > C, where r is the coefficient of relatedness, B is the benefit to the recipient, and C is the cost to the actor
distractors: B > rC — benefit exceeds cost scaled by relatedness
r + B > C — relatedness plus benefit exceeds cost
r > B/C — relatedness exceeds benefit-to-cost ratio

quiz.question: In Hymenoptera (ants, bees, wasps), females develop from fertilized eggs (diploid) and males from unfertilized eggs (haploid). A worker bee is more related to her sisters (r = 0.75) than to her own daughters (r = 0.5). How does this haplodiploidy hypothesis explain eusociality using Hamilton's rule?
quiz.correct: Rearing sisters (r = 0.75) propagates MORE copies of the worker's alleles than producing daughters (r = 0.5) would — so the rB term for helping sisters exceeds the rB term for direct reproduction, making worker sterility fitness-neutral or even favored
distractors: Males are haploid, so they carry only one copy of every gene; workers help the queen to protect the genetic integrity of the haploid males from mutation accumulation
Haplodiploidy raises relatedness between all colony members including males (r = 0.75), so any helping behavior among nestmates automatically satisfies rB > C
Worker bees have r = 0 to their brothers, so Hamilton's rule predicts they should harm brothers while helping sisters — eusociality arises from conflict avoidance within the colony

quiz.question: J.B.S. Haldane said "I would lay down my life for two brothers or eight cousins." What is the quantitative logic behind this statement?
quiz.correct: Two brothers each share r = 0.5 with Haldane, so 2 × 0.5 = 1.0 expected allele copies saved; eight cousins each share r = 0.125, so 8 × 0.125 = 1.0 — in both cases rB = 1.0 equals the C = 1.0 of sacrificing his own life
distractors: Two brothers can replace Haldane's reproductive output of two offspring, so the fitness cost is exactly balanced — cousins are included as extra insurance against accidental death
Haldane was referring to inclusive fitness of the FAMILY UNIT, not to allele-sharing — two brothers plus eight cousins together constitute a full social unit worth preserving
The calculation uses r = 1.0 for brothers and r = 0.25 for cousins, so two brothers (2 × 1.0 = 2.0 > 1.0) and eight cousins (8 × 0.25 = 2.0 > 1.0) both EXCEED the death cost with room to spare

quiz.question: Many haplodiploid insect species are NOT eusocial. What does this tell us about the haplodiploidy hypothesis for eusociality?
quiz.correct: Haplodiploidy increases relatedness among sisters and makes eusociality easier to evolve via kin selection, but it is neither necessary nor sufficient — other factors (ecological constraints, nest defense, iteroparity) must also be present
distractors: It proves haplodiploidy is irrelevant to eusociality and that eusociality must be explained entirely by reciprocal altruism among unrelated nest-mates
Haplodiploid species that are not eusocial must have unusually high dispersal rates that break up kin groups before inclusive fitness benefits can accumulate
Since most haplodiploid species lack eusociality, Hamilton's rule cannot apply to haplodiploid organisms — eusociality in Hymenoptera must have a non-kin-selection explanation

quiz.question: W.D. Hamilton published his foundational paper on inclusive fitness in what year, and what was its title?
quiz.correct: 1964 — "The Genetical Evolution of Social Behaviour" (in two parts, published in the Journal of Theoretical Biology). This paper formalized kin selection and introduced the rB > C rule
distractors: 1859 — "On the Origin of Species." Darwin himself formalized kin selection as part of his theory
1976 — "The Selfish Gene." Hamilton's ideas were first widely published by Richard Dawkins in this book
1930 — "The Genetical Theory of Natural Selection." Hamilton worked with R.A. Fisher to develop inclusive fitness

quiz.question: Calculate the coefficient of relatedness (r) for full siblings.
quiz.correct: r = 0.5 — full siblings share both parents; on average, half of their alleles are identical by descent because each inherits 50% of each parent's genome independently
distractors: r = 1.0 — full siblings share both parents and therefore share all their genes
r = 0.25 — full siblings share only a quarter of their genes because each parent contributes only half a genome
r = 0.125 — full siblings are equivalent to first cousins in terms of relatedness

quiz.question: What is the coefficient of relatedness (r) between first cousins, and between half-siblings?
quiz.correct: First cousins: r = 0.125 (they share one grandparent pair, and their common alleles are halved twice). Half-siblings: r = 0.25 (they share one parent, so half the alleles of full siblings)
distractors: First cousins: r = 0.25; half-siblings: r = 0.5 — these are the same as full siblings and first cousins reversed
First cousins: r = 0.5; half-siblings: r = 0.5 — both have the same relatedness as full siblings
First cousins: r = 0.0625; half-siblings: r = 0.125 — the values are halved because of additional generational distance

quiz.question: INCLUSIVE FITNESS is defined as:
quiz.correct: An individual's DIRECT fitness (from its own reproduction) PLUS its INDIRECT fitness (from helping relatives reproduce, weighted by relatedness r) — it is the total contribution of an individual's genes to the next generation, whether through its own offspring or through relatives' offspring
distractors: The total offspring produced by all members of a family group, divided by the number of members — it is a per-capita measure of group reproductive success
The lifetime reproductive success of an individual, including offspring from all matings but excluding any contributions from relatives
The number of alleles an individual contributes to the population, counted by direct descent only and excluding indirect contributions

quiz.question: Hamilton's rule is rB > C. If a ground squirrel can save 4 nieces/nephews (r = 0.25 each) by giving an alarm call that has a 30% chance of killing the caller, should alarm-calling evolve? (Assume 1 unit of fitness is 1 offspring equivalent)
quiz.correct: Yes — B = 4 saved relatives × 0.25 r = 1.0 expected gene copies; C = 0.3 (30% chance of death × 1 unit of self); rB = 1.0 > C = 0.3, so alarm calling is favored by kin selection
distractors: No — C = 1 (death), rB = 1.0, so rB is NOT greater than C and alarm calling is selectively neutral
Yes — C = 0.3 and B = 4, so B is much greater than C regardless of relatedness; kin selection always favors saving multiple individuals at any cost
No — saving non-offspring relatives never increases fitness because only direct descendants count in Darwinian selection

quiz.question: If altruism evolves via kin selection, what predicts the DIRECTION of helping behavior within a family group?
quiz.correct: Help should flow preferentially toward the MOST related individuals — an individual should prefer to help full siblings (r = 0.5) over half siblings (r = 0.25) over cousins (r = 0.125), with cost-benefit analysis also shaped by the ages and reproductive values of potential recipients
distractors: Help should be distributed equally among all family members regardless of relatedness — fairness rather than genetic calculation drives cooperation
Help should flow only to direct offspring — Hamilton's rule does not apply to sibling or cousin relationships because they are not genealogical descendants
Help should flow to less related individuals because more related individuals are already genetically close and gain less benefit from assistance

quiz.question: L1 RECALL: Give one classic example of kin selection behavior in mammals discussed in the lecture.
quiz.correct: Meerkat sentry behavior — individual meerkats take turns standing guard and calling alarm when predators approach, at personal risk, while their close kin forage safely. Because sentries and foragers are typically highly related within a group, rB > C is satisfied and alarm-calling is maintained by kin selection
distractors: Solitary hunting in tigers — tigers help unrelated individuals at high cost
Territorial defense in lions — lions help strangers defend territories regardless of relatedness
Long-distance migration in wildebeest — migration is driven by kin-selected altruism

quiz.question: L5 SYNTHESIS: Sterile worker bees cannot reproduce yet devote their lives to helping their mother (the queen) raise sisters. Integrate haplodiploidy, relatedness calculations, and Hamilton's rule to explain why sterile helping is evolutionarily favored, AND explain why haplodiploidy alone is not sufficient.
quiz.correct: HAPLODIPLOIDY: males haploid (1 chromosome set), females diploid. Full sisters share: (a) 100% of the father's haploid genome + (b) 50% of the mother's genome, averaging r = 0.75. Because sisters share MORE genes (r = 0.75) than workers would share with their own daughters (r = 0.5), helping a sister raise MORE sisters propagates worker genes MORE efficiently than direct reproduction would — Hamilton's rule (rB > C) is satisfied with high r. NOT SUFFICIENT: many haplodiploid species (most bees, wasps, ants) are NOT eusocial. Ecological factors — stable nest defense opportunities, iteroparity, protected larval development sites — must also be present. Termites (diploid-diploid) are eusocial without haplodiploidy, showing kin-selection logic works beyond haplodiploidy alone
distractors: Worker bees help because individual selection favors sterility — any bee that attempted to reproduce would be killed by the queen
Haplodiploidy is the sole cause of eusociality and any eusocial species must be haplodiploid
Sterile workers violate Hamilton's rule and persist only by phylogenetic inertia

quiz.question: L4 ANALYSIS: A biologist observes an altruistic behavior (C = 0.1) that yields a benefit (B = 0.3) to a cousin (r = 0.125). Does Hamilton's rule favor this altruism?
quiz.correct: No — rB = 0.125 × 0.3 = 0.0375, which is LESS than C = 0.1. Hamilton's rule requires rB > C (0.0375 > 0.1 is FALSE), so altruism toward a cousin at this cost-benefit ratio is NOT favored by kin selection. The relatedness is too low to compensate for the cost
distractors: Yes — rB = 0.125 > C = 0.1, so altruism is favored
Yes — any altruism toward relatives satisfies Hamilton's rule regardless of numerical values
Yes — the cost is discounted by relatedness (rC = 0.0125) which is less than B = 0.3, so it is favored

visual.mnemonic: Two brothers or eight cousins.
visual.trap: Altruism doesn't require conscious calculation — the rB > C condition operates through selection over evolutionary time.

diagram.title: Hamilton's Rule and Kin Selection

nodes.label: Altruism paradox
nodes.detail: Why would selection favor behaviors that REDUCE the actor's own fitness? A naive view of natural selection predicts altruism should always be lost. Yet alarm calls, helpers at the nest, and self-sacrificing workers exist throughout the animal kingdom.

nodes.label: Direct fitness
nodes.detail: Own reproductive output via personal offspring. Each of your offspring carries your alleles with probability r = 0.5 (for diploid sexual reproduction). This is the component captured by classical Darwinian fitness.

nodes.label: Indirect fitness
nodes.detail: Reproductive output of relatives that carry the same alleles by common descent. Helping a full sibling reproduce propagates your alleles at effective rate r = 0.5 per sibling offspring. This is the component Darwin missed.

nodes.label: Inclusive fitness
nodes.detail: The sum of direct and indirect fitness — the full accounting of an allele's propagation through the population (Hamilton 1964). The unit selection actually "sees" when weighing cooperative vs selfish behavior.
nodes.mnemonic: inclusive = me + my kin

nodes.label: Coefficient of relatedness r
nodes.detail: Probability that two individuals share an allele identical by descent. Offspring and full siblings r = 0.5. Half siblings and nieces/nephews r = 0.25. First cousins r = 0.125. Computed from the pedigree using Wright's path method.

nodes.label: rB > C
nodes.detail: Altruism is favored when (relatedness × benefit to recipient) > (cost to actor). The formal inequality that packages kin selection into one line of algebra. Hamilton derived it in his 1964 paper.
nodes.mnemonic: rB beats C
nodes.watchOut: rB > C, NOT B > rC and NOT rB > rC — only the BENEFIT is discounted by relatedness. The cost to the actor is unconditional.

nodes.label: Haldane: 2 brothers or 8 cousins
nodes.detail: 2 × 0.5 = 1 (break-even for a sibling). 8 × 0.125 = 1 (break-even for a cousin). Haldane's pub-napkin quip that captured kin selection decades before Hamilton formalized it.

nodes.label: Haplodiploidy + eusociality
nodes.detail: In Hymenoptera (ants, bees, wasps) males are haploid and females are diploid. Full sisters share r = 0.75 (higher than r = 0.5 to their own offspring), while brothers are only r = 0.25. This asymmetry may have facilitated the repeated evolution of eusociality in this clade.
nodes.watchOut: Haplodiploidy FACILITATES but does NOT automatically cause eusociality — many haplodiploid species are not eusocial, and termites are eusocial without it.

nodes.label: Meerkat helpers at the den
nodes.detail: Non-breeding meerkats babysit, feed, and teach pups from the dominant pair — usually siblings or half-siblings. Helpers gain indirect fitness and also reduced individual predation risk from group living. Clutton-Brock long-term Kalahari study is a textbook demonstration.

nodes.label: Peromyscus brother sperm
nodes.detail: Related sperm from a single male aggregate and swim faster than unrelated sperm. Kin selection operating at the gamete level — rB > C in miniature. Only observed in promiscuous species (P. maniculatus) where sperm competition exists, not in monogamous sisters (P. polionotus).

diagram.mnemonic: rB > C. Relatedness × Benefit > Cost.

flashcards.front: Define inclusive fitness and its two components.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Inclusive fitness is an individual's total genetic contribution to the next generation, comprising: (1) Direct fitness -- genes passed through the individual's own reproduction, and (2) Indirect fitness -- genes passed through the enhanced reproduction of relatives (who share alleles identical by descent), weighted by relatedness r. Inclusive fitness = direct fitness + indirect fitness. This concept (Hamilton 1964) explains why organisms sometimes help relatives at a personal cost: the indirect fitness gained through relatives' success can offset the direct cost.

**ELI5:** Inclusive fitness counts ALL the copies of your genes that make it to the next generation -- both through your own babies (direct) AND through your relatives' babies that you helped produce (indirect). It's like measuring your total contribution to a family business: what you earn yourself PLUS the sales your siblings make because of your help. Hamilton showed that helping family can be genetically 'profitable' even if it costs you personally.

flashcards.front: State Hamilton's Rule and define each variable.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Hamilton's Rule: rB > C, where r = coefficient of relatedness between the altruist and the beneficiary, B = fitness benefit to the recipient of the altruistic act, C = fitness cost to the altruist. An altruistic behavior will be favored by selection when the benefit to the recipient, discounted by relatedness, exceeds the cost to the altruist. IMPORTANT: only B is multiplied by r, NOT C, because the cost is paid by the altruist (who is 100% related to itself).

**ELI5:** Hamilton's Rule: r x B > C. r = how closely you're related (siblings = 0.5). B = how much your help boosts the other person's baby count. C = how much helping costs you in babies. Help your sibling if: 0.5 x their baby gain > your baby loss. The key trick: only the BENEFIT gets multiplied by relatedness, not the cost -- because you always pay 100% of your own costs.

flashcards.front: What are the coefficients of relatedness (r) for: parent-offspring, full siblings, half-siblings, and cousins?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Parent-offspring: r = 0.5 (share half of alleles identical by descent via direct inheritance). Full siblings: r = 0.5 (share half through both shared parents). Half-siblings: r = 0.25 (share one parent, so half of the full-sibling relatedness). First cousins: r = 0.125 (share one pair of grandparents out of four). These values assume outbred, diploid organisms. r measures the probability that a randomly chosen allele in one individual is identical by descent with an allele in the other.

**ELI5:** How related are you? Parent/child = 0.5 (you're half mom, half dad). Brother/sister (same parents) = 0.5 (same mix of mom and dad genes). Half-sibling (one parent shared) = 0.25 (half the connection). Cousins = 0.125 (share grandparents but not parents). These numbers tell you the 'genetic discount rate' -- how much helping them is worth compared to helping yourself.

flashcards.front: What is the famous Haldane quote about kin selection, and what does it mean?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** J.B.S. Haldane reportedly quipped: 'I would lay down my life for two brothers or eight cousins.' This captures Hamilton's Rule: your r with a brother is 0.5, so 2 brothers carry 2 x 0.5 = 1.0 copies of your alleles (equivalent to yourself). Your r with a cousin is 0.125, so 8 cousins carry 8 x 0.125 = 1.0 copies. Self-sacrificing for 2 brothers or 8 cousins breaks even genetically -- more than that, and altruism is favored.

**ELI5:** Haldane joked he'd die to save 2 brothers or 8 cousins. The math: each brother shares half your genes, so 2 brothers = 2 x 0.5 = 1.0 full 'you' saved. Each cousin shares 1/8 of your genes, so 8 cousins = 8 x 0.125 = 1.0 full 'you' saved. Die for less than that? Bad deal genetically. Die for more? Your genes actually come out ahead. It's cold, mathematical love.

flashcards.front: Why is it incorrect to discount BOTH B and C by relatedness in Hamilton's Rule?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Only B (the benefit to the recipient) is discounted by r because the altruist is related to itself with r = 1.0 -- the cost is fully borne by the altruist's own reproduction. The relatedness coefficient adjusts how much of the recipient's fitness gain translates to shared allele propagation for the altruist. If you discounted C by r too, you would undercount the actual cost to the altruist and overpredict when altruism should evolve. The correct formulation is: indirect benefit (rB) must exceed direct cost (C).

**ELI5:** You always pay 100% of your own bill -- you can't get a relatedness discount on YOUR costs. If helping your brother costs you 3 babies, you lose 3 babies, period. But the 10 babies your brother gains count as 0.5 x 10 = 5 genetic 'equivalents' for you. So 5 > 3, altruism wins. If you wrongly discounted your cost too (0.5 x 3 = 1.5), you'd think everything was worth it when it's not.

flashcards.front: How do meerkats exemplify kin-selected altruism?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Meerkats (Suricata suricatta) live in family groups where subordinate individuals (often older siblings) serve as sentinels -- standing guard and alarm-calling while others forage. Sentinel behavior is costly (lost foraging time, increased predation risk) but benefits the group, which consists largely of close relatives (r = 0.25-0.5). By Hamilton's Rule, the indirect fitness gained through protecting kin can exceed the direct fitness cost, making sentinel behavior a kin-selected altruistic trait.

**ELI5:** Meerkat babysitters stand on their hind legs watching for eagles while the family eats. The guard misses meals and could get snatched, but it's protecting its brothers, sisters, nieces, and nephews. Since the whole group is family, saving them saves copies of the guard's own genes. The guard 'pays' with missed lunch but 'earns' by keeping its genetic relatives alive.

flashcards.front: How does the Peromyscus maniculatus sperm cooperation example relate to kin selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In deer mice, sperm from the same male (r = 0.5 between haploid sperm sharing a diploid father) cooperate by forming aggregate trains that swim faster. Sperm from different males (r ~ 0) do not cooperate. This is kin-selected cooperation at the cellular level: individual sperm sacrifice their own fertilization potential by joining trains (some will not fertilize the egg) but increase the probability that a brother sperm (sharing ~50% of their genome through the common father) reaches the egg first. rB > C is satisfied among brother sperm.

**ELI5:** Sperm from the same dad are brothers (r = 0.5), and they team up like a rowing crew -- the ones in the back of the train help propel the front ones even though only one will actually fertilize the egg. They refuse to team up with 'stranger' sperm from other dads. It's kin selection at the tiniest scale: even individual cells cooperate with relatives and compete with non-relatives.

flashcards.front: Explain haplodiploidy and how it affects relatedness among sisters in Hymenoptera.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In haplodiploid species (Hymenoptera: ants, bees, wasps), females are diploid (2n, from fertilized eggs) and males are haploid (n, from unfertilized eggs). Sisters share ALL of their father's alleles (he's haploid, so he gives the same set to every daughter) plus on average half of their mother's alleles. This gives sisters r = 0.5 (from father) + 0.25 (from mother) = 0.75. Sisters are more related to each other (0.75) than to their own offspring (0.5), which may favor helping raise sisters over having daughters.

**ELI5:** In ants and bees, dad only has one set of genes (he's haploid), so he gives the EXACT same set to every daughter. Mom gives a random half as usual. Result: sisters share 75% of their genes -- more than a mother shares with her own children (50%)! This means a worker bee's genes are better served by raising more sisters than by having her own babies. That's a big part of why worker bees give up reproduction to help the queen.

flashcards.front: Why is haplodiploidy considered a facilitating factor for eusociality but not sufficient on its own?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Haplodiploidy creates asymmetric relatedness (sisters r = 0.75 > parent-offspring r = 0.5) that makes helping the queen raise sisters potentially more genetically profitable than independent reproduction -- facilitating the evolution of sterile worker castes. However, haplodiploidy is NOT sufficient because: (1) many haplodiploid species are NOT eusocial (most parasitoid wasps, many bees), and (2) eusociality has evolved in diploid species (naked mole rats, termites). Other factors (ecology, nest architecture, progressive provisioning) are also necessary.

**ELI5:** Haplodiploidy makes sisters super-related (75%), which helps explain why worker bees give up having babies to help the queen. BUT -- many haplodiploid species are NOT eusocial (lots of solitary wasps), and some non-haplodiploid species ARE eusocial (naked mole rats). So haplodiploidy is like a helpful head start, not the whole answer. It greases the wheels but doesn't drive the car.

flashcards.front: An altruist (r = 0.25 with the recipient) performs an act that costs 2 offspring and benefits the recipient by 10 offspring. Does Hamilton's Rule predict this behavior will be favored?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Apply Hamilton's Rule: rB > C. r = 0.25, B = 10, C = 2. rB = 0.25 x 10 = 2.5. C = 2. Since 2.5 > 2, YES, the altruistic act is favored by kin selection. The altruist loses 2 units of direct fitness but gains 2.5 units of indirect fitness (through the recipient's enhanced reproduction, weighted by their shared relatedness). Net inclusive fitness change = +0.5.

**ELI5:** Let's check the math: relatedness = 0.25 (half-sibling maybe), benefit = 10 extra babies for the relative, cost = 2 fewer babies for the helper. Hamilton's formula: 0.25 x 10 = 2.5 genetic 'credits' earned. Cost = 2 genetic 'credits' spent. 2.5 earned > 2 spent = good deal! The helper's genes come out ahead, so evolution says 'do it.' It's like spending $2 to earn $2.50 -- small but positive return.

flashcards.front: An altruist (r = 0.125 with the recipient) performs an act that costs 5 offspring and benefits the recipient by 30 offspring. Is this favored?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Hamilton's Rule: rB > C. r = 0.125, B = 30, C = 5. rB = 0.125 x 30 = 3.75. C = 5. Since 3.75 < 5, NO, this altruistic act is NOT favored. Despite the large benefit to the cousin, the cost to the altruist exceeds the indirect fitness gained. The altruist's inclusive fitness would decrease by 1.25 units. Selection would favor not performing this act.

**ELI5:** Check the math: relatedness = 0.125 (cousin), benefit = 30 extra babies for cousin, cost = 5 fewer babies for helper. Hamilton says: 0.125 x 30 = 3.75 'credits' earned. Cost = 5 'credits.' 3.75 < 5 = bad deal! Even though your cousin gains a lot, you're too distantly related for it to be worth your sacrifice. You'd lose more than you'd gain genetically. Evolution says 'skip this one.'

flashcards.front: What is 'altruism' in the biological sense, and how does it differ from everyday usage?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In biology, altruism is defined strictly in terms of fitness effects: a behavior that reduces the actor's direct fitness while increasing the recipient's direct fitness. It does NOT require conscious intent, emotional motivation, or awareness. The actor need not 'want' to help -- the classification depends solely on fitness consequences. This differs from everyday usage, where altruism implies selfless motivation and conscious choice. Biological altruism can evolve via kin selection without any cognition.

**ELI5:** In biology, 'altruism' just means: you lose babies, someone else gains babies, because of what you did. It doesn't matter if you're a mindless ant or a human saint -- if the FITNESS math works out that way, it's altruism. You don't need to feel generous or even understand what you're doing. A worker bee is 'altruistic' not because she's noble, but because she gives up her own reproduction to help the queen. Intent doesn't matter; only the numbers do.

flashcards.front: In a haplodiploid species, what is a female's relatedness to her brother?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In haplodiploidy, a female's relatedness to her brother is r = 0.25. Brothers are haploid (developed from unfertilized eggs), so they carry only the mother's genes. Sisters share their father's complete genome (r = 0.5 via father) plus half their mother's genome on average (r = 0.25 via mother). But a sister shares with her brother ONLY through their mother: the probability that a maternal allele in the sister is also in the brother is 0.5, and only half the sister's genome is maternal, giving r = 0.5 x 0.5 = 0.25.

**ELI5:** In ant and bee math, sisters are SUPER related to each other (0.75) but only distantly related to brothers (0.25). Why? Brothers only got mom's genes (dad didn't contribute). Sisters share ALL of dad's genes plus some of mom's. Brothers are basically half-strangers compared to sisters. This is why worker ants prefer raising sisters to brothers when they can control the sex ratio -- sisters are a better genetic investment.

flashcards.front: How does Hamilton's Rule explain why organisms sometimes sacrifice reproductive opportunities to help relatives?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Hamilton's Rule (rB > C) shows that foregoing personal reproduction can be genetically profitable if the indirect fitness gained through helping relatives exceeds the direct fitness lost. The key insight is that genes for helping can spread if they cause their carriers to boost the reproduction of other individuals who are likely to carry copies of those same genes (by shared descent). The gene 'doesn't care' whether it reaches the next generation through the altruist's own offspring or through the offspring of relatives.

**ELI5:** Your genes don't care WHO carries them into the next generation -- you or your sister. If helping your sister have 6 extra babies (at relatedness 0.5, that's 3 'genetic copies' of you) only costs you 2 of your own babies, your genes still come out ahead: 3 > 2. It's like investing: if you could give $2 to a savings account that returns $3, you'd do it every time. Your genes 'do the math' and sometimes it pays to help family instead of yourself.

flashcards.front: Would an allele for altruism toward non-relatives (r = 0) ever be favored by kin selection?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** No. When r = 0, Hamilton's Rule becomes 0 x B > C, which simplifies to 0 > C. Since any cost C > 0, this can never be satisfied. Kin selection cannot favor altruism toward genetically unrelated individuals because there is no indirect fitness gain. Apparent altruism toward non-kin must be explained by other mechanisms: reciprocal altruism (Trivers), mutualism (both parties benefit), manipulation, or by-product benefits. Kin selection requires r > 0.

**ELI5:** If you're helping a total stranger (r = 0), Hamilton's Rule says: 0 x any benefit = 0 credits earned. But you're still paying costs. Zero gain minus any cost = a loss. Kin selection can NEVER explain helping strangers. If animals help non-relatives, there must be another explanation: maybe the stranger helps you back later (reciprocity), or helping actually benefits you too (it's not really a cost). But pure genetic altruism toward strangers? Not through kin selection.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between eusociality, haplodiploidy, and ecological factors?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Eusociality (reproductive division of labor with sterile castes) has evolved most frequently in Hymenoptera, where haplodiploidy creates high sister-sister relatedness (r = 0.75), favoring workers to raise sisters rather than have daughters. However, haplodiploidy is insufficient alone because: (1) most haplodiploid species lack eusociality, and (2) diploid termites and naked mole rats are eusocial. Ecological factors -- such as defensible nest sites, progressive brood provisioning, high predation, and the difficulty of independent nest founding -- are necessary complements. Eusociality requires the confluence of genetic relatedness AND ecological conditions that make helping more profitable than dispersing.

**ELI5:** Eusociality needs two ingredients: (1) being closely related (haplodiploidy helps but isn't required), and (2) the right living situation. If you're a bee and your nest is amazing, food flows in, and starting a new nest alone is super dangerous, staying home and helping makes sense -- especially when your sisters share 75% of your genes. But if the ecology doesn't trap you at home (some wasps can easily start new nests), even high relatedness won't make you give up having babies. You need both the genetics AND the lifestyle.

nodes.title: Age of the Earth & Deep Time
nodes.subtitle: 4.568 billion years via radiometric dating (Lec 14 slides 1-5)

popup.heading: Lecture 14 — History of Life: Age of the Earth

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body:
Ussher (1664) used biblical chronology to date Earth to ~6,000 years. Lord Kelvin (late 1800s) calculated ~20 million years from rock-cooling rates — his math was correct, but he did not know Earth's interior generates heat via radioactive decay, so his ASSUMPTION was wrong.

Modern answer: Earth is 4.568 billion years old, determined by radiometric dating. A zircon crystal from Western Australia is dated at 4.4 By using BOTH U-235 AND U-238 as cross-checks.

Half-lives used in geochronology:
  Rb → Sr:   47 billion years
  U  → Pb:   4.5 billion years
  K  → Ar:   1.3 billion years
  C-14 → N-14: 5,730 years (useful to ~40,000 years)

Cross-checks: tree-ring dating, ice-core dating, and multiple radiometric systems all converge on the same ages — independent lines of evidence confirm deep time.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body:
• Age of the Earth was debated in Darwin’s time
• Special Creation – about 6,000 years old
• Charles Darwin – Several billion years old

CROSS-VALIDATION WITH MULTIPLE ISOTOPE SYSTEMS: Radiometric dating uses multiple isotope systems (U-Pb, K-Ar, Rb-Sr) on the same rock. Different isotope systems have different half-lives and chemical behaviors — if all converge on the same age, this eliminates the possibility of systematic contamination or error in any single system. This cross-validation approach is fundamental to the 4.54 Ga age of Earth.

USSHER vs. KELVIN: Ussher (1664) used textual authority — biblical genealogies in scripture — to calculate Earth's age as ~6,000 years. Kelvin (1897) used physical modeling (heat conduction) to estimate ~20-40 million years. Both were wrong, but for different reasons: Ussher relied on non-empirical sources; Kelvin didn't know about radioactive decay as a heat source. Modern radiometric dating gives 4.54 billion years.

examples: Special Creation: ~6,000 years (incorrect — contradicted by all physical evidence).
Darwin's estimate: several billion years based on sediment accumulation rates. Remarkably close for the 1800s.
Lord Kelvin: ~20 million years based on rock cooling rates. WRONG because Kelvin didn't know Earth's interior generates heat via radioactive decay.
Modern answer: 4.568 billion years via radiometric dating of the oldest zircon crystals (4.4 By old, Western Australia).
Counting to 1 million seconds = 11 days. Counting to 1 billion seconds = 31 years.
warnings: WATCH OUT (1): Kelvin's MATH was correct — his ASSUMPTION (that Earth was a homogeneous, non-heat-generating cooling rock) was wrong. Radioactive decay in the mantle provides a continuous internal heat source that invalidates his cooling model. Always check assumptions, not just arithmetic.

WATCH OUT (2): A half-life is a STATISTICAL property of a population of atoms, not a deterministic prediction for any individual atom. You cannot predict when a single atom will decay — only the rate at which a large population decays.
popup.mnemonic: 4.568 billion years — deep time beyond human intuition.

flashcard.front: How does radiometric dating work, and why is it trusted?
flashcard.back: Radiometric dating relies on radioactive isotopes decaying at a CONSTANT rate into daughter isotopes. The half-life (time for 50% of parent to decay) is fixed for each isotope — unaffected by temperature, pressure, or chemistry. To date a rock: (1) measure the ratio of parent to daughter isotope, (2) know the half-life, (3) calculate elapsed time. Multiple isotope systems (U-Pb, K-Ar, Rb-Sr, C-14) with different half-lives can be applied to the same sample as independent cross-checks. Zircon crystals trap uranium but exclude lead, making them excellent time capsules — the oldest known zircon (4.4 Bya, Western Australia) was dated using both U-235 and U-238 systems and gave the same age. Results also match tree rings and ice cores for recent dates.

quiz.question: Why was Lord Kelvin's estimate of Earth's age (20 million years) so far off?
quiz.correct: He didn't know that Earth's interior generates heat via radioactive decay
distractors: His math was wrong
He used the wrong isotopes
He assumed Earth was older than it is
