tooltips.plain: Bateson-Dobzhansky-Muller: alleles that each work fine alone become incompatible together in a hybrid because they never co-evolved.
Natural selection strengthens prezygotic barriers when hybrids are unfit — explains why prezygotic evolves faster in sympatry.

visual.mnemonic: "Be Happy To Play Music" for pre-mating prezygotic barriers.
visual.trap: Prezygotic evolves FASTER than postzygotic. Gametic incompatibility is PREZYGOTIC even though mating already occurred.

flashcards.front: What is the sequence of events in reproduction where isolating barriers can act?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The reproductive timeline: (1) find mate, (2) court/mate, (3) gamete transfer, (4) fertilization, (5) zygote formation, (6) embryo development, (7) offspring viability, (8) offspring fertility. Barriers can interrupt at any stage. Earlier barriers waste fewer resources and are thus more efficient at maintaining species boundaries.

**ELI5:** Making a baby has many steps: find a partner, convince them to mate, combine eggs and sperm, grow the baby, and then the baby has to survive AND be able to have its own babies. Species can be kept apart by a roadblock at ANY of these steps.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between prezygotic and postzygotic barriers?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Prezygotic barriers prevent the formation of a hybrid zygote -- they act before or during fertilization (habitat, temporal, behavioral, mechanical, gametic isolation). Postzygotic barriers act after fertilization -- the hybrid zygote forms but has reduced fitness (hybrid inviability, hybrid sterility, hybrid breakdown). Prezygotic barriers are more 'efficient' because they prevent wasted reproductive effort.

**ELI5:** Prezygotic = the date never happens or doesn't go well. Postzygotic = the date happened, but the kid has problems. It's cheaper to not go on a bad date than to raise a kid who can't have their own kids.

flashcards.front: What is the further subdivision of prezygotic barriers into pre-mating and post-mating?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Pre-mating prezygotic barriers prevent mating from occurring at all (behavioral, habitat, temporal, pollinator, mechanical isolation). Post-mating prezygotic barriers allow mating but prevent fertilization (gametic incompatibility -- sperm cannot reach, recognize, or penetrate the egg). The distinction matters because pre-mating barriers involve no physical contact while post-mating prezygotic barriers involve attempted copulation or gamete release.

**ELI5:** Pre-mating = they never even try to hook up (wrong place, wrong time, wrong dance). Post-mating prezygotic = they tried but the egg and sperm just don't work together. It's like the difference between never going to the restaurant versus going but finding nothing on the menu you can eat.

flashcards.front: Explain behavioral isolation using the firefly flash pattern example.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Different firefly species (genus Photinus) use species-specific bioluminescent flash patterns to attract mates. Each species has a unique combination of flash duration, interval, color, and flight pattern. Females only respond to the flash code of their own species. This behavioral difference prevents cross-species mating even when multiple species occupy the same habitat simultaneously. It is a pre-mating prezygotic barrier.

**ELI5:** Each firefly species has its own secret Morse code made of light flashes. Males flash their species' code, and females only answer back to the right code. Wrong code = no date. It's like a nightclub where you can only get in if you know the right password.

flashcards.front: What is habitat isolation? Describe the Rhagoletis apple/hawthorn example.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Habitat isolation occurs when two species occupy different habitats within the same geographic area, reducing encounter frequency. Rhagoletis pomonella flies originally infested hawthorn fruits but a population shifted to domesticated apples ~150 years ago. Apple and hawthorn races now mate on their respective host fruits, which differ in fruiting phenology. Since mating occurs on the host fruit, habitat preference directly reduces gene flow between races -- a classic case of ecological speciation in progress.

**ELI5:** These fruit flies mate on the fruit they grew up on. One group lives on apples, the other on hawthorns. Since they hang out on different fruits, they rarely meet and mate with each other. It's like two friend groups that only hang out at different coffee shops -- they never cross paths.

flashcards.front: What is temporal isolation? Give the coral spawning example.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Temporal isolation occurs when species reproduce at different times (different seasons, months, times of day). Many coral species on the same reef are reproductively isolated because they undergo mass spawning on different nights, often synchronized by lunar cycle, water temperature, and photoperiod. Even closely related species may spawn hours or days apart, preventing hybridization despite living side by side.

**ELI5:** Some species avoid mixing by having their babies at different times. Corals that live on the same reef broadcast their eggs and sperm into the water on different nights of the year. It's like two TV shows that air at different times -- they never compete for the same audience because they're never on at the same time.

flashcards.front: What is mechanical isolation? Describe the duck genitalia example.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Mechanical isolation occurs when differences in reproductive structures physically prevent mating or gamete transfer between species. Duck species exhibit extreme genital coevolution: males have long, spiraling, corkscrew-shaped penises while females have complex, spiraling vaginal tracts with dead-end pouches. The female tract coevolves to resist forced copulation from males of other species, acting as a lock-and-key mechanical barrier. Only conspecific males with compatible morphology successfully inseminate.

**ELI5:** Duck reproductive organs are like a lock and key -- the male's 'key' only fits the female's 'lock' if they're the same species. Females evolved twisty, maze-like anatomy to block the wrong species. It's nature's most extreme version of 'wrong puzzle piece, won't fit.'

flashcards.front: What is pollinator isolation and how does it maintain species boundaries in plants?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Pollinator isolation occurs when different plant species attract different pollinator species through differences in flower color, shape, scent, nectar composition, or UV patterns. Each pollinator faithfully visits only its preferred flower type, preventing cross-pollination. This is effectively behavioral isolation mediated through a third-party organism. Example: Mimulus cardinalis (red, tubular, hummingbird-pollinated) vs. M. lewisii (pink, wide, bee-pollinated).

**ELI5:** Two flower species stay separate by hiring different delivery services. One flower is red and tube-shaped for hummingbirds; the other is pink and wide for bees. Since hummingbirds don't visit bee-flowers and vice versa, pollen doesn't get mixed. It's like two stores that only deliver via different apps.

flashcards.front: What is gametic incompatibility? Explain using sea urchin bindin and VERL.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Gametic incompatibility is a post-mating prezygotic barrier where sperm and egg from different species cannot fuse. In sea urchins, the sperm protein bindin must bind to the egg receptor VERL (Vitelline Envelope Receptor for Lysin) for fertilization. Both bindin and VERL evolve rapidly and show species-specific molecular recognition -- sperm bindin from one species cannot attach to VERL of another species. This prevents hybridization even when gametes are released into the same water.

**ELI5:** Sea urchin sperm have a specific 'key' protein (bindin) that must match the egg's 'lock' protein (VERL). Each species has its own unique key-lock combo. Even though all the eggs and sperm are swimming in the same ocean water, the wrong key won't open the wrong lock, so no hybrid babies.

flashcards.front: What is hybrid inviability? Describe the Mimulus Hms1/Hms2 example.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Hybrid inviability is a postzygotic barrier where hybrid offspring fail to develop normally or die before reproduction. In Mimulus (monkeyflowers), the genes Hms1 and Hms2 (Hybrid Male Sterility) from different species interact negatively in hybrids. Each gene functions normally in its native genetic background, but when combined in a hybrid, the epistatic interaction causes hybrid seed inviability. This is a classic Bateson-Dobzhansky-Muller incompatibility.

**ELI5:** Each parent species has genes that work great in their own bodies. But when you mix genes from both parents in a hybrid baby, some genes clash and the baby dies. It's like combining parts from a Toyota and a Ford -- each car works fine, but a Frankenstein mix might not start.

flashcards.front: Why is the mule sterile? What type of reproductive barrier does this represent?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The mule (horse x donkey hybrid) is sterile because horses have 2n=64 chromosomes and donkeys have 2n=62, giving the mule 2n=63. During meiosis, the odd chromosome number means chromosomes cannot pair properly into bivalents, leading to unbalanced gametes and sterility. This is hybrid sterility, a postzygotic reproductive barrier. The hybrid is viable (mules are healthy) but cannot reproduce.

**ELI5:** Horses give 32 chromosomes and donkeys give 31, so the mule gets 63. When the mule tries to make eggs or sperm, it needs to split 63 chromosomes evenly -- but you can't split an odd number evenly. So the mule's reproductive cells are defective. It's like trying to divide 63 cards into two equal hands.

flashcards.front: What is hybrid breakdown and how does it differ from F1 hybrid sterility?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Hybrid breakdown occurs in the F2 generation or later -- the F1 hybrids are viable and fertile, but their offspring (F2) show reduced fitness, sterility, or inviability. This happens because recombination in the F1 creates new, untested combinations of alleles from both parental species that may be incompatible. Unlike F1 sterility (mule), the problem doesn't appear until the second generation, making it harder to detect.

**ELI5:** The first-generation hybrid kids seem fine and can have babies. But THEIR kids (the grandkids) are sickly or sterile. It's like mixing two different LEGO sets -- the first build looks okay, but when you take it apart and rebuild, the pieces don't fit together anymore.

flashcards.front: What are Bateson-Dobzhansky-Muller (BDM) incompatibilities?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** BDM incompatibilities explain how postzygotic isolation evolves without either population passing through a maladaptive intermediate. Ancestor has alleles a+b. Population 1 fixes new allele A (so A+b -- fine, since A was tested against b). Population 2 fixes new allele B (so a+B -- fine, since B was tested against a). But A and B have NEVER been tested together. In hybrids, A+B may cause inviability or sterility because these alleles are incompatible -- negative epistasis between independently evolved substitutions.

**ELI5:** Imagine two teams separately upgrading different parts of the same software. Team 1 changes Part A, and it works great with the old Part B. Team 2 changes Part B, and it works great with the old Part A. But nobody tested new-A with new-B together. When you combine both updates, the program crashes. That's a BDM incompatibility.

flashcards.front: What key finding did Coyne & Orr report about the relative speed of prezygotic vs. postzygotic isolation evolution?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Coyne & Orr (1989, 1997) analyzed data from Drosophila species pairs and found that prezygotic (pre-mating) isolation evolves FASTER than postzygotic isolation, especially in sympatric species pairs. In sympatry, prezygotic isolation was nearly complete even between closely related species, while postzygotic isolation accumulated more gradually with genetic distance. This pattern strongly supports reinforcement -- selection against hybridization drives rapid evolution of pre-mating barriers in sympatry.

**ELI5:** When two species live in the same place, they learn to avoid mating with each other really fast (prezygotic), but their hybrid babies' problems accumulate slowly (postzygotic). It's because making bad hybrid babies is wasteful, so natural selection quickly teaches them: 'don't even go on that date.' Like learning to avoid bad restaurants -- you stop going before you get food poisoning.

flashcards.front: What is reinforcement in the context of reproductive isolation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Reinforcement (also called the Wallace effect) is the process by which natural selection strengthens prezygotic isolation between two populations that produce unfit hybrids when they come into secondary contact. If hybrids are less fit, individuals who preferentially mate with their own type have higher fitness, driving the evolution of stronger pre-mating barriers. It converts postzygotic isolation into prezygotic isolation through selection. Key prediction: prezygotic isolation should be stronger in sympatry than allopatry.

**ELI5:** If two groups occasionally mate and their mixed kids do poorly, evolution rewards picky daters who stick to their own kind. Over time, the two groups get even better at avoiding each other. It's like if every time you ate at a particular restaurant you got sick -- you'd quickly learn to just not go there.

flashcards.front: Rank reproductive isolating barriers from those acting earliest to latest in the reproductive sequence.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Earliest to latest: (1) Habitat isolation -- never encounter. (2) Temporal isolation -- not active at same time. (3) Behavioral isolation -- reject as mate. (4) Mechanical isolation -- can't physically copulate. (5) Gametic incompatibility -- sperm/egg don't fuse. (6) Hybrid inviability -- embryo dies. (7) Hybrid sterility -- offspring sterile. (8) Hybrid breakdown -- F2+ fitness reduced. Each successive barrier wastes more reproductive effort than the one before it.

**ELI5:** The barriers go from 'never met' to 'met but not attracted' to 'attracted but parts don't fit' to 'parts fit but egg won't accept sperm' to 'baby dies' to 'baby lives but can't reproduce' to 'grandkids fail.' The earlier the barrier kicks in, the less energy wasted.

flashcards.front: Why do bindin and VERL proteins evolve so rapidly in sea urchins?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Bindin and VERL are under strong positive selection (diversifying selection) driven by sexual conflict and/or reinforcement. Females benefit from species-specific fertilization to avoid wasting eggs on hybrid matings. Males are under selection to match the evolving female receptor. This creates a coevolutionary arms race within species, while simultaneously driving divergence between species. The rapid evolution of these reproductive proteins is a general pattern -- reproductive proteins are among the fastest-evolving in the genome.

**ELI5:** Egg proteins keep changing to avoid being fertilized by the wrong species' sperm. Sperm proteins chase the changes. It's like a lock that keeps getting new tumblers and a key that keeps getting filed to match -- within a species they stay compatible, but between species they quickly become incompatible.

flashcards.front: How does the Rhagoletis system demonstrate both habitat isolation and temporal isolation simultaneously?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Rhagoletis pomonella apple and hawthorn races are isolated by BOTH habitat preference (mating on different host fruits) AND temporal differences (apples fruit ~3 weeks earlier than hawthorn). Flies emerging from apple hosts are synchronized to apple fruiting phenology, while hawthorn flies match hawthorn phenology. The combination of these two prezygotic barriers substantially reduces gene flow, even though the races live in the same geographic area (sympatric).

**ELI5:** Apple flies and hawthorn flies are kept apart by two walls: they hang out on different fruits (different address) AND those fruits ripen at different times (different schedule). Even if a fly wandered to the wrong fruit, it would show up at the wrong time anyway. Double barrier, double protection.

flashcards.front: What evidence would distinguish reinforcement from other causes of prezygotic isolation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The hallmark of reinforcement is reproductive character displacement: prezygotic isolation is stronger in sympatry (where species overlap) than in allopatry (where they don't). If pre-mating barriers evolved for other reasons (drift, adaptation to different environments), there would be no systematic difference between sympatric and allopatric populations. Coyne & Orr's Drosophila data showed exactly this pattern -- sympatric species pairs had stronger pre-mating isolation than allopatric pairs of similar genetic distance.

**ELI5:** If picky mating evolved just because groups drifted apart, it would be equally strong whether they live together or apart. But if pickiness is STRONGER when they live together (because mixing is punished), that's reinforcement. It's like being extra careful about locking your door when you live in a rough neighborhood vs. a safe one.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between genetic distance and the accumulation of postzygotic barriers in Drosophila?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** In Drosophila, postzygotic isolation accumulates gradually and roughly linearly with genetic distance (as a proxy for divergence time). Hybrid inviability appears before hybrid sterility in females, and hybrid sterility appears earlier in males (Haldane's Rule). The gradual accumulation reflects the progressive buildup of BDM incompatibilities -- as more substitutions occur in each lineage, more incompatible gene combinations arise, following a 'snowball' effect (incompatibilities accumulate faster than linearly).

**ELI5:** The longer two species have been apart, the worse their hybrids are. First the hybrid boys become sterile, then the girls, then the babies start dying. It's like two groups editing the same document separately -- the longer they work alone, the more conflicting changes pile up.

flashcards.front: What is Haldane's Rule and how does it relate to reproductive barriers?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Haldane's Rule (1922) states that when one sex of hybrid offspring is inviable or sterile, it is more often the heterogametic sex (XY males in mammals/flies, ZW females in birds/butterflies). This occurs because recessive incompatibility alleles on the X chromosome are exposed in the heterogametic sex (which has only one X) but masked in the homogametic sex (which has two X copies). It explains why hybrid male sterility typically appears before hybrid female sterility.

**ELI5:** In hybrids, the sex with mismatched sex chromosomes (like XY males in mammals) tends to have problems first. It's because they only have one copy of the X chromosome, so if it has a 'bad' gene from the other species, there's no backup copy to compensate. Females with XX have a spare copy to cover for problems.

flashcards.front: How can mechanical isolation and gametic incompatibility both be considered 'lock-and-key' mechanisms but at different scales?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Mechanical isolation operates at the organismal/anatomical scale -- physical structures (genitalia, flower morphology) must be compatible for mating to occur. Gametic incompatibility operates at the molecular scale -- protein-protein interactions between sperm and egg surface molecules (bindin-VERL) must be compatible for fertilization. Both follow a lock-and-key logic of species-specific complementarity, but mechanical isolation prevents sperm transfer while gametic incompatibility prevents sperm-egg fusion.

**ELI5:** It's the same idea at two different zoom levels. Mechanical isolation is like doors and keys -- the physical shapes must match to get in. Gametic incompatibility is like passwords -- even if you get through the door, the molecular 'password' on the sperm must match the egg's 'security system.' Both say: wrong match, no entry.

flashcards.front: Why are prezygotic barriers considered more 'efficient' than postzygotic barriers from a fitness perspective?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Prezygotic barriers are more efficient because they prevent wasted reproductive investment. With postzygotic barriers, both parents invest time, energy, and gametes in producing offspring that are inviable or sterile -- a total fitness loss. With prezygotic barriers, individuals avoid mating with the wrong species entirely, preserving their reproductive resources for conspecific matings. This efficiency differential drives reinforcement -- selection favors the evolution of prezygotic barriers to avoid costly postzygotic failures.

**ELI5:** It's cheaper to not go on a date with the wrong person than to marry them, have a kid, and find out the kid can't have kids of their own. Prezygotic barriers save you the trouble early. Postzygotic barriers let you waste all the effort first.

flashcards.front: A botanist finds two plant species that flower at different times. Is this temporal isolation, habitat isolation, or both?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** This is temporal isolation -- the reproductive barrier is the difference in flowering time (phenology). Even if the species grow in the same habitat, they cannot cross-pollinate because their flowers are not receptive simultaneously. However, if the species also grow in different microhabitats (e.g., wet vs. dry soil), BOTH temporal and habitat isolation could be operating. In practice, multiple barriers often act simultaneously, and the relative contribution of each can be quantified.

**ELI5:** If two flowers bloom at different times, that's temporal isolation -- they can't swap pollen because they're never open at the same time. It's like two stores that are never open during the same hours. They could also grow in different spots (habitat isolation too), but the timing difference alone is enough to keep them apart.

flashcards.front: Explain how coral mass spawning represents both a breakdown of isolation (within species) and maintenance of isolation (between species).
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Within a species, synchronized mass spawning breaks down isolation by ensuring that all conspecific gametes are released simultaneously, maximizing fertilization success in the dilute ocean environment. Between species, isolation is maintained because different species spawn on different nights or at different times within the same night, separated by hours or days. Gamete viability is short-lived, so even small temporal offsets effectively prevent hybridization. The same mechanism (precise spawning timing) serves both functions.

**ELI5:** All members of one coral species release their eggs and sperm at the exact same moment -- this guarantees they find each other in the vast ocean (good for same-species mating). But different species do this on different nights, so their gametes never meet (bad for hybridization). It's like synchronized flash mobs -- your team all shows up at 8pm Tuesday, theirs at 8pm Thursday. Perfect coordination within, perfect isolation between.

flashcards.front: How do pre-mating barriers relate to the concept of species recognition systems?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Pre-mating barriers are often the behavioral manifestation of species recognition systems -- the signals and receivers organisms use to identify conspecific mates. These include visual signals (plumage, coloration), acoustic signals (bird song, frog calls, cricket chirps), chemical signals (pheromones), and tactile signals (mating dances). The recognition system is a two-part mechanism: the signal and the preference/recognition template. Divergence in either component creates pre-mating isolation.

**ELI5:** Every species has its own dating app profile with specific features -- a unique song, smell, color, or dance. Potential mates have a mental checklist of what to look for. When two species' 'profiles' and 'checklists' no longer match each other, they stop recognizing each other as potential mates. They're effectively invisible to each other on the dating market.

nodes.title: Speciation Models
nodes.subtitle: Allopatric, parapatric, sympatric, ecological + allopolyploidy (Ch 13)

popup.heading: Chapter 13 — Speciation Models

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Four models: (1) ALLOPATRIC — geographic barrier splits population (MOST COMMON mode). (2) PARAPATRIC — adjacent populations with reduced gene flow along an environmental gradient. (3) SYMPATRIC — divergence without geographic barrier; requires strong disruptive selection. Controversial but documented: Rhagoletis pomonella apple/hawthorn host races in sympatry. (4) ECOLOGICAL SPECIATION — reproductive isolation evolves as a byproduct of divergent selection on different niches. ALLOPOLYPLOIDY produces INSTANT speciation via chromosome doubling — best documented in plants (Tragopogon; bread wheat hexaploid) but also insects, frogs, fish, some mammals. REINFORCEMENT: selection favors stronger PREZYGOTIC barriers in sympatry when hybrids are unfit. ISOLATION BY DISTANCE: populations spread across a gradient diverge gradually.

sections.label: Speciation = Cladogenesis — The 4 Stages
sections.body:
Speciation = cladogenesis — one lineage splits into two or more independently
evolving lineages.

Stage 1: Continuous gene flow, no barriers — one species.
Stage 2: Minor barriers emerge → partial genetic discontinuity.
Stage 3: Stronger barriers → more discontinuity (still reversible).
Stage 4: Complete, IRREVERSIBLE reproductive isolation = new species.

Key: If secondary contact occurs BEFORE stage 4, gene flow can erase divergence
and populations remerge into one species.

sections.label: Allopatric Speciation — Most Common
sections.body:
• What: A GEOGRAPHIC BARRIER splits a population. Both halves evolve independently
• via drift, mutation, and selection. Eventually diverge enough that reproductive
• isolation develops — even if the barrier later disappears.
Order of events:
• Geographic separation → Genetic divergence → Reproductive isolation
• Example: A river shifts and cuts across a salamander population. Two halves
• accumulate different alleles over thousands of generations. River dries up —
• they meet again but don't recognize each other as mates. → New species.
• Why most common: geographic barriers are abundant; they stop ALL gene flow
• simultaneously, making divergence most efficient.

sections.label: Parapatric Speciation
sections.body:
What: Barrier only PARTIALLY separates populations — gene flow is reduced but
not eliminated. Populations at the contact zone can still interbreed, but diverge
if selection + drift are STRONGER than the gene flow keeping them connected.

When it works: Strong differential selection across a habitat gradient (e.g.,
different soil types, elevations) can overcome low-level gene flow.

Example: Heavy-metal tolerant plant populations at mine tailings adjacent to
normal soil populations — diverge despite some pollen exchange at the boundary.

sections.label: Sympatric Speciation
sections.body:
What: NO geographic barrier. Same location. Individuals begin mating
nonrandomly — preferring genetically or phenotypically similar mates.
Two groups diverge in the same geographic range.

Mechanism: Disruptive selection + assortative mating.

Classic example: Rhagoletis flies (Robbins lecture)
• Originally bred exclusively on hawthorn trees in North America.
• ~140 years ago, some individuals switched to apple trees (apples introduced).
• Key mechanism: some hawthorn flies were ALREADY emerging slightly earlier.
  That earlier emergence matched when apples became ripe. Low competition for
  the new resource → higher fitness for early-emerging apple users → genetic
  component of emergence timing spread rapidly through the apple-using population.
• Now: hawthorn-race and apple-race flies prefer their own host species.
• Same field, different host = assortative mating = reproductive isolation beginning.
• No geographic barrier — FULLY SYMPATRIC. Robbins: "They watched it happen."

Why controversial: Requires strong disruptive selection to overcome the
homogenizing effect of gene flow within the same range. Hardest model to prove.
Robbins: "Micro-allopatry argument — maybe they're only truly in the same
place at a landscape scale, but at a local scale they're on different trees."

sections.label: Ecological Speciation
sections.body:
What: Reproductive barriers evolve as a BYPRODUCT of adapting to different
environments or ecological niches.

Two populations specialize on different prey, soil types, host plants, etc.
This:
1. reduces contact between them,
2. makes hybrids unfit for either niche,
3. may trigger REINFORCEMENT.

REINFORCEMENT (Robbins definition): When two previously allopatric populations
come back into secondary contact and their hybrids have low fitness, natural
selection REINFORCES (confirms and strengthens) the reproductive isolation.
It's "reinforcing their divergence" — now they can't hybridize even if they try.
Robbins: "It's also confirming it — there's no way they're gonna become one
species again because they can't. It's called reinforcement."

Key: Reproductive isolation emerges FROM ecological divergence, not random drift.

ISOLATION BY DISTANCE: No explicit barrier, but individuals disperse only short
distances. Gene flow decreases as a function of distance across the range.
Populations near each other = genetically similar; far apart = divergent

sections.label: Allopolyploidy — Instant Speciation (mostly plants)
sections.body:
• What: Two DIFFERENT species hybridize. Hybrid is sterile (chromosomes from two
• species cannot pair in meiosis). But if the hybrid ACCIDENTALLY DUPLICATES its
• entire genome, every chromosome now has a pairing partner → meiosis works →
• NEW SPECIES potentially in ONE GENERATION.
Step-by-step:
• Species A (2n=4) × Species B (2n=6)
• → Sterile hybrid (2n=5, chromosomes cannot pair)
→ Genome doubling
• → Fertile allotetraploid (2n=10, all chromosomes paired)
= NEW SPECIES
• Real example: Tragopogon salsify (plants, North America)
• T. dubius × T. pratensis → new species T. miscellus (~100 years ago)
• T. dubius × T. porrifolius → new species T. mirus (~100 years ago)
• Scale: POSSIBLY HALF of all ~300,000 flowering plant species originated through
allopolyploidy.
EXAM TRAP: "Allopolyploidy occurs ONLY in plants" → FALSE. Also found in
• insects, fish, amphibians, and some mammals — just rare outside plants.

examples: Allopatric — Galápagos finches: Ancestral finch population colonized islands. Each island population evolved independently. Secondary contact in some islands confirms reproductive isolation — separate species formed.

Sympatric — Rhagoletis flies: Same geographic range; hawthorn-race and apple-race flies diverged within ~150 years of host shift. Host plant = assortative mating criterion → sympatric speciation in real time.

Allopolyploidy — wheat: Bread wheat (Triticum aestivum, 6n=42) is a natural hybrid of three different grass species. Three rounds of hybridization + genome doubling over thousands of years produced the wheat we eat.

Ecological speciation — threespine stickleback: Marine (open-water) and lake (benthic) ecotypes of Gasterosteus aculeatus have evolved reproductive isolation driven by divergent habitat use — a textbook ecological speciation example.
warnings: EXAM TRAP: "Geographic isolation = new species under BSC" — FALSE. Geographic isolation is necessary but NOT sufficient. Reproductive isolation must ALSO develop.
EXAM TRAP: Allopatric speciation ORDER — Geographic separation → genetic divergence → reproductive isolation. NOT "isolation develops first, THEN separation occurs."
EXAM TRAP: Allopolyploidy is plants-only → FALSE. Rare but documented in insects, frogs, fish, and mammals.
Sympatric speciation is the most CONTROVERSIAL model — requires strong disruptive selection within a single freely mixing population.
popup.mnemonic: Speciation modes: "APPLE" = Allopatric, Parapatric, Polyploidy, Lateral (Lineage/Ecological), sympatric (Extra). Allopatric = wall between them. Parapatric = wall with a crack. Sympatric = no wall but they stop dating. Allopolyploidy = sterile baby somehow has double the chromosomes and is now a new species.

flashcard.front: What is allopolyploidy and why can it produce a new species in one generation?
flashcard.back:
Allopolyploidy = hybridization of TWO DIFFERENT SPECIES followed by doubling of the entire hybrid genome.

1. Two species (A: 2n=4, B: 2n=6) hybridize → sterile hybrid (2n=5, chromosomes from different species cannot pair in meiosis).
2. Genome doubles (via unreduced gametes or spontaneously) → 2n=10.
3. Every chromosome now has a homologous pair → meiosis works → FERTILE.

The new polyploid is reproductively isolated from BOTH parent species:
• Cross with species A (2n=4) → offspring with odd chromosome number → sterile.
• Cross with species B (2n=6) → same problem.
Therefore it can ONLY breed with other individuals of the same polyploid genotype → independently evolving lineage = new species by definition.

Real example: Tragopogon salsify — two new species arose in North America within the last ~100 years. Possibly half of all flowering plant species originated this way.

quiz.question: The Isthmus of Panama closed ~3 mya, splitting Atlantic and Pacific populations of snapping shrimp. Molecular data show the sister pairs diverged right at the closure event. Knowlton (1993) then placed Atlantic and Pacific pairs together in the lab — they refused to interbreed. Why is the LAB MATING EXPERIMENT considered a stronger test of allopatric speciation than molecular divergence data alone?
quiz.correct: Genetic divergence alone can reflect neutral drift or local adaptation without reproductive isolation — the BSC definition requires that the populations CANNOT interbreed. Lab mating tests directly verify that the geographic barrier produced COMPLETE reproductive isolation, not just genetic difference
distractors: Molecular clocks are unreliable for marine invertebrates, so mating experiments are needed to establish the true divergence time independently of the uncertain geological closure date

Shrimp have very low mutation rates, so any genetic divergence detected after only 3 million years must reflect balancing selection rather than allopatric divergence — behavioral tests rule out this alternative
The Isthmus closure changed ocean temperature and salinity on both sides, so behavioral experiments are needed to rule out ecological speciation as the cause rather than simple geographic isolation

quiz.question: In allopatric speciation, geographic isolation is NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT for speciation. What must additionally occur for two geographically separated populations to become separate species under the BSC?
quiz.correct: Reproductive isolation must develop while the populations are separated — through accumulated genetic divergence (mutation, drift, or local selection). If they come back into secondary contact before reproductive isolation is complete, gene flow can erase the divergence and merge them back into one species
distractors: The geographic barrier must persist long enough for one population to go extinct and be replaced by the other — speciation requires one lineage to outcompete and replace the other rather than two separate lineages both persisting

The populations must accumulate at least 2% nucleotide divergence — this threshold corresponds to the point where BDM incompatibilities become strong enough to prevent fertilization in nearly all crosses between the two populations

One population must colonize a new ecological niche during the period of separation — allopatry alone can produce only subspecies, while ecological divergence is required to produce fully separate species under the BSC

quiz.question: Sympatric speciation via the Rhagoletis host-shift has been challenged with the "micro-allopatry" argument. What is the argument, and how does it attempt to reduce sympatric speciation to allopatric speciation?
quiz.correct: At the landscape scale Rhagoletis races share the same field (sympatric), but at the local scale each race lives on a different tree species — flies rarely encounter heterospecific hosts. Critics argue that individual apple and hawthorn trees act as micro-allopatric patches, so the divergence is really allopatric at the scale where mating decisions are made
distractors: The micro-allopatry argument claims that Rhagoletis races are not truly diverging — the observed host preference differences are purely phenotypic plasticity without a genetic basis, so no speciation is occurring and the apparent race divergence will disappear if hosts are mixed

The micro-allopatry argument is that allopatry always precedes sympatric divergence — Rhagoletis populations must have been geographically separated during the last glacial maximum and secondary contact produced the host-race divergence, not sympatric host-shift selection within a continuously interbreeding population

Micro-allopatry refers to the temporal rather than spatial separation of host races — because apple and hawthorn flies emerge at different times, their temporal isolation is equivalent to geographic isolation, and the speciation process is therefore classified as allopatric by definition

quiz.question: Bread wheat (Triticum aestivum) has 6n=42 chromosomes and originated through two rounds of allopolyploidy involving three different ancestral grass species. Why is bread wheat reproductively isolated from ALL three of its parental species?
quiz.correct: Crossing wheat (6n=42) with any parental species produces offspring with an odd, unpaired chromosome number — the resulting hybrid has some sets from wheat and some from the parent that cannot pair in meiosis, making the hybrid sterile. Wheat can only reproduce with other wheat-ploidy individuals, making it an independently evolving lineage by definition
distractors: Bread wheat is reproductively isolated from its parents because the polyploidy process triggered a genome-wide wave of BDM mutations — the rapid genome doubling introduced thousands of new allele combinations that are incompatible with the parental gene backgrounds

Bread wheat is isolated from its parents because humans have selectively bred it for thousands of years, accumulating so many artificial selection mutations that gene flow with wild progenitors is prevented by hybrid breakdown in F2 populations grown without agricultural conditions

Bread wheat is only reproductively isolated from two of the three parental species — it retains the full chromosome set of the most recently added parental genome and can still exchange pollen with that donor species in experimental crosses, though this rarely occurs in nature

quiz.question: Parapatric speciation requires that STRONG DIFFERENTIAL SELECTION across a habitat gradient be able to overcome the homogenizing effect of ongoing gene flow at the contact zone. In the heavy-metal plant example (mine tailings adjacent to normal soil), what specific balance makes parapatric divergence possible?
quiz.correct: Plants on mine tailings experience extremely strong selection for metal tolerance (non-tolerant individuals die); plants on normal soil experience selection against metal-tolerance alleles because those alleles carry fitness costs when not needed. The selection differential at the boundary is strong enough to maintain genetic divergence despite pollen exchange between adjacent populations
distractors: Parapatric speciation works in this case because pollen from mine-tailings plants is physically heavier than normal pollen and cannot be carried by wind to normal-soil populations — the weight difference creates a physical barrier that blocks gene flow at the boundary

The parapatric plants actually evolve gametic incompatibility — their pollen tubes cannot grow in the stigmas of the adjacent soil type, creating a prezygotic barrier that functions at the boundary without requiring any geographic separation

Parapatric divergence is actually impossible in plants and has only been documented in animals — the heavy-metal plant case was later reclassified as sympatric speciation because plants cannot have discrete parapatric contact zones

quiz.question: Allopolyploidy in Tragopogon salsify produced two new species (T. miscellus and T. mirus) in North America within the last ~100 years. What makes this case especially powerful evidence that allopolyploidy can produce speciation in a single generation?
quiz.correct: The formation of these species can be DATED precisely — T. dubius, T. pratensis, and T. porrifolius were all introduced to North America as garden escapes in the early 20th century, and the new allopolyploid species appeared within decades; researchers could track the origin of entirely new species in real-time, something nearly impossible for slower speciation modes
distractors: The Tragopogon case is powerful because the allopolyploids are phenotypically identical to their parent species — this proves that new species can form without any morphological change, overturning the assumption that speciation requires visible phenotypic divergence

The power of the Tragopogon case comes from DNA barcoding — the new species were distinguished from their parents using mitochondrial markers, validating DNA barcoding as the primary tool for detecting allopolyploid speciation in all flowering plants

The Tragopogon case is unique because the species arose through autopolyploidy (doubling within a single species) rather than allopolyploidy — it provides the first evidence that single-species genome doubling is a viable speciation mechanism in plants

quiz.question: The threespine stickleback (Gasterosteus aculeatus) exists in sympatric marine (open-water) and benthic (lake-bottom) ecotypes that have evolved reproductive isolation. This is a textbook example of ECOLOGICAL speciation. What distinguishes ecological speciation from pure allopatric speciation?
quiz.correct: In ecological speciation, reproductive barriers evolve as a BYPRODUCT of adaptation to different ecological niches — divergent selection on traits like body shape, feeding apparatus, and mate preferences directly causes reproductive isolation without requiring a geographic barrier. In pure allopatric speciation, isolation can evolve via neutral drift alone during geographic separation, independent of ecological divergence
distractors: Ecological speciation specifically requires TWO stages: first allopatric separation for millions of years, then ecological divergence after secondary contact — without an initial allopatric phase, ecological speciation cannot occur because gene flow would prevent any divergence

Ecological speciation is distinguished by producing hybrids that are ecologically intermediate (unable to survive in either parental environment), while allopatric speciation produces hybrids with random phenotypes that can survive in any environment they encounter

The two modes are identical in mechanism and differ only in terminology — "ecological speciation" is a modern name for any case of allopatric speciation that happens to involve ecological differences between the separated populations

quiz.question: ISOLATION BY DISTANCE describes a situation where no discrete barrier exists, but individuals disperse only short distances, so gene flow decreases gradually with geographic distance across a species' range. Why can isolation by distance alone eventually produce speciation without any geographic event?
quiz.correct: In isolation-by-distance populations, genetic divergence accumulates at the ENDS of the range because mutations and drift in one end rarely reach the other end before being replaced; given enough time and range length, populations at opposite ends can become reproductively isolated even though intermediate populations are still connected — producing a "ring species" pattern where the two ends are separate species despite a chain of interbreeding populations between them
distractors: Isolation by distance cannot actually produce speciation — without a discrete barrier, gene flow always exceeds the divergence rate, so any populations along a continuous range will remain a single species indefinitely regardless of how large the range is

Isolation by distance produces speciation only when combined with strong reinforcement — selection must specifically favor shorter-range dispersers so that the populations become physically separated, at which point the mechanism becomes indistinguishable from allopatric speciation

Isolation by distance produces rapid speciation because mutation rates are highest in populations at the edges of a species range — edge populations accumulate new alleles faster than central populations and diverge into separate species within a few generations

quiz.question: Allopatric speciation is considered the most common speciation mode in animals, while sympatric speciation is the most CONTROVERSIAL. What specific conditions must be met for sympatric speciation to overcome its main theoretical obstacle?
quiz.correct: Sympatric speciation requires DISRUPTIVE SELECTION strong enough to overcome the homogenizing effect of gene flow within a freely mixing population — selection must favor two different phenotypes (e.g., host preference in Rhagoletis) while selecting against intermediate forms, AND a mechanism for assortative mating must link mate choice to the disruptive trait so that the two forms stop interbreeding
distractors: Sympatric speciation requires that the population first experience a genetic bottleneck — small founder populations can diverge through drift even in the face of gene flow, which is why sympatric speciation is usually associated with founder effects rather than selection

Sympatric speciation requires the population to be polyploid — only polyploid organisms can sustain the chromosomal divergence needed to overcome gene flow in a sympatric setting, which is why sympatric speciation is nearly restricted to flowering plants

Sympatric speciation occurs whenever a new mutation arises that affects mate choice — a single mutation in a mate-recognition gene is always sufficient to trigger sympatric speciation regardless of the selection environment

quiz.question: Allopolyploidy is common in flowering plants (possibly ~half of all species) but rare in animals. Why can allopolyploidy still produce new animal species despite being rare, and which animal groups have documented allopolyploid species?
quiz.correct: Allopolyploidy has been documented in insects, frogs, fish, and even some mammals — it is rare because most animals have separate sexes with chromosomal sex determination, which is disrupted by genome doubling. But in groups where sex determination is flexible or species can reproduce via parthenogenesis, allopolyploid speciation can occasionally succeed, producing real cases of one-generation speciation outside of plants
distractors: Allopolyploidy cannot actually produce animal species — every claimed animal allopolyploid has turned out to be a misidentified hybrid with diploid chromosome number; allopolyploidy is strictly a plant phenomenon

Allopolyploidy in animals only works for organisms that lack a circulatory system — without blood, the metabolic problems of doubled genome size do not prevent viability, which is why some insects and small invertebrates can be allopolyploid but no vertebrates can

Allopolyploidy in animals only occurs in species with multiple mating types (three or more sexes) — because standard male/female systems cannot accommodate doubled genomes, only protists and some fungi can speciate via this mechanism

visual.description: Speciation modes compared by geographic context
visual.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 620 300" width="620" height="300">
  <rect width="620" height="300" fill="#111118" rx="8"/>
  <text x="310" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#e8e4d8">Fig 13.8 — Allopatric Speciation</text>
  <!-- Panel labels -->
  <text x="20" y="50" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#4ea8de">A</text>
  <text x="175" y="50" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#4ea8de">B</text>
  <text x="330" y="50" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#4ea8de">C</text>
  <text x="485" y="50" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#4ea8de">D</text>
  <!-- Panel A — Single population -->
  <rect x="15" y="55" width="140" height="160" rx="6" fill="#1a2218" stroke="#3a5a30" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="85" y="74" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#7fb89a">Single population</text>
  <!-- beetles — orange dots -->
  <circle cx="45" cy="100" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="70" cy="90" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="95" cy="105" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="55" cy="125" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="80" cy="140" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="110" cy="118" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="125" cy="140" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="40" cy="155" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="100" cy="88" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <text x="85" y="228" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">Free gene flow</text>
  <!-- Panel B — River appears -->
  <rect x="170" y="55" width="140" height="160" rx="6" fill="#1a2218" stroke="#3a5a30" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="240" y="74" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#7fb89a">Barrier forms</text>
  <!-- River -->
  <rect x="228" y="55" width="25" height="160" fill="#1a4060" stroke="#2a6090" stroke-width="1" opacity="0.9"/>
  <text x="240" y="145" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="8" fill="#7dd3fc" transform="rotate(-90,240,145)">RIVER</text>
  <!-- Left beetles -->
  <circle cx="185" cy="100" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="200" cy="120" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="190" cy="145" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="215" cy="100" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <!-- Right beetles -->
  <circle cx="265" cy="95" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="285" cy="120" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="275" cy="148" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <circle cx="296" cy="100" r="7" fill="#d4720c"/>
  <text x="240" y="228" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">Gene flow blocked</text>
  <!-- Arrow A→B -->
  <text x="162" y="142" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="16" fill="#555">→</text>
  <!-- Panel C — Divergence (different colors) -->
  <rect x="325" y="55" width="140" height="160" rx="6" fill="#1a2218" stroke="#3a5a30" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="395" y="74" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#7fb89a">Independent divergence</text>
  <!-- River -->
  <rect x="383" y="55" width="25" height="160" fill="#1a4060" stroke="#2a6090" stroke-width="1" opacity="0.9"/>
  <!-- Left beetles — blue, evolved differently -->
  <circle cx="340" cy="100" r="7" fill="#4ea8de"/>
  <circle cx="355" cy="120" r="7" fill="#4ea8de"/>
  <circle cx="345" cy="148" r="7" fill="#4ea8de"/>
  <circle cx="368" cy="100" r="7" fill="#4ea8de"/>
  <!-- Right beetles — red, evolved differently -->
  <circle cx="418" cy="95" r="7" fill="#e63946"/>
  <circle cx="438" cy="120" r="7" fill="#e63946"/>
  <circle cx="428" cy="148" r="7" fill="#e63946"/>
  <circle cx="450" cy="100" r="7" fill="#e63946"/>
  <text x="395" y="228" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">Drift + selection diverge</text>
  <!-- Arrow B→C -->
  <text x="317" y="142" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="16" fill="#555">→</text>
  <!-- Panel D — Barrier gone, RI developed -->
  <rect x="480" y="55" width="130" height="160" rx="6" fill="#1a2218" stroke="#3a5a30" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="545" y="74" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#7fb89a">Barrier gone — no interbreeding</text>
  <!-- Mixed beetles, different colors, don't mate — X symbols between them -->
  <circle cx="500" cy="100" r="7" fill="#4ea8de"/>
  <circle cx="515" cy="128" r="7" fill="#4ea8de"/>
  <circle cx="500" cy="155" r="7" fill="#4ea8de"/>
  <circle cx="560" cy="100" r="7" fill="#e63946"/>
  <circle cx="575" cy="128" r="7" fill="#e63946"/>
  <circle cx="590" cy="100" r="7" fill="#e63946"/>
  <!-- X between the two groups -->
  <text x="535" y="128" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="16" fill="#e63946" font-weight="bold">✗</text>
  <text x="545" y="228" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#ffc857">Reproductive isolation = 2 species</text>
  <!-- Arrow C→D -->
  <text x="472" y="142" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="16" fill="#555">→</text>
  <!-- Bottom summary -->
  <rect x="15" y="252" width="590" height="32" rx="5" fill="#1e1e28" stroke="#4ea8de" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="310" y="265" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="10" fill="#4ea8de">Geographic separation → Genetic divergence → Reproductive isolation (even after barrier removed)</text>
  <text x="310" y="278" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="10" fill="#aaa">Most common speciation mode. Geographic isolation NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT — RI must also develop.</text>
</svg>

extraSvgs.title: Fig 13.9 — Isthmus of Panama: Allopatric Speciation Test (Knowlton 1993)
extraSvgs.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 520 280" width="520" height="280">
  <rect width="520" height="280" fill="#111118" rx="8"/>

  <text x="260" y="118" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#e8c87a">PANAMA</text>
  <text x="260" y="140" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="10" fill="#c8a85a">Formed ~3 mya</text>
  <!-- Ocean labels -->
  <text x="107" y="68" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#4ea8de">PACIFIC</text>
  <text x="107" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#4ea8de">OCEAN</text>
  <text x="413" y="68" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#60b0f4">ATLANTIC</text>
  <text x="413" y="84" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#60b0f4">OCEAN</text>
  <!-- Shrimp silhouettes (simplified) -->
  <ellipse cx="90" cy="145" rx="40" ry="14" fill="#4ea8de" opacity="0.7"/>
  <text x="90" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="10" fill="#111" font-weight="bold">shrimp A</text>

  <text x="413" y="150" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="10" fill="#111" font-weight="bold">shrimp B</text>

  <text x="413" y="168" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#7dd3fc">Atlantic species</text>
  <!-- Phylogeny at bottom showing sister relationship -->
  <text x="260" y="224" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#aaa">Phylogeny (Knowlton 1993):</text>
  <!-- Simple cladogram lines -->
  <line x1="120" y1="255" x2="120" y2="240" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="160" y1="255" x2="160" y2="240" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="120" y1="240" x2="160" y2="240" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <line x1="140" y1="240" x2="140" y2="233" stroke="#aaa" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <text x="100" y="268" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#4ea8de">Pacific sp.</text>
  <text x="166" y="268" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#60b0f4">Atlantic sp.</text>
  <text x="140" y="228" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">sister!</text>
  <!-- × for lab test -->
  <line x1="310" y1="248" x2="400" y2="248" stroke="#555" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="355" y="244" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="10" fill="#ffc857">Lab mating test: ✗</text>
  <text x="355" y="260" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">refuse to interbreed</text>
  <text x="355" y="272" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">→ confirmed RI</text>
</svg>

extraSvgs.title: Fig 13.10 — Rhagoletis Fly Host-Race Temporal Isolation
extraSvgs.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 460 280" width="460" height="280">
  <rect width="460" height="280" fill="#111118" rx="8"/>
  <text x="230" y="22" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#e8e4d8">Fig 13.10 — Rhagoletis Fly Emergence Timing</text>
  <text x="230" y="40" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="10" fill="#aaa">Sympatric host-race divergence via temporal isolation</text>
  <!-- Axes -->

  <line x1="60" y1="60" x2="60" y2="210" stroke="#555" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <!-- Axis labels -->
  <text x="245" y="248" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="11" fill="#aaa">Month</text>
  <text x="18" y="135" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="11" fill="#aaa" transform="rotate(-90,18,135)">Adult emergence</text>
  <!-- X tick labels -->
  <text x="95" y="226" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">Jul</text>
  <text x="150" y="226" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">Aug</text>
  <text x="205" y="226" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">Sep</text>
  <text x="260" y="226" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">Oct</text>
  <text x="315" y="226" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">Nov</text>
  <!-- Apple-race bell curve (green, earlier) -->
  <path d="M 65,205 Q 90,200 110,180 Q 130,145 155,95 Q 175,68 200,80 Q 225,95 240,130 Q 255,165 270,190 Q 290,205 320,208 L 430,210" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none"/>
  <!-- Hawthorn-race bell curve (red/orange, later) -->
  <path d="M 65,208 Q 120,207 150,205 Q 180,200 210,180 Q 240,145 265,95 Q 285,68 310,78 Q 335,92 350,130 Q 365,168 380,192 Q 400,206 430,210" stroke="#e63946" stroke-width="2.5" fill="none"/>
  <!-- Fill under apple curve -->

  <!-- Fill under hawthorn curve -->
  <path d="M 65,208 Q 120,207 150,205 Q 180,200 210,180 Q 240,145 265,95 Q 285,68 310,78 Q 335,92 350,130 Q 365,168 380,192 Q 400,206 430,210 L 65,210 Z" fill="#e63946" opacity="0.15"/>
  <!-- Offset arrow -->
  <line x1="195" y1="80" x2="305" y2="78" stroke="#ffc857" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#rharr)" marker-start="url(#rharrl)"/>
  <defs>
    <marker id="rharr" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="5" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#ffc857"/></marker>
    <marker id="rharrl" markerWidth="6" markerHeight="6" refX="1" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M6,0 L0,3 L6,6 Z" fill="#ffc857"/></marker>
  </defs>
  <text x="250" y="72" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#ffc857">~3–4 week offset</text>
  <!-- Legend -->
  <line x1="75" y1="52" x2="100" y2="52" stroke="#22c55e" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="110" y="57" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="10" fill="#22c55e">Apple-race</text>
  <line x1="185" y1="52" x2="210" y2="52" stroke="#e63946" stroke-width="2.5"/>
  <text x="220" y="57" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="10" fill="#e63946">Hawthorn-race</text>
  <!-- Caption -->
  <rect x="60" y="258" width="370" height="16" rx="4" fill="#1e1e28"/>
  <text x="245" y="269" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#aaa">Host-plant fruiting time drives temporal isolation → reproductive barrier within same field</text>
</svg>

extraSvgs.title: Fig 13.15 — Allopolyploidy: Step-by-Step Chromosome Diagram
extraSvgs.svg:
<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 500 560" width="500" height="560">
  <rect width="500" height="560" fill="#111118" rx="8"/>
  <text x="250" y="24" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="13" font-weight="bold" fill="#e8e4d8">Fig 13.15 — Allopolyploidy: Instant Speciation</text>

  <text x="145" y="170" text-anchor="middle" font-family="Georgia,serif" font-size="9" fill="#4ea8de">gamete (n=3)</text>
