**TECHNICAL:** Continental drift (plate tectonics) explains why closely related organisms occur on widely separated continents. As Pangaea broke apart starting ~200Mya, lineages that were once continuous were rafted apart on separating landmasses. Example: ratite birds (ostriches, emus, kiwis, rheas) occur on different southern continents not because of long-distance dispersal but because their ancestors were distributed on Gondwana before it fragmented. Southern beech trees (Nothofagus) show similar patterns across South America, Australia, and New Zealand.

**ELI5:** Why do ostriches live in Africa, emus in Australia, and rheas in South America? Because their common ancestor lived on one big continent (Gondwana) that broke apart. The birds didn't fly across oceans -- the continents carried them apart like passengers on different boats leaving the same dock.

flashcards.front: List the Big Five mass extinctions with their approximate dates and severity.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) End-Ordovician (~444Mya): ~85% species lost, glaciation. (2) Late Devonian (~372Mya): ~75% species, multiple pulses, reef collapse. (3) End-Permian (~252Mya): ~96% marine, ~70% terrestrial species -- worst ever, Siberian Traps volcanism. (4) End-Triassic (~201Mya): ~80% species, volcanism, opened ecological space for dinosaurs. (5) End-Cretaceous/K-Pg (~66Mya): ~76% species, asteroid impact (Chicxulub), ended non-avian dinosaurs.

**ELI5:** Earth has had five catastrophic die-offs: (1) 444Mya -- ice age killed 85%, (2) 372Mya -- slow burn killed 75%, (3) 252Mya -- THE BIG ONE, volcanoes killed 96% of ocean life, (4) 201Mya -- volcanoes again, cleared the way for dinosaurs, (5) 66Mya -- asteroid killed the dinosaurs. Each time, life came back, but completely different.

flashcards.front: Why is the end-Permian extinction considered the most severe?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The end-Permian extinction (~252Mya) eliminated approximately 96% of marine species and 70% of terrestrial vertebrate species -- the closest life has come to total annihilation. It was caused primarily by the Siberian Traps, massive volcanic eruptions that released enormous quantities of CO2 and SO2, causing runaway greenhouse warming, ocean acidification, ocean anoxia, and ozone depletion. Recovery took 5-10 million years, far longer than any other mass extinction.

**ELI5:** The end-Permian was the worst disaster in Earth's history -- 96% of ocean species died. Massive volcanoes in Siberia erupted for millions of years, filling the atmosphere with toxic gases that heated the planet, poisoned the oceans, and destroyed the ozone layer. Life almost ended completely and took 10 million years to bounce back.

flashcards.front: What is the '6th extinction' and how does it compare to background extinction rates?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The 6th extinction refers to the current, human-driven mass extinction event. Current extinction rates are estimated at 100-1000 times the background extinction rate (~1 species per million species per year). Causes are anthropogenic: habitat destruction, invasive species, pollution, climate change, and overexploitation (HIPCO). Unlike previous mass extinctions driven by geological/astronomical events, this one is caused by a single species (Homo sapiens). If current rates continue, it could rival the Big Five within centuries.

**ELI5:** Right now, species are going extinct 100 to 1000 times faster than normal, and it's all because of humans. We're chopping down forests, polluting, introducing invasive species, and changing the climate. We're basically doing what asteroids and volcanoes did, but faster. Scientists call it the 6th mass extinction because it could be as bad as the previous five.

flashcards.front: What is the Wallace Line and what does it demonstrate?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The Wallace Line is a biogeographic boundary running through the Malay Archipelago (between Bali and Lombok, between Borneo and Sulawesi). West of the line, fauna is Asian in character (primates, tigers, elephants). East of the line, fauna is Australasian (marsupials, cockatoos). The line marks the edge of the continental shelves of Asia and Australia -- even during ice age low sea levels, a deep water channel remained, preventing most organisms from crossing. Discovered by Alfred Russel Wallace in 1859.

**ELI5:** There's an invisible line through Indonesia where the animals completely change. West of the line: monkeys, elephants, tigers (Asian animals). East of the line: kangaroo relatives, cockatoos (Australian animals). Even though the islands are close together, a deep ocean trench has kept Asian and Australian animals separated for millions of years. Wallace figured this out just by noticing the animals changed dramatically between neighboring islands.

flashcards.front: What was the Great American Biotic Interchange and when did it occur?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The Great American Biotic Interchange (GABI) occurred approximately 3 million years ago when the Isthmus of Panama formed, connecting North and South America for the first time since the Cretaceous. North American mammals (cats, bears, deer, horses, elephants) migrated south, while South American mammals (armadillos, opossums, porcupines, ground sloths) migrated north. The exchange was asymmetric: North American species were more successful in South America, causing numerous South American lineage extinctions, possibly due to North American species being competitively superior from evolving in a larger, more connected landmass.

**ELI5:** Three million years ago, a land bridge formed between North and South America, and animals from both continents crossed over. North American animals (like big cats and bears) did really well in South America, but many South American natives went extinct. It was like two schools merging -- the students from the bigger school dominated. We still see survivors: armadillos and opossums came north, while jaguars went south.

flashcards.front: What is punctuated equilibrium (Gould & Eldredge 1972)?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Punctuated equilibrium, proposed by Stephen Jay Gould and Niles Eldredge (1972), posits that most evolutionary change occurs in brief bursts of rapid speciation (punctuation) separated by long periods of morphological stability (stasis). This contrasts with phyletic gradualism, which predicts slow, continuous change. The pattern is explained by: (1) stabilizing selection maintaining morphology in large populations, (2) speciation in small, peripheral populations where change can be rapid, (3) the geological incompleteness of the fossil record favoring detection of rapid transitions.

**ELI5:** Gould and Eldredge said evolution isn't like a slow escalator (gradual change). Instead, it's like a staircase -- long flat steps (nothing changes for millions of years) interrupted by quick jumps (rapid speciation). Species stay the same for ages, then suddenly new species appear and old ones disappear, like a class photo that stays the same for years then everyone graduates at once.

flashcards.front: How did the K-Pg extinction (66Mya) lead to the adaptive radiation of mammals?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The K-Pg asteroid impact eliminated non-avian dinosaurs and many other groups, vacating ecological niches that had been occupied for ~160 million years. Small, generalist mammals that survived the extinction rapidly diversified into these newly available niches (ecological release). Within ~10 million years of the extinction, all major mammalian orders had appeared, including primates, carnivores, ungulates, bats, and whales. The extinction removed competitive incumbents, allowing mammals to radiate into body sizes and ecological roles previously monopolized by dinosaurs.

**ELI5:** For 160 million years, dinosaurs hogged all the best ecological jobs (big herbivore, top predator, etc.) and mammals were stuck as small, rat-sized creatures. Then the asteroid killed the dinosaurs, and suddenly all those jobs were open. Mammals rushed to fill every vacancy -- becoming whales, elephants, bats, primates, and everything in between. The worst day for dinosaurs was the best career fair for mammals.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between island area and species number in island biogeography?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The species-area relationship follows a power law: S = cA^z, where S is species number, A is area, c is a constant depending on the taxon and region, and z is the slope (typically 0.2-0.35 for islands). On a log-log plot, this relationship is linear. Larger islands have more species because they: (1) have more habitats, (2) support larger populations less prone to extinction, (3) present larger targets for immigrants. Doubling island area typically increases species richness by ~15-25%.

**ELI5:** Bigger island = more species, following a mathematical formula. Double the island size, and you get about 20% more species. It's like apartments -- a bigger building has more room for different tenants, and the ones already there are less likely to get evicted because they have more space.

flashcards.front: How does the theory of island biogeography apply to habitat fragments on continents?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Habitat fragments (forest patches, nature reserves, mountaintops) function as ecological 'islands' surrounded by inhospitable matrix (farmland, urban areas). Island biogeography predicts that smaller, more isolated fragments will lose species over time (relaxation). This has major conservation implications: (1) larger reserves hold more species, (2) connected reserves (corridors) function like closer islands, (3) fragmented habitats experience 'extinction debt' -- species lost slowly after fragmentation. This application was a foundation of conservation biology and reserve design.

**ELI5:** A forest surrounded by farmland works like an island surrounded by ocean. Small, isolated forests lose species over time because they're too small to support many populations and too far from other forests for new animals to arrive. This is why wildlife corridors connecting parks are so important -- they're like bridges between islands.

nodes.title: Conservation & Humans as Selective Force
nodes.subtitle: How human activity drives evolution and threatens biodiversity (Ch8)

popup.heading: Conservation & Humans as Selective Force (Chapter 8)

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Conservation biology applies evolution to preserve biodiversity. SMALL-POPULATION GENETICS: INBREEDING DEPRESSION (increased homozygosity exposes deleterious recessives); LOSS OF ADAPTIVE POTENTIAL; GENETIC DRIFT fixing mildly deleterious alleles. HUMANS AS SELECTIVE FORCE: Trophy hunting on Ram Mountain bighorn sheep selected against large-horned males — average horn size declined ~20% in 30 years. Antibiotic overuse selects for MRSA. Fishing removes large individuals then shifts life history to earlier maturity. CHEETAH BOTTLENECK (~10,000 years ago): unrelated cheetahs accept skin grafts — massive disease vulnerability. FLORIDA PANTHER GENETIC RESCUE (1995): 8 Texas cougars reversed heart defects and reproductive failure. 50/500 RULE: effective pop size >=50 prevents short-term inbreeding; >=500 maintains long-term evolutionary potential. OUTBREEDING DEPRESSION: mixing distant populations disrupts locally adapted gene combinations.

sections.label: Humans as Evolutionary Agents
sections.body: Humans impose powerful selection pressures: artificial selection in agriculture/domestication, unintentional selection via hunting (trophy hunting selects for smaller tusks/horns), pollution (heavy metal tolerance in plants), urbanization (changes in bird song frequency, moth coloration). Antibiotic and pesticide resistance are direct results of human-imposed selection.

sections.label: Conservation Genetics
sections.body: Small populations lose genetic diversity through drift, reducing adaptive potential. Inbreeding depression increases homozygosity, exposing deleterious recessive alleles. Minimum viable population (MVP) size needed to maintain evolutionary potential. Genetic rescue: introducing individuals from other populations to restore diversity (e.g., Florida panther rescue with Texas pumas, 1995).

sections.label: Extinction & the 6th Mass Extinction
sections.body: Current extinction rates are ~100-1000x background rates. Habitat destruction, overexploitation, invasive species, climate change, and pollution are the main drivers (HIPCO). Island species are disproportionately vulnerable due to small populations and naivety to novel predators. Conservation strategies must account for evolutionary processes — preserving genetic diversity, maintaining gene flow corridors, managing evolutionary potential.

quotes: "The question is not whether evolution is relevant to conservation, but whether we can afford to ignore it." — adapted from conservation genetics literature
examples: Trophy hunting of bighorn sheep in Alberta selected for smaller horns over 30 years — rams' horn size declined ~20% because large-horned males were killed before reproducing.
Florida panther (Puma concolor coryi): rescued from inbreeding depression by introducing 8 Texas pumas in 1995 — survival and genetic diversity increased dramatically.
warnings: Genetic rescue (introducing outside genes) can sometimes cause outbreeding depression if populations are locally adapted to very different environments.
Minimum viable population estimates vary widely — 50/500 rule (50 for inbreeding, 500 for evolutionary potential) is a rough guideline, not a law.
popup.mnemonic: HIPCO: Habitat loss, Invasive species, Pollution, Climate change, Overexploitation — the five horsemen of extinction.

flashcard.front: How does trophy hunting act as a selective force, and what evolutionary consequence has been documented in bighorn sheep?
flashcard.back: Trophy hunting selectively removes individuals with the largest horns/tusks before or during peak reproduction. This imposes directional selection AGAINST large ornaments. In Alberta bighorn sheep, 30 years of selective hunting of large-horned rams caused average horn size to decline ~20%. This is evolution in action — human harvesting changes allele frequencies in wild populations by non-randomly removing certain phenotypes.

quiz.question: A small, isolated population of endangered birds shows high rates of genetic diseases and low reproductive success. The evolutionary diagnosis is:
quiz.correct: Inbreeding depression — small population size increases homozygosity, exposing deleterious recessive alleles that were previously masked in heterozygotes
distractors: Genetic drift alone — allele frequency change in small populations necessarily causes disease
Founder effect — the original colonizing population was always genetically compromised
Mutation accumulation — small populations have higher mutation rates than large ones

quiz.question: Trophy hunting selectively removes large-horned male bighorn sheep. Over 40 years, average horn size in the population has decreased. This is an example of:
quiz.correct: Unintentional artificial selection — humans act as a selective force by systematically removing a heritable phenotype, shifting allele frequencies over generations
distractors: Genetic drift — removal of individuals from small populations always causes random allele frequency change
Phenotypic plasticity — rams produce smaller horns in response to the stress of being hunted
Natural selection — horn size is no longer advantageous in this environment

quiz.question: Conservation biologists debate whether to manage populations for maximum genetic diversity or for local adaptation. Which principle underlies this tension?
quiz.correct: Outbreeding depression — mixing genetically diverged populations can disrupt locally adapted gene combinations, reducing fitness in hybrids despite increasing overall heterozygosity
distractors: The Hardy-Weinberg principle — gene flow always reduces fitness by changing allele frequencies
Genetic drift — large mixed populations experience stronger drift than small pure ones
Inbreeding depression — all gene mixing is harmful regardless of genetic distance

quiz.question: Antibiotic resistance in hospitals evolves rapidly because:
quiz.correct: Bacteria have short generations, large population sizes, and high mutation rates — strong directional selection on resistance traits can sweep through the population in days
distractors: Bacteria intentionally mutate to resist antibiotics when threatened
Antibiotics cause the mutations that create resistance
Hospital environments have higher radiation levels that increase bacterial mutation rates

quiz.question: The cheetah (Acinonyx jubatus) has extremely low genetic diversity — so low that unrelated cheetahs can accept skin grafts from each other without rejection. This is traced to a population bottleneck ~10,000 years ago. What specific conservation risk does this create?
quiz.correct: Genetic monomorphism leaves the species vulnerable to novel pathogens — if a single disease emerges that exploits a common allele (e.g., a shared MHC variant), there are no resistant individuals to survive and repopulate. Low genetic diversity also limits the raw material for adaptation to environmental change
distractors: Low genetic diversity means cheetahs can only breed with close relatives, causing rapid inbreeding depression that will cause extinction within a single generation if conservation efforts cease

Cheetahs have slower reproduction rates than other big cats because low genetic diversity causes meiotic errors in gamete formation — conservation efforts must use artificial insemination to overcome this limitation

The low genetic diversity is actually beneficial — it makes cheetahs more uniform and easier to manage in captivity, and conservation programs should aim to preserve this uniformity rather than introduce new genetic variation

quiz.question: Assisted gene flow (deliberately introducing genes from warmer-adapted populations into cooler populations) is being proposed for coral reefs threatened by climate change. What evolutionary principle underlies this strategy?
quiz.correct: Populations already adapted to warmer conditions carry heat-tolerance alleles that would take many generations to evolve de novo in cooler populations — deliberately transferring these alleles provides genetic material for selection to work on, potentially allowing threatened populations to adapt faster than climate change proceeds
distractors: Assisted gene flow works by triggering DNA methylation changes that induce heritable phenotypic plasticity — the introduced alleles act as epigenetic switches rather than as standing genetic variation under selection

The strategy relies on the principle that any gene flow, regardless of source, increases adaptive potential — randomly moving individuals from any population into any other will always increase fitness in the recipient population
Assisted gene flow works by creating triploid hybrids that have greater heat tolerance than either parent — the key mechanism is polyploidy rather than the transfer of specific adaptive alleles

quiz.question: Phylogenetic diversity (total branch length on a phylogeny) is sometimes used as a conservation priority metric instead of species counts. Why might conserving phylogenetically distinctive lineages (e.g., tuatara, horseshoe crabs, coelacanths) be especially valuable?
quiz.correct: Phylogenetically distinctive species represent millions of years of unique evolutionary history — they often preserve ancient traits and genes not found elsewhere in living biodiversity; losing them removes entire branches of the tree of life, whereas losing a recently diverged species only removes a twig
distractors: Phylogenetically distinctive species are always ecologically more important than recently diverged species — they occupy keystone positions in every ecosystem they inhabit because long-lived lineages are automatically dominant

Distinctive lineages have higher extinction risk because they have fewer close relatives that could replace their ecological function — each one's extinction automatically triggers cascading ecosystem collapse

Conserving distinct lineages maximizes the rate of future speciation — ancient lineages are more likely to undergo explosive radiations, so preserving them today produces more biodiversity tomorrow than preserving recent species

quiz.question: The Florida panther population was nearly extinct by the 1990s with severe genetic defects (kinked tails, heart murmurs, male sterility). In 1995, biologists introduced 8 female Texas cougars to boost genetic diversity. Within a decade, many defects decreased and population size increased. This is an example of:
quiz.correct: Genetic rescue — introducing individuals from a genetically diverse source population to restore heterozygosity and reduce inbreeding depression in a small isolated population; the introduced alleles mask deleterious recessive mutations and improve fitness
distractors: Artificial selection — the new cougars were selected for heritable traits missing from the Florida population, and selective breeding produced the observed improvements through directional selection rather than through genetic diversity per se

Species hybridization — Texas cougars and Florida panthers are separate species, and the introduction created an interspecies hybrid population; the Florida panther is now technically extinct and replaced by a hybrid

Outbreeding depression reversal — the Florida population had been suffering from outbreeding depression, and the Texas introduction restored locally adapted alleles that had been lost through excessive gene flow

quiz.question: Atlantic cod (Gadus morhua) off Newfoundland collapsed in the 1990s due to overfishing. Decades later, the population has been slow to recover and shows earlier maturation at smaller sizes. How is this an evolutionary phenomenon, not just an ecological one?
quiz.correct: Intense size-selective harvesting favors individuals that mature early at small sizes (so they can reproduce before being caught) — this creates strong directional selection on maturation timing and body size, and the evolved smaller-maturing phenotype persists even after fishing pressure is reduced because the allele frequencies have shifted
distractors: The population is slow to recover because fishing accidentally selects for fish that avoid fishing gear — the cod have evolved behavioral intelligence to recognize nets, and fishing is simply less effective than before, rather than the fish being genetically different

The small sizes reflect phenotypic plasticity rather than evolution — cod grow to whatever size food availability allows, and reduced prey populations (not genetic change) cause the observed small mature sizes

Earlier maturation is not an evolutionary response but a random consequence of the population crash — any severely reduced population shows smaller sizes due to founder effects rather than directional selection on maturation

quiz.question: The "50/500 rule" in conservation genetics states that a minimum effective population size (Ne) of 50 is needed to prevent short-term inbreeding depression, and 500 is needed to maintain long-term adaptive potential. What is the reasoning behind these specific numbers?
quiz.correct: Ne=50 corresponds to an inbreeding rate of ~1% per generation, below which deleterious recessives are not exposed too rapidly for selection to purge them; Ne=500 corresponds to the point where new mutations arising at adaptive loci balance the loss of genetic variation through drift, preserving the population's ability to respond to environmental change over evolutionary time
distractors: The 50/500 rule is based on the number of individuals needed to maintain a stable age structure — 50 adults are sufficient for short-term reproduction while 500 individuals are needed to sustain all life-history stages simultaneously across generations

The numbers reflect the IUCN's political thresholds for classifying species as endangered or vulnerable — populations below 50 are listed as critically endangered while populations below 500 are listed as vulnerable, without any genetic basis for the specific cutoffs

The 50/500 rule was derived from observations of the minimum number of individuals needed to sustain a successful captive breeding program — 50 founders are needed to start a zoo program while 500 are needed to maintain it indefinitely

quiz.question: Trophy hunting of bighorn sheep selectively removes males with the largest horns. Data from Ram Mountain (Alberta) showed that over ~30 years, average horn size declined by ~20% due to evolutionary response. Why does this represent particularly clean evidence of evolution in action on a conservation-relevant timescale?
quiz.correct: The Ram Mountain study directly tracked individual sheep across generations, documented that horn size was heritable (h² >0), measured the selection differential from hunting kills, and observed the predicted phenotypic decline over time — this matches the quantitative genetic prediction of the breeder's equation (R = h²S) exactly, demonstrating that contemporary human activities can drive measurable evolutionary change in wild populations within decades
distractors: The Ram Mountain data actually support an ecological rather than evolutionary interpretation — the declining horn size reflects reduced nutrition from climate change during the study period, and genetic change has not been demonstrated

The horn size decline is the result of genetic drift alone — Ram Mountain has a small isolated sheep population, and any observed trait change is most parsimoniously explained by drift rather than by selection from hunting

The Ram Mountain data cannot distinguish between evolution and phenotypic plasticity, so the observed decline is not evidence of any specific mechanism — larger sample sizes from multiple populations would be needed to confirm an evolutionary interpretation

visual.description: Threats to biodiversity and conservation genetics solutions

tooltips.plain: Reduced fitness from mating between close relatives — increases homozygosity and exposes harmful recessive alleles.
Introducing individuals from another population to increase genetic diversity and reduce inbreeding depression.

visual.mnemonic: HIPCO threatens, GENETICS saves. Small populations need gene flow like drought-stressed plants need rain.
visual.trap: Conservation isn't just about saving individuals — it's about preserving evolutionary potential (genetic diversity) so populations can adapt to future challenges.

flashcards.front: What are the three main genetic problems of small populations?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** (1) Inbreeding depression -- mating between relatives increases homozygosity, exposing deleterious recessive alleles, reducing fitness (survival, reproduction, disease resistance). (2) Loss of adaptive potential -- genetic drift erodes standing genetic variation in small populations, reducing the raw material for future natural selection and adaptation to environmental changes. (3) Drift fixing deleterious alleles -- in small populations, drift can overwhelm purifying selection, allowing mildly deleterious mutations to increase in frequency and become fixed.

**ELI5:** Small populations have three genetic nightmares: (1) Everyone's related, so hidden genetic diseases come out (inbreeding). (2) There's not enough genetic variety left to adapt to future challenges (like a toolbox with only one tool). (3) Bad genes spread by random luck because the population is too small for natural selection to filter them out.

flashcards.front: How does trophy hunting of bighorn sheep illustrate human-induced evolution?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Trophy hunting selectively removes individuals with the largest horns (most desirable trophies), which are also the most fit males. Over 30 years, average horn size in trophy-hunted populations declined approximately 20%. This is because: (1) large-horned males are preferentially killed before they can maximize reproduction, (2) genes for smaller horns increase in frequency, (3) the selection differential is strong and consistent. This is a clear case of human-imposed directional selection opposing natural selection (which favors larger horns for male competition).

**ELI5:** Hunters want the biggest-horned rams as trophies, so they always shoot the ones with the biggest horns. Over 30 years, this killed off the big-horn genes, and the average horn size dropped 20%. We literally evolved smaller horns into the sheep by always picking off the biggest ones. It's like always picking the tallest flowers in a garden -- eventually, only short ones are left.

flashcards.front: How does antibiotic resistance (MRSA) exemplify evolutionary principles?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** MRSA (Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus aureus) evolution demonstrates natural selection in real time: (1) Variation exists -- some bacteria carry resistance mutations (e.g., mecA gene encoding altered penicillin-binding protein). (2) Selection pressure -- antibiotics kill susceptible bacteria, leaving resistant ones to reproduce. (3) Heritability -- resistance genes are passed to offspring and spread by HGT. (4) Adaptation -- the population evolves resistance. MRSA has become a major hospital-acquired infection because antibiotic overuse creates intense, sustained selection for resistance.

**ELI5:** MRSA is evolution happening in fast-forward. We spray antibiotics, killing 99.9% of bacteria. But the 0.1% with resistance genes survive and multiply. Now the whole population is resistant. It's the exact same process Darwin described -- variation, selection, inheritance -- just happening in days instead of millennia. Every time we overuse antibiotics, we train bacteria to beat our medicine.

flashcards.front: How does commercial fishing shift life history traits in fish populations?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Commercial fishing preferentially removes the largest, oldest fish (using minimum size limits and gear selectivity). This imposes directional selection for: (1) earlier maturation (reproduce before reaching catchable size), (2) smaller adult body size, (3) increased reproductive investment at smaller sizes, (4) faster growth to reproductive size. Studies of Atlantic cod and other commercially fished species show measurable genetic shifts toward earlier maturation over several decades. These evolutionary changes may be slow to reverse even after fishing pressure is reduced.

**ELI5:** By always catching the biggest fish, we're evolving fish to be smaller and reproduce younger. Fish that grow big get caught, so only the ones that have babies while still small pass on their genes. Over decades, the average fish gets smaller and starts having babies earlier. We're accidentally breeding fish to be hard to catch -- and these changes don't easily reverse.

flashcards.front: What is the cheetah genetic bottleneck and how was it discovered?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Cheetahs have extremely low genetic diversity -- so low that skin grafts between unrelated cheetahs are accepted without immune rejection (normally, transplants between unrelated individuals are rejected due to MHC diversity). This suggests cheetahs passed through a severe genetic bottleneck, possibly ~10,000 years ago, reducing the effective population to perhaps a few hundred individuals. Consequences include: high susceptibility to disease (feline infectious peritonitis), low sperm quality (~70% abnormal sperm), and high cub mortality.

**ELI5:** Cheetahs are so genetically identical that you can transplant skin from one to another and their body doesn't reject it -- something that normally only works between identical twins. This means cheetahs went through a catastrophe that killed almost all of them, and the survivors were so few and so related that today's cheetahs are basically all cousins. This makes them vulnerable to diseases and reproductive problems.

flashcards.front: Describe the Florida panther genetic rescue of 1995.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** By the 1990s, the Florida panther population had declined to ~20-30 individuals showing severe inbreeding depression: kinked tails, cryptorchidism (undescended testes), heart defects, and low sperm quality. In 1995, 8 female Texas cougars (same species, different subspecies) were introduced to increase genetic diversity. The result was dramatic: within a generation, survival tripled, inbreeding symptoms decreased, and the population grew to >200 by 2020. This is one of the most successful genetic rescue programs in conservation history.

**ELI5:** Florida panthers were so inbred they had bent tails, heart problems, and couldn't reproduce well -- only about 25 were left. Scientists brought in 8 female mountain lions from Texas to add fresh genes. The mix worked brilliantly: health problems dropped, kittens survived better, and the population grew from ~25 to over 200. It's like a small town's health improving after new families move in.

flashcards.front: What is the 50/500 rule in conservation genetics?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The 50/500 rule is a guideline for minimum viable population size: (1) A minimum effective population size (Ne) of 50 is needed in the short term to prevent rapid inbreeding depression (limits inbreeding rate to ~1% per generation). (2) A minimum Ne of 500 is needed in the long term to maintain sufficient genetic variation for adaptive evolution. The actual census population (N) must be larger because Ne < N (due to unequal sex ratios, variance in reproductive success, population fluctuations). The rule is approximate and debated, with some geneticists suggesting 100/1000.

**ELI5:** Conservation genetics has a minimum population rule: you need at least 50 breeding individuals to avoid inbreeding problems in the near future, and at least 500 to have enough genetic variety for long-term survival. It's like a school -- you need at least 50 students to avoid everyone being related, and 500 to have enough diversity for a good sports team, band, and debate club.

flashcards.front: What is outbreeding depression and when does it occur?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Outbreeding depression is reduced fitness in offspring resulting from mating between genetically divergent populations or species. It occurs because: (1) F1 hybrids may have intermediate phenotypes poorly adapted to either parental environment. (2) F2 breakdown can occur when recombination disrupts co-adapted gene complexes (epistatic combinations that evolved together). It is a risk when populations have been isolated long enough to develop local adaptations or chromosomal differences. This must be considered when planning genetic rescue -- introducing too-divergent individuals can be counterproductive.

**ELI5:** If inbreeding is bad because relatives are too similar, outbreeding depression is the opposite problem -- mixing populations that are TOO different. Their offspring get a mismatched set of genes adapted to different environments, like wearing a winter coat on top and shorts on the bottom. Conservationists need to find the Goldilocks zone: not too similar (inbreeding) and not too different (outbreeding).

flashcards.front: What does HIPCO stand for and what does each letter represent?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** HIPCO is a mnemonic for the five main drivers of the current biodiversity crisis: H = Habitat destruction/fragmentation (deforestation, urbanization -- the #1 threat). I = Invasive species (e.g., brown tree snake in Guam, rats on islands). P = Pollution (pesticides, plastics, nitrogen runoff, light pollution). C = Climate change (shifting ranges, phenology mismatches, coral bleaching). O = Overexploitation (overfishing, poaching, bushmeat, illegal wildlife trade). These drivers often interact synergistically.

**ELI5:** HIPCO lists the five ways humans are killing species: (H)abitat destruction -- bulldozing their homes. (I)nvasive species -- bringing in harmful foreigners. (P)ollution -- poisoning their water and air. (C)limate change -- changing the thermostat too fast. (O)verexploitation -- taking too many. Together, they're causing the 6th mass extinction.

flashcards.front: How does inbreeding depression manifest at the molecular level?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Inbreeding increases homozygosity. At the molecular level: (1) Deleterious recessive alleles that are masked in heterozygotes become expressed in homozygotes (unmasking hypothesis). (2) Heterozygote advantage (overdominance) is lost at loci where heterozygosity is beneficial (e.g., MHC immune genes where heterozygotes recognize more pathogens). (3) Loss of heterozygosity reduces individual-level genetic diversity, lowering disease resistance and environmental tolerance. The net result is reduced survival and reproduction -- measured as inbreeding depression coefficient (delta = 1 - fitness_inbred/fitness_outbred).

**ELI5:** When close relatives breed, their babies get two copies of the same gene version instead of two different ones. Bad genes that were hidden (one good copy covering for one bad copy) are now exposed (two bad copies, no cover). Plus, having variety in immune genes helps fight more diseases -- inbreeding destroys that variety. It's like having a backup for everything vs. having no backups for anything.

flashcards.front: Why might evolutionary changes caused by trophy hunting be difficult to reverse?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Evolutionary reversal is slow for several reasons: (1) Alleles for large horns may have been completely lost from the population (you can't select for what doesn't exist). (2) Without hunting, natural selection for large horns is relatively weak compared to the strong artificial selection imposed by hunters. (3) Genetic drift in the now-reduced population may further erode horn-size alleles. (4) Mutation is too slow to regenerate complex polygenic traits. (5) Epigenetic and maternal effects may maintain small-horn phenotypes. Estimated recovery time: potentially centuries even with complete hunting cessation.

**ELI5:** Once you've bred the big-horn genes out of a sheep population, getting them back is really hard. The genes for big horns may be completely gone -- evolution can't bring back what doesn't exist anymore. Without hunting, there's only a weak push toward bigger horns, and random chance in a small population works against it too. It's much faster to break something than to fix it.

flashcards.front: What lessons does the Florida panther rescue provide for conservation genetics practice?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Key lessons: (1) Genetic rescue works -- introducing even a small number of individuals from a related population can dramatically improve fitness. (2) Timing matters -- intervention should happen before the population is critically small. (3) Source population selection is important -- Texas cougars were genetically different enough to provide new variation but not so different as to risk outbreeding depression. (4) Monitoring is essential -- tracking genetic and demographic changes after introduction. (5) Genetic rescue should complement, not replace, habitat protection and other conservation measures.

**ELI5:** The panther rescue teaches us: (1) Fresh genes help a LOT. (2) Don't wait until things are desperate. (3) Pick donors that are different enough to help but not so different that mixing causes problems. (4) Keep watching what happens after the rescue. (5) Gene mixing alone won't save a species if you don't also protect their habitat. It's like giving someone a blood transfusion AND making sure they have a safe home to recover in.

flashcards.front: How does the concept of effective population size (Ne) differ from census population size (N)?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Effective population size (Ne) is the size of an idealized population that would experience the same rate of genetic drift as the actual population. Ne is almost always smaller than N because of: (1) unequal sex ratios in breeding (if 10 males mate with 100 females, Ne is closer to 10 than 110), (2) variance in reproductive success (some individuals have many offspring, others have none), (3) fluctuating population size (bottlenecks disproportionately reduce Ne), (4) overlapping generations. Typically Ne/N ranges from 0.1-0.5.

**ELI5:** The census counts every individual, but only some of them actually contribute genes to the next generation. If a population has 1000 animals but only 100 actually breed, the 'effective' population is closer to 100. It's like a company with 1000 employees but only 100 make decisions -- the actual influence is smaller than the headcount suggests. For conservation, Ne is what matters, not N.

nodes.title: Human Evolution
nodes.subtitle: Hominin phylogeny and what makes us a unique kind of ape (Ch17)

popup.heading: Human Evolution (Chapter 17)

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Hominin evolution spans ~7 million years. ARDIPITHECUS (~4.4 Mya): partial biped. AUSTRALOPITHECUS (3-4 Mya): LUCY (Au. afarensis, 3.2 Mya, Ethiopia) — bipedal pelvis, ~430 cc brain (chimp-sized). CONFIRMS BIPEDALISM BEFORE BRAIN EXPANSION. Laetoli footprints (3.6 Mya). HOMO HABILIS (~2.4 Mya): first stone tools. HOMO ERECTUS (~1.9 Mya): first major out-of-Africa dispersal, controlled fire. HOMO SAPIENS (~300 kya origin, ~195 kya modern form). NEANDERTHALS (~400-40 kya Eurasia): non-African modern humans carry ~1-4% Neanderthal DNA from interbreeding. DENISOVANS: Melanesian and Papuan populations carry ~3-6% Denisovan DNA. HOMO FLORESIENSIS (~50 kya Flores): ~1 m tall, insular dwarfism. MITOCHONDRIAL EVE: ~200 kya in Africa. OBSTETRICAL DILEMMA: human babies skull near maximum the pelvis can accommodate — trade-off. LACTASE PERSISTENCE evolved INDEPENDENTLY in multiple cattle-herding populations via different mutations.

sections.label: Definition
sections.body: Hominins = humans and our bipedal ancestors, separate from chimps ~6-7 Ma. Modern Homo sapiens evolved ~300,000 years ago in Africa. We are one of ~8 million animal species, classified within Hominidae (great apes).

sections.label: Key Derived Traits
sections.body: Bipedalism (freeing hands, earlier than large brains), large neocortex (~3x larger than body size predicts), language and symbolic thought, prolonged childhood (extended learning period), reduced sexual dimorphism vs. earlier hominins.

sections.label: Hominin Timeline
sections.body: Ardipithecus ~4.4 Ma (facultative biped). Australopithecus ~4-2 Ma (obligate biped, small brain ~450cc). Homo habilis ~2.4 Ma (stone tools, brain ~600cc). Homo erectus ~1.9 Ma (fire, migrated out of Africa). H. sapiens ~300 ka (Africa); ~60-70 ka out of Africa.

sections.label: KEY POINTS
sections.body:
DENISOVANS: Identified in 2010 from a single finger bone in Denisova Cave (Siberia). Genetically distinct from both Neanderthals and modern Homo sapiens. Denisovans interbred with anatomically modern humans — modern Melanesians, Papuans, and other Oceania populations carry 3-6% Denisovan DNA, suggesting interbreeding occurred in Southeast Asia.

HOMININ PHYLOGENY: The transition from early hominins (Ardipithecus ~4.4 Mya, Australopithecus/Lucy ~3.2 Mya) to modern Homo sapiens was NOT a linear ladder. Multiple hominin species overlapped in time and geography — they were contemporaneous, not sequential. Homo erectus, Homo neanderthalensis, Homo floresiensis, and Denisovans all coexisted with early Homo sapiens at various points. Ancestry lines are often unclear because the fossil record is fragmentary.

quotes: "We are a very peculiar kind of ape." — Zimmer & Emlen, Ch17
examples: Lucy (Australopithecus afarensis): 3.2 Ma, walked upright but had chimp-sized brain — proves bipedalism preceded brain expansion.
Neanderthal-Homo sapiens hybridization: 1-4% of non-African human genomes are Neanderthal.
warnings: Human evolution is NOT a linear chain (caveman → modern human). It's a branching bush with many coexisting species.
Bipedalism evolved ~2-3 million years BEFORE our large brains — do not assume they co-evolved simultaneously.
popup.mnemonic: ABLE: Ardipithecus → Bipedalism first. Lucy → Evidence. Brain enlargement comes Later. Ever-expanding: we interbred with Neanderthals.

flashcard.front: What does the fossil record reveal about the sequence of bipedalism and brain expansion in human evolution, and why does this sequence matter?
flashcard.back: Bipedalism evolved first (~4.4 Ma in Ardipithecus, obligate in Australopithecus ~3.5 Ma) with chimp-sized brains (~400-500cc). Brain expansion to human size began ~2 Ma with Homo habilis and continued in H. erectus and H. sapiens. This matters because it disproves the 'big brain caused bipedalism' hypothesis — upright walking freed the hands for tool use, which may have then driven selection for larger brains.

quiz.question: A fossil hominin shows a pelvis adapted for bipedal walking but a brain volume of only 430cc (similar to a chimpanzee). This fossil most likely dates from:
quiz.correct: ~3–4 million years ago, consistent with Australopithecus — bipedalism evolved before large brains in our lineage
distractors: ~1.8 million years ago, consistent with Homo erectus, which had both bipedalism and larger brains
~200,000 years ago, consistent with early Homo sapiens with modern brain size
Bipedalism and large brains always evolved together — a small-brained biped is anatomically impossible

quiz.question: Genetic evidence shows that modern humans outside Africa carry 1–4% Neanderthal DNA. The BEST interpretation is:
quiz.correct: Interbreeding occurred between Homo sapiens and Neanderthals after the out-of-Africa migration, before Neanderthal extinction ~40,000 years ago
distractors: Humans and Neanderthals share a common ancestor within the last 100,000 years — the similarity is recent common ancestry, not hybridization
Modern lab contamination introduced Neanderthal DNA into ancient samples
Neanderthal DNA entered the modern human genome through viral lateral gene transfer

quiz.question: The "aquatic ape hypothesis" proposes that human ancestors went through an aquatic phase explaining hairlessness, bipedalism, and fat distribution. Why do most paleoanthropologists reject it?
quiz.correct: The fossil record places hominins in savanna and woodland environments, not aquatic ones, and each proposed feature has better-supported alternative explanations that do not require an aquatic phase
distractors: Humans cannot swim, which contradicts the hypothesis
The hypothesis requires Lamarckian inheritance of acquired characters
DNA evidence shows humans are more closely related to savanna mammals than to aquatic mammals

quiz.question: Lactase persistence — the ability to digest milk in adulthood — evolved independently in multiple cattle-herding populations in Europe, East Africa, and the Middle East. This is evidence of:
quiz.correct: Convergent evolution driven by parallel positive selection — the same phenotype (lactase persistence) evolved via different mutations in different populations under the same selective pressure (cattle domestication)
distractors: Gene flow between cattle-herding populations spread the same allele globally
A single ancestral mutation for lactase persistence predates cattle domestication and was maintained everywhere
Cultural transmission of dairy practices caused genetic changes via Lamarckian inheritance

quiz.question: The famous hominin fossil "Lucy" (Australopithecus afarensis, ~3.2 Mya) was discovered in Ethiopia in 1974 by Donald Johanson. Lucy's pelvis and femoral angle show clear bipedalism, but her brain was only ~400 cc and she retained curved finger bones suggesting some tree-climbing ability. What does Lucy's mosaic morphology tell us about human evolution?
quiz.correct: Human evolution did not proceed as a simple linear progression toward modern form — different traits evolved at different rates and times. Bipedalism preceded brain expansion, and tree-climbing ability was retained long after bipedalism emerged, showing that hominin evolution was a patchwork of features rather than a coordinated march toward Homo sapiens
distractors: Lucy proves that all modern human features (bipedalism, large brain, tool use, reduced body hair) evolved simultaneously ~3.2 Mya — the mosaic appearance is an illusion caused by fossil reconstruction errors, and Lucy was essentially anatomically modern

Lucy demonstrates that Australopithecus was actually a dead-end lineage unrelated to modern humans — the mosaic morphology places her outside the hominin line, and she has no descendants among living species

Lucy's small brain shows that bipedalism evolved LATER than brain expansion — earlier hominins had large brains but walked on four legs, and Lucy's lineage reversed this by shrinking the brain to enable bipedalism

quiz.question: Denisovans were identified in 2010 from a single finger bone found in Denisova Cave (Siberia). They are genetically distinct from both Neanderthals and modern humans, but modern Papuans, Melanesians, and some East Asian populations carry ~3-6% Denisovan DNA. What does this pattern tell us about human-Denisovan interactions?
quiz.correct: Denisovans were a separate hominin population that coexisted with Neanderthals and modern humans, and interbreeding occurred specifically between Denisovans and the ancestors of Papuans and Melanesians — the high Denisovan ancestry in these populations, but not in Europeans or Africans, shows the interbreeding was geographically restricted to Southeast Asia or Oceania
distractors: Denisovans were the direct ancestors of all modern humans outside Africa — all non-African populations carry Denisovan DNA in proportion to their distance from Africa, with Papuans having the most because they migrated the farthest

Denisovan DNA in modern Papuans entered the human genome through a lab contamination event in the original sequencing — the 3-6% figure is an artifact and Denisovans did not actually interbreed with any modern human population

Denisovan DNA is identical to Neanderthal DNA and the two groups are actually the same species — the geographic distribution of "Denisovan" DNA reflects the range of Neanderthal populations that encountered early Pacific voyagers

quiz.question: Homo floresiensis ("the hobbit") was discovered in 2003 on Flores, Indonesia, dating to ~50,000–100,000 years ago. Adults were ~1 m tall with brain volumes of ~420 cc. What evolutionary phenomenon likely explains the small body size of H. floresiensis?
quiz.correct: Island dwarfism — large-bodied mammals on isolated islands often evolve smaller body sizes due to limited food resources and reduced predation pressure; H. floresiensis is hypothesized to have evolved from Homo erectus or earlier Homo through this common island pattern, losing body mass while maintaining relatively large brain-to-body ratios
distractors: H. floresiensis was not a new species but rather a diseased individual (microcephalic or pathological dwarf) with extremely unusual development — the small size has no evolutionary significance and does not represent a separate lineage

H. floresiensis evolved small body size through rapid directional selection for tool-making efficiency — smaller hands allowed finer tool manipulation, and this drove the entire body to shrink proportionally

H. floresiensis was a pygmy population of modern Homo sapiens that had undergone extreme inbreeding — their small size reflects loss of growth alleles through genetic drift in a tiny isolated population rather than any specific selection pressure

quiz.question: Homo erectus arose ~1.9 Mya, was the first hominin to leave Africa (reaching Asia ~1.8 Mya), and persisted until ~50,000 years ago. What anatomical and behavioral innovations distinguished H. erectus from earlier hominins?
quiz.correct: H. erectus had a modern human-like body plan (long legs, short arms, relatively large brain ~900 cc), was the first hominin to use fire and make sophisticated Acheulean hand axes, and had the endurance running physiology that enabled long-distance persistence hunting — a combination of features that allowed dispersal across Eurasia
distractors: H. erectus was the first hominin to evolve a fully modern brain size (~1400 cc) equal to Homo sapiens, and this allowed it to invent symbolic language, art, and agriculture ~1.9 Mya, long before modern humans

H. erectus was primarily characterized by its small size and arboreal lifestyle — unlike earlier hominins, it retreated to the trees and specialized as a fruit eater, which is why it could spread to tropical forests across Asia

H. erectus had a sagittal crest on the skull (a ridge for large jaw muscles) and powerful forelimbs for climbing — its adaptations are consistent with a chimpanzee-like lifestyle rather than with any modern human features

quiz.question: The "Out of Africa" model proposes that modern Homo sapiens originated in Africa ~200,000–300,000 years ago and later migrated to replace all other hominin populations globally. What mitochondrial DNA evidence originally supported this model?
quiz.correct: "Mitochondrial Eve" — the most recent common maternal ancestor of all living humans — was traced to Africa ~150,000-200,000 years ago based on mtDNA sequence diversity; African populations also show the greatest mtDNA diversity, consistent with Africa being the ancestral homeland where the lineage has existed longest
distractors: Mitochondrial DNA from ~200,000-year-old fossils in Europe and Asia matches modern African sequences exactly, proving that African mitochondria replaced all pre-existing European and Asian variants through ancient migration

Mitochondrial "Eve" was a single Homo sapiens woman who lived exactly 200,000 years ago in Africa, and all modern humans are her direct descendants — no other women at her time contributed to the modern human gene pool
The Out of Africa model was supported by the discovery that Neanderthal mitochondrial DNA is identical to modern African mtDNA, proving that Neanderthals were actually Africans who migrated to Europe

quiz.question: The hominin lineage is traditionally traced from Ardipithecus (~4.4 Mya) to Australopithecus (e.g., Lucy, ~3.2 Mya) to early Homo (H. habilis, ~2.3 Mya) to H. erectus (~1.9 Mya) to H. sapiens (~200 kya). What best describes the shape of this transition over time?
quiz.correct: The transition from early hominins to modern humans was not a linear ladder but a bushy tree with multiple contemporaneous species coexisting at various points — Australopithecus and early Homo overlapped, H. erectus overlapped with Neanderthals and H. sapiens, and the direct ancestral line of modern humans is often unclear, with many extinct side branches rather than a single progressive sequence
distractors: The hominin lineage proceeded as a strict linear progression with each species fully replacing its predecessor — at any given time only one hominin species existed, and each evolved directly into the next without any overlap or side branches

The lineage shows exponentially accelerating morphological change — Ardipithecus and Australopithecus were extremely similar, but each subsequent species shows progressively larger changes, culminating in Homo sapiens as the most derived hominin form

Modern humans are not descended from any of the listed species — H. sapiens evolved from an unknown lineage that is unrelated to Ardipithecus, Australopithecus, or early Homo, and the "hominin transition" is a classification error

quiz.question: The obstetrical dilemma proposes that human birth is difficult because of conflicting selection on pelvis width (narrow for bipedalism) and fetal head size (large for brain expansion). Human babies are born relatively altricial (underdeveloped) compared to other primates. How does this relate to the dilemma?
quiz.correct: The conflict between bipedalism and brain expansion was partially resolved by shifting brain growth from prenatal to postnatal development — human babies are born with ~25% of adult brain size, then complete brain development outside the womb, allowing mothers to give birth before the head becomes too large to fit through the pelvic canal. This extends the period of infant dependency and makes human parenting uniquely prolonged
distractors: The dilemma was resolved by evolving wider hips in females only — modern women have wider pelvises than men by as much as 50%, fully accommodating even the largest brained babies without any change in birth timing or infant development

The dilemma was resolved when modern medicine began delivering babies by c-section — before surgical intervention, the dilemma produced routine maternal death, and altricial birth is unrelated to the evolutionary response

Human babies are altricial because of reduced nutrient supply to the fetus during pregnancy — mothers cannot absorb enough nutrients to support a fully developed infant, so babies must be born early and continue growing on breastmilk, unrelated to pelvis/head conflict

tooltips.plain: Humans and our bipedal relatives after the split from chimpanzees — NOT all great apes.
Walking upright on two legs — the first major derived trait distinguishing hominins.

visual.mnemonic: ABLE: Apes → Bipedal → Larger brains → Everywhere (out of Africa). The body walked before the mind raced.
visual.trap: Human evolution is a BUSH not a ladder. Many hominin species coexisted — H. sapiens and Neanderthals overlapped for thousands of years.

flashcards.front: What is Ardipithecus and what is its significance?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Ardipithecus ramidus, dated to ~4.4 million years ago (Ethiopia), is one of the earliest known hominins. 'Ardi' was a partial biped -- it could walk upright but also retained adaptations for tree climbing (grasping big toe, long arms). Its brain was chimpanzee-sized (~300-350cc). Its canine teeth were smaller than in chimpanzees, suggesting reduced male-male aggression. Ardi demonstrates that bipedalism preceded brain enlargement and that the common ancestor of humans and chimps was NOT chimp-like.

**ELI5:** Ardi is a 4.4-million-year-old fossil that walked on two legs (sometimes) but also climbed trees like an ape. Its brain was tiny -- chimp-sized. This tells us that our ancestors started walking upright LONG before getting smart. Walking came first, big brains came later. It also shows our common ancestor with chimps didn't look like a modern chimp.

flashcards.front: Who was 'Lucy' and what did her skeleton reveal?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Lucy (Australopithecus afarensis) was discovered in 1974 in Ethiopia, dated to ~3.2Mya. Her skeleton was ~40% complete -- exceptional for a hominin fossil. Key features: (1) Brain size ~430cc (slightly larger than chimp), (2) bipedal locomotion confirmed by pelvic anatomy (short, wide ilium) and angled femur (valgus knee), (3) small body size (~1.1m tall, ~29kg), (4) retained some arboreal features (curved fingers). Lucy powerfully demonstrated that BIPEDALISM EVOLVED BEFORE BRAIN EXPANSION -- the 'brains first' hypothesis was wrong.

**ELI5:** Lucy was a 3.2-million-year-old human ancestor found in Ethiopia. She walked upright (we can tell from her hip and leg bones) but had a brain barely bigger than a chimp's. She proved the biggest surprise in human evolution: our ancestors stood up and walked BEFORE getting smart. Everyone assumed big brains came first, but Lucy showed it was walking that started our journey.

flashcards.front: What are the Laetoli footprints and why are they significant?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The Laetoli footprints, discovered in Tanzania and dated to ~3.6Mya, are a trail of hominin footprints preserved in volcanic ash. They show: (1) clear bipedal gait with heel strike and toe-off, (2) no divergent big toe (unlike apes), (3) arch structure similar to modern humans, (4) two individuals walking side by side. Attributed to Australopithecus afarensis, they provide direct behavioral evidence of habitual bipedalism in hominins, independent of skeletal anatomy. They are 400,000 years older than Lucy.

**ELI5:** In Tanzania, ancient footprints were found preserved in volcanic ash from 3.6 million years ago. They look remarkably like modern human footprints -- upright walking, no grasping toe. This is a snapshot of our ancestors literally walking across the landscape, millions of years before anyone had a big brain. It's like finding ancient shoe prints that prove someone was walking even before we have their skeleton.

flashcards.front: Explain the principle 'bipedalism before brain expansion.' What evidence supports it?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Multiple lines of evidence confirm bipedalism evolved millions of years before significant brain expansion: (1) Ardipithecus (4.4Mya): partial biped, chimp-sized brain (~300cc). (2) A. afarensis/Lucy (3.2Mya): obligate biped, brain ~430cc. (3) Laetoli footprints (3.6Mya): bipedal gait confirmed. (4) Significant brain expansion didn't begin until Homo habilis (~2.4Mya, ~600cc) and accelerated with H. erectus (~1.9Mya, ~900cc). The gap of ~2 million years between bipedalism and brain expansion refutes the 'brains first' hypothesis that dominated early paleoanthropology.

**ELI5:** Our ancestors walked upright for at least 2 million years BEFORE their brains started getting bigger. Ardi walked at 4.4 million years ago with a chimp brain. Lucy walked at 3.2 million years ago with a chimp brain. Brains didn't start growing until about 2.4 million years ago. Walking freed up our hands, which eventually led to tool use, which eventually drove brain growth. Legs first, brains second.

flashcards.front: What were the key innovations of Homo habilis?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** H. habilis ('handy man'), appearing ~2.4Mya in East Africa, is the first member of genus Homo. Key innovations: (1) first stone tool maker (Oldowan tools -- simple flaked cobbles used for cutting, scraping, and pounding), (2) brain size ~600-700cc (50% larger than australopithecines), (3) slightly more modern hand proportions allowing precision grip, (4) possible early meat eating (cut marks on animal bones). H. habilis bridges Australopithecus and later Homo, marking the beginning of the technology-brain coevolution spiral.

**ELI5:** Homo habilis, living 2.4 million years ago, was the first human relative to make stone tools -- simple rocks chipped to make sharp edges for cutting meat. Their brain was bigger than earlier species (about the size of a softball). With tools, they could eat meat more efficiently, which may have fueled further brain growth. They're the first in our family to pick up a tool and never put it down.

flashcards.front: Describe Homo erectus and its major achievements.
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** H. erectus appeared ~1.9Mya and survived until ~100kya. Major achievements: (1) First hominin to leave Africa (found across Asia and Europe by ~1.8Mya). (2) Brain size ~900-1100cc (approaching modern range). (3) Controlled use of fire (~1Mya, some evidence earlier). (4) More sophisticated tools (Acheulean hand axes). (5) Modern body proportions (long legs, short arms, similar stature to modern humans). (6) Possible early language capacity. H. erectus was the most successful hominin in terms of geographic range and temporal duration (~1.8 million years).

**ELI5:** Homo erectus was the first globe-trotter -- leaving Africa 1.8 million years ago and spreading across Asia and Europe. They had brains close to our size, tamed fire, made better tools, and had modern body proportions. They're the most successful human species ever, surviving for nearly 2 million years (we've only been around for 300,000). They proved that having a big brain + fire + tools = world domination.

flashcards.front: When did Homo sapiens originate and when do 'modern' humans appear in the fossil record?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The oldest H. sapiens fossils are from Jebel Irhoud, Morocco, dated to ~300kya, showing a mix of modern and archaic features. Anatomically modern humans (AMH) with fully modern skull morphology appear by ~195kya (Omo Kibish, Ethiopia). Behaviorally modern humans (symbolic art, complex tools, long-distance trade) appear clearly by ~50-70kya, with some evidence as early as ~100kya in Africa (Blombos Cave ochre engravings). The 'Out of Africa' migration of modern humans occurred ~60-70kya.

**ELI5:** Our species, Homo sapiens, first appeared about 300,000 years ago in Africa. By 195,000 years ago, we looked fully modern. But acting modern -- making art, complex tools, jewelry -- kicked in around 50,000-70,000 years ago. Then about 60,000-70,000 years ago, we left Africa and spread everywhere. So we've been around for 300,000 years, but only 'culturally modern' for a fraction of that time.

flashcards.front: What was the relationship between Neanderthals and modern humans?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Neanderthals (H. neanderthalensis) lived ~400-40kya in Europe and western Asia. Genomic analysis (Svante Paabo, 2010 Nobel Prize) revealed that non-African modern humans carry 1-4% Neanderthal DNA, proving interbreeding occurred during the overlap period (~50-40kya in Europe/Middle East). Neanderthals had large brains (~1400cc, larger than modern average), made tools (Mousterian), buried dead, and may have had language. They went extinct ~40kya, possibly due to competition with modern humans, climate change, or demographic disadvantages.

**ELI5:** Neanderthals were our close cousins who lived in Europe for hundreds of thousands of years. When modern humans arrived from Africa, the two groups met and sometimes had children together. Today, everyone with non-African ancestry carries 1-4% Neanderthal DNA. So Neanderthals didn't completely go extinct -- a piece of them lives on in our genes. They were smart, strong, and big-brained, but something about us (maybe better social networks or culture) gave us the edge.

flashcards.front: Who were the Denisovans and what is their genetic legacy?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Denisovans are an archaic hominin group identified primarily through DNA extracted from a finger bone found in Denisova Cave, Siberia (2010). Known from very few fossils but extensive genomic data. Denisovans interbred with modern humans: modern Melanesian populations carry 3-6% Denisovan DNA, and traces are found in other Asian and Indigenous Australian populations. Some Denisovan genes have been positively selected in modern humans, including EPAS1 (high-altitude adaptation in Tibetans). They diverged from Neanderthals ~400kya.

**ELI5:** Denisovans are a mysterious group of ancient humans known mainly from DNA in a single finger bone found in a Siberian cave. They bred with our ancestors, and today people from Melanesia (Papua New Guinea, etc.) carry 3-6% Denisovan DNA. One Denisovan gene even helps Tibetans breathe at high altitudes! We discovered an entire branch of the human family tree mostly through genetics, not fossils.

flashcards.front: What is Homo floresiensis and what caused its small size?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** H. floresiensis, discovered on the Indonesian island of Flores (2003), dated to ~100-50kya. Adults stood ~1.1m tall with brain size ~420cc (smaller than a chimp's). Despite their tiny brains, they made stone tools and hunted pygmy elephants. Their small stature is attributed to insular dwarfism -- the evolutionary tendency for large animals on islands to become smaller due to limited resources and reduced predation. H. floresiensis likely descended from H. erectus that colonized Flores and subsequently dwarfed.

**ELI5:** 'Hobbits' were real! Homo floresiensis were tiny humans (about 3.5 feet tall) who lived on an Indonesian island until about 50,000 years ago. They shrank because of 'island dwarfism' -- on small islands, big animals evolve to be smaller because there's less food. They had tiny brains but still made tools and hunted mini-elephants. They show that evolution can shrink even human relatives when resources are limited.

flashcards.front: What is Mitochondrial Eve and what does she represent?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Mitochondrial Eve is the most recent common ancestor (MRCA) of all living humans through the matrilineal line, estimated to have lived ~200,000 years ago in Africa. She is identified through mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA), which is inherited exclusively from the mother. Important caveats: (1) She was NOT the only woman alive at the time -- she is simply the one whose matrilineal line survived to the present while all others went extinct. (2) She is NOT the first H. sapiens. (3) Nuclear DNA has many different MRCAs living at many different times. (4) 'Y-chromosomal Adam' (patrilineal MRCA) lived at a different time.

**ELI5:** Mitochondrial Eve is the great-great-great-...-grandmother that ALL living humans share if you trace only through mothers. She lived about 200,000 years ago in Africa. But she wasn't the only woman alive -- she was just the luckiest in terms of having an unbroken line of daughters. It's like tracing every family's mother-to-daughter line backwards until they all meet at one woman. She's not the first human, just our common maternal ancestor.

flashcards.front: What is the obstetrical dilemma in human evolution?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The obstetrical dilemma is the evolutionary conflict between two selection pressures: (1) bipedalism requires a narrow pelvis for efficient walking/running, and (2) large brain size requires a wide birth canal. Human infants are born with brains ~25% of adult size (versus ~45% in chimps) and are extremely helpless (altricial), requiring extended postnatal care. This compromise means human birth is more dangerous than in other primates. Some recent research questions whether the pelvis is really constrained by locomotion, suggesting thermoregulation and metabolic limits may also play roles.

**ELI5:** Walking upright requires narrow hips, but big-brained babies need wide hips to be born. Evolution's compromise: human babies are born 'early' with relatively small, undeveloped brains, then do most of their brain growing outside the womb. That's why human babies are so helpless compared to, say, a newborn horse that walks within hours. Our big brains and bipedal walking created a design conflict that evolution 'solved' by making babies essentially premature.

flashcards.front: What is the significance of lactase persistence for human evolution?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Lactase persistence (the ability to digest lactose in adulthood) evolved independently at least 5 times in different human populations (European, East African, West African, Middle Eastern, South Asian). The ancestral state is lactase non-persistence -- most mammals (and most humans) lose the ability to digest lactose after weaning. Populations with a history of dairying evolved regulatory mutations near the LCT gene that keep lactase expression 'on' into adulthood. This is one of the strongest examples of recent positive selection in humans, occurring within the last ~5,000-10,000 years.

**ELI5:** Most mammals (and most humans) can't digest milk as adults -- they lose the enzyme after childhood. But populations that herded cattle and goats evolved the ability to keep digesting milk into adulthood. This happened independently at least 5 times in different parts of the world -- Europe, Africa, Middle East. It's one of the clearest examples of recent human evolution: our culture (dairy farming) changed our genes. You can literally taste evolution every time you drink milk.

flashcards.front: How does Neanderthal DNA in modern humans illustrate incomplete speciation?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The presence of 1-4% Neanderthal DNA in non-African modern humans demonstrates that H. sapiens and H. neanderthalensis were not completely reproductively isolated despite ~600,000 years of separation. Hybrid offspring were viable and fertile enough for introgressed alleles to persist. However, Neanderthal DNA is non-randomly distributed in modern genomes -- it is depleted near genes (especially brain and testes genes), suggesting some hybrid incompatibilities were purged by selection. This pattern of speciation-with-gene-flow parallels what we see in many animal species.

**ELI5:** If Neanderthals were a completely separate species, we shouldn't have their DNA. But we do (1-4%), proving they were more like a 'subspecies' -- different enough to be distinct but similar enough to interbreed. Interestingly, their DNA is mostly in less important parts of our genome -- evolution kept the useful bits and deleted the harmful ones. It's like a partial merger between two companies where only the compatible departments were combined.

flashcards.front: What evidence supports the 'Out of Africa' model for modern human origins?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** Multiple lines of evidence: (1) Oldest H. sapiens fossils are in Africa (300kya). (2) African populations have the greatest genetic diversity (expected for the oldest population). (3) Non-African populations are nested within African genetic diversity (founder effect). (4) Mitochondrial DNA and Y-chromosome phylogenies root in Africa. (5) Genetic diversity decreases with distance from Africa (serial founder effects). (6) Archaeological evidence of modern behavior first appears in Africa. The model posits a single major dispersal ~60-70kya, with subsequent global spread.

**ELI5:** All evidence points to Africa as humanity's homeland: (1) the oldest human fossils are in Africa, (2) Africans have the most genetic diversity (like the trunk of a tree has more rings than the branches), (3) the farther from Africa you go, the less diverse people are (like photocopies of photocopies losing quality). Modern humans evolved in Africa, then a small group left ~60,000 years ago and populated the rest of the world.

flashcards.front: How does insular dwarfism explain H. floresiensis, and what is the general rule?
flashcards.back:
**TECHNICAL:** The 'island rule' states that large species on islands tend to evolve smaller body size (insular dwarfism) while small species evolve larger body size (insular gigantism). Limited resources, reduced predation, and competitive release drive these shifts. H. floresiensis likely descended from H. erectus, which colonized Flores and dwarfed over time -- a ~60% reduction in body mass and ~70% reduction in brain volume. Parallel cases exist: pygmy elephants, dwarf buffalo, and island foxes all show the same pattern.

**ELI5:** On islands, big animals shrink and small animals grow. Less food and fewer predators mean big bodies are wasteful and small bodies are less risky. Homo floresiensis is a human relative that shrank from Homo erectus size over thousands of years on Flores island -- same reason island elephants became pony-sized. Islands are like fun-house mirrors for evolution: they distort body size.

nodes.title: Evolutionary Medicine
nodes.subtitle: Why evolution explains disease vulnerability and drug resistance (Ch18)

popup.heading: Evolutionary Medicine (Chapter 18)

sections.label: CORE CONCEPT
sections.body: Evolutionary medicine applies evolution to health. Four frameworks: (1) TRADE-OFFS — selection optimizes early reproduction at cost of late-life health (antagonistic pleiotropy). (2) MISMATCH — ancestral metabolism and behavior mismatched to modern industrial environments; explains obesity, T2D, allergies, myopia. (3) HOST-PATHOGEN ARMS RACES — pathogens evolve rapidly (MRSA, HIV, flu). (4) DEFENSES MISTAKEN FOR PATHOLOGY — fever, morning sickness, inflammation are evolved responses. SICKLE CELL BALANCED POLYMORPHISM: HbS homozygotes suffer anemia but heterozygotes have ~25% protection against severe malaria; in malaria-endemic regions HbS is maintained at ~10-20% frequency. MORNING SICKNESS (Profet): evolved to make pregnant women avoid toxins during first trimester. MRSA evolves rapidly in hospitals due to strong selection plus many susceptible hosts plus short generations plus large populations. CFTR cystic fibrosis mutation may have had heterozygote advantage historically (cholera or typhoid resistance). FEVER is a defense not a disease.

sections.label: Definition
sections.body: The application of evolutionary principles to understand why we are vulnerable to disease. Asks: why didn't selection eliminate this disease susceptibility? Often reveals trade-offs, mismatch, and coevolution.

sections.label: Core Frameworks
sections.body: 1) Trade-offs: sickle cell anemia protects against malaria — one allele copy is beneficial, two are harmful. 2) Mismatch: evolved for ancestral environments; modern diet causes type 2 diabetes, obesity. 3) Arms race: pathogens and hosts coevolve rapidly (Red Queen). 4) Defenses (fever, nausea): may be adaptive, not just symptoms.

sections.label: Antibiotic Resistance
sections.body: Resistance evolves by natural selection: random mutations arise, antibiotics kill susceptible bacteria, resistant survivors reproduce. MRSA, XDR-TB are direct results. Evolutionary solution: cycling antibiotics, combination therapy, phage therapy.

sections.label: KEY EXAMPLES
sections.body:
HYGIENE HYPOTHESIS: Rising rates of allergies and autoimmune disease in industrialized countries may result from reduced parasite exposure. The human immune system evolved in environments rich with parasites (especially helminths/worms). Without these stimuli, the immune system becomes dysregulated — attacking inappropriate targets (self-tissue in autoimmune disease, harmless allergens in allergies). This is an evolutionary mismatch: our immune system is calibrated for an ancestral parasite-rich environment, not modern hyper-clean conditions.

OBSTETRICAL DILEMMA: Human birth involves an unusually tight fit between the baby's head and the mother's pelvis. Bipedalism selected for narrow pelvises (efficient walking requires a narrow pelvic canal), while encephalization (brain enlargement) selected for larger infant heads. The compromise: human babies are born relatively underdeveloped (altricial) compared to other primates — the head must pass through before it gets too large, making the tight fit feasible but risky.

FOUR FRAMEWORKS OF EVOLUTIONARY MEDICINE: (1) Trade-offs — antagonistic pleiotropy, life history trade-offs. (2) Mismatch — human metabolic physiology evolved under ancestral conditions of food scarcity and high activity; modern abundance of calories and sedentary lifestyles lead to obesity, diabetes (thrifty genotype: insulin resistance was adaptive when food was scarce, maladaptive now). (3) Arms races — host-pathogen coevolution, antibiotic resistance. (4) Phylogenetic constraints — imperfect design due to evolutionary history (crossed airway, blind spot).

quotes: "Nothing in biology makes sense except in the light of evolution." — Dobzhansky, 1973 (adapted for medicine by Nesse & Williams as: 'Nothing in medicine makes sense except in the light of evolution')
examples: Sickle cell trait: heterozygotes have 10x lower malaria mortality in sub-Saharan Africa — balanced polymorphism maintained by selection.
Morning sickness peaks during organogenesis (weeks 6-18) when embryo is most vulnerable to teratogens — Profet's hypothesis: nausea protects embryo.
warnings: Not all disease symptoms are adaptive — fever above 41°C is dangerous, not beneficial. Distinguish 'designed' defense from harmful pathology.
